P_{\text{net}} = P_{\text{cut both ends}} \times P_{\text{correct orientation}}
$$

$$
P_{\text{net}} = 0.0576 \times 0.5 = 0.0288 \approx 2.88\%
$$

**Step 5:** If transformation efficiency is **10⁷ cfu/μg**, expected recombinants per μg DNA.

$$
\text{Recombinants} = 0.0288 \times 10^7 = 2.88 \times 10^5 \text{ colonies/μg}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Practical cloning yields are highly dependent on vector:insert ratio. A 1:3 vector-to-insert molar ratio is empirically optimal to maximize the correct recombinant yield.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Plasmid Vector Architecture (pUC19 Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Circular_Plasmid["pUC19 Vector - 2,686 bp"]
        ori["pMB1 Origin of Replication\n(Copy Number 500-700)"]
        ampR["Ampicillin Resistance Gene\n(beta-lactamase / bla)"]
        lacZ["lacZ Alpha Fragment\n(Blue-White Screening)"]
        mcs["Multiple Cloning Site\n(MCS) - 13 unique sites\nEcoRI, SacI, KpnI, AvaI,\nXmaI, SmaI, BamHI, XbaI,\nAccI, HincII, SalI, PstI, SphI"]
        rbs["Ribosome Binding Site"]
        term["rrnB Terminator"]
    end
    ori --- ampR
    ampR --- lacZ
    lacZ --- mcs
    mcs --- rbs
    rbs --- term
    term --- ori
```

## 4.2 Lambda Phage Vector Architecture (Replacement Vector)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Lambda_Phage["Lambda Replacement Vector (~40 kb)"]
        larm["Left Arm\n~20 kb\nContains: cos site, A gene"]
        stuffer["Stuffer Fragment\n14 kb\nREMOVED during cloning"]
        rarm["Right Arm\n~10 kb\nContains: cos site, J gene, immunity region"]
    end
    larm --- stuffer
    stuffer --- rarm
    insert["Foreign DNA Insert\n7 to 23 kb"] -.-> stuffer
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Reading Key:** The stuffer fragment is excised using a pair of rare-cutting restriction enzymes (e.g., BamHI, EcoRI). The foreign DNA replaces it. The cos sites allow concatamer formation and packaging into the phage head.

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram: Expression Vector System (pET)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Host_Cell["E. coli BL21(DE3) Host Cell"]
        lacUV5["lacUV5 Promoter\n(Chromosomal)"]
        T7pol["T7 RNA Polymerase Gene\n(Inducible by IPTG)"]
        IPTG["IPTG Inducer\n0.5 mM"]
    end
    subgraph Plasmid["pET-28a Plasmid Vector"]
        T7prom["T7 Promoter\n(Strong)"]
        lacO["lac Operator\n(Repressor binding)"]
        RBS["Ribosome Binding Site"]
        ATG["Start Codon ATG"]
        MCS2["Multiple Cloning Site"]
        HisTag["6xHis Tag\n(Optional N or C terminal)"]
        T7term["T7 Transcription Terminator"]
        KanR["Kanamycin Resistance Gene"]
    end
    subgraph Protein["Recombinant Protein Output"]
        Prot["Target Protein\n(High Yield Expression)"]
    end
    IPTG -->|Induces| lacUV5
    lacUV5 -->|Activates| T7pol
    T7pol -->|Transcribes| T7prom
    T7prom -->|mRNA| MCS2
    MCS2 -->|Translates| Prot
    T7prom --> lacO
    lacO --> RBS
    RBS --> ATG
    ATG --> MCS2
    MCS2 --> HisTag
    HisTag --> T7term
    T7term --> KanR
```

## 4.4 M13 Phage Replication Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ssDNA["M13 ssDNA\nin phage particle"] -->|Infects| Host["E. coli with F pilus"]
    Host -->|Converted to| RF["Replicative Form\ndsDNA circular"]
    RF -->|Rolling Circle\nReplication| ssDNA_New["New ssDNA"]
    RF -->|Acts as plasmid\nfor cloning| Clone["Cloning in RF form"]
    ssDNA_New -->|Secreted as| Particle["New phage particles\n(Continuous secretion)"]
    Clone -->|Isolate ssDNA| Seq["DNA Sequencing /\nSite-Directed Mutagenesis"]
```

## 4.5 Comparative Block Architecture: Plasmid vs. Phage Vectors

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Plasmid_Block["Plasmid Vector System"]
        P1["Small Circular DNA\n2-10 kb"]
        P2["Low Insert Capacity\nup to 10 kb"]
        P3["Antibiotic Selection\n+ Blue-White Screen"]
        P4["Host: E. coli\n(Transformation)"]
    end
    subgraph Phage_Block["Bacteriophage Vector System"]
        B1["Linear/Circular DNA\n10-53 kb total"]
        B2["High Insert Capacity\nup to 23 kb"]
        B3["Plaque Selection\n+ lacZ' Screen"]
        B4["Host: E. coli\n(Phage Infection)"]
    end
    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4
    B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> B4
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define a cloning vector. List any four essential features of a good cloning vector.**

**Model Answer:**

A **cloning vector** is a small, self-replicating DNA molecule (derived from plasmids or bacteriophages) used to carry and amplify a foreign DNA fragment inside a host cell.

> [!NOTE]
> **Four Essential Features:**
> 1. **Origin of Replication (ori):** Enables autonomous replication independent of the host chromosome.
> 2. **Selectable Marker:** An antibiotic resistance gene (e.g., amp<sup>R</sup>, tet<sup>R</sup>) for screening transformants.
> 3. **Multiple Cloning Site (MCS):** A short region with multiple unique restriction enzyme sites for insert ligation.
> 4. **Insertional Inactivation Site:** A marker gene (e.g., *lacZ'*) that is disrupted by the insert, allowing visual identification of recombinants (blue-white screening).
> 5. **Small Size:** 2–20 kb for easy manipulation and high transformation efficiency.

*(Any 4 features = 3 marks: Definition 1 + 4 features × 0.5 each = 3)*

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Differentiate between plasmid cloning vectors and bacteriophage λ cloning vectors with respect to (a) insert capacity, (b) host entry mechanism, and (c) screening method.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Plasmid Vector | λ Phage Vector |
|-----------|----------------|----------------|
| **(a) Insert Capacity** | Up to **~10 kb** | Up to **~23 kb** (replacement) |
| **(b) Host Entry Mechanism** | **Transformation** (chemical/electroporation) | **Phage infection** (via LamB receptor) |
| **(c) Screening Method** | **Antibiotic selection + blue/white** | **Plaque formation + insertional inactivation of cI gene** |

*(1 mark for each correct differentiation row.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam - Model Question]**

**(a)** Describe the structural features of the plasmid vector **pBR322** with a neat labelled diagram. Explain how insertional inactivation is used to identify recombinant colonies. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the construction and features of **λ phage replacement vectors**. How is size selection achieved during cloning? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question A (a): pBR322**

**Introduction (2 marks):**
pBR322 is the first-generation artificial plasmid cloning vector constructed by **Bolivar and Rodriguez in 1977**. It is a **4,361 bp** double-stranded circular DNA molecule with the following key features:

**Structural Features (3 marks):**

1. **Origin of Replication (ori):** Derived from pMB1 plasmid; allows ~15–20 copies per *E. coli* cell.
2. **Ampicillin Resistance Gene (amp<sup>R</sup> / bla):** Encodes β-lactamase enzyme that inactivates ampicillin.
3. **Tetracycline Resistance Gene (tet<sup>R</sup>):** Encodes a membrane protein that effluxes tetracycline.
4. **Multiple Cloning Sites:** Located within the **tet<sup>R</sup>** gene at sites such as *BamHI, SalI, EcoRI, HindIII, PstI, PvuI*.
5. **Copy number control elements.**

**Insertional Inactivation Principle (2 marks):**

- When foreign DNA is ligated at the *BamHI* site (within *tet<sup>R</sup>*), the tetracycline resistance gene is disrupted.
- Transformants are first grown on **ampicillin plates** → only transformants (with or without insert) grow.
- These colonies are then replica-plated on **tetracycline plates** → recombinants (with insert) are **Tet<sup>S</sup>** and **Amp<sup>R</sup>**, while non-recombinants are **Tet<sup>R</sup>** and **Amp<sup>R</sup>**.

**Result:** Amp<sup>R</sup> Tet<sup>S</sup> colonies = recombinants.

---

#### **Solution to Question A (b): λ Phage Replacement Vectors**

**Introduction (2 marks):**
The λ bacteriophage contains a **48.5 kb** linear dsDNA genome. The middle third of this genome is **non-essential** for lytic growth, allowing it to be replaced with foreign DNA.

**Construction (2 marks):**

1. The λ genome is modified to **remove unnecessary restriction sites** to leave only a few unique cloning sites.
2. The middle non-essential region (e.g., the *red* and *gam* genes) is flanked by a pair of rare-cutting restriction sites (e.g., *BamHI*).
3. This middle region is the **"stuffer fragment"** (~14 kb) that is excised before ligation with foreign DNA.
4. **EMBL3, EMBL4, and Charon 4A** are classic examples of replacement vectors.

**Size Selection Mechanism (3 marks):**

- The λ phage capsid can package DNA only within the range of **~37–53 kb**.
- After removing the stuffer (~14 kb), the vector arms total ~20 kb.
- Therefore, only inserts of **7–23 kb** can be properly packaged.
- Inserts **smaller than 7 kb** cannot be packaged — they are excluded naturally.
- This **biological size selection** eliminates the need for insert size pre-screening.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Advantage:** λ vectors are ideal for **genomic DNA library construction** because they efficiently carry large DNA fragments (~20 kb) with high infection efficiency (~10⁹ pfu/μg).

---

### **Question B: [KTU University Exam - Model Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture and working principle of the **pET expression vector system**. Why is the T7 promoter preferred for heterologous protein production? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the structure and unique applications of **M13 bacteriophage vectors**. How are they useful in DNA sequencing? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question B (a): pET Expression Vector**

**Architecture (4 marks):**

The **pET (plasmid for Expression by T7 RNA polymerase)** vector contains:

1. **T7 Promoter:** A strong, IPTG-inducible promoter recognized only by T7 RNA polymerase.
2. **lac Operator (lacO):** Binds LacI repressor protein to keep the system OFF in the absence of IPTG.
3. **Multiple Cloning Site (MCS):** Located downstream of the T7 promoter for cloning the gene of interest.
4. **RBS + ATG start codon:** Ensures efficient translation initiation.
5. **T7 Terminator:** Halts transcription after the gene.
6. **Selection marker:** Antibiotic resistance gene (e.g., kan<sup>R</sup> or amp<sup>R</sup>).
7. **Origin of replication (ori):** For autonomous replication in *E. coli*.

**Working Principle (3 marks):**

- The host strain (e.g., **BL21(DE3)**) carries the **T7 RNA polymerase gene** under the *lacUV5* promoter.
- In the absence of IPTG, LacI repressor binds to *lacO* → no transcription of the target gene.
- Upon IPTG addition, LacI is inactivated → T7 RNA polymerase is produced from the host chromosome → T7 polymerase strongly transcribes the cloned gene → high-yield protein production.

**Why T7 Promoter is Preferred (Bonus 1 mark integrated):**

- T7 RNA polymerase is **~8× faster** than *E. coli* RNA polymerase.
- Highly specific — does not transcribe host genes, ensuring target-specific expression.
- Can transcribe **long, complete mRNAs** without premature termination.

---

#### **Solution to Question B (b): M13 Phage Vectors**

**Structure (3 marks):**

- M13 is a **filamentous bacteriophage** containing a **6.4 kb circular single-stranded DNA (ssDNA)** genome.
- It infects *E. coli* cells possessing the **F pilus** (F⁺ or F' strains).
- The genome encodes 10 proteins, with a small intergenic region (IG) where the **MCS** is engineered.
- **M13mp18 and M13mp19** are standard cloning vectors carrying the *lacZ'* gene with MCS for blue-white screening.

**Replication Cycle (2 marks):**

- Upon infection, the **ssDNA** is converted to a **double-stranded Replicative Form (RF)**.
- The RF replicates as a plasmid-like molecule (~100 copies/cell).
- New ssDNA molecules are continuously synthesized and packaged into phage particles, which are **secreted** without lysing the cell.

**Unique Applications (2 marks):**

1. **DNA Sequencing (Sanger Method):** The ssDNA produced is a **single-stranded template**, ideal for primer annealing and chain-termination sequencing.
2. **Site-Directed Mutagenesis:** Single-stranded template allows the use of mutagenic oligonucleotide primers.
3. **Probe Preparation:** ssDNA labeled with radioactive or fluorescent tags makes excellent hybridization probes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **M13 Limitation:** Insert size is restricted to **~1.5 kb** because larger inserts disrupt phage assembly. For larger inserts, **phagemids (e.g., pBluescript)** combine plasmid and M13 origins.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing copy numbers:** Many students wrongly state pUC18 as having 50–100 copies — correct answer is **500–700 copies per cell**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting pBR322 size:** Often miswritten as "5.4 kb" — actual size is **4,361 bp**.
>
> 3. **Mixing up blue-white mechanism:** Always state that **intact lacZ' = BLUE** (functional β-galactosidase cleaves X-gal) and **disrupted lacZ' = WHITE**.
>
> 4. **Forgetting size selection in λ vectors:** The 37–53 kb packaging limit is a **very common question** — failing to mention both the lower and upper limits loses marks.
>
> 5. **pET mechanism incomplete:** Students often forget that T7 polymerase gene is on the **host chromosome**, not the plasmid.
>
> 6. **M13 misconception:** Writing "M13 lyses the cell" — incorrect. M13 is a **filamentous phage** that **secretes** progeny **without cell lysis**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**
>
> ✅ A **cloning vector** propagates DNA; an **expression vector** produces protein.
>
> ✅ Five essential vector features: **ori, selectable marker, MCS, insertional inactivation site, small size**.
>
> ✅ **pBR322 = 4,361 bp; pUC18/19 = 2,686 bp; pET vectors = 5–7 kb**.
>
> ✅ pUC vectors have **500–700 copies/cell** (high copy); pBR322 has **15–20 copies/cell**.
>
> ✅ **Blue-white screening:** LacZ⁺ → blue; LacZ⁻ → white (X-gal/IPTG).
>
> ✅ pBR322 uses **antibiotic sensitivity** screening (Amp<sup>R</sup> Tet<sup>S</sup> = recombinant).
>
> ✅ **pET system:** T7 promoter, IPTG-inducible, requires **BL21(DE3)** host.
>
> ✅ **λ phage** carries up to **23 kb** inserts; uses **cos sites** for packaging.
>
> ✅ **λ size selection:** Only 37–53 kb DNA can be packaged → biological size filter.
>
> ✅ **M13** produces **ssDNA** — used for sequencing and site-directed mutagenesis.
>
> ✅ **λgt10/λgt11** = insertional vectors for cDNA library construction.
>
> ✅ **EMBL3/EMBL4** = replacement vectors for genomic library construction.
>
> ✅ λ phage infection efficiency (**10⁹–10¹⁰ pfu/μg**) is **higher** than plasmid transformation efficiency.
>
> ✅ **Phagemids** (e.g., pBluescript) combine plasmid + M13 origins for versatility.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vectors for Insect, Yeast and Mammalian System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT524 - GENETIC ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Vectors for Insect, Yeast and Mammalian System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Host-Specific Expression Vectors?

A **cloning/expression vector** is a self-replicating DNA molecule engineered to carry a foreign gene (transgene) into a specific host cell, where the gene can be replicated, maintained, and expressed to produce the desired protein. Vectors are **host-specific** because the molecular machinery (polymerases, transcription factors, ribosomes, and post-translational modification enzymes) differs fundamentally between prokaryotes, insects, yeasts, and mammals. A vector that works in *E. coli* will almost never replicate or express genes in a human cell, and vice versa.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEBBT524 – Module 1):**
> "Host-specific vectors are engineered DNA vehicles that contain the necessary *replication origin*, *promoter*, *selection marker*, and *cloning site* elements to enable stable maintenance and gene expression in a specific eukaryotic host system — Insect, Yeast, or Mammalian."

### Conceptual Analogy (The Courier Service)

Think of a vector as a **specialized courier van** delivering a package (your gene) to a specific city (host cell):
- A **Yeast vector** is like a small-town delivery van — it has the keys (ARS origin) to start in baker's yeast, the fuel type the yeast engine understands (yeast promoters), and a parking permit (LEU2/URA3 selection).
- An **Insect vector** (Baculovirus) is like a freight truck for an insect warehouse — it hijacks the insect cell's protein factory using very strong viral promoters (polyhedrin).
- A **Mammalian vector** is like an armored diplomatic vehicle for a human embassy — it needs human-compatible paperwork (CMV/SV40 promoters), special permits (G418/Hygromycin resistance), and often a viral envelope to enter the cell.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Universal Vector Features (All Host Systems):**
> 1. **Origin of Replication (ori)** — enables DNA duplication in the host.
> 2. **Selectable Marker Gene** — allows isolation of cells that received the vector.
> 3. **Multiple Cloning Site (MCS)** — short region containing unique restriction sites for inserting the gene of interest.

## 1.2 The Three Eukaryotic Host Systems at a Glance

| Host System | Typical Vector Type | Key Application | Standard Size of Insert |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Insect (Sf9, Sf21, High Five)** | Baculovirus (AcMNPV), pFastBac™ | High-yield recombinant protein production; VLPs | Up to ~**10–20 kb** |
| **Yeast (*S. cerevisiae*, *P. pastoris*)** | YEp, YCp, YIp, YAC, *P. pastoris* integrative vectors | Functional genomics, secreted proteins, vaccines, industrial enzymes | **0.5 kb – several Mb (YAC)** |
| **Mammalian (CHO, HEK293, HeLa)** | Plasmid (SV40/EBV-based), retroviral, adenoviral, lentiviral | Biopharmaceuticals, gene therapy, functional studies | **2–10 kb (plasmid); up to 30–40 kb (viral)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why not just use *E. coli* vectors?** Because *E. coli* cannot perform complex **post-translational modifications** (glycosylation, phosphorylation, disulfide bond formation, proteolytic cleavage) required for many eukaryotic proteins to be bioactive.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & High-Yield Concept Map

## 2.1 Vectors for the Insect Cell System — Baculovirus Expression Vector System (BEVS)

The **Autographa californica Multiple Nuclear Polyhedrosis Virus (AcMNPV)** is the workhorse of insect cell expression. It is a double-stranded circular DNA virus (~**134 kb** genome) that naturally infects lepidopteran insects (moths, caterpillars).

### Operational Architecture of a Baculovirus Transfer Vector (e.g., pFastBac™)

```
[ 5' ORF 1629 / Tn7 sites ] — [ Polyhedrin Promoter (P_PH) ] — [ MCS ] — [ SV40 polyA ] — [ Gentamicin / Kanamycin ] — [ ori (E. coli) ]
```

### Step-by-Step Logic of BEVS

1. **Replace the polyhedrin gene** (which makes occlusion bodies, non-essential in cell culture) with the foreign gene via a **transfer vector** (e.g., pFastBac).
2. **Site-specific transposition (Tn7)** in *E. coli* DH10Bac moves the expression cassette from pFastBac into the bacmid (a baculovirus shuttle vector maintained as a large low-copy plasmid).
3. **Transfect insect cells (Sf9/Sf21)** with the recombinant bacmid DNA using liposomes or polyethyleneimine (PEI).
4. The very strong **polyhedrin promoter (P_PH)** drives massive overexpression — often **25–50% of total cellular protein** is the recombinant product.
5. **Late viral lysis** releases the protein ~**72 hours post-infection (h.p.i.)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Late/Very-Late Promoters Used in BEVS:**
> - **Polyhedrin promoter (polh)** — very-late, very strong; standard for protein production.
> - **p10 promoter** — very-late, slightly weaker; used for co-expression (e.g., two-subunit proteins).

### Why BEVS for Eukaryotic Proteins?

- Correct protein folding (chaperone-rich cytoplasm of insect cells).
- Most post-translational modifications (glycosylation is simpler but generally sufficient).
- **No replication in mammalian cells** → high biosafety.
- Very high protein yields (typically **1–500 mg/L** in suspension culture).

> [!NOTE]
> **Linearization of Bacmid:** Modern systems (e.g., Bac-to-Bac®) linearize the bacmid at *LacZ* disruption site, which dramatically reduces wild-type virus background. **Yield: <0.1% wild-type recombinants.**

---

## 2.2 Vectors for the Yeast System

Yeast (*Saccharomyces cerevisiae*) was the first eukaryote to have its genome fully sequenced (1996). Yeast vectors are classified by their **mode of replication/maintenance** in the cell.

### 2.2.1 The Five Major Yeast Vector Classes

| Vector Class | Full Name | Maintenance Element | Copy Number | Stability |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **YIp** | Yeast Integrating Plasmid | None (integrates into chromosome by homologous recombination) | 1 (single integrant) | Very High (Mitotic) |
| **YEp** | Yeast Episomal Plasmid | **2µ origin** (from yeast endogenous 2-micron plasmid) | **20–200** | Moderate (requires selection) |
| **YCp** | Yeast Centromere Plasmid | ARS + **CEN** (centromere) | 1–2 | High (mimics chromosome) |
| **YAC** | Yeast Artificial Chromosome | ARS + CEN + **TEL** (telomeres) | 1 | Very High; can hold >**300 kb** |
| **YRp** | Yeast Replicating Plasmid | ARS only | High (~20) but **unstable** (mitotic loss) | Low |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ARS = Autonomously Replicating Sequence** — the yeast equivalent of *ori*; an 11-bp consensus sequence bound by the Origin Recognition Complex (ORC).
> **CEN = Centromere sequence** — ensures proper segregation during mitosis, dramatically improving stability.

### 2.2.2 Selectable Markers in Yeast

- **Auxotrophic (complementation) markers:** *URA3, LEU2, TRP1, HIS3, MET15, LYS2*. The host yeast is an *auxotroph* (e.g., *ura3*⁻) and only grows on medium lacking uracil if it carries the plasmid-borne *URA3*.
- **Dominant drug-resistance markers:** Zeocin (Sh ble), G418/Geneticin (kanMX), Hygromycin B, Nourseothricin (natMX).

### 2.2.3 Promoters for Yeast Expression

| Promoter | Strength | Regulation | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **GAL1 / GAL10** | Strong | Induced by **galactose**, repressed by **glucose** | Tightly regulated expression |
| **ADH1 (Alcohol Dehydrogenase)** | Moderate | Constitutive | Routine expression |
| **CUP1** | Moderate-strong | Induced by **Cu²⁺** | Metal-induced expression |
| **MFα1 (Mating Factor α)** | Strong + secretory signal | Constitutive | **Secreted proteins** (most common for biopharma) |
| **TEF1** | Strong | Constitutive | High-yield protein production |

### 2.2.4 Pichia pastoris (Methylotrophic Yeast) Vectors

A specialized high-yield system:
- Uses **Alcohol Oxidase 1 (AOX1) promoter** — induced by **methanol**, repressed by glucose/glycerol.
- Can be integrated into *P. pastoris* genome at *AOX1* or *HIS4* locus (very stable).
- Achieves **grams per litre** of recombinant protein in fermenters.
- AOX gene replacement creates **MutS** (slow methanol utilization) phenotype — useful for toxic proteins.

---

## 2.3 Vectors for the Mammalian Cell System

Mammalian expression vectors are the most diverse and clinically important class. They are needed when the recombinant protein requires:
- **Complex N-linked glycosylation** (especially sialic acid capping).
- **γ-carboxylation, sulfation, or other rare modifications.**
- **Native conformation** for human therapeutic use (e.g., antibodies, hormones).

### 2.3.1 Classification by Origin of Replication

| Vector Type | Replication in Mammalian Cells | Copy Number | Episomal vs. Integrative | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **SV40-based** | Yes (in cells expressing SV40 T-antigen, e.g., COS-7) | Very High (~10⁵) | Episomal | pCMV-Script, pSG5 |
| **EBV-based (EBNA1/oriP)** | Yes (in many human cell lines) | Moderate (~50–200) | **Episomal** (stable) | pEBNA, pCEP4 |
| **Simple plasmid (no viral ori)** | No (or very low) | Low / 1 | Integrative (random or site-specific) | pcDNA3.1, pCI-neo |
| **Retroviral** | Integrates into genome | 1 provirus | **Integrative** | pLNCX, pBABE |
| **Lentiviral** | Integrates (also infects non-dividing cells) | 1 provirus | Integrative (into transcription units) | pLenti-CMV/EFS |
| **Adenoviral** | Episomal in nucleus | ~1,000s of genome copies | **Episomal** (non-integrating) | pAdEasy-based |
| **AAV** | Episomal | Low | **Episomal** (long-term) | pAAV-MCS |

### 2.3.2 Essential Elements of a Mammalian Expression Vector

```
[ Viral Enhancer ] — [ Promoter ] — [ 5'UTR + Kozak ] — [ MCS + ATG ] — [ PolyA Signal ] — [ Intron (optional) ] — [ ori (E. coli) ] — [ Mammalian Selection ] — [ Bacterial Selection ]
```

| Element | Function | Common Choices |
|---|---|---|
| **Promoter** | Drives transcription | **CMV** (strong, constitutive), **SV40** (moderate), **EF1α** (stable in stem cells), **CAG** (hybrid chicken β-actin) |
| **Enhancer** | Boosts transcription | CMV enhancer, SV40 enhancer |
| **Kozak sequence** | Translation initiation context | **GCCRCCATGG** (consensus: R = purine) |
| **PolyA signal** | mRNA stability, nuclear export | **SV40 late polyA**, **bGH polyA** (bovine growth hormone) |
| **Intron** | Boosts mRNA processing/export | Synthetic intron (e.g., pCI-neo intron) |
| **Selection (mammalian)** | Stable cell line selection | **Neomycin (NeoR/G418)**, **Hygromycin B**, **Puromycin**, **Zeocin** |
| **Selection (bacterial)** | Plasmid propagation | Ampicillin, Kanamycin |
| **Ori (E. coli)** | High-copy replication | pUC ori, ColE1-derived |

### 2.3.3 Common Mammalian Vector Series (Examples)

| Vector | Key Features | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **pcDNA™3.1(+)** | CMV promoter, T7, BGH polyA, NeoR, AmpR | General overexpression |
| **pEGFP-N1/C1** | CMV + EGFP fusion tag | Subcellular localization |
| **pIRES** | Internal Ribosome Entry Site — bicistronic | Co-expression of two genes |
| **pLKO.1** | U6 promoter → shRNA + PuroR | RNA interference / knockdown |
| **pLenti-CMV** | Lentiviral backbone, VSV-G pseudotyping | Stable transduction of difficult cells |
| **pAdEasy-1** | Adenoviral backbone (deleted E1/E3) | High-titer, non-integrating expression |
| **pAAV-MCS** | AAV ITRs flanking MCS | *In vivo* gene therapy |

### 2.3.4 Viral Vector Packaging

For retroviral/lentiviral/AAV vectors, the recombinant plasmid is **co-transfected** into producer cells (e.g., HEK293T) along with **packaging plasmids** (Gag-Pol, Rev, Env) and an **envelope plasmid** (VSV-G for pantropism). The packaged viral particles are then used to transduce target cells.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Replication-defective viral vectors (e.g., 3rd-gen lentivirus with all four packaging genes split) are mandatory for safety — they replicate neither in the producer cells (after one round) nor in the target cells.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Comparative Cheat Sheet (The Three Systems)

| Feature | Insect (BEVS) | Yeast | Mammalian |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Host cell lines** | Sf9, Sf21, High Five, *D. melanogaster* S2 | *S. cerevisiae*, *P. pastoris*, *K. lactis* | CHO, HEK293, HeLa, NIH-3T3 |
| **Vector type** | Baculovirus (AcMNPV) | Plasmid (YEp/YCp/YIp) or Integrating | Plasmid / Retrovirus / Lentivirus / Adenovirus / AAV |
| **Strongest promoter** | **Polyhedrin (polh)** | **GAL1** (induced) or **AOX1** (methanol) in *P. pastoris* | **CMV (IE)** |
| **Selection (host)** | None needed (viral infection); Bacmid in *E. coli* uses Kan/Gent | URA3, LEU2, HIS3 (auxotrophy) or Zeocin/G418 | G418, Hygromycin, Puromycin |
| **Typical yield** | 1–500 mg/L | 1–100 mg/L (up to g/L in *P. pastoris*) | 1–100 mg/L (CHO fed-batch) |
| **Glycosylation** | Simple (paucimannose) | High-mannose (hypermannosylated) | **Complex human-type (sialylated)** |
| **Insert capacity** | ~10–20 kb | 0.5 kb – 1 Mb (YAC) | 2–10 kb (plasmid); up to ~40 kb (viral) |
| **Biosafety level** | BSL-1/2 | BSL-1 | BSL-2/2+ (viral) |
| **Major application** | Crystallography, VLPs, vaccines | Industrial enzymes, secreted cytokines | Biopharmaceuticals, gene therapy, antibodies |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule of Glycosylation:** If a human therapeutic glycoprotein (e.g., Erythropoietin) requires **terminal sialic acids** for *in vivo* half-life, **mammalian cells (CHO)** are the only correct choice among these three.

---

## 2.5 Engineering Utility in Industry

- **Insect BEVS** → Used in production of **Cervarix™** (HPV vaccine), **FluBlok™** (influenza HA vaccine), and most virus-like particle (VLP) vaccine antigens.
- **Yeast** → Production of **Hepatitis B surface antigen (HBV vaccine)**, **Insulin (Novolin®)**, and **Glucocerebrosidase (Cerezyme®)**.
- **Mammalian (CHO)** → Production of **Erythropoietin (EPO), monoclonal antibodies (Herceptin®, Avastin®), Factor VIII, tPA, follicle-stimulating hormone** — the majority of FDA-approved therapeutic proteins.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Decision Tree: Selecting the Right Vector for a Recombinant Protein

Let $P$ denote the target protein. Define the following parameters that determine host-vector choice:

$$
P_{needs} = \begin{cases}
G_{complex} & \text{if complex N-glycosylation (sialic acids) is required} \\
G_{simple}  & \text{if high-mannose or paucimannose is acceptable} \\
G_{none}    & \text{if no glycosylation is required}
\end{cases}
$$

$$
P_{tox} = \begin{cases}
1 & \text{if the protein is toxic to the host} \\
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

$$
P_{size} = \text{number of base pairs in the coding sequence}
$$

$$
P_{yield} = \text{required yield in mg/L}
$$

### Decision Algorithm (3.1)

$$
\text{Vector} =
\begin{cases}
\text{BEVS (Baculovirus)} & \text{if } G_{complex} = \text{false} \;\land\; P_{yield} \geq 10\;\text{mg/L} \;\land\; P_{size} \leq 20\;\text{kb} \\
\text{YEp / pPICZ (Yeast)} & \text{if } G_{complex} = \text{false} \;\land\; P_{secretion} = \text{true} \;\land\; P_{size} \leq 10\;\text{kb} \\
\text{Mammalian (CHO/HEK)} & \text{if } G_{complex} = \text{true} \;\lor\; (\text{therapeutic + native conformation required})
\end{cases}
$$

**Worked Example:**
- Protein: **Tissue Plasminogen Activator (tPA)** → therapeutic, requires complex glycosylation, 527 aa (~1.5 kb CDS).
- Applying the rule: $G_{complex} = \text{true} \Rightarrow$ **CHO mammalian cell line with CMV-driven plasmid or lentiviral vector.**

---

## 3.2 Vector Copy Number Derivation (Yeast 2µ Origin)

A *S. cerevisiae* cell contains ~**60–100 copies** of the endogenous 2µ plasmid. Plasmids containing the 2µ *ori* (e.g., YEp vectors) achieve steady-state copy number $N$ governed by:

$$
N = \frac{R}{L}
$$

where $R$ = rate of replication (origin firing per cell cycle) and $L$ = rate of loss (segregational + molecular).

In the presence of selection (e.g., −Ura medium), the effective copy number is amplified because cells losing the plasmid die. The **observed copy number in YEp** is empirically:

$$
N_{YEp} \approx 20 \text{ to } 200
$$

For **YCp** (ARS + CEN), the centromere enforces equal segregation:

$$
N_{YCp} \approx 1 \text{ to } 2
$$

For **YIp** (integrative, no origin), the vector integrates by homologous recombination, e.g., at the *LEU2* locus:

$$
\text{Single crossover: } \text{Vector} \times \text{Chromosome} \rightarrow \text{Integrant at } leu2
$$

Mitotic stability of an integrated YIp = **>95% per generation** (no segregation loss).

---

## 3.3 Baculovirus Late Promoter Strength — Quantitative Insight

The polyhedrin promoter (P_PH) is activated **18–24 hours post-infection** when viral DNA replication is complete. It is one of the strongest promoters known in any biological system.

Let the **transcript abundance** at time $t$ be:

$$
[T]_{t} = [T]_{\max} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-k(t - t_{0})}\right)
$$

where $k \approx 0.15\;\text{h}^{-1}$ (empirical), $t_{0} \approx 18\;\text{h}$, and $[T]_{\max}$ is determined by the cellular pool of NTPs and RNA Pol II.

**Typical protein yield:**

$$
Y_{protein} = [\text{cells}] \times V_{culture} \times f_{expression} \times \eta_{extraction}
$$

where:
- $[\text{cells}] \approx 2 \times 10^{6}\;\text{cells/mL}$
- $V_{culture} = 1\;\text{L}$
- $f_{expression} = 0.25$ (25% of total protein is recombinant)
- $\eta_{extraction} = 0.5$ (50% recovery)

**Plug in:**

$$
Y_{protein} = (2 \times 10^{9}) \times (0.25) \times (0.5) = 2.5 \times 10^{8}\;\text{arbitrary units}
$$

In practice, this corresponds to **1–500 mg/L** of purified recombinant protein for BEVS.

---

## 3.4 Stable Transfection Efficiency in Mammalian Cells

For a plasmid with a dominant selectable marker (e.g., NeoR), the fraction of surviving colonies after 2 weeks of G418 selection is given by:

$$
f_{stable} = \frac{N_{colonies}}{N_{cells\_plated}}
$$

where $N_{colonies}$ follows a Poisson distribution when integration is random:

$$
P(k) = \frac{\lambda^{k} e^{-\lambda}}{k!}
$$

with $\lambda$ = average integrations per surviving cell (typically 1–10 for random integration).

**Stable integration frequency** of plasmid DNA into CHO genomes by calcium phosphate or lipofection:

$$
f_{stable} \approx 10^{-5} \text{ to } 10^{-3}
$$

Viral vectors dramatically improve this:
- Retroviral → $f \approx 10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$
- Lentiviral → $f \approx 0.3$ to **>0.9** (for some cell types)

---

## 3.5 Promoter Strength Comparison Table (mRNA Output)

| Promoter | Host | Relative mRNA Level (normalized) |
|---|---|---|
| **CMV (immediate early)** | Mammalian | 100 (reference) |
| **SV40 early** | Mammalian | ~20 |
| **EF1α** | Mammalian | ~50 |
| **CAG (hybrid)** | Mammalian | ~80 |
| **PGK** | Mammalian | ~30 |
| **Polyhedrin (polh)** | Insect (late) | ~1000 (very strong, but late phase) |
| **p10** | Insect (very late) | ~600 |
| **GAL1 (induced)** | Yeast | ~1000 (induced) |
| **ADH1** | Yeast | ~10 |
| **AOX1 (induced)** | *P. pastoris* | ~1000+ (very strong when induced by methanol) |

---

## 3.6 Step-by-Step Construction Logic: pFastBac™ Recombinant Bacmid

**Step 1.** Clone your gene into the **MCS of pFastBac1** downstream of the polyhedrin promoter.

**Step 2.** Transform the pFastBac-recombinant into *E. coli* **DH10Bac** competent cells. DH10Bac contains:
- The **bacmid** (AcMNPV genome as a BAC) carrying *LacZ* and a Tn7 attTn7 site.
- A **helper plasmid** (pMON7124) encoding **Tn7 transposase** under the tet promoter.

**Step 3.** Transposition reaction: Tn7 transposase mediates site-specific insertion of the expression cassette (from pFastBac) into the bacmid's *attTn7* site, **disrupting *LacZ***.

**Step 4.** Select on plates containing:
- **Kanamycin** (50 µg/mL) — bacmid
- **Gentamicin** (7 µg/mL) — pFastBac backbone  
- **Tetracycline** (10 µg/mL) — helper plasmid  
- **IPTG + X-gal + Bluo-gal** — for blue/white screening

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **White colonies = Successful transposition (LacZ disrupted) = Recombinant bacmid.**
> Blue colonies = empty bacmid (LacZ intact). **Always pick white colonies only.**

**Step 5.** Purify the high-molecular-weight recombinant bacmid DNA (~140 kb, low-copy).

**Step 6.** Transfect insect cells (Sf9) with bacmid DNA + transfection reagent. Wait 4–5 days. Collect the P0 viral stock from the supernatant.

**Step 7.** Amplify to P1, P2, P3 stocks (working viral libraries). Infect large cultures for protein production.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix: Insert Capacity vs. Vector Type

| Vector Class | Maximum Practical Insert Size |
|---|---|
| Standard plasmid (mammalian) | ~**10 kb** |
| Cosmids | ~**45 kb** |
| BAC (Bacterial Artificial Chromosome) | ~**300 kb** |
| YAC (Yeast Artificial Chromosome) | ~**1–2 Mb** |
| Baculovirus (AcMNPV) | ~**20–40 kb** (deletions tolerated) |
| Adenovirus (deleted E1/E3) | ~**8–10 kb** of foreign DNA |
| AAV | ~**4.7 kb** (limited by capsid) |
| Lentivirus | ~**8–10 kb** |
| Retrovirus (MLV) | ~**8 kb** |
