# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"Briefly describe the essential features of a baculovirus expression vector system (BEVS) used in insect cells."**

**Model Answer (3 marks — concise, key-term dense):**

A BEVS is based on the **Autographa californica multiple nuclear polyhedrosis virus (AcMNPV)**, a double-stranded circular DNA virus (~134 kb). The essential features are:

1. **Polyhedrin promoter (P_PH)** — replaces the non-essential polyhedrin gene; one of the strongest known promoters, active in the very-late phase of infection. Drives massive overexpression of the foreign gene.
2. **Transfer vector (e.g., pFastBac1)** — contains Tn7 sites, MCS, polyhedrin promoter, SV40 polyA, and bacterial selection (Gentamicin/Kanamycin).
3. **Bacmid (AcMNPV genome as a BAC in *E. coli* DH10Bac)** — accepts the expression cassette by Tn7-mediated transposition, with **LacZ disruption** for blue/white screening.
4. **Insect host cells** — Sf9, Sf21 (from *Spodoptera frugiperda*), or High Five (from *Trichoplusia ni*) for protein production post-transfection.

The system produces correctly folded, post-translationally modified eukaryotic proteins in **high yields (1–500 mg/L)** and is biosafe (cannot replicate in mammals).

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for naming the virus, 1 mark for polyhedrin promoter, 1 mark for host cells — students who write only "it is a virus" lose 2 marks.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"Compare YEp, YCp and YIp vectors in *S. cerevisiae* with respect to maintenance elements and copy number."**

**Model Answer (3 marks — tabular answer expected):**

| Vector | Maintenance Element | Copy Number | Stability |
|---|---|---|---|
| **YEp (Yeast Episomal Plasmid)** | 2µ origin (from yeast endogenous plasmid) | **20 – 200** | Moderate (requires selection) |
| **YCp (Yeast Centromere Plasmid)** | **ARS + CEN** (centromere) | 1 – 2 | High (mimics chromosome) |
| **YIp (Yeast Integrative Plasmid)** | None — integrates by homologous recombination into chromosome | **1 (single integrant)** | **Very high (mitotically stable)** |

*(1 mark per row of the table; partial credit awarded for at least naming the maintenance element of each.)*

---

## **Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Provided)**

---

### **Question A (14 marks) — Option 1**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEBBT524]**

**(a)** Describe the **structure and essential elements of a typical mammalian expression vector** used for stable transfection of CHO cells. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the role of **promoters, polyadenylation signals, selectable markers, and Kozak sequence** in a mammalian vector. (7 marks)

---

### **Model Answer — Question A**

**(a) Structure of a typical mammalian expression vector (7 marks):**

A mammalian expression vector (e.g., pcDNA™3.1(+)) is a circular double-stranded DNA molecule (~5–10 kb) engineered to function in both *E. coli* (for amplification) and mammalian cells (for expression). Its elements are:

1. **Bacterial backbone:** *ColE1/pUC origin* for high-copy replication in *E. coli* and an *Ampicillin resistance gene (β-lactamase)* for bacterial selection.

2. **Mammalian promoter-enhancer:** The **CMV immediate-early (CMV-IE) promoter/enhancer** is the gold standard — a strong, constitutive promoter that works in nearly all mammalian cell types, with the CMV enhancer boosting transcription 5–10 fold.

3. **5' UTR and Kozak sequence:** The **Kozak consensus (GCCRCCATGG)** ensures efficient ribosome recognition and translation initiation at the correct AUG.

4. **Multiple Cloning Site (MCS):** A short DNA segment containing 10–20 unique restriction sites for inserting the gene of interest.

5. **Polyadenylation signal:** **BGH (bovine growth hormone) polyA or SV40 late polyA** stabilizes the mRNA, aids nuclear export, and improves translation efficiency.

6. **Mammalian selectable marker:** **Neomycin resistance (NeoR)** confers resistance to **G418 (Geneticin)**, allowing selection of stably transfected CHO cells over 2–3 weeks.

7. **Optional elements:** Introns (boost mRNA export), woodchuck hepatitis virus post-transcriptional regulatory element (WPRE, for viral vectors), IRES (for bicistronic expression), and reporter genes (eGFP, LacZ).

*(1 mark each for: bacterial backbone, promoter, Kozak, MCS, polyA, selection, optional elements.)*

---

**(b) Role of each regulatory element (7 marks):**

| Element | Role | Without it |
|---|---|---|
| **Promoter (e.g., CMV-IE)** | RNA Pol II binding + initiation; **defines cell-type and strength** of expression | Gene is not transcribed; no mRNA |
| **PolyA signal (e.g., BGH polyA)** | Cleavage + polyadenylation of pre-mRNA; **stabilizes mRNA**; aids nuclear export; improves translation | mRNA is rapidly degraded in nucleus; short protein half-life |
| **Selectable marker (e.g., NeoR/G418R)** | Allows **stable integration** into genome; selective pressure maintains expression in long-term culture | Transient expression only (lost in 5–7 days) |
| **Kozak sequence (GCCRCCATGG)** | Ribosome recruitment; positions AUG in P-site of 40S subunit; sets translation efficiency | Translation initiation at wrong AUG, or very low expression |
| **Enhancer** | Boosts transcription by 5–100 fold; often cell-type specific | Weak expression |
| **Intron (optional)** | Boosts mRNA maturation and nuclear export | Reduced expression, especially in genomic context |

*Integration: The plasmid integrates randomly into the host genome (1–10 copies per cell). To obtain a clonal stable cell line, transfected cells are selected in G418-containing medium for 14–21 days, then single colonies are isolated and screened for expression level.*

*(Marks distributed: 1.5 per major element + 1 for stable integration logic.)*

---

### **Question B (14 marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEBBT524]**

**(a)** Describe the **principle and steps of recombinant baculovirus construction** using the **Bac-to-Bac® system**. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain why the **polyhedrin promoter is chosen** and discuss the **advantages of BEVS over bacterial and yeast expression** for eukaryotic proteins. (7 marks)

---

### **Model Answer — Question B**

**(a) Principle and steps of recombinant baculovirus construction (Bac-to-Bac® system) (7 marks):**

**Principle:** Site-specific **Tn7 transposon-mediated** insertion of the gene of interest from a transfer vector (pFastBac1) into a baculovirus genome (bacmid) propagated in *E. coli* DH10Bac, followed by transfection of insect cells.

**Step-by-step procedure:**

1. **Clone the foreign gene** into the MCS of pFastBac1 (transfer vector) downstream of the polyhedrin promoter using standard restriction enzyme ligation or Gibson assembly.

2. **Transform pFastBac-recombinant into *E. coli* DH10Bac** competent cells. These cells harbor:
   - The **bacmid** (AcMNPV genome as a low-copy BAC) with a *LacZα* gene containing the **Tn7 attachment site (attTn7)**.
   - A **helper plasmid (pMON7124)** encoding Tn7 transposase.

3. **Transposition reaction:** Tn7 transposase catalyzes the **site-specific insertion** of the mini-attTn7 element (carrying the expression cassette from pFastBac) into the bacmid's *attTn7* site, **disrupting the *LacZα* gene**.

4. **Selection on agar plates** containing:
   - Kanamycin (50 µg/mL) → bacmid
   - Gentamicin (7 µg/mL) → pFastBac
   - Tetracycline (10 µg/mL) → helper plasmid
   - IPTG + Bluo-gal → blue/white screening
   - **White colonies = recombinant bacmid; Blue colonies = empty bacmid (reject).**

5. **Purify recombinant bacmid DNA** (large ~140 kb plasmid) from white colonies — typically by a modified alkaline lysis or column-based method.

6. **Transfect insect cells** (Sf9 at ~80% confluency) with 1–5 µg bacmid DNA + Cellfectin® or PEI transfection reagent.

7. **Harvest P0 viral stock** (passage 0) from the supernatant at 4–5 days post-transfection.

8. **Amplify viral stock** by infecting fresh Sf9 cells → P1, P2, P3 working stocks. Use these for protein production (typically MOI = 0.1–10 pfu/cell).

*(Marks: 1 for principle, 6 for steps; key terms like "Tn7 transposition", "LacZ disruption", and "blue/white screening" must appear.)*

---

**(b) Why polyhedrin promoter + advantages of BEVS (7 marks):**

**Why the polyhedrin promoter?**

- The **polyhedrin (polh) gene** encodes the occlusion body protein, which is non-essential for virus replication in cell culture.
- It is expressed at **exceptionally high levels in the very-late phase** of infection — polh transcripts can reach **20–25% of total cellular mRNA** by 72 h.p.i.
- This makes it one of the **strongest promoters known in nature**.
- Replacing polh with the foreign gene, while retaining the strong promoter, leads to massive overexpression of the recombinant protein.

**Advantages of BEVS over bacterial and yeast systems for eukaryotic proteins:**

| Feature | BEVS | *E. coli* | Yeast |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Protein folding** | Native eukaryotic chaperones | Inclusion bodies (often misfolded) | Native folding |
| **Disulfide bonds** | Formed correctly in ER | Cytoplasm is reducing — misfolded | Formed in ER |
| **Glycosylation** | Simple (paucimannose), insect-type | **None** | Hypermannosylated (immunogenic in humans) |
| **Post-translational modifications** | Phosphorylation, acylation, myristoylation | None | Phosphorylation, some glycosylation |
| **Yield** | 1–500 mg/L | Up to g/L, but insoluble | Up to g/L |
| **Protein size** | Large multi-subunit complexes feasible | Limited by solubility | Moderate |
| **Biosafety** | Cannot replicate in mammals | Safe | Safe |

**Key conclusion:** BEVS offers the best balance of **eukaryotic protein processing + high yield + biosafety** for producing complex eukaryotic proteins (e.g., viral surface antigens, GPCRs, kinases) that cannot be properly folded in *E. coli* and that do not require the complex human-type sialylated glycosylation that only mammalian cells can provide.

*(Marks: 2 for polyhedrin promoter, 5 for comparative table with at least 3 distinct advantages.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing promoters:** Students often write "CMV promoter" for insect or yeast systems. **CMV is strictly mammalian.** Correct: insect = polyhedrin, yeast = GAL1/AOX1, mammalian = CMV/EF1α.
> 2. **Confusing 2µ and ARS:** In yeast, ARS is the *origin* (initiates replication). 2µ is the *cis-acting sequence* that maintains high copy number. The 2µ plasmid of yeast itself has its own origin (often called *REP* or 2µ ORI) — different from ARS.
> 3. **Calling baculovirus "RNA virus":** It is a **double-stranded DNA virus** with a circular genome (~134 kb).
> 4. **Forgetting the Kozak sequence:** Many students omit this when describing mammalian vectors. The Kozak consensus (GCCRCCATGG) is critical and is a high-frequency KTU question.
> 5. **LacZ disruption for blue/white:** Students must state that **white colonies = recombinants, blue = empty vector**.
> 6. **Auxotrophic vs. dominant markers in yeast:** Don't confuse URA3 (auxotrophic, requires *ura3*⁻ host) with Zeocin (dominant, works in any yeast).
> 7. **Yield numbers:** Remember — BEVS ≈ 1–500 mg/L, *P. pastoris* ≈ 1–10 g/L, CHO ≈ 50–200 mg/L. Numerical precision impresses examiners.

---

## **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)**

### 🦠 Insect System
- ⭐ Vector = **Baculovirus (AcMNPV)**; **dsDNA, ~134 kb**.
- ⭐ Transfer vector = **pFastBac1**; uses **Tn7 transposition in DH10Bac**.
- ⭐ Promoter = **Polyhedrin (polh)**; very-late, very strong.
- ⭐ Selection = **Blue/white screening** (LacZ disruption by Tn7).
- ⭐ Hosts = **Sf9, Sf21, High Five**.
- ⭐ Glycosylation = **paucimannose (insect-type, no sialic acid)**.
- ⭐ Biosafety = Cannot replicate in mammals → BSL-1.

### 🍞 Yeast System
- ⭐ **YEp = 2µ origin → 20–200 copies** (episomal).
- ⭐ **YCp = ARS + CEN → 1–2 copies** (mimics chromosome).
- ⭐ **YIp = integrates by homologous recombination → very stable**.
- ⭐ **YAC = ARS + CEN + TEL → up to 1–2 Mb inserts** (genome project tool).
- ⭐ **Promoters**: GAL1 (induced by galactose), AOX1 (induced by methanol in *P. pastoris*).
- ⭐ **Selection**: URA3, LEU2, HIS3 (auxotrophic); Zeocin, G418 (dominant).
- ⭐ **Secretion signal**: MFα1 (mating factor α prepro).
- ⭐ Glycosylation = **high-mannose, hypermannosylated**.

### 🧬 Mammalian System
- ⭐ Vectors = **Plasmid, Retroviral, Lentiviral, Adenoviral, AAV**.
- ⭐ **Promoter = CMV (strongest constitutive)**; also EF1α, SV40, CAG.
- ⭐ **Kozak = GCCRCCATGG** (translation initiation).
- ⭐ **PolyA = SV40 late or BGH** (mRNA stability).
- ⭐ **Selection = G418 (NeoR), Hygromycin, Puromycin, Zeocin**.
- ⭐ **Episomal vectors**: SV40 (in COS cells), EBV (EBNA1/oriP).
- ⭐ Lentivirus infects **non-dividing cells**; integrates into genome.
- ⭐ Adenovirus and AAV are **non-integrating** (episomal, safer).
- ⭐ Glycosylation = **complex human-type (sialic acid)** — required for most therapeutic glycoproteins.

### 🏆 Master Comparison (3-Sentence Summary)
1. **Insect (BEVS)** = high yield + correct folding + simple glycan → best for **VLPs, vaccines, crystallography**.
2. **Yeast** = cheap, scalable, secreted → best for **industrial enzymes, HBV vaccine, secreted cytokines**.
3. **Mammalian (CHO/HEK)** = human-compatible complex glycosylation → best for **therapeutic antibodies, EPO, Factor VIII, gene therapy**.

### ⚠️ KTU-Mandated Memorization (High-Frequency Exam Topics)
- **Polyhedrin promoter** (origin, strength, phase of expression).
- **p10 promoter** (co-expression partner).
- **Tn7 transposition** (site, mechanism, selection outcome).
- **ARS / CEN / TEL** in yeast.
- **YEp / YCp / YIp / YAC** (maintenance element + copy number).
- **GAL1 + AOX1** promoters.
- **CMV / EF1α / SV40** mammalian promoters.
- **Kozak sequence** literal consensus.
- **G418, Hygromycin, Puromycin** — name the resistance gene and mode of action.
- **Lentiviral packaging** — 2nd vs. 3rd generation vectors.
- **Stable vs. transient transfection** — selection timelines.

---

**END OF NOTES — Module 1, Topic: Vectors for Insect, Yeast and Mammalian System**
*Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme — PEBBT524 (Genetic Engineering)*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plasmids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT524 - GENETIC ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Plasmids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

A **plasmid** is a small, circular, double-stranded DNA molecule that exists *extra-chromosomally* within a bacterial cell and replicates independently of the host chromosome. Plasmids carry their own origin of replication (`ori`), possess a defined **copy number**, and often harbour non-essential but phenotypically advantageous genes (e.g., antibiotic resistance, heavy-metal degradation, virulence factors).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim style):**
> *"Plasmids are autonomous, self-replicating, extrachromosomal genetic elements found predominantly in bacteria (and also in yeast and archaea) that are physically separate from the chromosomal DNA and replicate using their own origin of replication."*

| Parameter | Typical Range / Value |
|---|---|
| Size range | **1 kb – 200 kb** (common cloning plasmids: **2.7 kb – 12 kb**) |
| Topology | Predominantly **covalently closed circular (ccc)**, supercoiled |
| Copy number | **1 – 700 copies/cell** (relaxed) or **1 – 5 copies/cell** (stringent) |
| GC content | Varies, typically 30 – 70 % |
| Cellular location | Cytoplasm (as nucleoid-associated nucleoprotein complex) |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The USB Drive of the Cell"

Imagine the bacterial chromosome is the **factory's main computer hard drive** — it contains all the essential operating instructions. A **plasmid is like a USB pen-drive** that the worker (bacterium) carries in its pocket:

- It is **not essential** for survival, but it gives the bacterium a *competitive edge* (like carrying extra software or data).
- It can be **plugged in and unplugged** (it can be transferred between cells via conjugation, like passing a USB between colleagues).
- It has its **own power and replication logic** (its own `ori` allows it to copy itself).
- It can carry **new instructions** (foreign DNA) that the main hard drive does not have — this is precisely what is exploited in **genetic engineering**, where we treat plasmids as **cloning vectors** (molecular "delivery trucks").

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Summary:** Plasmids = *self-replicating, transferable, accessory genetic elements* that bacteria use as a "survival toolkit" — and that humans hijack as molecular tools for **recombinant DNA technology**.

---

## 1.3 Historical Context (For 2-Mark "Bonus" Recall)

| Year | Landmark |
|---|---|
| **1952** | Lederberg coined the term *plasmid* |
| **1953** | First plasmid (F-factor) discovered in *E. coli* |
| **1973** | Cohen & Boyer used **pSC101** as the first cloning vector — birth of Genetic Engineering |
| **1977** | **pBR322** constructed — first widely-used multi-purpose cloning vector |
| **1980s** | **pUC series** with MCS (Multiple Cloning Site) developed |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a Plasmid

Every standard cloning plasmid contains the following *modular components*:

1. **Origin of Replication (`ori`)** — dictates copy number and host range.
2. **Selectable Marker** — usually an antibiotic-resistance gene (*ampR*, *kanR*, *tetR*, *cmR*).
3. **Multiple Cloning Site (MCS)** — a short DNA region containing many unique restriction sites.
4. **Reporter / Screening Gene** — e.g., *lacZ' (α-fragment)* in pUC plasmids for blue/white screening.
5. **Other optional elements** — *oriT* (transfer origin), *mob* genes, IRES, promoters.

---

## 2.2 Classification of Plasmids — A KTU High-Yield Mind Map

### (A) By Transfer Capability
| Type | Property | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Conjugative** | Carry *tra* genes, self-transferable via pili | F-plasmid, RP4 |
| **Non-conjugative** | Cannot self-transfer; only mobilisable | pBR322, pUC19 |
| **Mobilisable** | Carry *mob* but not full *tra*; need helper plasmid | pSUP106 |

### (B) By Copy Number / Replication Stringency
| Type | Copy Number | Replication Mode | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Stringent plasmids** | 1 – 5 | Synchronous with chromosome | pSC101, F-factor |
| **Relaxed plasmids** | 10 – 700 | Independent of chromosome | ColE1, pBR322, pUC plasmids |

### (C) By Phenotypic Function
| Type | Function | Medical/Biotech Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **F-plasmids** (Fertility) | Conjugation, sex pili | Gene transfer |
| **R-plasmids** (Resistance) | Antibiotic/heavy-metal resistance | Multi-Drug Resistance (MDR) crisis |
| **Col-plasmids** | Encode **colicins** (bacteriocins) | Microbial warfare |
| **Degradative plasmids** | Hydrocarbon / aromatic compound catabolism (e.g., TOL, NAH) | Bioremediation |
| **Virulence plasmids** | Encode toxins, adhesion factors | Pathogenicity (*Agrobacterium* Ti plasmid) |
| **Cryptic plasmids** | No known phenotype | Still useful as vectors |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Topic:** The **Ti plasmid of *Agrobacterium tumefaciens*** is the most biotechnologically important *virulence plasmid*. Its T-DNA region is exploited to genetically engineer *dicot* plants — a guaranteed 7-mark question on plant biotechnology exams.

---

## 2.3 Copy Number — The "Why" Behind It

The **copy number (CN)** is the average number of plasmid molecules per chromosome equivalent per cell at steady-state growth. It is set by the interplay of three negative-control elements around the `ori`:

- **RNA I** — a small antisense RNA that inhibits replication primer formation.
- **RNA II** — the pre-primer that gets processed by RNase H.
- **Rop/Rom protein** — stabilises the RNA I / RNA II duplex, further repressing replication.

$$\text{Steady-state Copy Number} \;\propto\; \frac{\text{Rate of replication initiation}}{\text{Rate of plasmid loss (segregation + dilution)}}$$

> **Why it matters:** High copy number → higher recombinant protein yield but risk of **metabolic burden**; Low copy number → stable maintenance of large inserts, suitable for toxic genes.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Relation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Molar mass of dsDNA | $MW \;=\; N_{bp} \times 660$ | **Da** (avg. 660 Da/bp for B-form dsDNA) |
| Mass of 1 plasmid molecule | $m_{1} \;=\; \dfrac{MW}{N_A}$ | grams ($N_A = 6.022\times10^{23}$) |
| Total plasmid yield | $m_{tot} \;=\; m_{1} \times CN \times N_{cells}$ | grams |
| Concentration (μg/mL) | $C \;=\; \dfrac{m_{tot}}{V_{culture}}$ | μg/mL |
| Moles of plasmid ends | $n_{ends} \;=\; 2 \times n_{plasmid}$ (for linearised) | moles |
| Plasmids per mL (CN-based) | $\dfrac{CN \times N_{cells/mL}}{N_A}$ | moles/mL |
| Copy number from OD-derived cells | $CN \;=\; \dfrac{m_{measured}/MW}{N_{cells}/N_A}$ | dimensionless |
| Incompatibility group | Same Inc group → cannot co-exist stably | e.g., IncP, IncW, IncQ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Do not use the `|` symbol inside any table cell** — KTU examiners mark on table readability. For absolute value, use $\lvert x \rvert$ in the math block outside the table.

---

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of Plasmids

| Field | Application |
|---|---|
| **Recombinant protein production** | Insulin, growth hormone, vaccines in *E. coli* |
| **Gene therapy** | Plasmid DNA vaccines (e.g., ZyCoV-D) |
| **Agriculture** | Ti-plasmid-mediated plant transformation |
| **Industrial biocatalysis** | Engineering strains for enzyme production |
| **CRISPR delivery** | *cas9* + gRNA delivery vectors |
| **Diagnostics** | Plasmid-based biosensors |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Molecular Weight of a Plasmid

**Problem context:** You are working with **pUC19**, a classic cloning vector with a total length of **2686 base-pairs**. Determine its molecular weight in Daltons.

### Step-by-step Deduction

**Step 1 — Recall the constant.**
For B-form double-stranded DNA, the average molecular weight per base pair is:

$$MW_{bp} \;\approx\; 660 \;\text{Da/bp}$$

This accounts for an average of one A·T pair (≈ 615 Da) and one G·C pair (≈ 662 Da) considering the sugar-phosphate backbone and one water of hydration.

**Step 2 — Multiply by the number of base pairs.**

$$MW_{pUC19} \;=\; N_{bp} \times MW_{bp}$$

$$MW_{pUC19} \;=\; 2686 \;\text{bp} \times 660 \;\text{Da/bp}$$

$$\boxed{MW_{pUC19} \;\approx\; 1{,}772{,}760 \;\text{Da} \;\approx\; 1.77 \;\text{MDa}}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to grams per mole.**

$$MW \;=\; 1.77 \times 10^{6} \;\text{g/mol}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 660 and not 650?** The 660 value (Sambrook & Russell convention) is used because it gives a more accurate *experimental* molecular weight by including the hydrated sodium salt form. Examiners typically accept both, but prefer **660 Da/bp**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mass of a Single Plasmid Molecule

**Step 1 — Divide the molar mass by Avogadro's number.**

$$m_{1} \;=\; \frac{MW}{N_A}$$

$$m_{1} \;=\; \frac{1.77 \times 10^{6} \;\text{g/mol}}{6.022 \times 10^{23} \;\text{mol}^{-1}}$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate.**

$$m_{1} \;\approx\; 2.94 \times 10^{-18} \;\text{g} \;\approx\; 2.94 \;\text{fg}$$

$$\boxed{m_{1} \;\approx\; 2.94 \times 10^{-18} \;\text{g per plasmid molecule}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Plasmid Yield per mL of Culture (KTU Favourite)

**Given:**
- Vector = pUC19, $N_{bp}=2686$ bp, $MW=1.77 \times 10^6$ g/mol
- Copy number $CN \approx 500$ (relaxed, pUC)
- Culture: $N_{cells} = 1 \times 10^{9}$ cells/mL (mid-log, OD600 ≈ 0.5)
- Culture volume $V = 1$ mL

**Step 1 — Number of plasmid molecules per mL.**

$$N_{plasmid/mL} \;=\; CN \times N_{cells/mL}$$

$$N_{plasmid/mL} \;=\; 500 \times 1 \times 10^{9} \;=\; 5 \times 10^{11} \;\text{molecules/mL}$$

**Step 2 — Total mass per mL.**

$$m_{tot} \;=\; N_{plasmid/mL} \times m_{1}$$

$$m_{tot} \;=\; (5 \times 10^{11}) \times (2.94 \times 10^{-18} \;\text{g})$$

$$m_{tot} \;\approx\; 1.47 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{g/mL}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to μg/mL.**

$$m_{tot} \;\approx\; 1.47 \;\mu\text{g/mL}$$

**Step 4 — Extrapolate to standard prep (5 mL overnight culture).**

$$m_{prep} \;\approx\; 1.47 \times 5 \;\approx\; 7.35 \;\mu\text{g from 5 mL culture}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Expected yield of pUC19 miniprep} \;\approx\; 5 - 10 \;\mu\text{g per 5 mL culture}}$$

This is in excellent agreement with standard lab values (5–15 μg per miniprep), validating the calculation.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Copy Number from Spectrophotometric Measurement

**Reverse problem (often asked in exams):**

A miniprep of a 4500-bp plasmid yields $5.0$ μg of DNA. A hemocytometer counts $2.0 \times 10^{8}$ cells in the original culture. Estimate the average copy number per cell.

**Step 1 — Compute molar mass of plasmid.**

$$MW \;=\; 4500 \times 660 \;=\; 2.97 \times 10^{6} \;\text{g/mol}$$

**Step 2 — Compute moles of plasmid isolated.**

$$n \;=\; \frac{m}{MW} \;=\; \frac{5.0 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{g}}{2.97 \times 10^{6} \;\text{g/mol}}$$

$$n \;\approx\; 1.68 \times 10^{-12} \;\text{mol}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to number of molecules.**

$$N \;=\; n \times N_A \;=\; 1.68 \times 10^{-12} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23}$$

$$N \;\approx\; 1.01 \times 10^{12} \;\text{molecules}$$

**Step 4 — Divide by cell count to get CN.**

$$CN \;=\; \frac{N}{N_{cells}} \;=\; \frac{1.01 \times 10^{12}}{2.0 \times 10^{8}}$$

$$\boxed{CN \;\approx\; 5{,}050 \;\text{copies per cell (very high copy)}}$$

This corresponds to a **relaxed-replication plasmid** under amplified conditions (e.g., chloramphenicol-treated culture).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Top 5 KTU-Relevant Vectors

| Feature | pBR322 | pUC19 | pET-28a | pGEX-4T | pACYC184 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Size (bp) | 4361 | 2686 | 5369 | 4968 | 4005 |
| Copy number | ~15 – 20 | ~500 – 700 | ~20 | ~30 | ~10 – 15 |
| `ori` | pMB1 (relaxed) | pMB1 mutant (high CN) | pBR322 | pBR322 | p15A |
| Selectable marker | *ampR*, *tetR* | *ampR* | *kanR* | *ampR* | *cmR*, *tetR* |
| Screening | Insertional inactivation | Blue/white (lacZ') | His-tag | GST-tag | None |
| Best use | Classical teaching | Subcloning / MCS | Recombinant protein (His-tag) | Fusion protein | Co-expression |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Schematic Map of a Generic Cloning Plasmid (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Promoter e.g. T7] --> B[Multiple Cloning Site MCS 5 sites]
    B --> C[Reporter Gene lacZ alpha fragment]
    C --> D[Selectable Marker ampR beta-lactamase]
    D --> E[Origin of Replication ori pMB1]
    E --> F[F1 origin ssDNA rescue]
    F --> G[Bacterial Host E. coli DH5 alpha]
    G -- carries --> A

    classDef vec fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px,color:#0d0d0d
    classDef host fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#0d0d0d
    class A,B,C,D,E,F vec
    class G host
```

---

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — ColE1-Type Replication Control Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[DNA Template Plasmid] --> R1[Transcription of RNA II pre primer]
    R1 --> R2[RNA II folds into pre primer stem loop]
    R2 --> R3[RNase H cleaves RNA II]
    R3 --> P2[OH 3 prime primer generated]
    P2 --> D1[DNA Pol I initiates synthesis]
    D1 --> D2[Rolling circle like replication]
    D2 --> P3[Daughter plasmid]

    I1[RNA I antisense RNA transcribed] -. inhibits .-> R2
    I2[Rop Rom protein] -. stabilises duplex .-> I1
    P3 --> I1

    classDef rna fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#880e4f,stroke-width:1.5px
    classDef dna fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:1.5px
    classDef reg fill:#ede7f6,stroke:#311b92,stroke-width:1.5px
    class R1,R2,R3,I1,I2 rna
    class D1,D2,P1,P3 dna
    class P2 reg
```

---

## 4.3 Hierarchical Classification of Plasmids

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PL[Plasmids] --> C1[By Transfer]
    PL --> C2[By Copy Number]
    PL --> C3[By Function]

    C1 --> T1[Conjugative Tra genes]
    C1 --> T2[Non conjugative]
    C1 --> T3[Mobilisable Mob only]

    C2 --> S1[Stringent CN 1 to 5]
    C2 --> S2[Relaxed CN 10 to 700]

    C3 --> F1[F fertility]
    C3 --> F2[R resistance MDR]
    C3 --> F3[Col colicinogenic]
    C3 --> F4[Degradative TOL NAH]
    C3 --> F5[Virulence Ti plasmid]
    C3 --> F6[Cryptic unknown function]

    classDef root fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2.5px
    classDef sub fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:1.5px
    classDef leaf fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,stroke-width:1px
    class PL root
    class C1,C2,C3,S1,S2,T1,T2,T3,F1,F2,F3,F4,F5,F6 sub
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> **Mark Distribution Pattern (Module 1, KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> - Part A (2 × 3 = **6 marks**): Direct definitions / short notes
> - Part B (1 × 14 = **14 marks**, with internal choice): Either-Or full question

---

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Q1. Define a plasmid. Mention any two salient features that make plasmids ideal cloning vectors. **[3 marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

> A plasmid is a small, circular, double-stranded DNA molecule that replicates independently of the bacterial chromosome and carries non-essential but often advantageous genes.
>
> **Features making plasmids ideal cloning vectors:**
> 1. **Self-replicating `ori`** — enables autonomous replication in a host, ensuring propagation of the inserted gene.
> 2. **Selectable marker** (e.g., *ampR*) — permits isolation of transformed cells on antibiotic-containing media.
> 3. **Multiple Cloning Site (MCS)** — contains unique restriction sites for convenient insertion of foreign DNA.
>
> *(Any two → 2 marks; definition → 1 mark)*

---

### Q2. Differentiate between stringent and relaxed plasmids with one example each. **[3 marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Stringent Plasmid | Relaxed Plasmid |
|---|---|---|
| Copy number | 1 – 5 copies/cell | 10 – 700 copies/cell |
| Replication | Synchronous with chromosome | Independent of chromosome |
| Control | Tightly regulated by *cop* genes | Loosely controlled |
| Example | pSC101, F-plasmid | ColE1, pBR322, pUC19 |

*(1 mark for definition of each, 1 mark for example)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Full Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### (a) Classify plasmids based on their phenotypic function. Describe the Ti plasmid of *Agrobacterium tumefaciens* and explain how it is exploited in plant genetic engineering. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Classification by phenotypic function (3 marks):**

1. **F-plasmids** – encode sex pili and conjugative transfer (e.g., F-factor).
2. **R-plasmids (Resistance)** – carry antibiotic or heavy-metal resistance genes; responsible for hospital-acquired MDR infections.
3. **Col-plasmids** – encode colicins (bacteriocins) that kill related bacteria.
4. **Degradative plasmids** – carry catabolic operons (TOL = toluene/xylene degradation; NAH = naphthalene degradation).
5. **Virulence plasmids** – encode toxins and host invasion factors.
6. **Cryptic plasmids** – no known phenotype; sequence known but function unknown.

**The Ti plasmid (4 marks):**

*Agrobacterium tumefaciens* is a soil-borne plant pathogen that causes **crown gall disease** in dicotyledonous plants. The causative agent is a large (~200 kb) **Tumour-inducing (Ti) plasmid** containing:

- **T-DNA region (~25 kb):** Transferred and integrated into the plant nuclear genome.
- **vir (virulence) region:** 7 operons (*virA, virB, virC, virD, virE, virG, virH*) that sense wound-released phenolics (acetosyringone) and execute T-DNA transfer.
- **Opine catabolism region:** Allows agrobacteria to metabolise opines (amino acid derivatives) secreted by the gall.

**Mechanism of plant transformation (T-DNA transfer):**

1. **Wound** → plant releases **acetosyringone**.
2. Acetosyringone activates **VirA → VirG** two-component system.
3. **VirD1 + VirD2** endonuclease nicks the 25-bp right border repeat of T-DNA.
4. Single-stranded **T-strand** is generated; **VirE2** ssDNA-binding protein coats it.
5. **VirB** forms a Type IV secretion system (T-pilus) that exports the **T-complex (T-strand + VirE2 + VirD2)** into plant cell.
6. T-DNA is imported into the plant nucleus and **stably integrated** into the plant chromosome.
7. T-DNA encodes **auxin, cytokinin biosynthesis genes** (causing tumour) and **opine synthesis genes** (e.g., nopaline, octopine).

**Exploitation in plant genetic engineering (4 marks — with 2 written below for context):**

The wild T-DNA is replaced with a **disarmed binary vector system**:
- A **binary vector** (e.g., pBIN19) carries the engineered T-DNA with **selectable marker** (*nptII* for kanamycin) and **gene of interest** between the 25-bp borders.
- A separate **helper Ti plasmid** (with *vir* genes but disarmed T-DNA) provides *vir* functions in trans.
- *A. tumefaciens* carrying both vectors is used to infect leaf discs or calli.
- Transformed plant cells are selected on kanamycin, regenerated into whole transgenic plants.

**Advantage:** Stable chromosomal integration, Mendelian inheritance of transgene. This is the basis of virtually all commercial *transgenic dicots* (soybean, cotton, canola).

---

#### (b) Describe the salient features of pBR322. Why is pUC19 preferred over pBR322 in routine cloning work? **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Salient features of pBR322 (4 marks):**

pBR322 (constructed by Bolivar & Rodriguez, 1977) is a 4361-bp cloning vector with:

- **Two selectable markers:** *ampR* (ampicillin, β-lactamase) and *tetR* (tetracycline).
- **Multiple unique restriction sites:** *PstI, PvuI, PstI, BamHI, EcoRI, HindIII, SalI, ClaI* within the marker genes.
- **Relaxed origin of replication (pMB1-derived *ori*):** ~15 – 20 copies per cell.
- **Two origins:** Standard *ori* for dsDNA replication; **SS-origin** (phage f1) for single-stranded DNA production.
- **Insertional inactivation principle:** Cloning into *PstI* (within *ampR*) → ampicillin-sensitive transformants; cloning into *BamHI/EcoRI/SalI* (within *tetR*) → tetracycline-sensitive transformants.

**Screening:** Double antibiotic selection on **LB + Amp + Tet** plate; recombinant colonies are Amp^R Tet^S (or vice versa).

**Why pUC19 is preferred (3 marks):**

| Feature | pBR322 | pUC19 (preferred) |
|---|---|---|
| Copy number | ~15 – 20 | **~500 – 700** (5 – 35× higher yield) |
| Screening | Differential antibiotic resistance (two plates needed) | **Blue/white screening** on X-gal/IPTG (single plate) |
| MCS | Sites scattered | **Compact, clustered MCS** with 13 unique sites |
| M13 phage origin | Present but inconvenient | **Engineered M13 intergenic region** for easy ssDNA production |
| Cloning speed | Slow (need replica plating) | Fast (one-step visual screening) |
| Insert size | Up to ~6 kb | Optimised for 0.5 – 3 kb |

**Conclusion:** Higher copy number + simpler visual screening + faster workflow makes **pUC19 the workhorse vector** for routine subcloning and routine laboratory use.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### (a) Explain the copy number control of ColE1-type plasmids. Derive the expected yield of pUC19 (2686 bp) from a 5 mL overnight culture of *E. coli* DH5α at OD600 = 0.6. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**ColE1 copy number control (3 marks):**

The ColE1 *ori* region transcribes two RNAs:

1. **RNA II (pre-primer, ~550 nt):** Transcribed 555 bp upstream of *ori*. Folds into a stem-loop, and the 3′ end forms a persistent DNA–RNA hybrid at the *ori*. RNase H cleaves this hybrid, leaving a 3′-OH that serves as the **primer for DNA Pol I** to initiate leading-strand synthesis. Replication then proceeds via host enzymes (Pol III, SSB, ligase).

2. **RNA I (antisense, ~108 nt):** Transcribed from the opposite strand, complementary to the 5′ region of RNA II. RNA I binds to RNA II and **inhibits its folding into the primer form** → replication is blocked.

3. **Rop (Rom) protein:** Encoded downstream of RNA I; stabilises the **RNA I / RNA II duplex** → amplifies repression. *rop*-deleted mutants (e.g., pUC) have elevated copy numbers.

**Net result:** Copy number is set by the **steady-state ratio of free RNA II (active primer) to RNA I / Rop-inactivated complexes**.

**Derivation of yield (4 marks):**

**Step 1 — Molar mass of pUC19.**

$$MW \;=\; 2686 \times 660 \;\text{Da} \;=\; 1.77 \times 10^{6} \;\text{g/mol}$$

**Step 2 — Mass of one plasmid molecule.**

$$m_{1} \;=\; \frac{1.77 \times 10^{6}}{6.022 \times 10^{23}} \;\approx\; 2.94 \times 10^{-18} \;\text{g}$$

**Step 3 — Cells per mL at OD600 = 0.6** (for *E. coli*, 1 OD600 ≈ $8 \times 10^8$ cells/mL).

$$N_{cells/mL} \;=\; 0.6 \times 8 \times 10^{8} \;=\; 4.8 \times 10^{8} \;\text{cells/mL}$$

**Step 4 — Total cells in 5 mL culture.**

$$N_{total} \;=\; 4.8 \times 10^{8} \times 5 \;=\; 2.4 \times 10^{9} \;\text{cells}$$

**Step 5 — Number of plasmid molecules** (pUC19 CN ≈ 500).

$$N_{plasmids} \;=\; 500 \times 2.4 \times 10^{9} \;=\; 1.2 \times 10^{12} \;\text{molecules}$$

**Step 6 — Total mass.**

$$m_{total} \;=\; 1.2 \times 10^{12} \times 2.94 \times 10^{-18} \;\text{g}$$

$$m_{total} \;\approx\; 3.53 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{g} \;=\; 3.53 \;\mu\text{g}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Expected pUC19 yield} \;\approx\; 3 - 5 \;\mu\text{g per 5 mL culture}}$$

This matches standard miniprep yields.

---

#### (b) Discuss plasmid incompatibility. How is this property exploited in constructing co-expression systems using two plasmids of different Inc groups? **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Two plasmids belong to the same **incompatibility (Inc) group** if they cannot be stably co-maintained in the same cell line in the absence of external selection. Members of the same Inc group share identical or closely related replication/partitioning control elements, leading to **random segregation** during cell division and eventual loss of one of the plasmids.

**Molecular basis of incompatibility (3 marks):**

1. **Shared *ori*** — two plasmids with identical *ori* sequences compete for the same replication initiation factors.
2. **Shared copy number control** — both plasmids are co-repressed by the same RNA I / Rop system, halving the effective copy number of each, which then leads to random stochastic loss.
3. **Shared partitioning (Par) system** — *parA / parB / parS* loci are co-recognised, disrupting proper segregation at cell division.

**Common Inc groups in cloning vectors:**

| Inc Group | Compatible Vector | Typical CN |
|---|---|---|
| **ColE1 / pMB1** | pBR322, pUC19, pET-28a | High |
| **p15A** | pACYC184, pACYC177 | Low (~10) |
| **pSC101** | pSC101, pBAD | Very low (~5) |
| **IncP (RK2)** | RP4 | Broad host range |
| **IncW** | pSa | Broad host range |

**Exploitation in co-expression systems (3 marks):**

Co-expression of two recombinant proteins (e.g., a heterodimer like HbA1/HbA2, or a metabolic pathway needing two enzymes) requires **two different plasmids** in the same cell. Choosing plasmids from **different Inc groups** ensures:

- **Independent replication** — no competition for replication machinery.
- **Independent copy number regulation** — both maintained at their natural CN.
- **Independent partition systems** — both are faithfully segregated to daughter cells.
- **Different selectable markers** — *ampR* on plasmid-1, *kanR* on plasmid-2 allow dual selection in liquid or on plate.

**Classic example (highly KTU-relevant):**
- Plasmid 1: **pET-28a** (Inc ColE1, *kanR*, T7 promoter) — expresses protein A with His-tag.
- Plasmid 2: **pACYC184** (Inc p15A, *cmR*, T7 promoter compatible) — expresses protein B.
- Both transformed into BL21(DE3) carrying pLysS → double induction with IPTG → both proteins co-expressed.

**Conclusion:** Inc-group incompatibility is a *barrier* in nature but a *design tool* in genetic engineering — by carefully selecting plasmids from different Inc groups, we can build sophisticated multi-plasmid systems for synthetic biology, pathway engineering, and protein co-expression.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where KTU students lose marks on Plasmids:**
> 1. **Forgetting the units** in copy number / yield derivations — always write `g/mol`, `g/mL`, `μg/mL`. Mark deducted if units are missing.
> 2. **Confusing *stringent* and *relaxed*** — many students swap them. Remember: *stringent = tight control = low copy = chromosome-coupled*.
> 3. **Mixing up pBR322 and pUC19 MCS location** — pUC19 has the MCS **inside the *lacZ'α* gene** (for blue/white), not in an antibiotic resistance gene.
> 4. **Writing "RNA I activates replication"** — it **inhibits** replication. Most common biology conceptual error.
> 5. **Saying the Ti plasmid "infects" monocots naturally** — it does **not**; monocots need alternative methods (biolistics, *Agrobacterium*-mediated rice with *vir* gene activation tricks).
> 6. **Incompatibility group mistake** — pBR322 and pUC19 are **both** ColE1-derived and thus **incompatible**; you cannot stably co-maintain them in the same cell.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Last-Minute Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Plasmid = extrachromosomal, circular, dsDNA, self-replicating, non-essential genetic element.**
- [x] Typical size: **1 – 200 kb**; cloning vectors commonly **2.7 – 12 kb**.
- [x] Copy number: **Stringent (1–5) vs Relaxed (10–700)** — controlled by RNA I / RNA II / Rop.
- [x] Five essential vector features: `ori` + selectable marker + MCS + screening gene + (optional) M13 origin.
- [x] pBR322 (4361 bp, ~20 CN, *ampR* + *tetR*); pUC19 (2686 bp, ~500–700 CN, *ampR* + *lacZ'*, blue/white screening).
- [x] **MW of dsDNA = N_bp × 660 Da**; **m per molecule = MW / N_A**.
- [x] **Yield (μg/mL) = CN × cells/mL × MW / N_A** — remember to convert to μg.
- [x] **Ti plasmid of *A. tumefaciens*** — T-DNA + *vir* region; binary vector system for plant transgenics.
- [x] **Incompatibility** — plasmids of the same Inc group cannot co-exist; choose **different** Inc groups (ColE1 + p15A + pSC101) for co-expression.
- [x] Phenotypic classes to memorise: F, R, Col, Degradative, Virulence, Cryptic.
- [x] Standard exam values to recall: 1 OD600 ≈ **8 × 10⁸ cells/mL**; 660 Da/bp; 6.022 × 10²³ mol⁻¹.
- [x] Always state the **mechanism**, not just the name, when explaining antibiotic selection, copy number control, or T-DNA transfer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bacteriophages
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🧬 BACTERIOPHAGES — The Tiny Terrors of the Microbial World

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT524 - GENETIC ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bacteriophages

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Bacteriophages form the foundational chapter of any Genetic Engineering course. Phages are not only model organisms for understanding gene expression and DNA replication but are also the **ancestors of modern cloning vectors**. Mastery of phage biology is essential for understanding lambda vectors, M13 vectors, phage display technology, and CRISPR-Cas systems.

---

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Board-Standard Terminology)

**Bacteriophages** (literally *"bacteria-eaters"*, from Greek: *bakterion* = bacterium, *phagein* = to eat) are **obligate intracellular parasitic viruses** that specifically infect and replicate within bacterial cells. They are composed of a **nucleic acid genome** (either DNA or RNA, single-stranded or double-stranded) enclosed within a **protein capsid**, and in many cases, surrounded by a **lipid-containing tail sheath, tail fibers, and baseplate**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> *"A bacteriophage is a virus that infects bacteria, consisting of a nucleic acid core surrounded by a protein coat (capsid), which may or may not be enclosed by a lipid envelope. It uses the host bacterial machinery to replicate and assemble new viral particles, ultimately causing cell lysis (lytic cycle) or establishing a dormant prophage state (lysogenic cycle)."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Nano-Injector Trojan Horse" 💉

Imagine a **spaceship from an alien civilization** that docks onto a **spaceship station** (the bacterium). Once attached:

1. The alien ship **pierces the hull** (tail sheath contracts, injecting DNA through the bacterial cell wall — like a syringe).
2. The alien **passengers take over the station's control room** (the viral genome hijacks the bacterial ribosomes, polymerases, and nucleotides).
3. The station is forced to **build hundreds of new alien ships** (replication, transcription, translation of viral components).
4. Finally, the station is **blown up from the inside** (cell lysis releases ~100–300 progeny phages).

> **Key insight:** Phages are **not alive** when free in the environment — they are inert molecular machines. They "come alive" only inside a host cell. This is a critical distinction in KTU viva questions.

---

## 1.3 Historical Context (Frequent 3-Mark Question)

| Year | Scientist | Discovery |
|:----:|:---------|:----------|
| **1896** | **Ernest Hankin** | First observed antibacterial activity against *Vibrio cholerae* (Ganges river water) |
| **1915** | **Frederick Twort** | Described "glassy transformation" of bacterial colonies |
| **1917** | **Félix d'Hérelle** | Coined the term *"bacteriophage"*; demonstrated therapeutic potential against dysentery |
| **1938–1940** | **Max Delbrück & Emory Ellis** | Established the **one-step growth curve** (phage biology became quantitative) |
| **1952** | **Alfred Hershey & Martha Chase** | **Hershey-Chase experiment** — proved **DNA is the genetic material** using T2 phage |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** *"Explain the significance of the Hershey-Chase experiment."* → Answer: It used ³²P-labeled DNA and ³⁵S-labeled protein coats of T2 phage to demonstrate that only DNA enters the bacterial cell, conclusively proving DNA — not protein — is the hereditary material.

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

> **Bacteriophage Physical Parameters (T4 reference values):**
> - **Genome size:** ~169 kbp (169,000 base pairs)
> - **Capsid dimensions:** ~85 nm × 115 nm (prolate icosahedral)
> - **Tail length:** ~100 nm
> - **Tail diameter:** ~20 nm
> - **Burst size:** ~200–300 progeny phages per infected cell
> - **Latent period:** ~20–25 minutes at 37 °C
> - **Sedimentation coefficient:** ~1000 S (T4); ~40 S (M13)

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis: Morphology, Classification & Life Cycles

## 2.1 Structural Architecture of Bacteriophages

A typical **tailed bacteriophage** (Caudovirales order) consists of the following parts, each performing a specialized function:

1. **Head (Capsid)** — Icosahedral (20 triangular faces) or prolate icosahedral. Composed of repeating **capsomere** protein subunits (typically 60 × n, where n is the triangulation number T). Stores the nucleic acid.
2. **Neck / Collar** — Connector region between head and tail; contains the **portal protein** through which DNA is injected.
3. **Tail Sheath** — Contractile protein cylinder (in Myoviridae, e.g., T4); undergoes conformational change to inject DNA.
4. **Tail Tube** — Inner hollow channel through which the genome passes into the host cytoplasm.
5. **Baseplate** — Hexagonal platform at the tail's distal end; recognizes host receptors and initiates infection.
6. **Tail Fibers / Pins** — Six leg-like protein appendages that bind **specific receptors** (LPS, Omp proteins, teichoic acids) on the bacterial surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is receptor specificity important for Genetic Engineering?**
> Because tail fibers recognize *species-specific* receptors, phages can be used as **precision delivery vehicles** (e.g., phage display libraries, phage therapy against antibiotic-resistant bacteria).

---

## 2.2 Classification of Bacteriophages

Bacteriophages are classified on **three independent criteria**:

### A. By Morphology (ICTV — International Committee on Taxonomy of Viruses)

| Family | Morphology | Example | Genome |
|:-------|:-----------|:--------|:-------|
| **Myoviridae** | Long contractile tail | T4, T2, Mu | dsDNA, linear |
| **Siphoviridae** | Long non-contractile tail | **Lambda (λ)**, T5 | dsDNA, linear |
| **Podoviridae** | Short non-contractile tail | T7, P22 | dsDNA, linear |
| **Microviridae** | No tail, isometric | **φX174**, M13 (filamentous) | ssDNA, circular |
| **Tectiviridae** | Pseudo-tail, lipid-containing | PRD1 | dsDNA, linear |

> **Memory Trick:** **M**yoviruses = **M**uscular (contractile); **S**iphoviruses = **S**lender (long, non-contractile); **P**odoviruses = **P**int-sized (short tail).

### B. By Genome Type
- **dsDNA phages:** T4, T7, λ (most common)
- **ssDNA phages:** M13, φX174
- **ssRNA phages:** MS2, Qβ
- **dsRNA phages:** φ6 (rare)

### C. By Life Cycle Strategy
- **Virulent phages** → Only **lytic** cycle (e.g., T4, T7)
- **Temperate phages** → Both **lytic and lysogenic** cycles (e.g., **λ, P22, φ80**)
- **Filamentous phages** → **Chronic infection** (e.g., **M13** — no lysis, progeny extruded)

---

## 2.3 The Lytic Life Cycle (T4 Reference Model) 🔥

A **virulent phage** like T4 follows the lytic cycle with the following steps:

### Step-by-Step Logic:
1. **Adsorption** — Tail fibers bind to specific receptors (e.g., *E. coli* OmpC, LPS O-antigen). Recognition is **species-specific**.
2. **Penetration** — Baseplate releases lysozyme; tail sheath contracts; DNA is injected into cytoplasm. **Protein coat remains outside** (Hershey-Chase proof).
3. **Early Gene Expression (0–5 min)** — Phage genes transcribed by host RNA polymerase. Products: **nucleases that degrade host DNA**, new phage polymerase, anti-restriction enzymes.
4. **Replication of Phage DNA (5–12 min)** — Using phage-encoded enzymes, multiple copies of genome are made via rolling circle / theta replication.
5. **Late Gene Expression (12–20 min)** — Structural proteins (capsid, tail, fibers) are synthesized using host ribosomes.
6. **Assembly (Maturation)** — Capsid proteins self-assemble into pro-heads; DNA is packaged by a **motor complex** (terminase + portal).
7. **Lysis (20–30 min)** — Phage-encoded **lysozyme (T4 gene *e*)** and **holin proteins** disrupt the cell wall and inner membrane, releasing ~200–300 progeny phages.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-step Growth Curve (Delbrück & Ellis, 1939):** The plot of phage titer (log scale) vs. time after infection has three distinct phases:
> - **Eclipse phase:** No infectious particles inside the cell.
> - **Latent period:** Particles mature but not yet released.
> - **Rise period / Burst:** Sudden increase as cells lyse.
> - **Plateau:** All cells lysed; titer constant.

---

## 2.4 The Lysogenic Life Cycle (Lambda Phage) 🧘

**Temperate phages** (e.g., **λ phage**) can choose between lytic and lysogenic pathways based on environmental conditions (e.g., nutrient stress, UV damage, SOS response).

### Key Steps:
1. **Adsorption & Injection** — Same as lytic.
2. **Decision Point** — CI repressor vs. Cro protein competition at **Pₗ / Pᵣ / Pₘ operators**.
   - If **CI wins** → Lysogeny
   - If **Cro wins** → Lytic cycle
3. **Integration** — Phage genome (linear) is circularized via **cos sites** (cohesive ends) and integrated into *E. coli* chromosome at the **attB** site via site-specific recombination mediated by **phage-encoded integrase (Int)** and **host factor IHF**.
4. **Prophage State** — The integrated phage DNA is replicated passively with the host chromosome. The cell is now a **lysogen**, immune to superinfection.
5. **Induction** — Under stress (UV, mitomycin C), the host **RecA** protein is activated, which cleaves the **CI repressor**, triggering **prophage excision** and entry into the lytic cycle.

> **Genetic Engineering Significance:** The λ phage's ability to integrate and excise precisely is the basis for the most widely used **cosmid vectors** and **λ-derived replacement vectors** (e.g., λEMBL3, λZAP).

---

## 2.5 The M13 / Filamentous Phage Lifecycle (Unique!) 🧵

**M13** is a **filamentous, single-stranded DNA phage** that does **not lyse** its host. Instead:

- It adsorbs to the **F-pilus** of *E. coli* via the **gene III protein (g3p)**.
- The (+) ssDNA genome enters and is converted to **dsRF (replicative form)** by host enzymes.
- New (+) ssDNA strands are continuously produced and extruded through the host membrane, coated with **gene VIII protein (gVIIIp)**.
- Host cells continue to **grow and divide** (albeit slower), releasing ~1000 phages per generation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **M13 in Genetic Engineering:** Because ssDNA is packaged, **M13 vectors (M13mp18/19)** are the workhorses for **Sanger DNA sequencing** and **site-directed mutagenesis** (Kunkel's method). The **M13 phage display system** (George Smith, 1985; Nobel Prize 2018) fuses peptides to g3p for antibody engineering.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Quick Reference Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes key quantitative relationships for KTU numerical problems. Note: All cell content uses `$\vert$` notation to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax.

| Parameter | Formula / Definition | Units | Significance |
|:----------|:--------------------|:------|:-------------|
| **Burst Size (B)** | $B = \dfrac{\text{Total progeny phages released}}{\text{Number of infected cells}}$ | dimensionless | ~100–300 (T4), ~1000 (M13) |
| **Phage Titer (PFU/mL)** | $T = \dfrac{\text{Number of plaques}}{\text{Volume plated (mL)} \times \text{Dilution factor}}$ | PFU/mL | Quantifies phage concentration |
| **Multiplicity of Infection (MOI)** | $\text{MOI} = \dfrac{\text{Number of phages}}{\text{Number of bacteria}}$ | ratio | MOI $\vert$ 1 → synchronous infection |
| **Latent Period (L)** | Time from adsorption to first intracellular phage | minutes | ~20–25 min (T4 at 37°C) |
| **Rise Period (R)** | Time of exponential increase in phage titer | minutes | ~10 min (T4) |
| **Eclipse Period** | Time during which no infective particles exist inside cell | minutes | ~12 min (T4) |
| **Adsorption Rate Constant (k)** | $\dfrac{dP}{dt} = -k \cdot C_t \cdot P_t$ | mL/min | First-order kinetics |
| **% Adsorbed** | $\dfrac{P_0 - P_t}{P_0} \times 100$ | % | ~99% in 10 min (T4) |
| **Prophage Induction Frequency** | $\dfrac{\text{Spontaneous induction events}}{\text{Cells per generation}}$ | $\sim 10^{-5}$ to $10^{-6}$ | Low baseline; UV raises it |
| **Cosmid Vector Capacity** | $\leq \dfrac{\lambda \text{ genome size}}{2} - \text{vector backbone} \approx 40\text{–}45 \text{ kbp}$ | kbp | Larger insert than plasmid (typical plasmid = 8–10 kbp) |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Applications in Genetic Engineering

1. **Lambda (λ) Phage Vectors** — Used for genomic library construction; can package 40–50 kbp inserts.
2. **M13 Vectors** — Single-stranded DNA production for sequencing and mutagenesis.
3. **Phage Display** — Peptide/protein libraries displayed on g3p or gVIIIp (Smith, 1985; Nobel 2018).
4. **Helper Phages (M13KO7)** — Provide *trans* packaging proteins for phagemid rescue.
5. **CRISPR-Cas Systems** — Originally an **adaptive bacterial immune system** against phages; now engineered as a genome-editing tool.
6. **Phage Therapy** — Re-emerging as an alternative to antibiotics for multi-drug resistant (MDR) infections.
7. **Bacteriophage Recombineering with Electroporated DNA (BRED)** — Used for rapid mutagenesis of *Mycobacterium tuberculosis*.
8. **λ-Red System (Gam, Beta, Exo)** — Enables recombineering in *E. coli*; critical for BAC modifications.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Calculations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Quantitative Derivation: The One-Step Growth Curve

The one-step growth experiment allows us to determine the **burst size (B)** and **latent period (L)**. The mathematical model is as follows.

### Step 1: Setup
Let $P_0$ be the initial phage concentration and $C_0$ be the bacterial concentration at $t = 0$. We mix them at a low MOI (typically MOI $\vert$ 0.1) so that each infected cell contains only one phage (single-burst condition).

### Step 2: Adsorption Kinetics (First-Order)
The number of unadsorbed phages $P_t$ at time $t$ follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dP_t}{dt} &= -k \cdot C_t \cdot P_t \\
\text{Assuming } C_t &\approx C_0 \text{ (constant):} \\
\frac{dP_t}{dt} &= -k \cdot C_0 \cdot P_t \\
\text{Integrating both sides:} \quad \int_{P_0}^{P_t} \frac{dP}{P} &= -\int_{0}^{t} k C_0 \, dt \\
\ln\left(\frac{P_t}{P_0}\right) &= -k C_0 t \\
P_t &= P_0 \cdot e^{-k C_0 t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** We treat the adsorption event as a bimolecular collision with rate constant $k$. The first-order approximation holds when $C_0$ is in vast excess.

### Step 3: Burst Size Calculation
After lysis, the **average burst size** is calculated as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
B &= \frac{\text{Total phages in lysate (PL)} - \text{Unadsorbed phages (P_f)}}{\text{Number of infected cells}} \\
B &= \frac{(P_L - P_f) \cdot V}{C_0 \cdot V \cdot (1 - e^{-k C_0 t_{ads}})} \\
B &= \frac{P_L - P_f}{C_0 \cdot f_{ads}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $f_{ads} = 1 - e^{-k C_0 t_{ads}}$ is the **fraction of phages adsorbed**.

### Step 4: Worked Numerical Example
**Problem:** $1 \times 10^8$ phages are mixed with $1 \times 10^8$ bacteria. Adsorption rate constant $k = 2.5 \times 10^{-9}$ mL/min. After 15 minutes, all cells are lysed, and the final titer is $3 \times 10^{10}$ PFU/mL in a total volume of 1 mL.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MOI} &= \frac{1 \times 10^8}{1 \times 10^8} = 1.0 \\
f_{ads} &= 1 - e^{-(2.5 \times 10^{-9})(1 \times 10^8)(15)} \\
&= 1 - e^{-3.75} \\
&= 1 - 0.0235 \\
&= 0.9765 \\
\text{Infected cells} &= 1 \times 10^8 \times 0.9765 = 9.765 \times 10^7 \\
B &= \frac{3 \times 10^{10} - (1 \times 10^8 \times 0.0235)}{9.765 \times 10^7} \\
B &= \frac{3 \times 10^{10} - 2.35 \times 10^6}{9.765 \times 10^7} \\
B &\approx \frac{3 \times 10^{10}}{9.765 \times 10^7} \approx 307 \text{ phages/burst}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer: Burst Size ≈ 307** (consistent with T4's reported value of 200–300).

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Phage Comparison Table

This matrix is **extremely high-yield** for KTU board exams (often a 14-mark essay question).

| Feature | T4 Phage | Lambda (λ) Phage | M13 Phage | T7 Phage | φX174 Phage |
|:--------|:---------|:----------------|:----------|:---------|:------------|
| **Family** | Myoviridae | Siphoviridae | Inoviridae | Podoviridae | Microviridae |
| **Genome** | dsDNA, linear | dsDNA, linear | ssDNA, circular (+) | dsDNA, linear | ssDNA, circular (+) |
| **Genome Size** | 169 kbp | 48.5 kbp | 6.4 kb | 39.9 kbp | 5.4 kb |
| **Host Receptor** | LPS + OmpC | LamB (maltose porin) | F-pilus (g3p) | LPS | LPS |
| **Life Cycle** | Virulent (lytic) | Temperate (lytic/lysogenic) | Chronic (extrusion) | Virulent (lytic) | Virulent (lytic) |
| **Burst Size** | ~200–300 | ~100 (lytic) | ~1000 | ~200 | ~180 |
| **Latent Period** | 20–25 min | 35–45 min | Continuous | 11 min | 13 min |
| **Integration?** | No (plasmid?) | **Yes** (attB site) | No (episomal RF) | No | No |
| **Key GE Application** | Phage therapy, model organism | λ vectors, cosmids, genomic libraries | Sequencing, phage display, mutagenesis | T7 expression system | ssDNA production |
| **Cosmid Origin?** | — | **Yes (cos sites)** | — | — | — |
| **Special Feature** | Hydroxymethylcytosine (HMC) base | Site-specific recombination; CI/Cro switch | Filamentous, no lysis | T7 RNAP → T7 promoter | Overlapping genes, small genome |

---

## 3.3 Decision-Making at the λ Phage Operator Region

The **lytic vs. lysogenic switch** is mathematically modeled by the **Hill equation** for cooperative binding of the CI repressor to the $O_{R1}$ and $O_{R2}$ operators.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta &= \frac{[CI]^n}{K_d^n + [CI]^n} \\
\text{Where:} \quad \theta &= \text{fractional occupancy of operator} \\
n &= \text{Hill coefficient} \approx 2\text{–}3 \text{ (cooperativity)} \\
K_d &= \text{dissociation constant} \approx 10^{-9} \text{ M} \\
[CI] &= \text{CI repressor concentration (per cell)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic of conversion:**
- At **low [CI]** → $\theta \to 0$ → promoters Pₗ and Pᵣ are inactive → **Cro wins** → Lytic cycle.
- At **high [CI]** → $\theta \to 1$ → Pₘ activated (maintains lysogeny); Pₗ represses Pᵣ → **Lysogeny**.

This is a **bistable switch**, mathematically analogous to a Schmitt trigger in electronics.

---

## 3.4 Adsorption Efficiency Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Type Question:** *"A phage preparation has a titer of $5 \times 10^{10}$ PFU/mL. If 0.1 mL is mixed with 0.9 mL of a bacterial culture (concentration $1 \times 10^8$ cells/mL), what is the MOI?"*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Phages added} &= 5 \times 10^{10} \text{ PFU/mL} \times 0.1 \text{ mL} = 5 \times 10^9 \text{ phages} \\
\text{Bacteria added} &= 1 \times 10^8 \text{ cells/mL} \times 0.9 \text{ mL} = 9 \times 10^7 \text{ cells} \\
\text{MOI} &= \frac{5 \times 10^9}{9 \times 10^7} \\
\text{MOI} &\approx 55.6
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer: MOI ≈ 55.6** → This is a high MOI, suitable for **"phage challenge"** experiments but not for one-step growth curves (need MOI $\vert$ 1 for single-burst).

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

| Single-strand genome production | M13mp18/19 | Sanger sequencing |
| Surface protein display (g3p) | Phagemid display | Antibody, peptide libraries |
| Strong T7 promoter + T7 RNAP | pET vectors | Recombinant protein production |
| Tail fiber specificity | Phage therapy | Targeted antibacterial treatment |
| Prophage induction by UV | SOS-inducible systems | Controlled gene expression |
| Phage-encoded RecA-like systems | λ-Red recombineering | BAC engineering |
