> 2. **Using wrong formula** — confusing $2^{-\Delta\Delta C_t}$ with $2^{\Delta\Delta C_t}$ (the latter gives inverse results, often below 1).
> 3. **Skipping the ΔC_t step** — using raw Ct values directly without normalizing to the endogenous reference (loses 2 marks).
> 4. **Forgetting to specify** that the formula assumes **100% PCR efficiency** — examiners expect this assumption to be stated.
> 5. **Unit confusion** — writing "fold increase" vs. "fold change" interchangeably without stating the baseline (calibrator = 1×).

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — qPCR Chemistries**

- **qPCR detects amplicon during exponential phase** — Ct is read here; **never in plateau**.
- **SYBR Green I** = generic dsDNA-binding cyanine dye; cheap, requires **mandatory melt curve**; **cannot multiplex**.
- **TaqMan probe** = dual-labeled oligonucleotide (reporter + quencher); **5′ nuclease activity of *Taq* polymerase** cleaves probe; **gold standard for clinical diagnostics**; high multiplexing capacity (5–6 channels).
- **Molecular Beacon** = stem-loop probe; fluorescence ON only when loop binds target and stem opens; **best for SNP/mismatch discrimination**; **not enzymatically cleaved**.
- **FRET** (Förster Resonance Energy Transfer) governs quenching in both TaqMan and Molecular Beacon; distance dependency follows $E_{\text{FRET}} = \frac{1}{1 + (r/R_0)^6}$.
- **PCR amplification equation:** $N = N_0 \cdot (1+E)^n$ — efficiency E is ideally **1.0 (100%)**, with corresponding standard curve slope of **−3.32**.
- **ΔΔCt (Livak) method:** Fold change $= 2^{-\Delta\Delta C_t}$ — assumes 100% efficiency, equal target and reference primer efficiencies.
- **MIQE guidelines** (Minimum Information for Publication of Quantitative Real-Time PCR Experiments, Bustin et al., 2009) — KTU expects awareness of these publication standards.
- **Tm formula** for SYBR Green melt-curve analysis: $T_m \approx 81.5 + 16.6\log_{10}([Na^+]) + 0.41(\%GC) - 675/L$.
- **Engineering trade-off:** SYBR Green = cheapest, lowest specificity; TaqMan = mid-cost, highest specificity & multiplexing; Molecular Beacon = highest specificity & lowest background but most complex design.
- **Common applications:** Gene expression (RT-qPCR), viral load monitoring (HIV, HBV, SARS-CoV-2), GMO detection, pathogen detection, SNP genotyping, copy number variation (CNV) analysis.
- **Typical Ct range for valid quantification:** **5–35 cycles**; Ct > 35 indicates low-copy or non-specific amplification.
- **Dynamic range:** **5–7 logs** of template concentration (superior to end-point PCR).
- **Probe vs. primer design rule:** Probe Tm should be **7–10 °C higher** than primer Tm to ensure probe binds before primers during annealing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** When asked to "compare and contrast" any two qPCR chemistries, **always structure your answer as a table** followed by a short concluding statement on application suitability. This guarantees full marks for the comparison criterion.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Site-directed Mutagenesis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT524 - GENETIC ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Site-directed Mutagenesis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Site-Directed Mutagenesis (SDM)** is a molecular biology technique used to create *specific*, *pre-designed* and *intentional* alterations (substitutions, insertions, or deletions) at precisely defined positions within a DNA sequence of interest. The modification is targeted to a particular site — typically a single nucleotide or a small cluster of nucleotides — encoded entirely by the design of a synthetic mutagenic oligonucleotide primer that is complementary to the template DNA except at the desired point of mutation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Definition (Examination Gold):**
> *"Site-directed mutagenesis is an in-vitro technique that uses a synthetic oligonucleotide primer containing the desired mutation to introduce a defined, predetermined change at a specific locus of a cloned DNA sequence."*

In the KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, this topic maps to:
- **CO2:** *Apply various in-vitro mutagenesis strategies to engineer nucleic acid sequences for functional studies and recombinant protein engineering.*
- **BT-CO2 / PO3 / PO5**

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Overview)

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Word-Processor Spell-Check Analogy"** 🧬✍️
>
> Imagine you have a printed novel (your gene) and you want to change the word "**RED**" on page 142, line 3, to "**BLUE**" — *without* changing any other letter in the entire 1,000-page book.
>
> - **Traditional random mutagenesis (UV/chemical):** is like spilling coffee on random pages — unpredictable, messy, requires massive screening.
> - **Site-directed mutagenesis:** is the molecular biology equivalent of a "Find & Replace" function. You design a small "search-and-replace" primer (like a sticky note) that matches everything around the target *except* the deliberate change. When the cell's own DNA-copying machine (polymerase) reads this primer, it obediently copies in your "BLUE" change forever.

**The Three Pillars of an SDM Experiment:**

| Pillar | Real-World Equivalent | Molecular Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Template DNA** | The "novel" you want to edit | Plasmid, M13 ssDNA, or dsDNA |
| **Mutagenic Primer** | The "search-and-replace" sticky note | Synthetic oligo with internal mismatch |
| **DNA Polymerase** | The "copy machine" | Klenow, Taq, Pfu, Phusion, Q5 |

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Standard primer length for SDM:** **18–30 nucleotides** (optimally **25–45 nt** for cassette-free methods).
> - **Mismatch position rule:** The deliberate mismatch should be placed at the **center** of the primer to maximize mismatch-repair rejection of the parental strand.
> - **Standard annealing temperature:** **5–10 °C below** the calculated primer **Tm** (Melting temperature).
> - **Mutagenesis efficiency benchmark:** ≥ **80%** for a high-quality commercial kit; **> 50%** is acceptable for academic purposes.
> - **Universal genetic code constant:** **64 codons / 20 amino acids / 3 stop codons.**

---

## 1.4 Classification of Site-Directed Mutagenesis (KTU 2024 Module 2)

```
Site-Directed Mutagenesis
│
├── 1. Oligonucleotide-Directed Mutagenesis (Classical)
│       ├── a) Kunkel's Method (ssDNA / uracil template)
│       ├── b) Eckstein's Method (phosphorothioate selection)
│       └── c) Double-Primer Method (Amersham/Muta-Gene)
│
├── 2. PCR-Based Mutagenesis
│       ├── a) Overlap Extension PCR (Horton, 1989)
│       ├── b) Megaprimer Method (Sarkar & Sommer, 1990)
│       └── c) Inverse PCR (Ochman, 1988)
│
├── 3. Cassette / Region-Directed Mutagenesis
│       (uses synthetic dsDNA "cassette" with pre-made changes)
│
└── 4. Commercial Kit-Based (QuickChange™ / Q5 Site-Directed Mutagenesis)
       (whole-plasmid, single-round, DpnI digestion)
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Logic — The "Why" Behind the Technique

A mutagenic primer is a chemically synthesized oligonucleotide (typically **18–45 nt**) that is **complementary to the template DNA** at every position *except* the designed mismatch (substitution), or *plus/minus* a small insertion/deletion. When this primer is extended by a DNA polymerase, the newly synthesized strand carries the desired mutation as a permanent, heritable change.

### 2.1.1 The Core Operational Logic (Step-wise)

1. **Primer Design:** A mutagenic primer is synthesized with the desired base change (or codon swap) placed **at the center**, flanked by 8–15 perfectly matching bases on each side.
2. **Annealing:** The primer hybridizes to the template DNA (single-stranded M13, or denatured plasmid) at a calculated *T<sub>m</sub>*.
3. **Extension & Ligation:** DNA polymerase extends the 3′-OH of the primer, and DNA ligase seals the nick — generating a circular, double-stranded, *heteroduplex* molecule.
4. **Mismatch-Repair Resolution:** In vivo or in vitro, the *parental (unmodified)* strand is selectively degraded or biologically rejected, leaving the *mutant strand* to propagate.
5. **Screening:** Transform the product into a bacterial host and screen by colony PCR, restriction-site loss/gain, or direct sequencing.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Heteroduplex Molecule**
> After one round of synthesis, the plasmid exists as a *heteroduplex* — one wild-type parental strand and one mutant daughter strand. The two strands are physically distinct but base-paired. This is the molecular foundation of nearly every SDM strategy.

---

## 2.2 The KTU 2024 High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Variables & Units | Engineering / Exam Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Mutation Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{N_{mut}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$ | $\eta$ = efficiency (%), $N_{mut}$ = mutant colonies, $N_{total}$ = total transformants | Reporting quality of an SDM experiment |
| 2 | **Primer Melting Temperature (Wallace rule, ≤25 nt)** | $T_{m} = 2^{\circ}C\,(A+T) + 4^{\circ}C\,(G+C)$ | Sum of A,T and G,C bases in primer | Quick $T_m$ for short oligos |
| 3 | **Primer $T_m$ (NN thermodynamics, >25 nt)** | $T_{m} = \dfrac{\Delta H}{\Delta S + R \ln(C_T / x)} - 273.15 + 16.6 \log_{10}[Na^+]$ | $\Delta H, \Delta S$ = enthalpy/entropy; $C_T$ = primer conc.; $x=4$ for self-complementary, $1$ otherwise; $[Na^+]$ in molar | Accurate design of mutagenic primers |
| 4 | **GC clamp requirement** | $\ge 1$ G or C within last **3 bases** of 3′-end | — | Prevents "primer sliding" |
| 5 | **Mismatch $\Delta T_m$ penalty** | $\Delta T_m \approx 0.5\text{–}1.5^{\circ}C$ per mismatch | Empirical | Predicts polymerase acceptance |
| 6 | **Template amount (QuickChange)** | $m = 5\text{–}50 \text{ ng dsDNA}$ (per 50 µL rxn) | — | Standard input mass |
| 7 | **PCR cycle number (overlap extension)** | $N_{cycles} \le 30$ | — | Avoids random PCR errors |
| 8 | **Number of transformants required** | $N_{colonies} = \dfrac{\ln(1-P)}{\ln(1-f)}$ | $P$ = probability of finding mutant, $f$ = mutation efficiency fraction | Statistical design of screening effort |
| 9 | **Codon usage bias** (silent mutation) | $\text{CAI} = \dfrac{\text{codon usage of gene}}{\text{codon usage of highly expressed genes}}$ | CAI $\in [0,1]$ | Optimizing heterologous expression |
| 10 | **Plasmid yield (mini-prep)** | $m_{dsDNA} = 3\text{–}5\,\mu g$ per 5 mL culture | Mass yield | Sufficient input for SDM |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Pipe Collision Avoidance:** In the formulas above, all absolute-value and conditional bars are written using LaTeX's `\vert` or descriptive phrasing (e.g., "GC content", "$\ge 1$") — *never* as bare pipes `|x|` — to prevent breaking the table parser.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Specific Use of SDM |
|---|---|
| **Protein Engineering** | Swap an active-site residue (e.g., His → Ala in serine proteases) to dissect catalytic mechanism |
| **Drug Discovery** | Introduce a resistance mutation in a viral target to study drug binding |
| **Vaccine Design** | Attenuate a pathogen by precise knockout of a virulence gene |
| **Industrial Enzymology** | Raise the $T_{opt}$ of a thermolabile lipase by introducing disulfide bridges |
| **Synthetic Biology** | Re-code a gene to use non-standard amino acids (e.g., amber suppression at a single UAG codon) |
| **Crystallography** | Introduce a single Cys for Hg-derivatization in X-ray phasing |
| **CRISPR Optimization** | Engineer high-fidelity Cas9 variants (e.g., eSpCas9) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Take-Home:** SDM is the *cornerstone* of modern protein engineering. *Every* FDA-approved monoclonal antibody or industrial enzyme has, at some point, been "improved" by a site-directed mutation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Primer $T_m$ Using the Wallace Rule (For Short Primers)

### Problem Statement
A KTU 2024 student is designing a mutagenic primer of length 20 nt with the sequence:

> 5′-G A T C **A** G C T A C G T T A A C C G G T T-3′
> (where the bold "A" is the *deliberate mismatch* with the template, replacing a "G")

Calculate $T_m$ using the Wallace rule.

---

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Count the A, T, G, C bases (excluding the mismatch, which is part of the same primer).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= 4 \quad (\text{positions: G, A, A, A}) \\
T &= 4 \quad (\text{positions: T, T, T, T}) \\
G &= 5 \quad (\text{positions: G, G, G, G, G}) \\
C &= 5 \quad (\text{positions: C, C, C, C, C}) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Verify that A+T + G+C = total length:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(A+T) + (G+C) &= 4+4+5+5 = 18 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

(The 2 remaining bases are part of the flanking region; total = 20. For simplicity, we treat all 20 bases uniformly, as is done in the original Wallace *et al.* 1979 method.)

> **Conversion Logic:** Wallace's rule assumes each A–T base pair contributes **2 °C** and each G–C base pair contributes **4 °C** to the melting temperature. This is a linear empirical approximation valid for oligos $\le 25$ nt and $[\text{Na}^+] = 50$ mM.

**Step 3 — Apply the Wallace formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{m} &= 2^{\circ}C \cdot (A+T) + 4^{\circ}C \cdot (G+C) \\
T_{m} &= 2(4+4) + 4(5+5) \\
T_{m} &= 2(8) + 4(10) \\
T_{m} &= 16 + 40 \\
T_{m} &= 56^{\circ}C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply mismatch penalty.** The empirical $T_m$ reduction for a single internal mismatch is approximately **0.5–1.5 °C**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{m}^{\text{eff}} &= T_m - \Delta T_m^{\text{mismatch}} \\
T_{m}^{\text{eff}} &\approx 56 - 1.0 \\
T_{m}^{\text{eff}} &\approx 55^{\circ}C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Set annealing temperature for PCR-based SDM:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{a} &= T_{m}^{\text{eff}} - 5^{\circ}C \\
T_{a} &\approx 50^{\circ}C
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Real-World Significance:** This 5–10 °C safety margin compensates for the *two* primers binding simultaneously, ion concentration variability, and enzyme kinetics. A KTU student who miscalculates $T_a$ will obtain *zero* colonies.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Statistical Screening Effort (How Many Colonies to Pick?)

### Problem Statement
You performed a QuickChange™ SDM reaction. The expected mutation efficiency is $\eta = 75\%$. How many colonies must you pick and sequence to be **99% certain** ($P = 0.99$) of finding at least one mutant?

---

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — State the underlying probability.**

The probability that a *single* randomly picked colony is **mutant** is $f = \eta = 0.75$.

The probability that a *single* colony is **wild-type** is $1 - f = 0.25$.

**Step 2 — Probability that $N$ independent colonies are *all* wild-type:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{all WT}}(N) &= (1 - f)^{N} = (0.25)^{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Probability of finding at least *one* mutant among $N$ colonies:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\ge 1 \text{ mutant}}(N) &= 1 - (1 - f)^{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Set the required confidence and solve for $N$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 - (1 - f)^{N} &= P \\
(1 - f)^{N} &= 1 - P \\
N \ln(1 - f) &= \ln(1 - P) \\
N &= \dfrac{\ln(1 - P)}{\ln(1 - f)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Plug in numbers** ($P = 0.99, f = 0.75$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \dfrac{\ln(1 - 0.99)}{\ln(1 - 0.75)} \\
N &= \dfrac{\ln(0.01)}{\ln(0.25)} \\
N &= \dfrac{-4.6052}{-1.3863} \\
N &= 3.32
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Round up (you cannot pick 0.32 of a colony!):**

$$
\boxed{N = 4 \text{ colonies}}
$$

> **Engineering Take-Home:** With 75% efficient mutagenesis, picking just **4 colonies** and screening them (e.g., by colony PCR + sequencing) gives 99% confidence. With only 25% efficiency, you would need $\mathbf{N = \dfrac{\ln(0.01)}{\ln(0.75)} = 16}$ colonies.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Overlap Extension PCR Mutagenesis Cycle Math

In the **Horton *et al.* (1989) overlap extension method**, two primary PCRs and one secondary PCR are run.

### Step 3.1 — Yield of the Final Mutant Amplicon

If the two primary PCRs produce $Y_1$ and $Y_2$ micrograms of DNA, and the secondary PCR uses $a$ µL of each as template, the yield of the *final full-length mutant product* is approximately:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{final} &\approx Y_1 \cdot f_{overlap} \cdot Y_2 \cdot (1 + E_{PCR})^{n}
\end{aligned}
$$

where:
- $f_{overlap}$ = fraction of primary products with correctly overlapping ends (typically $f_{overlap} \approx 0.5$),
- $E_{PCR}$ = per-cycle efficiency of the polymerase (e.g., $E = 0.9$ for Taq, $0.95$ for Pfu),
- $n$ = number of cycles in the secondary PCR.

### Step 3.2 — Analytical Matrix (Comparison of SDM Methods)

| Parameter | Kunkel (ssDNA) | Overlap Extension | Megaprimer | QuickChange™ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Template** | M13 ssDNA (uracil) | Two separate PCR products | PCR product + 1 oligo pair | dsDNA plasmid |
| **Steps** | 2 | 3 | 2 | 1 |
| **Primer cost** | 1 oligo | 2 oligo pairs | 1 oligo pair (2nd = megaprimer) | 1 oligo pair (back-to-back) |
| **Efficiency** | 50–90% | 60–95% | 50–85% | 75–95% |
| **Cloning needed?** | Yes (sub-clone) | No (PCR amplicon) | No (PCR amplicon) | No (whole plasmid) |
| **Insertion/deletion possible?** | Yes (small) | Yes (any size) | Yes (small) | Yes (small) |
| **KTU 2024 popularity** | ⭐⭐ Historical | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Time** | 2–3 days | 1 day | 1 day | 4–6 hours |

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Kunkel's Method (Single-Stranded Uracil Method)

**The Kunkel Method** (1985) is the **classical KTU 2024 syllabus must-know**. It exploits *dUTPase-deficient* (*dut⁻*) and *uracil-N-glycosylase-deficient* (*ung⁻*) *E. coli* strains (e.g., CJ236) to package uracil-containing ssDNA templates.

### The Biochemical Logic (Why It Works)

1. The template is grown in a *dut⁻ ung⁻* host, so it incorporates **dUTP** in place of dTTP → ssDNA is rich in **uracil**.
2. A mutagenic primer is annealed, and a DNA polymerase (Klenow fragment, no strand-displacement needed) extends it in vitro → produces a new strand that is **thymine-rich**.
3. The product is transformed into a *ung⁺ dut⁺* normal host (e.g., DH5α).
4. The host's **uracil-N-glycosylase (UNG)** excises the uracil bases from the parental strand, generating **abasic sites**.
5. Cellular endonucleases nick and degrade the abasic parental strand.
6. The mutant, thymine-rich daughter strand is replicated and propagated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Molecular Trick:** The cell *deliberately* destroys the template strand because it is "marked" with uracil, but leaves the mutant strand untouched. No in vitro enrichment step is required.

### Mathematical Consequence — Enrichment Factor

Let $\varepsilon$ be the survival fraction of the uracil-containing parental strand after UNG treatment. The **enrichment factor** for the mutant strand is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Enrichment} &= \dfrac{1 - \varepsilon}{\varepsilon}
\end{aligned}
$$

If UNG degrades **99%** of the parental strand ($\varepsilon = 0.01$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Enrichment} &= \dfrac{0.99}{0.01} = 99\text{-fold}
\end{aligned}
$$

This 99-fold enrichment means that even with an initial 50% mutation efficiency, the *practical* yield of mutants after UNG digestion rises to nearly 100%.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — QuickChange™ Site-Directed Mutagenesis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Wild Type Plasmid Template 5 to 10 ng dsDNA"] --> B["Thermal Denaturation 95C for 30 sec"]
    B --> C["Annealing of Two Back to Back Mutagenic Primers T m minus 5C"]
    C --> D["Polymerase Extension High Fidelity Pfu Turbo 68C 1 min per kb"]
    D --> E["Linear dsDNA Product with 5 Prime Phosphates and Nicked Ends"]
    E --> F["DpnI Digestion 37C 1 hour Targets Methylated Parental Strand"]
    F --> G["Selectively Degraded Parental Template dGm6ATC Methyl Sites"]
    G --> H["Remaining Circular Mutant Plasmid with Designed Substitution"]
    H --> I["Transformation into DH5alpha Competent Cells"]
    I --> J["Repair of Nick by Host Ligase"]
    J --> K["Mutant Colonies Screened by Colony PCR and Sequencing"]
    K --> L["Confirmed SDM Clone Glycerol Stock 80C"]

    style A fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1976D2,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#FFE0E0,stroke:#C62828,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px
    style L fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#F57C00,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The **red box (F)** is the *selective kill step* — DpnI recognizes the methylated sequence **5′-G<sup>m6</sup>A↓T C-3′** (typical of *dam*-methylated *E. coli* DNA) and cleaves only the *parental* strand. The newly synthesized mutant strand is *unmethylated* and survives. This is the *molecular sleight-of-hand* of QuickChange™.

---

## 4.2 Block Architecture — Overlap Extension PCR (Horton Method)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PCR1["Primary PCR Reaction 1"]
        P1A["Forward Flanking Primer F1"] --> X1["Amplicon 1 Contains Desired Mutation at 3 Prime End"]
        X1 --> P1B["Reverse Mutagenic Primer R mut"]
    end

    subgraph PCR2["Primary PCR Reaction 2"]
        P2A["Forward Mutagenic Primer F mut"] --> X2["Amplicon 2 Contains Desired Mutation at 5 Prime End"]
        X2 --> P2B["Reverse Flanking Primer R2"]
    end

    X1 --> MIX["Mix Purify Combine Amplicon 1 and Amplicon 2"]
    X2 --> MIX
    MIX --> DEN["Denaturation 95C Annealing 55C Extension 72C 25 cycles"]
    DEN --> OVL["Overlap Annealing 3 Prime Ends of Amplicon 1 and 5 Prime of Amplicon 2 are Complementary"]
    OVL --> EXT["Mutagenic Primer Sequences act as Overhangs"]
    EXT --> AMP["Full Length Mutant Amplicon 100 percent of template"]
    AMP --> SEQ["Confirm by Sanger Sequencing"]

    style PCR1 fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px
    style PCR2 fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px
    style AMP fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px
    style SEQ fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#880E4F,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Which SDM Method Should I Use?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Start Choose Your SDM Method"] --> Q1{"Is your target change a single amino acid or fewer than 6 nucleotides"}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{"Is your gene already cloned in a plasmid"}
    Q2 -- Yes --> QC["Use QuickChange or Q5 Site Directed Mutagenesis 4 to 6 hours"]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{"Do you have a high quality plasmid prep"}
    Q3 -- Yes --> QC
    Q3 -- No --> MP["Use Megaprimer Method 1 day"]

    Q1 -- No --> Q4{"Do you want to insert a large cassette or a tag over 20 aa"]
    Q4 -- Yes --> CS["Use Cassette Mutagenesis Replace Restriction Fragment"]
    Q4 -- No --> Q5{"Do you have the full length gene available as PCR amplicon"}
    Q5 -- Yes --> OE["Use Overlap Extension PCR Most Versatile"]
    Q5 -- No --> KN["Consider Kunkel Method with M13 Sub clone"]

    style START fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,stroke-width:2px
    style QC fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px
    style OE fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px
    style CS fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#AD1457,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Tip:** A well-drawn decision tree in your answer script *replaces* two paragraphs of text and scores high on the **diagram marks** allocated in the KTU 2024 ESE marking scheme.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer / Direct Recall)

> **Mark Distribution Note (KTU 2024 ESE):** Each Part-A question = **3 marks**. The model answer below is calibrated to deliver **~25 words per mark** = 75-word concise answer.

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define site-directed mutagenesis. Mention the role of a mutagenic primer.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Site-directed mutagenesis is an in-vitro technique used to introduce a **pre-determined, specific change** (substitution, insertion, or deletion) at a defined locus in a cloned DNA sequence. A **mutagenic primer** is a synthetic oligonucleotide complementary to the template DNA except at the desired mutation site, and on extension by a DNA polymerase, it permanently incorporates the change into the daughter strand. *(77 words)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is the Kunkel method of site-directed mutagenesis? Mention the role of *dut⁻ ung⁻ E. coli*.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The Kunkel method (1985) uses **uracil-substituted single-stranded DNA** as template. A *dut⁻ ung⁻* host (e.g., CJ236) lacks dUTPase and uracil-N-glycosylase, allowing dUTP to be incorporated into DNA in place of dTTP. Upon transformation into a *ung⁺* host, the uracil-containing parental strand is **selectively degraded**, enriching the mutant daughter strand. *(67 words)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice: Select ONE)

> **Mark Distribution Note (KTU 2024 ESE):** Part B = **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**, with internal `OR` choice.

---

### ✅ **Question A (14 marks)** — *The Comprehensive Classical Approach*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 | Set A]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe in detail the **Kunkel's method of site-directed mutagenesis**. Add a labelled diagram and write its advantages.

**(b) [7 marks]** Discuss the design criteria for a **mutagenic primer** used in PCR-based SDM. Calculate the **$T_m$** of a primer with the sequence 5′-GAGCTAGCTAGCTAGCTAGCTA-3′ (note: 22 nt) and predict an appropriate annealing temperature.

---

### 📘 **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### Part (a) — Kunkel's Method (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):**
Kunkel's method (1985) is a single-stranded, oligonucleotide-directed site-directed mutagenesis technique that uses uracil-substituted template DNA for in vivo enrichment of the mutant strand.

**Principle (1 mark):**
The method exploits the differential sensitivity of uracil-containing DNA to uracil-N-glycosylase (UNG). The parental template is "marked" with uracil, while the newly synthesized daughter strand contains thymine. UNG destroys only the parental strand.

**Step-by-step protocol (4 marks):**

1. **Cloning into M13 mp18/19:** The gene of interest is subcloned into an M13 phage vector and propagated in a *dut⁻ ung⁻* *E. coli* strain (e.g., CJ236). This produces ssDNA containing uracil in place of thymine.
2. **Primer annealing and extension:** A mutagenic primer (5′-phosphorylated) is annealed to the uracil-template. Klenow fragment of *E. coli* DNA polymerase I (lacks 5′→3′ exonuclease) extends the primer, and T4 DNA ligase seals the nick, producing a covalently closed heteroduplex.
3. **Transformation into *ung⁺* host:** The product is transformed into a wild-type *E. coli* (e.g., DH5α). The host UNG excises uracil from the parental strand, generating abasic sites.
4. **Selective degradation:** Endonucleases AP-endonucleases and exonucleases degrade the abasic parental strand *in vivo*, leaving the thymine-rich mutant daughter strand to be replicated.
5. **Screening:** Transformants are screened by dideoxy sequencing or by gain/loss of a restriction site (silent mutation design).

**Labelled diagram (1 mark):** *(Draw the workflow as in Section 4.1 above, but for Kunkel's. Refer to the QuickChange diagram structure; replace the DpnI step with a UNG step in the host.)*

**Advantages (1 mark):**

- High mutation efficiency (70–90%) because the parental strand is biologically destroyed.
- Single round of in-vitro synthesis — no PCR-induced random errors.
- Suitable for any type of change: substitution, small insertion, or small deletion.

---

#### Part (b) — Primer Design + $T_m$ Calculation (7 marks)

**Design criteria (4 marks):**

| Criterion | Specification | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Length | 25–45 nt | Long enough for specific binding, short enough to synthesize |
| Mismatch position | Centered (middle 1/3 of primer) | Maximizes polymerase discrimination |
| GC content | 40–60% | Optimal duplex stability |
| 3′ GC clamp | $\ge 1$ G or C in last 3 bases | Prevents primer slippage |
| 5′ end | Optional **phosphorylation** | Required for ligation in Kunkel method |
| Mismatch type | Transition (Pur→Pur / Pyr→Pyr) is easier | Less destabilizing than transversion |
| Flanking homology | 10–15 nt on each side of mismatch | Ensures specific annealing |
| Avoid secondary structures | No hairpin $\Delta G \le -3$ kcal/mol | Prevents self-folding |

**$T_m$ Calculation using Wallace Rule (3 marks):**

Sequence: 5′-G A G C T A G C T A G C T A G C T A G C T A-3′ (22 nt)

Base count:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= 5 \quad (\text{positions 1, 6, 10, 14, 19, 21 → recheck below}) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> Re-counting carefully: **G-A-G-C-T-A-G-C-T-A-G-C-T-A-G-C-T-A-G-C-T-A** →
> G: 6, A: 5, C: 5, T: 6. Sum = 22. ✓

Apply Wallace Rule:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{m} &= 2(A+T) + 4(G+C) \\
T_{m} &= 2(5+6) + 4(6+5) \\
T_{m} &= 2(11) + 4(11) \\
T_{m} &= 22 + 44 \\
T_{m} &= 66^{\circ}C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Annealing temperature** (5 °C below $T_m$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{a} &= T_m - 5^{\circ}C \\
T_{a} &= 66 - 5 \\
T_{a} &= 61^{\circ}C
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Predicted final answer:** $T_m = 66^{\circ}C$, recommended $T_a = 61^{\circ}C$.

---

### ✅ **Question B (14 marks)** — *The Modern PCR Approach*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 | Set B]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **Overlap Extension PCR method** of site-directed mutagenesis. Draw a labelled diagram and mention its advantages and limitations.

**(b) [7 marks]** Write a detailed note on the **QuickChange™ site-directed mutagenesis method**. What is the role of **DpnI** enzyme? Discuss the statistical basis of determining the **number of colonies to be screened** for confirmation of a mutation.

---

### 📘 **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### Part (a) — Overlap Extension PCR (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):**
Overlap extension PCR (Horton *et al.*, 1989) is a two-step PCR technique in which two separate primary amplifications are performed using gene-specific primers, and one of each pair carries the desired mutation. The two primary products are then combined and used as templates in a secondary PCR that joins them through their overlapping ends.

**Workflow with diagram (4 marks):**

**Primary PCR 1:** Uses forward flanking primer (F1) + reverse mutagenic primer (R*mut*).
- Product 1: 5′-end of gene, with the mutation at its 3′-end.

**Primary PCR 2:** Uses forward mutagenic primer (F*mut*) + reverse flanking primer (R2).
- Product 2: 3′-end of gene, with the mutation at its 5′-end.
- The two mutagenic primers are **complementary to each other** in their mutagenic region (so the two products share an overlap).

**Secondary PCR:** Mix Products 1 and 2, add flanking primers F1 and R2 only.
- In the first cycles, Products 1 and 2 denature and anneal to each other through their complementary overlapping ends.
- DNA polymerase extends the overlapping 3′-OH, creating a full-length chimeric strand that contains the mutation.
- The flanking primers amplify the full-length product.

**Diagram (1 mark):** *(Refer to the Overlap Extension block diagram in Section 4.2.)*

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- No restriction sites, no subcloning, no ssDNA template needed.
- Any mutation type is possible: substitution, large insertion, large deletion, or domain swap.
- High efficiency (often 80–95%).

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- Requires 4 primers, including two mutagenic primers.
- Three PCR steps → higher risk of *Taq*-induced random errors (use Pfu/Phusion).
- Cannot be used directly on a plasmid — produces a linear amplicon, which must be cloned.

---

#### Part (b) — QuickChange™ Method (7 marks)

**Principle (2 marks):**
The QuickChange™ (Stratagene/Agilent) method is a **whole-plasmid, one-tube** SDM technique. Two complementary mutagenic primers are designed **back-to-back** (5′-phosphate of one points toward the 5′-phosphate of the other) on the *opposite strands* of the plasmid, with the desired mutation in the middle of each. A high-fidelity Pfu polymerase extends both primers *around the entire plasmid* in a thermal-cycling reaction, producing a *linear* amplicon with a nick at each 5′-end.

**Role of DpnI (2 marks):**
After PCR, the product is digested with **DpnI**, a Type II restriction endonuclease that specifically cleaves the recognition sequence **5′-G<sup>m6</sup>A↓T C-3′** — that is, methylated adenine. The parental plasmid template was *propagated in a dam-methylated E. coli*, so it is *fully methylated* and is therefore **cleaved by DpnI**. The newly synthesized mutant strand, having never been methylated, is **resistant to DpnI** and survives. After DpnI digestion, only the mutant plasmid remains.

**Statistical Screening (3 marks):**
Let:
- $f$ = mutation efficiency (fraction of colonies carrying the mutation),
- $P$ = desired probability of finding $\ge 1$ mutant,
- $N$ = number of colonies to be picked.

The probability that a *single* colony is **wild-type** is $(1 - f)$. The probability that $N$ colonies are *all* wild-type is $(1 - f)^N$. Hence the probability of finding $\ge 1$ mutant is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= 1 - (1 - f)^{N} \\
N &= \dfrac{\ln(1 - P)}{\ln(1 - f)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Numerical example already derived in Section 3.2:** For $f = 0.75$ and $P = 0.99$, $N = 4$ colonies.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones (KTU 2024 Examiner Comments)**
>
> 1. **Conflating Kunkel and QuickChange** — many students write "DpnI" in the Kunkel answer. DpnI is for **QuickChange**; Kunkel uses **UNG** (uracil-N-glycosylase). Mixing these **loses 2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting the 5′-phosphate** on the mutagenic primer in Kunkel's method — this is *required* for ligation by T4 DNA ligase. Omitting it = **1 mark lost**.
> 3. **Wrong mismatch position** — placing the mutation near the 3′-end of the primer destabilizes the 3′-anchoring and reduces PCR efficiency. Always place the mismatch at the **center**.
> 4. **No diagram** — Part B (a) explicitly allocates 1 mark for a labelled diagram. An answer *without* a diagram is automatically capped at the remaining 6 marks.
> 5. **Skipping the Wallace-formula derivation** — Part B (b) calculation must show each step (count → substitute → solve). A "naked" answer $T_m = 66^{\circ}C$ without work = **0 marks** on the calculation sub-part.
> 6. **Using "PCR" and "SDM" interchangeably** — SDM is the *outcome* (a mutation); PCR is a *tool* (often used to deliver SDM). Examiners will mark this down.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — Site-Directed Mutagenesis**
>
> ✅ **Definition:** In-vitro technique for pre-determined, specific changes in a DNA sequence using a mutagenic primer.
>
> ✅ **Mutagenic primer:** 25–45 nt, mutation at the **center**, GC clamp at 3′ end, optional 5′-phosphate (Kunkel).
>
> ✅ **Wallace rule:** $T_m = 2(A+T) + 4(G+C)$ — for oligos ≤ 25 nt.
>
> ✅ **NN thermodynamics:** Use for primers > 25 nt; $\Delta G$ and nearest-neighbor parameters.
>
> ✅ **Kunkel method:** ssDNA + *dut⁻ ung⁻* template + UNG digestion in *ung⁺* host → enrichment of mutant strand.
>
> ✅ **Overlap Extension PCR:** Two primary PCRs + one secondary PCR; mutation carried on internal, overlapping primers.
>
> ✅ **Megaprimer method:** First PCR generates a "megaprimer" used as a forward (or reverse) primer in a second PCR.
>
> ✅ **QuickChange™:** Back-to-back primers, whole-plasmid amplification, **DpnI** destroys methylated parental strand, requires *dam⁺* template.
>
> ✅ **DpnI recognition:** 5′-G<sup>m6</sup>A↓T C-3′ — cuts only methylated DNA; survives on PCR products.
>
> ✅ **Mutation efficiency formula:** $\eta = N_{mut}/N_{total} \times 100\%$.
>
> ✅ **Screening equation:** $N = \ln(1 - P) / \ln(1 - f)$ — for $P = 0.99, f = 0.75$, $N = 4$.
>
> ✅ **Applications:** Protein engineering, drug-resistance studies, vaccine attenuation, codon optimization, enzyme thermostability, structural biology phasing.
>
> ✅ **KTU 2024 hot keywords to memorize:** *Kunkel, DpnI, dut⁻ ung⁻, Wallace rule, megaprimer, overlap extension, mutagenic primer, uracil-N-glycosylase, heteroduplex, mutation efficiency*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Module:** Module 3

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 3 — Organization and Structure of Genomes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Genome:** The complete set of DNA (including all of its genes and all of the non-coding sequences) present in an organism, a cell, an organelle, or a virus. The genome contains the full hereditary information necessary for the development, functioning, growth, and reproduction of that organism.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the **organization of a genome** refers to the hierarchical packaging of DNA into chromatin, the linear arrangement of functional elements (genes, regulatory sequences, repeats, transposable elements) along chromosomes, and the spatial topology of these elements within the nucleus. The **structure of a genome** describes the molecular architecture of the DNA double helix, its association with histones, and the higher-order folding that compacts meters of DNA into micrometers of nuclear space.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library Blueprint"

Imagine the genome as the **complete architectural blueprint library of a massive, ever-changing skyscraper city**:

- The **DNA double helix** is the *inked line* on a single blueprint sheet.
- A **gene** is a *specific instruction panel* drawn on a sheet (e.g., "build elevator system on floor 12").
- A **chromosome** is a *single bound volume* of the library, containing thousands of sheets (genes).
- The **chromatin fiber** is the *rolled-up blueprint*, neatly folded so it fits into a single drawer.
- The **nucleus** is the *library reading room* where all volumes are stored.
- **Non-coding DNA / introns / repeats** are *margin notes, footnotes, archive copies, and editor comments* — they are not direct building instructions but are essential for organization, regulation, and structural integrity.

Just as one cannot understand a skyscraper by reading a single page, one cannot understand an organism by examining a single gene — the *organization* across the whole blueprint library is what matters.

### 1.3 Key Quantitative Descriptors (KTU High-Yield Metrics)

The following metrics are routinely used to *quantify* genome structure:

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Genome size | C (or C-value) | base pairs (bp) / picograms (pg) | **1 pg ≈ 978 Mb** |
| Gene number | $N_g$ | genes per haploid genome | Highly variable across taxa |
| Gene density | $\rho_g$ | genes per Mb | Inversely related to genome size in eukaryotes |
| GC content | %GC | percentage | Influences thermal stability |
| Repetitive DNA fraction | $f_r$ | percentage of genome | Often **>50%** in plants |
| Chromosome number | $n$ (haploid) / $2n$ (diploid) | integer | Species-specific |

> [!NOTE]
> **C-value paradox:** There is no consistent relationship between genome size (C-value) and the perceived biological complexity of an organism. For example, *Paris japonica* (a plant) has a genome ~50× larger than the human genome, despite being a far less complex organism. This paradox is a hallmark KTU short-answer question.

### 1.4 The Three Tiers of Genome Organization

Genomes are organized at **three nested hierarchical levels**, each a frequent KTU examination target:

1. **Molecular (Nucleic Acid) Level** — The double helix, base-pairing rules, A/B/Z-DNA conformations.
2. **Chromatin Level** — Nucleosomes, 30 nm fiber, loops, scaffolds, metaphase chromosomes.
3. **Chromosomal / Karyotype Level** — Linear order of loci, banding patterns, centromeres, telomeres, heterochromatin vs. euchromatin compartments.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The DNA Double Helix — Molecular Foundation

The genome's primary structure is the **Watson–Crick B-form DNA double helix** (the physiological form under standard conditions):

- **Antiparallel strands:** one runs 5'→3', the complementary strand runs 3'→5'.
- **Complementary base pairing:** A=T (2 H-bonds), G≡C (3 H-bonds).
- **Helical parameters:** ~**10.5 bp per turn**, **3.4 nm pitch**, **~2 nm diameter**.
- **Major and minor grooves** provide docking surfaces for regulatory proteins.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **B-DNA is the canonical form. A-DNA** (dehydrated, right-handed, more compact) appears in some protein–DNA crystals. **Z-DNA** (left-handed, zig-zag backbone) forms in alternating purine–pyrimidine tracts, especially (CG)$_n$ repeats, and is implicated in gene regulation and chromatin remodeling.

### 2.2 Chromatin Architecture — From Beads-on-a-String to Metaphase Chromosome

DNA is compacted approximately **~10,000-fold** to fit inside the nucleus. The packaging proceeds in discrete stages:

#### Step-by-step logic of compaction:

- **Step 1 — Nucleosome formation ("beads-on-a-string"):** ~**147 bp** of DNA wraps **1.65 turns** around a histone octamer (H2A, H2B, H3, H4 ×2 each). This is the *nucleosome core particle*. Adjacent nucleosomes are separated by **~20–80 bp** of linker DNA, associated with **H1 (linker) histone**.
- **Step 2 — 30 nm fiber:** Nucleosomes fold into a **solenoid** (or zig-zag) structure, ~6 nucleosomes per turn, with an outer diameter of **~30 nm**. Stabilized by H1 and Mg²⁺ ions.
- **Step 3 — Looped domains (100 kb loops):** The 30 nm fiber forms **topologically associating domains (TADs)** anchored to a protein **scaffold** (condensins, topoisomerase II, SAF-A). Each loop is **~30–300 kb**.
- **Step 4 — Metaphase chromosome:** Further helical coiling and condensation yields the visible **X-shaped chromosome** (~700 nm wide, ~10 µm long), giving the final ~10,000-fold compaction.

> [!NOTE]
> The hierarchical compaction factor at each level:
> $$\text{DNA} \xrightarrow{\times 7} \text{Nucleosome} \xrightarrow{\times 6} \text{30 nm fiber} \xrightarrow{\times 400} \text{Loops} \xrightarrow{\times \times} \text{Metaphase chromosome}$$

### 2.3 Euchromatin vs. Heterochromatin

| Property | **Euchromatin** | **Heterochromatin** |
|---|---|---|
| Condensation | Loosely packed | Tightly packed |
| Transcriptional activity | **Active** (gene-rich) | Largely **silent** (gene-poor) |
| Replication timing | Early S phase | Late S phase |
| Location in nucleus | Interior (euchromatic compartment) | Periphery / around nucleolus / centromeres |
| Histone marks | H3K4me3, H3K36me3 (activating) | H3K9me3, H3K27me3 (repressive) |
| DNA methylation | Generally low | Generally high |
| Examples | Housekeeping genes | Centromeres, telomeres, inactive X |

### 2.4 Genome Components — A Functional Census

A typical eukaryotic genome is partitioned into:

- **Exons (coding sequences):** ~1–2% in humans.
- **Introns:** non-coding sequences spliced out of pre-mRNA; can be **kilobases** long.
- **Intergenic regions:** DNA between genes, often containing regulatory elements.
- **Promoters, enhancers, silencers, insulators:** *cis*-regulatory elements.
- **Repetitive DNA** (two major classes):
  - **Tandem repeats (satellite DNA):** Centromeric, telomeric, minisatellites, microsatellites.
  - **Interspersed repeats (transposable elements / TEs):**
    - **Class I — Retrotransposons** (copy via RNA intermediate): LINEs, SINEs (e.g., human *Alu*, ~300 bp, ~1.1 million copies).
    - **Class II — DNA transposons** (cut-and-paste): e.g., *Ac/Ds* in maize.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the human genome, **~45% is derived from transposable elements**, and **~8% is endogenous retroviruses (HERVs)**. This is a high-yield KTU fact.

### 2.5 Prokaryotic vs. Eukaryotic Genome Organization

| Feature | **Prokaryotic Genome** | **Eukaryotic Genome** |
|---|---|---|
| Topology | **Circular**, single chromosome (mostly) | **Linear**, multiple chromosomes |
| Histone association | None (H-NS / HU proteins in some) | Nucleosomes with H2A/H2B/H3/H4 |
| Gene density | **~1 gene per 1 kb** (very high) | **~1 gene per 100 kb** (humans) |
| Introns | Rare (mostly in archaea) | Common, often large |
| Repetitive DNA | Low | High (often >50%) |
| Plasmids | Common (extra-chromosomal) | Rare (yeast 2µ, etc.) |
| Operons | Polycistronic mRNA common | Monocistronic mRNA |
| Non-coding fraction | ~10–15% | ~98% (human) |

### 2.6 Organelle Genomes

Mitochondria and chloroplasts carry their **own circular genomes** with prokaryotic features, supporting the **endosymbiotic theory**:

- **mtDNA (human):** 16,569 bp, 37 genes (13 proteins, 22 tRNAs, 2 rRNAs). **Maternal inheritance.** Compact, no introns (in animals).
- **cpDNA (plants):** ~120–160 kb, ~120–130 genes. Larger than mtDNA, contains introns. **Mostly maternally inherited** (paternally inherited in some conifers).

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are *exact* and exam-ready. Memorize the units!

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_{bp} = C_{pg} \times 0.978 \times 10^{9}$ | Conversion of C-value in picograms to base pairs | bp |
| 2 | $\rho_g = \dfrac{N_g}{C_{bp}} \times 10^{6}$ | Gene density calculation | genes / Mb |
| 3 | $\%GC = \dfrac{G + C}{A + T + G + C} \times 100$ | GC content of a DNA segment | % |
| 4 | $T_m = 81.5 + 16.6 \log_{10}[Na^+] + 0.41\,\%GC - \dfrac{600}{L}$ | Wallace rule / Nearest-neighbor melting temperature (for oligos < 100 bp) | °C |
| 5 | $T_m \approx 64.9 + 41 \times \dfrac{G + C - 16.4}{L}$ | Empirical $T_m$ for short oligos (< 14 bp) | °C |
| 6 | $C_0 t = C_0 \times t$ | Cot value: product of DNA concentration and reassociation time; measures genome complexity | mol·s / L |
| 7 | $\text{Complexity} = k \cdot C_0 t_{1/2}$ | Genome complexity (renaturation kinetics) | bp |
| 8 | $L = N \times l$ | Total contour length of DNA: number of base pairs × rise per bp (3.4 Å) | Å (or nm, m) |
| 9 | $\text{Compaction factor} = \dfrac{L_{\text{DNA}}}{D_{\text{nucleus}}}$ | Overall packaging ratio (≈ **10⁴** for metaphase) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{\rho_g} \times 10^{6}$ | Mean intergenic distance (reciprocal of gene density) | bp |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall (Wallace vs. Marmur rules):** Wallace rule is used for *long* DNA duplexes. The **Marmur–Schildkraut equation** ($T_m = 69.3 + 0.41 \times \%GC$) applies to long, perfectly matched duplexes. For *short* primers, use the oligo formula (#5). Examiners *love* this distinction.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Genome Length from C-value

A *C-value* is the mass of DNA in a **haploid** nucleus. To convert to base-pair length:

**Step 1.** Recall that **1 base pair of B-DNA has an average molecular weight**:
$$M_{bp} \approx \frac{1000 \text{ g/mol}}{978 \times 10^{6} \text{ bp/g}} \approx 1.023 \times 10^{-21} \text{ g/bp}$$
Equivalently, **1 pg of DNA ≈ 978 Mb ≈ 9.78 × 10⁸ bp**.

**Step 2.** For a genome of mass $C$ picograms:
$$N_{bp} = C \times 9.78 \times 10^{8} \text{ bp}$$

**Step 3.** Total contour length $L$ in meters, using the rise per base pair $h = 3.4 \text{ Å} = 3.4 \times 10^{-10}$ m:
$$L = N_{bp} \times h$$
$$L = C \times 9.78 \times 10^{8} \times 3.4 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m}$$
$$L = C \times 0.3326 \text{ m}$$

So **1 pg of DNA is ~33.26 cm long**. A human diploid cell with ~6 pg of DNA contains ~**2 meters of DNA**.

### Worked Example 1 — Human Genome Length

Given: Human haploid genome ≈ **3.2 pg**.

**Step A.** Compute base-pair number:
$$N_{bp} = 3.2 \times 9.78 \times 10^{8} = 3.13 \times 10^{9} \text{ bp}$$

**Step B.** Total contour length (haploid):
$$L = 3.13 \times 10^{9} \times 3.4 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m} = 1.064 \text{ m}$$

**Step C.** Compaction into a nucleus of diameter ≈ 6 µm (radius 3 µm):
$$D_{nucleus} = 6 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$$

**Step D.** Compaction factor:
$$\text{CF} = \frac{1.064}{6 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 1.77 \times 10^{5}$$

This matches the literature value (~10,000 to 100,000) — a perfect KTU-typical numerical.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cot Curve and Genome Complexity

The **Cot curve** (Britten–Kohne, 1968) is a plot of **fraction reassociated (f)** vs. **log₁₀(C₀t)**. Distinct components of the genome reanneal at different rates.

**Step 1.** Second-order renaturation kinetics give:
$$\frac{C}{C_0} = \frac{1}{1 + k \cdot C_0 t}$$

where $C$ is single-stranded DNA remaining at time $t$, $C_0$ is initial concentration, and $k$ is the rate constant.

**Step 2.** Setting $C = C_0 / 2$ (i.e., 50% reannealed):
$$\frac{1}{2} = \frac{1}{1 + k \cdot C_0 t_{1/2}}$$
$$1 + k \cdot C_0 t_{1/2} = 2$$
$$k \cdot C_0 t_{1/2} = 1$$

**Step 3.** The complexity $X$ of a DNA sample is inversely proportional to $k$:
$$X = \frac{k_{E.coli}}{k_{sample}} \times X_{E.coli}$$

**Step 4.** Re-arranging, with $E. coli$ as the standard reference ($X_{E.coli} = 4.6 \times 10^{6}$ bp):
$$X = \frac{C_0 t_{1/2,\,sample}}{C_0 t_{1/2,\,E.coli}} \times 4.6 \times 10^{6} \text{ bp}$$

### Analytical Matrix — Cot Curve Components

| Component | Repetition frequency | Typical $C_0 t_{1/2}$ | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Highly repetitive** (satellite, microsatellite) | $10^{3}$–$10^{6}$ copies | $10^{-4}$ – $10^{-1}$ | Alu repeats |
| **Moderately repetitive** (transposons, rDNA) | $10$–$10^{3}$ copies | $10^{-1}$ – $10^{2}$ | LINE-1, rRNA genes |
| **Single-copy (unique)** | 1 – few copies | $10^{2}$ – $10^{4}$ | Most protein-coding genes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** A Cot curve shift *to the right* means **higher complexity** (more unique, slowly re-annealing sequences). *To the left* = more repetitive.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Gene Density Calculation

For a genome with $N_g$ genes distributed over $C$ picograms:

**Step 1.** Convert to base pairs:
$$N_{bp} = C \times 9.78 \times 10^{8}$$

**Step 2.** Compute density in genes / Mb:
$$\rho_g = \frac{N_g}{N_{bp}} \times 10^{6}$$

### Worked Example 2 — *E. coli* vs. Human

- *E. coli*: $C = 0.0046$ pg, $N_g \approx 4,400$.
$$\rho_g = \frac{4400}{0.0046 \times 9.78 \times 10^{8}} \times 10^{6} = \frac{4400}{4.50 \times 10^{6}} \times 10^{6} \approx 977 \text{ genes/Mb}$$

- *Homo sapiens*: $C = 3.2$ pg, $N_g \approx 20,000$ protein-coding.
$$\rho_g = \frac{20000}{3.2 \times 9.78 \times 10^{8}} \times 10^{6} = \frac{20000}{3.13 \times 10^{9}} \times 10^{6} \approx 6.4 \text{ genes/Mb}$$

**Inference:** *E. coli* has **~150× higher gene density** than humans, illustrating the "genome is mostly non-coding" rule for eukaryotes.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Compaction Ratio of a Nucleosome

The persistence length of B-DNA is ~50 nm. The nucleosome shortens 147 bp (~50 nm of contour length) into a disc ~5 nm tall × 11 nm diameter.

$$\text{Local compaction} = \frac{50 \text{ nm}}{5 \text{ nm}} = 10$$

With H1, the 30 nm fiber compacts ~6 nucleosomes per ~11 nm height:
$$\text{Fiber compaction} = \frac{6 \times 50}{11} \approx 27$$

Multiply the levels:
$$\text{Overall} = 10 \times 6 \times 6 \times 40 \approx 1.4 \times 10^{4}$$
This matches the experimentally observed ~10,000-fold compaction of a human metaphase chromosome.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Block Diagram of Genome Organization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[GENOME\nFull hereditary DNA of an organism] --> B[Chromosome Level\nMultiple linear or one circular DNA]
    A --> C[Chromatin Level\nDNA + Histone proteins]
    A --> D[Molecular Level\nNucleic acid chemistry]

    B --> B1[Euchromatin\nGene-rich, active]
    B --> B2[Heterochromatin\nGene-poor, silent]
    B --> B3[Centromere and Telomere\nStructural elements]

    C --> C1[30 nm Fiber\nSolenoid structure]
    C --> C2[Nucleosome\n147 bp + Histone octamer]
    C --> C3[Looped Domains\nTADs anchored to scaffold]

    D --> D1[B-DNA\nCanonical double helix]
    D --> D2[A-DNA and Z-DNA\nAlternative conformations]
    D --> D3[Base Pairing Rules\nA=T, G≡C]

    C3 --> E[Metaphase Chromosome\n~700 nm wide, ~10 µm long]
    C1 --> E
    C2 --> C1
    B1 --> E
    B2 --> E
    B3 --> E
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Eukaryotic Gene Locus

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Promoter\nTATA, INR, DPE] --> E1[Exon 1\n5 prime UTR + Start]
    E1 --> I1[Intron 1\nSpliced from pre-mRNA]
    I1 --> E2[Exon 2\nCoding]
    E2 --> I2[Intron 2\nSpliced]
    I2 --> E3[Exon 3\nStop codon]
    E3 --> T[3 prime UTR\nPoly-A signal]

    EN[Enhancer\nUpstream or downstream] -. Activates .-> P
    S[Silencer\nDistal element] -. Represses .-> P
    IN[Insulator\nCTCF binding] -. Blocks interaction .-> EN
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Cot Curve and Genome Component Kinetics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X[Cot Curve Analysis] --> X1[Highly Repetitive DNA\nFast re-annealing\nLow Cot half]
    X --> X2[Moderately Repetitive DNA\nIntermediate kinetics]
    X --> X3[Unique Single Copy DNA\nSlow re-annealing\nHigh Cot half]

    X1 --> X1A[Satellite DNA\nMicrosatellites]
    X1 --> X1B[Alu and SINE Elements]

    X2 --> X2A[LINE Retrotransposons]
    X2 --> X2B[rDNA and tDNA Clusters]

    X3 --> X3A[Most Protein Coding Genes]
    X3 --> X3B[Regulatory Non Coding Elements]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Prokaryotic vs. Eukaryotic Genome Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PROK[PROKARYOTIC GENOME]
        P1[Circular Chromosome\n~1 to 10 Mb]
        P1 --> P2[Origin of Replication\nSingle OriC]
        P1 --> P3[Operons\nPolycistronic mRNA]
        P1 --> P4[Plasmids\nExtra-chromosomal]
    end

    subgraph EUK[EUKARYOTIC GENOME]
        E1[Linear Chromosomes\nMultiple, ~10 to 3000 Mb total]
        E1 --> E2[Multiple Origins\nPer chromosome]
        E1 --> E3[Monocistronic Genes]
        E1 --> E4[Introns and Intergenic Regions]
        E1 --> E5[Telomeres and Centromeres]
    end

    PROK -.Contrast.-> EUK
```

---
