# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the C-value paradox. Why is it considered a paradox? Mention two biological examples that illustrate it.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The **C-value paradox** refers to the lack of correlation between an organism's *genome size (C-value)* and its *morphological or biological complexity*. It is a paradox because, intuitively, a "more complex" organism would be expected to have more DNA — but many simple organisms (e.g., amoeba, lungfish) have far more DNA than humans, while some complex organisms (e.g., pufferfish *Fugu*, *Arabidopsis*) have very small genomes. **Examples:** (i) *Paris japonica* (~150 Gb) vs. *Homo sapiens* (~3.2 Gb) — the plant has a ~50× larger genome despite being far less complex. (ii) *Fugu rubripes* (pufferfish, ~0.4 Gb) develops a complex nervous system with a genome 8× smaller than human.
> *(1 mark — definition; 1 mark — why paradoxical; 1 mark — two correct examples.)*

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Distinguish between euchromatin and heterochromatin. Mention any three distinguishing features.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Euchromatin** is loosely packed, transcriptionally *active*, gene-rich, replicates in *early* S phase, and is enriched in activating histone marks (e.g., H3K4me3). **Heterochromatin** is tightly packed, largely *transcriptionally silent*, gene-poor, replicates in *late* S phase, and is enriched in repressive marks (e.g., H3K9me3) and high DNA methylation. Heterochromatin is typically located at the *nuclear periphery*, around centromeres, and at telomeres. *(1 mark each for any three valid contrasting features.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat labeled diagram, describe the hierarchical organization of DNA in a eukaryotic chromosome. Mention the role of histone H1 and the significance of the ~10,000-fold compaction factor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the Britten–Kohne Cot curve. How does it distinguish highly repetitive, moderately repetitive, and unique-sequence DNA fractions? Derive the relationship between $C_0 t_{1/2}$ and genome complexity. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) — Hierarchical DNA packaging (7 marks):**
- **Level 1 — Nucleosome ("beads-on-a-string"):** 147 bp wrapped 1.65 times around the H2A/H2B/H3/H4 octamer, with ~20–80 bp linker DNA. *(1 mark)*
- **Level 2 — 30 nm fiber (solenoid / zig-zag):** Nucleosomes fold into a higher-order fiber, ~6 nucleosomes per turn, stabilized by **histone H1** (linker histone). H1 binds the entry/exit DNA and facilitates the higher-order folding. *(1 mark)*
- **Level 3 — Looped domains (TADs):** The 30 nm fiber forms **30–300 kb loops** anchored to a non-histone protein scaffold (condensins, topoisomerase II). *(1 mark)*
- **Level 4 — Condensed metaphase chromosome:** Further helical coiling produces the visible X-shaped chromosome (~700 nm wide). *(1 mark)*
- **Compaction factor ~10,000:** Without this, ~2 m of human DNA could not fit in a 6 µm nucleus. The factor is the *product* of the small compactions at each level (~7 × 6 × 6 × 40 ≈ 10,000). *(2 marks)*
- **Neat labeled diagram** (refer to mermaid block 4.1 / a textbook figure) — 1 mark.

**(b) — Cot curve derivation (7 marks):**
- **Concept:** The Cot curve plots *fraction reassociated* (y-axis) vs. $\log_{10}(C_0 t)$ (x-axis). $C_0$ is the initial concentration of single-stranded DNA; $t$ is the reassociation time. *(1 mark)*
- **Kinetic equation:** $C/C_0 = 1/(1 + k C_0 t)$ — second-order reannealing. *(1 mark)*
- **Three kinetic components:** (i) *Highly repetitive* — fast reannealing, low $C_0 t_{1/2}$ ($10^{-4}$–$10^{-1}$); (ii) *Moderately repetitive* — intermediate ($10^{-1}$–$10^{2}$); (iii) *Unique* — slow ($10^{2}$–$10^{4}$). *(2 marks)*
- **Derivation of complexity:** Setting $C = C_0/2$ gives $C_0 t_{1/2} = 1/k$. Since $k$ is inversely proportional to genome complexity $X$, $X \propto C_0 t_{1/2}$. Using *E. coli* as reference ($X = 4.6 \times 10^{6}$ bp): $X_{sample} = (C_0 t_{1/2,\,sample} / C_0 t_{1/2,\,E.coli}) \times 4.6 \times 10^{6}$ bp. *(3 marks)*

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Compare the organization of prokaryotic and eukaryotic genomes across *at least six* structural and functional parameters. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A researcher isolates 2.4 pg of genomic DNA from a *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* haploid cell. The genome is known to contain ~6,300 protein-coding genes.
   (i) Calculate the total number of base pairs in the genome.
   (ii) Calculate the gene density in genes/Mb.
   (iii) Calculate the total contour length of the DNA in micrometers.
   (iv) If a typical yeast nucleus has a diameter of 3 µm, estimate the compaction factor. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) — Prokaryotic vs. Eukaryotic (7 marks, 1 mark each for six points + 1 for neat tabular/intro):**

| # | Parameter | Prokaryote | Eukaryote |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Topology | Circular, single chromosome | Linear, multiple chromosomes |
| 2 | Histones | Absent (HU/H-NS in some) | Core histones H2A/H2B/H3/H4 |
| 3 | Gene density | ~1 gene/kb | ~1 gene/100 kb (humans) |
| 4 | Introns | Rare | Common, sometimes very long |
| 5 | Repetitive DNA | Minimal | Often >50% of genome |
| 6 | mRNA | Polycistronic | Monocistronic |
| 7 | Plasmids/Organelles | Common plasmids | mtDNA, cpDNA in plants/animals |

**(b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i)** Total base pairs (use 1 pg = 9.78 × 10⁸ bp):
$$N_{bp} = 2.4 \times 9.78 \times 10^{8} = 2.347 \times 10^{9} \text{ bp}$$ *(2 marks)*

**(ii)** Gene density:
$$\rho_g = \frac{6300}{2.347 \times 10^{9}} \times 10^{6} = \frac{6300}{2347} \approx 2.68 \text{ genes/Mb}$$ *(2 marks)*

**(iii)** Contour length (using $h = 3.4 \text{ Å} = 3.4 \times 10^{-4}$ µm per bp):
$$L = 2.347 \times 10^{9} \times 3.4 \times 10^{-4} \text{ µm} = 7.98 \times 10^{5} \text{ µm} \approx 798 \text{ mm} \approx 0.8 \text{ m}$$ *(1.5 marks)*

**(iv)** Compaction factor (nuclear diameter 3 µm):
$$\text{CF} = \frac{7.98 \times 10^{5} \text{ µm}}{3 \text{ µm}} \approx 2.66 \times 10^{5}$$ *(1.5 marks)*

This is a slightly higher estimate than the textbook value (~10⁴–10⁵) because yeast chromatin is more loosely organized than in higher eukaryotes; the calculation is nevertheless correct.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Organization and Structure of Genomes):**
> 1. **Unit errors in C-value conversion:** *Always* state "1 pg = 9.78 × 10⁸ bp" before the calculation; partial marks are forfeited otherwise.
> 2. **Confusing 1C vs. 2C:** 1C is the *haploid* genome (e.g., 3.2 pg in humans), 2C is the *diploid* unreplicated genome (6.4 pg). Examiners explicitly test this.
> 3. **Cot curve labels:** Plot is *fraction reassociated* (not *DNA concentration*) on the y-axis. Many students lose 1 mark on this.
> 4. **Histone octamer composition:** Students often write H2A/H2B/H3/H4 *without* "×2 each" — losing 0.5 mark. Always say "two copies of each."
> 5. **Distinguishing "C-value paradox" from "G-value paradox":** G-value is the *number of genes* paradox (gene count doesn't correlate with complexity). Many students interchange them — examiners deduct 1 mark.
> 6. **Neglecting 5'/3' directionality:** In any DNA-related question, mark the strands' polarity to earn full marks for the diagram.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Genome** = the entire DNA content of a cell/organism, including coding and non-coding regions.
- **C-value** is the haploid DNA mass; **1 pg ≈ 978 Mb ≈ 9.78 × 10⁸ bp**.
- **C-value paradox:** No correlation between genome size and organismal complexity. **G-value paradox:** No correlation between gene number and complexity either.
- DNA packaging hierarchy: **B-DNA → nucleosome (147 bp) → 30 nm fiber → looped domains (TADs) → metaphase chromosome**. Overall compaction ~**10,000-fold**.
- Histone octamer = **(H2A, H2B, H3, H4)₂**; **H1** is the *linker* histone stabilizing 30 nm fiber.
- **Euchromatin** = active, gene-rich, H3K4me3, early-replicating, internal. **Heterochromatin** = silent, gene-poor, H3K9me3, late-replicating, peripheral/centromeric.
- **Repetitive DNA** = *tandem repeats* (satellites, minisatellites, microsatellites) + *interspersed repeats* (SINEs, LINEs, DNA transposons).
- In humans, **~45%** of the genome is transposable-element-derived; **Alu** (SINE) is the most abundant.
- **Cot curve** measures genome complexity via reassociation kinetics; three kinetic components: *highly repetitive*, *moderately repetitive*, *unique*.
- **Prokaryotic** genomes: circular, ~1 gene/kb, polycistronic, no introns, no histones. **Eukaryotic**: linear, ~1 gene/100 kb (humans), monocistronic, introns, nucleosomes.
- **mtDNA** (human): 16,569 bp, 37 genes, circular, maternal. **cpDNA** (plant): 120–160 kb, ~120 genes.
- **Wallace rule** for short oligo $T_m$: $T_m = 64.9 + 41 \cdot (G + C - 16.4)/L$. **Marmur rule** for long DNA: $T_m = 69.3 + 0.41 \cdot \%GC$.
- **Gene density** = $N_g / C_{bp} \times 10^{6}$ (genes/Mb).
- **Compaction factor** = contour length of DNA / diameter of compartment containing it.
- **Karyotype** includes chromosome number, banding pattern (G-bands for euchromatin-rich / heterochromatin-rich regions), centromere position (metacentric, submetacentric, acrocentric, telocentric), and sex chromosomes.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Genome Sequencing Methods
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT524 - GENETIC ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Genome Sequencing Methods

# 🧬 Genome Sequencing Methods

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Genome Sequencing Methods* encompass the integrated set of biochemical, enzymatic, and computational protocols used to determine the precise linear order of nucleotides (Adenine, Thymine, Cytosine, Guanine) within an organism's complete DNA complement, including both nuclear and organellar (mitochondrial/chloroplastic) genomes.

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Genome Sequencing** is the laboratory process of ascertaining the complete DNA sequence of an organism's genome at a single-base resolution. A "genome" refers to all the genetic information encoded in the DNA of an organism — ranging from ~**0.6 megabases (Mb)** in *Mycoplasma genitalium* to ~**3,200 Mb** in the human genome. The methods employed span four major technological generations:

1. **First Generation (1970s–2000s):** Sanger dideoxy sequencing; Maxam–Gilbert chemical sequencing.
2. **Second Generation / NGS (2005–present):** Illumina (sequencing by synthesis), Ion Torrent, Roche 454.
3. **Third Generation (2010–present):** PacBio SMRT, Oxford Nanopore.
4. **Fourth Generation / *In Situ* (Present):** 10x Genomics, Hi-C chromatin conformation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Metric — Coverage Depth:**
> The *coverage* (or depth) of sequencing is defined as the average number of times each base is read by independent sequencing reads. It is a dimensionless quantity: **Coverage = (N × L) / G**, where *N* is the number of reads, *L* is the read length, and *G* is the haploid genome length. Standard human WGS requires **30× coverage** for clinical-grade accuracy.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have **50 copies of a 1,000-page book**, but the pages of each copy have been shredded into millions of tiny paper strips of random length, and the strips from all 50 copies have been mixed in a giant bin.

- **Your task:** Reassemble the original book page-by-page, letter-by-letter.
- **Sequencing** is the act of reading individual strips (these are "reads").
- **Assembly** is the act of using overlapping strips to reconstruct the original page order.
- **Coverage** is the average number of times a particular letter on a particular page appears across all the strips you read.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Intuition (Book Analogy Expanded):**
> In **Sanger sequencing**, you read one strip at a time, but only about **500–1,000 letters** long, very carefully. In **Illumina (NGS)**, you read **millions of short strips (150–300 bases)** in parallel — like having millions of readers simultaneously. In **Nanopore**, you thread a single *very long* strip through a microscopic pore and detect changes in electrical current to "read" the letters.

---

## 1.3 Classification Snapshot

| Generation | Method | Read Length | Throughput | Cost per Mb (USD, 2024 est.) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1st | Sanger dideoxy | 500–1,000 bp | Low | ~$0.50 |
| 1st | Maxam–Gilbert | 100–200 bp | Very Low | Obsolete |
| 2nd | Illumina (SBS) | 50–600 bp | Very High | **~$0.001** |
| 2nd | Ion Torrent | 200–600 bp | High | ~$0.01 |
| 2nd | Roche 454 | 400–800 bp | High (defunct) | — |
| 3rd | PacBio SMRT | 10–25 kb | High | ~$0.10 |
| 3rd | Oxford Nanopore | 1 kb – 2 Mb | Very High | ~$0.10 |

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Sanger Dideoxy Chain-Termination Method

**Principle:** Frederick Sanger (1977) exploited the incorporation of **2′,3′-dideoxynucleoside triphosphates (ddNTPs)** that, upon incorporation, lack a 3′-OH group, thereby preventing formation of the next phosphodiester bond → **chain termination**.

#### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Template preparation:** Single-stranded DNA template, a primer, DNA polymerase, all four dNTPs, and a *trace* of radiolabeled (or fluorescently labeled) ddNTPs.
2. **Four parallel reactions (originally):** One for each ddNTP (ddATP, ddTTP, ddCGT, ddGTP). Modern automated Sanger uses **dye-terminator chemistry** → all four ddNTPs in a *single* tube, each tagged with a unique fluorophore.
3. **Random incorporation:** DNA polymerase randomly incorporates either a normal dNTP (chain continues) or a ddNTP (chain terminates at that base).
4. **Statistical distribution:** This generates a population of fragments of *every possible length*, each ending with a labeled ddNTP.
5. **Separation:** Capillary electrophoresis (CE) separates fragments by size at single-base resolution.
6. **Detection:** Laser excitation identifies the terminal fluorophore, and the order of detection → **the DNA sequence**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the population of all lengths?** Because ddNTP:dNTP ratio is low (typically 1:100 to 1:1000), the polymerase incorporates a dNTP most of the time, but occasionally incorporates a ddNTP at *every* possible position — creating a "ladder" of fragments.

---

### 2.2 The Maxam–Gilbert Chemical Sequencing Method

**Principle (Allan Maxam & Walter Gilbert, 1977):** Base-specific *chemical cleavage* of a radiolabeled DNA fragment.

| Reagent | Base Cleaved | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Dimethyl sulfate (DMS)** | **G** | Methylation of N7-G, then piperidine cleaves depurinated site |
| **Hydrazine (N₂H₄)** | **C + T** | Hydrazinolysis of pyrimidine rings; presence of NaCl suppresses T → C-specific |
| **Formic acid (or DMS at acidic pH)** | **A + G** (purines) | Ring opening and depurination |
| **Piperidine** | All | β-elimination at depurinated/depyrimidinated sites |

> [!NOTE]
> **Current status:** Maxam–Gilbert sequencing is largely **obsolete** for routine sequencing but remains historically important and is occasionally used for analyzing **DNA-protein interactions** (e.g., methylation footprints).

---

### 2.3 Shotgun Sequencing vs. Hierarchical (Map-Based) Sequencing

These are two **strategies** for sequencing a large genome, not specific methods:

#### A. Shotgun Sequencing
- The whole genome is randomly sheared into fragments (~2–10 kb).
- Each fragment is subcloned and sequenced from both ends (**paired-end** or **mate-pair** sequencing).
- Computational assembly uses overlap-layout-consensus (OLC) or de Bruijn graph algorithms.
- **Used by:** Celera Genomics (human genome, 2001).

#### B. Hierarchical (BAC-by-BAC) Sequencing
- A physical map is built first using **Bacterial Artificial Chromosomes (BACs)**.
- Each BAC (~100–200 kb insert) is individually sequenced by shotgun.
- **Used by:** Human Genome Project (public consortium, 2003).

---

### 2.4 Next-Generation Sequencing (NGS) — Illumina Bridge Amplification

**Sequencing by Synthesis (SBS) workflow:**

1. **Library prep:** Genomic DNA sheared → adapters ligated to both ends.
2. **Surface attachment:** dsDNA library denatured; ssDNA fragments bind to oligos on the flow cell.
3. **Bridge amplification (cluster generation):** Each bound ssDNA bends over and hybridizes to a neighboring oligo → bridge → DNA polymerase extends → double-bridged → denatured → two copies. Repeated 30–35 cycles → **a cluster of ~1,000 identical copies** (a "polony").
4. **Sequencing:** Modified DNA polymerase incorporates fluorescently labeled reversible-terminator nucleotides. After each base incorporation, all 4 channels are imaged simultaneously.
5. **Cleavage of terminator:** The fluorophore and the 3′-blocking group are chemically removed, allowing the next cycle.

---

### 2.5 Third-Generation Sequencing

#### A. Pacific Biosciences (PacBio SMRT — Single Molecule Real-Time)
- **Zero-Mode Waveguides (ZMWs):** Tiny wells (~70 nm diameter, ~20 zeptoliters) where single DNA polymerase molecules are immobilized at the bottom.
- Phospholinked nucleotides (dNTP incorporation releases a fluorescent PPi byproduct) are detected in **real time**.
- Read lengths: **10–25 kb**; consensus accuracy **>99.9%** with circular consensus (HiFi reads).

#### B. Oxford Nanopore Technology
- A motor protein feeds a single DNA strand through a **biological nanopore** embedded in a membrane.
- As each base passes through, it disrupts an applied ionic current in a *base-specific* manner.
- The change in current is "base-called" using neural network algorithms (e.g., **MinKNOW**, Guppy).
- **Read lengths:** up to **2 Mb** (ultra-long reads).

---

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Definition | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Coverage Depth (C)** | $C = \dfrac{N \times L}{G}$ | dimensionless (×) | N = # reads; L = read length; G = genome size |
| **Phred Quality Score (Q)** | $Q = -10 \log_{10}(P)$ | dimensionless | P = base-calling error probability; Q20 = 1% error, Q30 = 0.1% |
| **Lander–Waterman Equation** | $C = N \cdot L / G$ (same form) | — | Used for physical coverage |
| **Probability of k-mer coverage** | $P(\text{not covered}) = e^{-C}$ | dimensionless | Poisson approximation for k-mers |
| **Haploid Genome Size (H. sapiens)** | $\approx 3.2 \times 10^{9}$ bp | bases | — |
| **E. coli genome size** | $\approx 4.6 \times 10^{6}$ bp | bases | — |
| **Avogadro's constant (Nₐ)** | $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ | mol⁻¹ | Used in molarity calculations for library prep |
| **DNA molarity from mass** | $\text{pmol} = \dfrac{\mu g \times 10^{6}}{660 \times N_{bp}}$ | pmol | 660 g/mol = avg MW per bp (for dsDNA) |
| **Molar Extinction Coefficient (ssDNA)** | $\varepsilon = 8,900 \times (nA + nT) + 11,800 \times (nC + nG)$ | L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹ | For UV absorbance quantification |
| **Sanger chain-terminator ratio** | ddNTP : dNTP ≈ 1 : 100 to 1 : 1000 | ratio | Controls fragment length distribution |
| **Illumina cluster density** | $\sim 200{,}000 \text{–} 250{,}000 / \text{mm}^2$ | clusters/mm² | Optimal for NovaSeq |
| **Nanopore current change per base** | $\sim 0.1$ pA shifts | picoamperes | Base-specific "squiggle" |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are essential in:
> - Designing NGS library concentrations.
> - Calculating required sequencing output (number of flow cells).
> - Estimating coverage for variant calling pipelines.
> - Quality control of sequencing data (FASTQ Phred scores).

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Phred Quality Score

The **Phred quality score** is the cornerstone of NGS data analysis. Let us derive it formally.

**Step 1 — Start with error probability.**
By definition, a sequencing base-call has an associated error probability $P_e$. This is the probability that the called base is *incorrect* (e.g., an 'A' is reported when the true base is 'G').

**Step 2 — Convert to a logarithmic scale.**
We define a quality score $Q$ that is *proportional* to the negative logarithm of the error probability (because low error → high quality):

$$
Q \;\propto\; -\log_{10}(P_e)
$$

**Step 3 — Choose a multiplicative constant of 10.**
The constant **10** is selected so that a 10-fold change in $P_e$ corresponds to a 10-unit change in $Q$ — analogous to a decibel scale:

$$
Q = -10 \cdot \log_{10}(P_e)
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $P_e$ in terms of $Q$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{Q}{-10} &= \log_{10}(P_e) \\
P_e &= 10^{-Q/10}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the implied accuracy (1 − Pₑ):**

$$
\text{Accuracy} = 1 - P_e = 1 - 10^{-Q/10}
$$

**Step 6 — Tabulate standard thresholds:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{20} &\Rightarrow P_e = 10^{-20/10} = 10^{-2} = 0.01 \quad (\text{99\% accuracy}) \\
Q_{30} &\Rightarrow P_e = 10^{-30/10} = 10^{-3} = 0.001 \quad (\text{99.9\% accuracy}) \\
Q_{40} &\Rightarrow P_e = 10^{-40/10} = 10^{-4} = 0.0001 \quad (\text{99.99\% accuracy})
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical significance:** A "Q30" base call is the industry gold standard — it is **1 error in 1,000 bases**. Illumina typically reports **>80% of bases ≥ Q30** on a PhiX control lane.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Coverage Depth (Lander–Waterman Model)

**Step 1 — Set up the problem.**
We sequence a haploid genome of size $G$ bases. The sequencing process produces $N$ reads, each of uniform length $L$ bases, randomly distributed across the genome.

**Step 2 — Total bases sequenced.**

$$
\text{Total sequenced bases} = N \times L
$$

**Step 3 — Definition of coverage.**
The *average* number of times any single base is sequenced (averaged over all positions in the genome) is defined as:

$$
C = \frac{\text{Total bases sequenced}}{\text{Genome size}} = \frac{N \times L}{G}
$$

**Step 4 — Determine the number of reads needed for a target coverage.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= \frac{N \cdot L}{G} \\
\Rightarrow N \cdot L &= C \cdot G \\
\Rightarrow N &= \frac{C \cdot G}{L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Worked numerical example for the human genome.**
Suppose we use Illumina NovaSeq with $L = 150$ bp (paired-end, so effectively 2 × 150 = 300 bp per fragment, but per read = 150). We target $C = 30\times$ coverage of $G = 3 \times 10^9$ bp:

$$
N = \frac{30 \times (3 \times 10^{9})}{150} = \frac{9 \times 10^{10}}{150} = 6 \times 10^{8} \text{ reads}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the implied total output:**

$$
\text{Output} = N \times L = (6 \times 10^{8}) \times 150 = 9 \times 10^{10} \text{ bp} = 90 \text{ Gb}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The Lander–Waterman model assumes **uniform random sampling** — in practice, **GC bias**, **mappability**, and **PCR duplication** reduce the *effective* coverage below the nominal value.

---

### 3.3 Probability That a Given Base Is *Not* Sequenced (Lander–Waterman Extension)

**Step 1 — Poisson approximation.**
For random sequencing of a genome, the number of reads covering a particular base is approximately **Poisson-distributed** with mean $\lambda = C$.

**Step 2 — Probability of zero coverage at a base.**

$$
P(\text{0 reads at base } i) = \frac{e^{-\lambda} \cdot \lambda^{0}}{0!} = e^{-\lambda} = e^{-C}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical examples:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C = 10 \times \quad &\Rightarrow \quad P(\text{no read}) = e^{-10} \approx 4.54 \times 10^{-5} \\
C = 30 \times \quad &\Rightarrow \quad P(\text{no read}) = e^{-30} \approx 9.36 \times 10^{-14}
\end{aligned}
$$

This justifies why **30× coverage** is the clinical WGS standard — the probability of an uncovered base is astronomically small.

---

### 3.4 Sanger Chain-Terminator Concentration — Relationship to Read Length

**Step 1 — Define termination probability per base.**
Let $p$ = probability that a ddNTP (rather than dNTP) is incorporated at any given position. This is approximately:

$$
p \approx \frac{[\text{ddNTP}]}{[\text{ddNTP}] + [\text{dNTP}]}
$$

**Step 2 — Probability of a fragment of length ≥ L bases.**

$$
P(\text{length} \geq L) = (1 - p)^{L}
$$

(We need $L$ consecutive dNTP incorporations before a ddNTP event.)

**Step 3 — Set $L = 1$ kb = 1000 bases; p = 0.01 (1:100 ratio):**

$$
P(\text{length} \geq 1000) = (0.99)^{1000} \approx e^{-10} \approx 4.54 \times 10^{-5}
$$

So at a 1:100 ddNTP:dNTP ratio, fragments of 1 kb or longer are vanishingly rare. This is why **typical Sanger reads** are **500–1000 bases** — they are limited by the rate of ddNTP incorporation.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Major Methods

| Feature | Sanger | Illumina | PacBio HiFi | Oxford Nanopore |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Read length** | 500–1,000 bp | 50–600 bp | 10–25 kb (HiFi) | 1 kb – 2 Mb |
| **Accuracy (per read)** | **>99.999%** | >99.9% (Q30) | >99.9% (HiFi) | ~95–98% (single) |
| **Throughput/run** | ~0.5 Mb/day | 1.2–6 Tb (NovaSeq X) | 500 Gb (Revio) | 50–300 Gb (PromethION) |
| **PCR required?** | Yes (cycle sequencing) | Yes (bridge amp) | No (single molecule) | No (single molecule) |
| **Cost/Mb (USD, 2024)** | ~$0.50 | **~$0.001** | ~$0.10 | ~$0.10 |
| **Error type** | Substitution | Substitution | Indel (random) | Indel (homopolymer) |
| **GC bias** | Low | Moderate | Low | Low |
| **Detects methylation?** | No (bisulfite needed) | No (bisulfite) | **Yes (native)** | **Yes (native)** |

```mermaid
    E --> F[Repeat 30 to 35 cycles for cluster of 1000 copies]

```

```mermaid
    E --> F[Base caller neural network]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the principle of Sanger's dideoxy chain-termination method of DNA sequencing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Sanger's method (1977) uses **2′,3′-dideoxynucleoside triphosphates (ddNTPs)** in addition to the normal dNTPs required for DNA synthesis. ddNTPs lack a hydroxyl group at the 3′ carbon, so once a ddNTP is incorporated by DNA polymerase, **no further phosphodiester bond can form** — terminating the elongating strand. The reaction contains a low ratio of ddNTP to dNTP (≈1:100), so termination occurs randomly at every possible position, generating a population of fragments of all possible lengths. Each ddNTP is **fluorescently labeled** (in modern dye-terminator chemistry, all four ddNTPs are in a single reaction, each with a distinct fluorophore). The fragments are separated by **capillary electrophoresis** in order of size. As each fragment passes a laser detector, the fluorophore's identity is recorded; the order of detected colors gives the DNA sequence. Each fragment's length corresponds to a specific base position, and the fluorescence to a specific base identity — producing an electropherogram that is converted to a textual DNA sequence.

**Mark distribution:** (1) ddNTP principle — 1 mark; (2) Random termination & fragment population — 1 mark; (3) Detection (CE + fluorescence) → sequence — 1 mark.

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define "coverage depth" in genome sequencing. Calculate the number of reads required to achieve 30× coverage of a 4.6 Mb bacterial genome using 150 bp paired-end Illumina reads.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Definition (1 mark):** *Coverage depth* (or coverage) is the average number of times each base in the genome is represented in the sequencing reads. It is a dimensionless quantity, often reported as "×" (e.g., 30×).

**Formula (1 mark):**
$$
C = \frac{N \times L}{G} \quad \Rightarrow \quad N = \frac{C \times G}{L}
$$

**Calculation (1 mark):**
$$
N = \frac{30 \times (4.6 \times 10^{6})}{150} = \frac{1.38 \times 10^{8}}{150} = 9.2 \times 10^{5} \text{ reads}
$$

**∴ 920,000 paired-end reads** are required to achieve 30× coverage of the *E. coli* genome.

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Model Paper]`

**(a) Describe in detail the workflow of Illumina sequencing by synthesis (SBS) technology. List its key advantages and limitations.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Workflow steps (5 marks):**

1. **Library Preparation:** Genomic DNA is fragmented (e.g., by acoustic shearing, Covaris) into ~200–500 bp pieces. **Sequencing adapters** (containing flow-cell binding sequences, index barcodes, and priming sites) are ligated to both ends. The product is a "library" of fragments flanked by known adapter sequences.

2. **Cluster Generation (Bridge Amplification):** The library is denatured to single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) and loaded onto the Illumina flow cell — a glass slide coated with covalently attached oligos complementary to the library adapters. Each ssDNA fragment binds to a complementary oligo on the flow cell. The 3′ end of the bound ssDNA is then extended by DNA polymerase to create a double-stranded bridge; the other end of the bridge anneals to a nearby oligo. After extension, the bridge is denatured, leaving two ssDNA copies anchored at one end each. This bridge-amplification cycle is repeated **30–35 times**, generating a tight **cluster** of ~1,000–2,000 clonal copies at each original binding location.

3. **Sequencing Priming:** One strand of each cluster is cleaved and washed away, leaving a single template strand. A sequencing primer is hybridized to the adapter.

4. **Sequencing by Synthesis (Cycle-by-Cycle):** A mixture of **four reversible terminator nucleotides** (each with a unique fluorophore and a 3′-OH blocking group) is added along with DNA polymerase. Only **one nucleotide is incorporated per cycle** (because of the 3′ block). After incorporation, the flow cell is **imaged in 4 channels** (one per base). The fluorophore and the 3′ block are then chemically cleaved, regenerating a 3′-OH for the next cycle. This is repeated for **N cycles** (N = read length, e.g., 150, 250, 300, or 600 cycles).

5. **Paired-End Sequencing:** After the first read is completed, the template is re-synthesized and the opposite end is sequenced similarly. The two reads are separated by the **insert size** (typically 300–800 bp), enabling accurate alignment.

6. **Base Calling and Output:** Image analysis software identifies clusters and emits fluorescence intensity for each cycle. Base callers (e.g., **Bustard**, **Carla**) convert intensities to base calls and assign a **Phred quality score** per base. Output: **FASTQ files** with sequences + quality.

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- **Massive parallelism** → terabases of data per run.
- **Highest per-base accuracy** among NGS (>99.9% Q30).
- **Lowest cost per base** (~$0.001/Mb).
- **Mature bioinformatics ecosystem** (BWA, GATK, etc.).

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- **Short reads** (≤600 bp) → difficult for *de novo* assembly, repeat regions.
- **PCR amplification bias** → uneven coverage; GC bias.
- **Long run time** (1–3 days per run).
- **Cannot directly detect epigenetic modifications** (e.g., 5-mC) without bisulfite conversion.

---

**(b) Explain the principle of Oxford Nanopore sequencing. How is the base identity determined? Discuss its major applications.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle (3 marks):**
Oxford Nanopore Technology (ONT) sequences **single DNA molecules** in real time by measuring changes in **ionic current** as a DNA strand is translocated through a **biological nanopore**. The nanopore is a **transmembrane protein** (e.g., *CsgG* from *E. coli*, or *MspA* from *Mycobacterium smegmatis*) embedded in a **polymer membrane** with hundreds of individual channels per device (e.g., the MinION flow cell has 2,048 channels). A voltage is applied across the membrane, driving an ionic current through the pore. As a single-stranded DNA molecule is threaded through the pore (aided by a **motor protein** that ratchets the strand at ~400 bases/second), each k-mer of ~4–6 bases occupying the pore's "reading head" constricts the flow of ions in a **base-specific manner**. The current disruption is a unique "squiggle" pattern corresponding to the k-mer sequence.

**Base-calling mechanism (2 marks):**
The current trace (raw signal) is a continuous time-series measured by an **ASIC chip** beneath the membrane. **Base-calling algorithms** — historically **HMM-based** (e.g., Metrichor), now dominated by **deep neural networks** (e.g., **Guppy**, **Bonito**, **Dorado**) — translate these current patterns into nucleotide sequences. Modern base callers use **multi-layer CNN/transformer architectures** trained on known reference sequences to predict base identity and quality scores. Each base is reported with a Phred-like quality score. The device software (**MinKNOW**) performs live base-calling and data acquisition.

**Applications (2 marks):**
1. **Ultra-long read assembly:** Read lengths of 1–2 Mb enable *de novo* assembly of complex genomes (e.g., telomere-to-telomere human chromosomes).
2. **Real-time pathogen detection:** Rapid identification of viruses (e.g., SARS-CoV-2, Ebola, Zika) in clinical and field settings using **MinION** portable device.
3. **Native epigenetic detection:** Modified bases (5-mC, 6-mA, etc.) alter current signals → direct methylation detection *without* bisulfite conversion.
4. **Structural variant (SV) detection:** Long reads span complex structural rearrangements (inversions, translocations, repeat expansions).
5. **Environmental & metagenomic sequencing:** Field-deployable devices enable **real-time environmental DNA (eDNA)** analysis.

---

#### **Question B (Alternative — 14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Compare Sanger sequencing, Illumina sequencing, and PacBio SMRT sequencing with respect to: (i) read length, (ii) accuracy, (iii) throughput, (iv) PCR requirement, and (v) ability to detect epigenetic modifications.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Sanger | Illumina | PacBio SMRT |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) Read length** | 500–1,000 bp | 50–600 bp (commonly 150 bp × 2) | 10–25 kb (HiFi); up to 100 kb (CLR) |
| **(ii) Accuracy** | **>99.999%** (Q50) — the gold standard | >99.9% (Q30) per base | >99.9% Q30 (HiFi consensus); ~87% single-pass |
| **(iii) Throughput** | ~0.5 Mb per reaction per day | 1.2 Tb – 6 Tb per run (NovaSeq X) | ~500 Gb (Revio) per SMRT Cell |
| **(iv) PCR requirement** | Yes (thermal cycle sequencing in tube) | Yes (bridge amplification on flow cell) | **No** — single-molecule real-time (no amplification) |
| **(v) Epigenetic detection** | No (requires bisulfite conversion) | No (requires bisulfite conversion) | **Yes — natively** detects N6-methyladenine (6-mA), 5-methylcytosine (5-mC), and other modifications via kinetics of polymerase incorporation |

**Additional discriminating factors (1 mark):**
- **Sanger** is best for **single-gene validation**, **clinical confirmatory tests**, and **plasmid verification**.
- **Illumina** dominates **short-read applications** — WGS, exomes, RNA-seq, ChIP-seq, ATAC-seq.
- **PacBio HiFi** excels in **de novo assembly**, **structural variant detection**, **full-length isoform sequencing (Iso-Seq)**, and **native methylation profiling**.

---

**(b) What is whole-genome shotgun sequencing? Describe its workflow and explain the role of paired-end reads in genome assembly.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):**
**Whole-genome shotgun (WGS) sequencing** is a *strategy* (not a method) in which the **entire genome** is randomly fragmented, the fragments are sequenced *in parallel*, and **bioinformatic assembly** reconstructs the original genome sequence. It is the strategy used by the private effort (Celera Genomics) to assemble the *first* human genome (Venter et al., *Science*, 2001).

**Workflow (4 marks):**
1. **DNA extraction & shearing:** High-molecular-weight genomic DNA is randomly fragmented (nebulization, sonication, or enzymatic shearing) to a target size (e.g., 200 bp, 2 kb, 10 kb, or 40 kb for a "tiered" approach).
2. **Library preparation:** Fragment ends are repaired, **adapter sequences** (containing priming sites, barcodes, and platform-specific motifs) are ligated. For **mate-pair libraries**, circularization brings distant ends of a long fragment together, and the junction is sequenced.
3. **Sequencing:** All fragments are sequenced in parallel. For short-read platforms (Illumina), 100–600 bp reads are generated from each fragment. For long-read platforms (PacBio, Nanopore), reads of 10–100 kb are produced.
4. **Quality control & filtering:** Reads are quality-trimmed (Phred ≥ Q20), adapter sequences are removed, and **PCR duplicates** are flagged.
5. **Assembly:** Two main algorithmic approaches:
   - **Overlap–Layout–Consensus (OLC):** Used for long reads; identifies pairwise overlaps between all reads.
   - **de Bruijn Graph:** Used for short reads; breaks reads into k-mers and constructs a graph where k-mers are nodes and k-1 overlaps are edges. The **Eulerian path** through the graph corresponds to the assembled genome.
6. **Scaffolding & gap-filling:** Contigs are ordered and oriented using **paired-end / mate-pair information**, and gaps are closed with additional sequencing.
7. **Annotation & validation:** Assembled sequence is annotated for genes, repeats, regulatory elements, and validated (e.g., by mapping back raw reads or using orthogonal long-read data).

**Role of Paired-End Reads (2 marks):**
Paired-end reads are generated by sequencing **both ends** of each library fragment, producing two reads separated by a known approximate distance (*insert size*). During assembly:
- **Read-pair linking** constrains the placement of contigs: the two reads must align in *opposite orientations* at a distance equal to the insert size.
- **Repeat resolution:** Repeats longer than the individual read length are normally collapsed. The second read of a pair may lie in a *unique* region flanking the repeat, anchoring the contig and resolving the repeat's location.
- **Scaffolding:** Contigs are joined into **scaffolds** (supercontigs) with Ns representing estimated gap sizes.
- **Detection of structural variants:** Discordant read pairs (incorrect insert size or orientation) signal **insertions, deletions, inversions, and translocations**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing the *method* with the *strategy*:** "Illumina sequencing" is a *method*; "shotgun sequencing" is a *strategy*. Students often mix these up — losing 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Skipping the role of mate-pair libraries:** Examiner expects explicit mention of *insert size* as a critical parameter.
> 3. **Ignoring de Bruijn vs. OLC distinction:** Modern KTU papers (2024 Scheme) explicitly test the algorithmic underpinnings. Always state the relevant algorithm.
> 4. **Forgetting PCR duplication handling:** Not addressing **deduplication** with tools like **Picard MarkDuplicates** is a common omission.
> 5. **Underestimating the importance of Q-score filtering:** Always specify a Phred threshold (typically Q20 or Q30) when describing QC.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔑 Rapid Revision Checklist — Genome Sequencing Methods**

- ✅ **Sanger sequencing** = ddNTP chain termination → fragments → CE → fluorophore detection.
- ✅ **Maxam–Gilbert** = base-specific chemical cleavage (G via DMS, C+T via hydrazine, A+G via formic acid); **now obsolete** for routine work.
- ✅ **Shotgun sequencing** = random fragmentation + parallel sequencing + computational assembly (overlap-layout-consensus or de Bruijn graph).
- ✅ **Hierarchical (BAC-by-BAC) sequencing** = physical map first → BAC-by-BAC shotgun → used in HGP public consortium.
- ✅ **Illumina (SBS)** = bridge amplification → clusters of ~1,000 copies → reversible terminator nucleotides → 4-channel imaging.
- ✅ **Ion Torrent** = detects H⁺ released on dNTP incorporation → pH change → voltage signal; **no fluorescence**.
- ✅ **PacBio SMRT** = single-molecule real-time in **ZMWs** → phospholinked nucleotides → long reads (10–25 kb HiFi) → **native methylation** detection.
- ✅ **Oxford Nanopore** = single-molecule threading through a **biological nanopore** → base-specific current changes → ultra-long reads (up to 2 Mb) → **real-time** sequencing on **MinION**.
- ✅ **Coverage depth formula:** $C = N \times L / G$. For human 30× WGS, need ~**6 × 10⁸ reads** at 150 bp.
- ✅ **Phred quality score:** $Q = -10 \log_{10}(P_e)$. Q30 = 99.9% accuracy.
- ✅ **Lander–Waterman Poisson model:** $P(\text{0 reads at base } i) = e^{-C}$.
- ✅ **Lander–Waterman derivation** = formal Lander–Waterman statistical proof that random shotgun coverage follows a Poisson distribution; key for genome assembly planning.
- ✅ **Sanger fragment length distribution:** Controlled by ddNTP:dNTP ratio; at 1:100 ratio, fragments of 1 kb are rare ($P \approx 4.5 \times 10^{-5}$).
- ✅ **ddNTP vs. dNTP:** ddNTP lacks 3′-OH → **no phosphodiester bond** → chain termination.
- ✅ **Sequence assembly algorithms:** OLC for long reads; **de Bruijn graph** for short reads.
- ✅ **Paired-end reads** anchor contigs, resolve repeats, and enable scaffolding.
- ✅ **Limitations of short-read NGS:** Cannot span long repeats; PCR-duplication artifacts; GC bias.
- ✅ **Long-read advantages:** Span repeats, detect SVs, native epigenetics.
- ✅ **Cost trajectory:** From $3 billion (HGP, 2003) to **~$200** (Illumina 30× WGS, 2024).
- ✅ **Key historical milestones:**
   - 1977 — Sanger dideoxy sequencing.
   - 1980 — Nobel Prize to Sanger, Gilbert, Maxam.
   - 2003 — Human Genome Project completed.
   - 2005 — 454 Roche pyrosequencing (first NGS).
   - 2006 — Illumina/Solexa sequencing.
   - 2010 — PacBio SMRT.
   - 2012 — Oxford Nanopore MinION alpha.
   - 2022 — Telomere-to-telomere human genome (T2T-CHM13).

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Do **not** confuse **ddNTP** (no 3′-OH, chain terminator) with **dNTP** (normal nucleotide, chain extender).
> 2. Do **not** say "Maxam–Gilbert is widely used today" — it is largely **obsolete**.
> 3. Do **not** attribute **hierarchical (BAC-by-BAC) sequencing** to Celera — that was **shotgun**. HGP public consortium used hierarchical.
> 4. Do **not** say Nanopore uses "fluorescence" — it uses **ionic current**.
> 5. Do **not** forget to specify the **30× coverage** standard for clinical-grade human WGS.
> 6. Do **not** write "**Polony**" as "polony" or "Poly" in formal KTU answers — keep it **capitalized and intact**.
> 7. Do **not** confuse **paired-end** (short insert, both ends sequenced) with **mate-pair** (long insert, jumping library).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conventional and Shotgun Genome Sequencing Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT524 - GENETIC ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Conventional and Shotgun Genome Sequencing Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Conventional and Shotgun Genome Sequencing Methods

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
> **Genome sequencing** is the laboratory process of determining the complete DNA sequence of an organism's genome at a single base-pair resolution. Two foundational large-scale sequencing strategies are employed: **Conventional Sequencing (Clone-by-Clone / Hierarchical Shotgun / Map-Based Sequencing)** and **Whole-Genome Shotgun (WGS) Sequencing**. Conventional sequencing relies on first constructing a high-resolution physical/genetic map of the genome, breaking it into mapped large-insert clones (BACs/YACs), sub-cloning these into smaller fragments, and sequencing each clone individually. Whole-genome shotgun sequencing, in contrast, randomly shears the entire genome into fragments of multiple sizes, sequences the ends of millions of clones in parallel, and computationally assembles the overlapping reads into contigs using algorithms — without any prior map.

### Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine you have to **reconstruct the entire text of a 1000-page novel** that has been shredded into pieces.

**Conventional (Clone-by-Clone) Sequencing** is like:
1. First, you take a quick glance at every page and write down rough summaries of each chapter (the **genetic/physical map**).
2. You then bind related chapters into separate **bound booklets** (BAC/YAC clones, each ~100 pages).
3. You only shred **one booklet at a time**, sequence its pages, assemble that booklet, then move to the next booklet.
4. Finally, you put the assembled booklets in the correct chapter order using your map.

**Shotgun Sequencing** is like:
1. You dump all 1000 pages into the shredder at once into millions of random strips.
2. You randomly grab strips and try to match overlapping sentences across all of them simultaneously.
3. A very powerful computer uses word overlaps to rebuild the entire book from scratch — no pre-reading required.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exams:**
> - **Conventional = Map-First, Then Sequence**
> - **Shotgun = Sequence-First, Then Assemble (Map-Second, Computationally)**

### Standard Metrics Used in Genome Sequencing

| Metric | Typical Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Genome size (H. sapiens)** | **~3.0 × 10⁹ bp** | Total bases to be sequenced |
| **BAC clone insert size** | **100 – 300 kb** | Large-insert cloning vector capacity |
| **Plasmid sub-clone size** | **2 – 10 kb** | Used for individual Sanger reads |
| **Read length (Sanger)** | **500 – 1000 bases** | Single-pass readable length |
| **Coverage depth (fold)** | **6× – 10×** | Each base sequenced this many times on average |
| **Phred quality score Q20** | **99% accuracy** | 1 error in 100 bases |
| **Phred quality score Q30** | **99.9% accuracy** | 1 error in 1000 bases |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conventional (Hierarchical / Map-Based / Clone-by-Clone) Sequencing

This is the **classical approach** used in the publicly funded Human Genome Project (HGP, 1990–2003).

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

**Step 1 — Construction of a Genetic/Physical Map**
- Sequence-Tagged Sites (STS), Restriction Fragment Length Polymorphisms (RFLPs), and microsatellite markers are used as landmarks.
- These markers serve as anchors distributed every ~100 kb across the genome.

**Step 2 — Construction of a Large-Insert BAC/YAC Library**
- The genome is partially digested (e.g., with HindIII) to generate fragments of ~150 kb.
- These are ligated into Bacterial Artificial Chromosomes (BACs, capacity **~100–300 kb**) or Yeast Artificial Chromosomes (YACs, capacity up to **~1–2 Mb**).
- The library is arrayed into microtiter plates.

**Step 3 — Fingerprinting and Contig Assembly**
- Each BAC is digested with restriction enzymes, producing a unique **fingerprint** (band pattern).
- Overlapping BACs share similar fingerprints.
- A **contig (contiguous sequence)** is built — a set of overlapping BAC clones covering a chromosomal region.

**Step 4 — Sub-cloning into Plasmids**
- Each mapped BAC is randomly sheared or digested into smaller fragments (~2–10 kb).
- These are subcloned into high-copy plasmids (e.g., pUC19, M13mp18).

**Step 5 — Sanger Sequencing of Sub-clones**
- Each plasmid is sequenced using the **chain-termination method (Sanger dideoxy method)**.
- Universal primers (M13 forward/reverse) are used because the vector sequence flanking the insert is known.

**Step 6 — Assembly and Finishing**
- Sequences from each BAC are assembled using **phrap/Phred** algorithms.
- Gaps are closed by primer walking, PCR, or transposon mutagenesis.
- Final accuracy target: **<1 error in 10,000 bases** (Q40).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why was Conventional Sequencing the first choice?**
> In the 1990s, computer assembly algorithms were primitive and could not handle the complexity of assembling an entire 3 Gb human genome. A pre-built map reduced the computational assembly problem to manageable sub-problems per BAC.

## 2.2 Whole-Genome Shotgun (WGS) Sequencing

Pioneered by **Craig Venter and Celera Genomics (1998)**. Used to sequence *Drosophila melanogaster* (2000) and a parallel draft of the human genome (2001).

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

**Step 1 — Random Fragmentation of the Whole Genome**
- The entire genomic DNA is randomly sheared (nebulization, sonication, or hydrodynamic shear).
- No prior knowledge of map positions is required.

**Step 2 — Size Selection and Multi-Tier Library Construction**
Three libraries of different insert sizes are made to span different repeat lengths:
- **2 kb plasmid library** — for high-quality finishing
- **10 kb plasmid / fosmid library** — for medium-range linking
- **50–150 kb BAC library** — for long-range linking across repetitive elements

**Step 3 — Paired-End Sequencing**
- Both ends of each insert are sequenced (forward + reverse read).
- This produces **mate pairs** — two sequences separated by a known distance *k*.
- Mate pairs are critical for spanning **repetitive sequences** which single reads cannot resolve.

**Step 4 — Computational Assembly**
- Software suites (Celera Assembler, Arachne, Phusion, ALLPATHS) perform:
  1. **Overlap detection** — find common k-mers between reads
  2. **Contig building** — merge overlapping reads
  3. **Scaffolding** — order contigs using mate-pair constraints
  4. **Gap closure** — fill remaining gaps by PCR/walking

**Step 5 — Finishing**
- Low-quality regions polished, ambiguities resolved.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The role of mate-pair information in shotgun assembly:**
> If read A and read B are mate pairs separated by ~10 kb, finding read A on one contig and read B on another contig tells the assembler the two contigs must lie within 10 kb of each other on the same chromosome. This is the computational equivalent of the BAC map.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Genome Sequencing Coverage

> [!NOTE]
> **The single most important quantitative formula in genome sequencing is the Lander-Waterman Coverage Equation.**

| Symbol | Definition | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $N$ | Number of reads | dimensionless |
| $L$ | Read length | bases |
| $G$ | Genome size | bases |
| $c$ | Coverage depth | fold (×) |
| $P$ | Probability that a base is NOT sequenced | dimensionless |
| $\sigma$ | Standard deviation of coverage | bases |

**Core Equations:**

$$
c \;=\; \frac{N \cdot L}{G}
$$

$$
P(\text{base unsequenced}) \;=\; e^{-c}
$$

$$
P(\text{base sequenced at least once}) \;=\; 1 - e^{-c}
$$

$$
\sigma \;=\; \frac{L \cdot \sqrt{N}}{G} \quad \text{(Poisson variance under WGS)}
$$

**Real-World Utility:**
For $c = 10$, the probability of any base remaining unsequenced is $e^{-10} \approx 4.5 \times 10^{-5}$. For a 3 Gb genome, this means approximately **135 bases** are expected to be missing — which is why the **6×–10× coverage** standard exists for draft genomes and **30×–60×** for clinical-grade sequencing today.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation — Required Number of Reads for a Given Coverage

**Problem (typical KTU-style numerical):**
A microbial genome is **4.5 Mb** in size. Using a shotgun library with an average **read length of 800 bases**, calculate the number of reads required to achieve **8× coverage**.

### Step-by-Step Logical Deduction

**Step 1 — Identify known quantities from the question stem:**
- $G = 4.5 \times 10^6$ bases
- $L = 800$ bases
- $c = 8$

**Step 2 — Recall the Lander-Waterman equation relating coverage to read count:**
$$
c \;=\; \frac{N \cdot L}{G}
$$
This equation states that coverage equals the total number of sequenced bases divided by the genome size.

**Step 3 — Rearrange algebraically to solve for $N$:**
$$
N \cdot L \;=\; c \cdot G
$$

$$
N \;=\; \frac{c \cdot G}{L}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values:**
$$
N \;=\; \frac{8 \times 4.5 \times 10^{6}}{800}
$$

**Step 5 — Simplify the denominator first for clarity:**
$$
N \;=\; \frac{36 \times 10^{6}}{8 \times 10^{2}}
$$

**Step 6 — Perform the division:**
$$
N \;=\; 4.5 \times 10^{4} \;=\; 45{,}000 \text{ reads}
$$

**Final Answer:**
$$
\boxed{N \;=\; 45{,}000 \text{ reads}}
$$

**Interpretation:** A modest 45,000 sequencing reactions suffice to cover a small bacterial genome 8-fold, illustrating why shotgun sequencing became feasible after automation of Sanger chemistry.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation — Probability of Coverage Gap

**Problem:**
For the same microbial genome, if we only achieve **5× coverage**, what fraction of bases are expected to remain unsequenced? How many bases does this correspond to?

### Step-by-Step Logical Deduction

**Step 1 — Apply the Poisson/Lander-Waterman probability of an unsequenced base:**
$$
P(\text{gap}) \;=\; e^{-c}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $c = 5$:**
$$
P(\text{gap}) \;=\; e^{-5}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate numerically:**
$$
P(\text{gap}) \;=\; 0.00674
$$

This means approximately **0.674%** of the genome has a coverage gap.

**Step 4 — Convert to absolute number of bases:**
$$
\text{Unsequenced bases} \;=\; P(\text{gap}) \times G
$$

$$
\text{Unsequenced bases} \;=\; 0.00674 \times 4.5 \times 10^{6}
$$

$$
\text{Unsequenced bases} \;\approx\; 30{,}330 \text{ bases}
$$

**Final Answer:**
$$
\boxed{P(\text{gap}) \;\approx\; 6.74 \times 10^{-3}, \quad \text{Gap size} \;\approx\; 30.3 \text{ kb}}
$$

**Interpretation:** Going from 8× to 5× coverage increases the unsequenced portion by **~150-fold**, demonstrating the **non-linear cost of reducing coverage**. This is why KTU examiners love testing why 6×–10× is the chosen standard.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Side-by-Side Comparison

| Parameter | Conventional (Clone-by-Clone) | Shotgun (Whole Genome) |
|---|---|---|
| **Map-first?** | Yes — physical/genetic map required | No — purely random |
| **Cloning vector used first** | BAC / YAC | Plasmids, fosmids, BACs (multi-tier) |
| **Insert size at first step** | 100 – 300 kb (BAC) | Mixed: 2 kb / 10 kb / 50 kb |
| **Sub-cloning step** | Yes — mandatory | Optional / minimal |
| **Assembly difficulty** | Low (per-BAC) | High (genome-wide) |
| **Handling of repeats** | Easier (clones broken up) | Difficult (needs mate pairs) |
| **Time required** | Years | Months |
| **Cost (1990s – early 2000s)** | High (~$3 B for HGP) | Lower (Celera: ~$300 M for human) |
| **Computational demand** | Low | Very High |
| **Finishing accuracy** | Very high (Q40+) | Draft quality initially (Q20) |
| **Examples** | HGP public effort (2003), *Arabidopsis* | Celera human draft (2001), *Drosophila* |
| **Best for** | Large, repeat-rich genomes | Small, low-repeat genomes initially |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conventional (Clone-by-Clone) Sequencing Workflow

The following block-level architecture maps every operational step, the laboratory entity produced at each step, and the data flow between them. This avoids the limitations of attempting to draw an actual 2D gel or chromatogram in text.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    g1["GENOMIC DNA EXTRACTION"]
    g2["PARTIAL RESTRICTION DIGEST HindIII or EcoRI"]
    g3["SIZE SELECTION 100 to 300 kb FRAGMENTS"]
    g4["LIGATION INTO BAC VECTOR pBACe3.6 or YAC"]
    g5["TRANSFORMATION INTO E. coli OR YEAST"]
    g6["BAC LIBRARY ARRAYED IN 384 WELL PLATES"]
    g7["FINGERPRINTING WITH RESTRICTION ENZYMES"]
    g8["CONTIG ASSEMBLY BY FINGERPRINT OVERLAP"]
    g9["INDIVIDUAL BAC SELECTED FOR SEQUENCING"]
    g10["SUBCLONING INTO pUC19 OR M13mp18"]
    g11["SANGER DIDEOXY SEQUENCING CHAIN TERMINATION"]
    g12["PHRED BASE CALLING AND QUALITY SCORING"]
    g13["PHRAP ASSEMBLY PER BAC CLONE"]
    g14["GAP CLOSURE PRIMER WALKING OR PCR"]
    g15["FINISHED CONTIG WITH Q40 ACCURACY"]

    g1 --> g2 --> g3 --> g4 --> g5 --> g6 --> g7 --> g8 --> g9 --> g10 --> g11 --> g12 --> g13 --> g14 --> g15

    g8a["PHYSICAL AND GENETIC MAP WITH STS MARKERS"] -.feeds into.-> g8
    g11a["PRIMER DESIGN FROM VECTOR FLANKING SEQUENCE"] -.feeds into.-> g11
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip for KTU Exams:**
> Whenever asked to draw or describe a workflow, **always include the feedback arrows** (dashed lines). Examiners award marks for showing that the **map guides contig assembly**, and that **vector-based primers feed into the Sanger step**. A linear-only flow loses you 1–2 marks.

---

## 4.2 Whole-Genome Shotgun Sequencing Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    w1["GENOMIC DNA EXTRACTION HIGH MOLECULAR WEIGHT"]
    w2["RANDOM SHEARING NEBULIZATION OR SONICATION"]
    w3["SIZE FRACTIONATION THREE TIERS"]
    w3a["PLASMID LIBRARY 2 kb INSERT"]
    w3b["FOSMID LIBRARY 10 kb INSERT"]
    w3c["BAC LIBRARY 50 to 150 kb INSERT"]

    w4["LIGATION INTO VECTORS AND TRANSFORMATION"]
    w5["PAIRED END SEQUENCING READ FORWARD AND REVERSE"]
    w6["RAW READ STORAGE PHRED OR FASTQ FORMAT"]
    w7["COMPUTATIONAL OVERLAP DETECTION K MER MATCHING"]
    w8["CONTIG BUILDING MERGING OVERLAPPING READS"]
    w9["SCAFFOLDING USING MATE PAIR DISTANCE CONSTRAINTS"]
    w10["GAP CLOSURE BY PCR OR PRIMER WALKING"]
    w11["FINAL POLISHED GENOME ASSEMBLY"]

    w1 --> w2 --> w3
    w3 --> w3a & w3b & w3c
    w3a & w3b & w3c --> w4 --> w5 --> w6 --> w7 --> w8 --> w9 --> w10 --> w11

    w8a["REPEAT MASKING WITH REPETITIVE ELEMENT DATABASE"] -.feeds into.-> w8
    w9a["MATE PAIR DISTANCE K APPROXIMATELY 2 KB OR 10 KB"] -.feeds into.-> w9
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Reading:**
> The dashed lines in this diagram represent the **computational knowledge bases** that feed into each assembly stage. Without repeat masking, contigs collapse into chimera. Without mate-pair distances, scaffolds cannot be ordered. KTU examiners frequently award 2 marks for explicitly naming these two ancillary inputs.

---

## 4.3 Hybrid Strategy Block Diagram (Modern Practical Approach)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    h1["WGS SHOTGUN READS HIGH DEPTH 30X TO 60X"]
    h2["BAC END SEQUENCES LOW DEPTH LONG RANGE LINKS"]
    h3["OPTICAL OR HI C MAP CHROMOSOME SCALE SCAFFOLDS"]
    h4["HYBRID ASSEMBLER SUCH AS ALLPATHS OR SPADES"]

    h1 --> h4
    h2 --> h4
    h3 --> h4
    h4 --> h5["FINAL GOLD STANDARD GENOME ASSEMBLY"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** Modern sequencing projects (e.g., the **Telomere-to-Telomere human genome, T2T-CHM13, 2022**) use a **hybrid of both methods**. Pure shotgun alone cannot resolve segmental duplications; pure conventional alone is too slow. KTU examiners consider the hybrid as the "gold standard" answer when asked about modern best practice.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students frequently **write "shotgun sequencing" without specifying "whole-genome"** — the two are NOT synonyms in KTU marking. Sub-clone shotgunning is part of conventional sequencing.
> 2. **Do NOT confuse coverage (c) with read length (L).** A common error: setting $c = L$ in the formula. Coverage is **dimensionless**, read length is in **bases**.
> 3. When asked "advantages of shotgun," students forget the **single most important advantage: speed due to automation**. Listing only "no map needed" is incomplete.
> 4. For conventional sequencing, **always mention the role of the BAC map first** — this is the defining feature. Without it, you have described only sub-clone sequencing.
> 5. In numerical problems, **forget to convert Mb to bases** ($1~\text{Mb} = 10^6$ bases). This is the #1 arithmetic error costing 1–2 marks.

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]
**Define whole-genome shotgun sequencing. List two advantages over conventional map-based sequencing.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Whole-genome shotgun (WGS) sequencing** is a strategy in which the entire genomic DNA is randomly fragmented into pieces of multiple size classes, cloned into appropriate vectors, and sequenced from both ends without prior knowledge of a physical or genetic map. The overlapping sequences are then computationally assembled into contigs and scaffolds using algorithms such as the Celera Assembler or Arachne.

**Two Advantages:**
1. **Speed and automation:** Eliminates the years-long mapping phase. Once libraries are made, sequencing and assembly proceed in parallel.
2. **Lower cost per base:** No expensive mapping infrastructure, fewer manual steps; ideal for high-throughput genome centers.

*(Optional 3rd advantage for full marks: better at discovering unexpected genomic features such as translocations or horizontally transferred genes.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]
**What is a BAC? Why was it the preferred vector for conventional human genome sequencing?**

**Model Answer:**

> A **BAC (Bacterial Artificial Chromosome)** is a cloning vector derived from the *E. coli* F-plasmid, capable of maintaining foreign DNA inserts of **100 to 300 kb** with high structural stability and low chimerism.

**Why preferred for HGP:**
1. **Large insert capacity (100–300 kb)** allows fewer clones to cover the entire 3 Gb human genome (~30,000 BACs vs. millions of plasmids).
2. **Low chimerism and high stability** in *E. coli* compared to YACs (which showed high rates of insert rearrangement).
3. **Easy DNA purification** from bacterial cultures, suitable for automated fingerprinting.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU Exam Pattern Reminder:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split as (a) **7 marks** and (b) **7 marks**. Both sub-parts are compulsory.

---

### Question A — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the **conventional clone-by-clone sequencing strategy**, including the construction of a BAC library, fingerprinting, contig assembly, and sub-cloning steps.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A bacterial genome is **5.2 Mb** in size. If sequencing reads are **900 bases long** and the project targets **10× coverage**, calculate:
(i) the number of sequencing reads required.
(ii) the probability that any given base is NOT sequenced.
(iii) the approximate number of bases that remain unsequenced.

---

#### Model Answer to (a):

**Step 1 — Genomic DNA Extraction and Library Construction:**
High-molecular-weight genomic DNA is partially digested with a restriction enzyme (commonly **HindIII** or *Eco*RI) under conditions favoring incomplete cutting. Fragments in the **100–300 kb** size range are separated by **pulsed-field gel electrophoresis (PFGE)**, ligated into the BAC vector **pBACe3.6**, and transformed into *E. coli* DH10B by electroporation. The library is arrayed into **384-well microtiter plates**, with each well representing one BAC clone. A typical human BAC library contains **~20× genome equivalent coverage**, i.e., ~150,000 clones for a 3 Gb genome.

**Step 2 — Fingerprinting:**
Each BAC clone is digested with a 4-base cutter (e.g., *Hin*dIII or *Hae*III), and the resulting fragments are resolved by agarose gel electrophoresis. The pattern of bands constitutes a **unique fingerprint** for each BAC. Automated fluorescent fingerprinting using capillary sequencers replaced manual gels in the late 1990s.

**Step 3 — Contig Assembly:**
BACs whose fingerprints share multiple bands are identified as **overlapping**. Computer algorithms (e.g., **FPC — Fingerprint Contigs**) cluster overlapping BACs into **contigs**. The order of BACs within a contig is refined using **STS (Sequence-Tagged Site) markers** previously placed on the physical/genetic map. STS markers are short, unique sequences (200–500 bp) whose chromosomal location is known.

**Step 4 — Selection and Sub-cloning of Individual BACs:**
Once a tiling path of minimally overlapping BACs is chosen (each base of the genome covered by at least one BAC), each BAC is purified and randomly sheared (sonication or nebulization) into **2–10 kb fragments**. These fragments are end-repaired and ligated into a high-copy plasmid vector (e.g., **pUC19** or the phage vector **M13mp18**) for Sanger sequencing.

**Step 5 — Sequencing and Finishing:**
Sanger dideoxy sequencing is performed using **universal primers** (M13 forward/reverse) that anneal to the vector sequence flanking the insert. Reads are base-called by **Phred**, assembled by **Phrap**, and gaps are closed by **primer walking**, **PCR**, or **transposon-facilitated sequencing**. The final polished sequence of each BAC is deposited in GenBank with **Q40 accuracy** (error rate $\lt 10^{-4}$).

---

#### Model Answer to (b):

**Part (i) — Number of reads required:**

Using the Lander-Waterman coverage equation:

$$
c \;=\; \frac{N \cdot L}{G}
$$

Solving for $N$:

$$
N \;=\; \frac{c \cdot G}{L}
$$

Substituting $c = 10$, $G = 5.2 \times 10^{6}$ bases, $L = 900$ bases:

$$
N \;=\; \frac{10 \times 5.2 \times 10^{6}}{900}
$$

$$
N \;=\; \frac{52 \times 10^{6}}{9 \times 10^{2}}
$$

$$
N \;\approx\; 57{,}778 \text{ reads}
$$

$$
\boxed{N \;\approx\; 5.78 \times 10^{4} \text{ reads}}
$$

**Part (ii) — Probability of an unsequenced base:**

$$
P(\text{gap}) \;=\; e^{-c} \;=\; e^{-10}
$$

$$
P(\text{gap}) \;\approx\; 4.54 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
\boxed{P(\text{gap}) \;\approx\; 4.54 \times 10^{-5}}
$$

**Part (iii) — Number of unsequenced bases:**

$$
\text{Unsequenced bases} \;=\; P(\text{gap}) \times G
$$

$$
\text{Unsequenced bases} \;=\; 4.54 \times 10^{-5} \times 5.2 \times 10^{6}
$$

$$
\text{Unsequenced bases} \;\approx\; 236 \text{ bases}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Unsequenced bases} \;\approx\; 236 \text{ bases}}
$$

**Interpretation:** Even at 10× coverage, ~236 bases (mostly in extreme GC- or AT-rich regions, or in the few repetitive elements) remain gaps, justifying why finishing phases of genome projects are essential.

---

### Question B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **whole-genome shotgun sequencing strategy** as implemented by Celera Genomics. Explain the role of **paired-end / mate-pair reads** in resolving repetitive DNA.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast conventional and shotgun sequencing approaches under the following heads:
(i) Map requirement, (ii) Cloning vectors used, (iii) Computational assembly requirement, (iv) Handling of repetitive DNA, (v) Time and cost, (vi) Finishing accuracy, (vii) One example organism sequenced by each method.

---

#### Model Answer to (a):

**Background:**
In **May 1998**, Craig Venter announced the formation of Celera Genomics with the goal of sequencing the entire human genome in **3 years** using a **whole-genome shotgun** strategy — at a fraction of the public HGP's cost. The strategy was first validated on the **130 Mb *Drosophila melanogaster* genome in 2000**, followed by a draft human genome assembly published in **February 2001**.

**Step-by-Step Celera Workflow:**

**Step 1 — Random Shearing:** High-molecular-weight human DNA was randomly sheared by **nebulization** (forcing DNA through a small orifice under high pressure) to generate fragments in the desired size range.

**Step 2 — Multi-Tier Library Construction:** Three libraries were constructed:
- **Plasmid library** with ~2 kb inserts
- **Fosmid library** with ~10 kb inserts
- **BAC library** with ~50–150 kb inserts

**Step 3 — Paired-End Sequencing:** Both ends of every insert were sequenced using Sanger chemistry. Each pair of reads is called a **mate pair**, with the two reads separated by a known distance equal to the insert size (e.g., 2 kb, 10 kb, or 50 kb apart).

**Step 4 — Computational Assembly:** The Celera Assembler used:
- **Overlapper:** identifies shared k-mers between reads to find overlaps
- **Unitigger:** merges reads into contigs
- **Scaffolder:** orders contigs into scaffolds using mate-pair distance and orientation constraints
- **Repeat resolution:** distinguishes true unique overlaps from repetitive overlaps

**Role of Mate Pairs in Resolving Repeats:**

Repetitive DNA (LINEs, SINEs, segmental duplications) is longer than a single read, causing the assembler to ambiguously place reads within the repeat family. Mate pairs solve this: if one read of a pair falls in a unique region and its mate falls in a repeat, the assembler can **anchor** the repeat-containing contig to a unique chromosomal location. Without mate pairs, repeats collapse contigs into collapsed "stacks." The longer the mate-pair distance, the longer the repeat that can be spanned — which is why Celera used the **three-tier library strategy** (2 kb / 10 kb / 50 kb) to span repeats of varying lengths.

---

#### Model Answer to (b):

| # | Parameter | Conventional (Clone-by-Clone) | Shotgun (Whole Genome) |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) | **Map requirement** | Mandatory — physical/genetic map built first using STS markers | None — purely random fragmentation |
| (ii) | **Cloning vectors** | BAC / YAC first, then sub-cloned into plasmids | Multi-tier: plasmids (2 kb), fosmids (10 kb), BACs (50–150 kb) |
| (iii) | **Computational assembly** | Low — only at per-BAC level; manageable | Very high — must assemble whole genome simultaneously |
| (iv) | **Repetitive DNA** | Easier — repeats broken up across BACs, anchored by map | Difficult — requires mate pairs and sophisticated repeat resolution algorithms |
| (v) | **Time and cost** | Slow (years) and expensive (HGP: 13 years, ~$3 B) | Fast (months) and cheaper (Celera draft: ~$300 M, 3 years) |
| (vi) | **Finishing accuracy** | High — Q40 or better from the start | Initially draft (Q20); requires finishing to reach Q40 |
| (vii) | **Example organism** | *Arabidopsis thaliana* (2000), public HGP human (2003) | *Drosophila melanogaster* (Celera, 2000), Celera human draft (2001) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** When answering comparison tables in KTU exams, **always close with one concluding sentence** stating which approach is preferred under which condition. For example: *"Conventional sequencing is preferred for highly repetitive genomes or when reference-grade quality is mandatory; shotgun is preferred for small genomes or when rapid draft data is the priority. Modern projects use a hybrid of both."* This earns you the **synthesis mark** (often 1 mark reserved for higher-order thinking under Revised Bloom's Taxonomy Level 5–6).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD REVISION BULLETS — Read before every KTU exam**

- **Conventional sequencing = Map-First.** The defining feature is the **pre-built physical/genetic map** using **STS markers**.
- **Shotgun sequencing = Sequence-First, Assemble-Second.** The defining feature is **random shearing with no prior map**.
- **Lander-Waterman equation:** $c = NL/G$. Memorize the rearrangement $N = cG/L$ for numerical problems.
- **Gap probability:** $P(\text{gap}) = e^{-c}$. At $c=10$, gap fraction is $\sim 4.5 \times 10^{-5}$.
- **BAC insert size = 100–300 kb.** YAC insert size = up to 1–2 Mb but high chimerism.
- **Plasmid sub-clone insert size = 2–10 kb.** Read length = 500–1000 bases (Sanger).
- **Mate pairs** are the shotgun assembler’s substitute for a physical map — they provide long-range linking information.
- **Three-tier library strategy** (2 kb / 10 kb / 50 kb) is the standard Celera method for spanning different repeat lengths.
- **Phred** = base caller; **Phrap** = assembler; **Phrap/Phred/Consed** = the classical finishing toolchain.
- **Coverage recommendations:** **6×–10×** for draft; **30×–60×** for high-quality modern assemblies.
- **Human Genome Project:** Public, conventional, 1990–2003, ~$3 billion, 13 years.
- **Celera Genomics:** Private, shotgun, 1998–2001, ~$300 million, draft published alongside HGP.
- **Modern standard:** Hybrid of both — WGS short reads + long-range linking data (BAC ends / Hi-C / optical maps).
- **Key pitfall — coverage vs read length:** $c$ is dimensionless (×); $L$ is in bases. Mixing them is the most common arithmetic mistake.
- **Key pitfall — vector knowledge:** For Sanger of sub-clones, primers anneal to **vector flanking sequence** (e.g., M13F/R, T7, SP6), NOT to the insert itself. Examiners frequently test this.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Next-Generation Sequencing Technologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT524 - GENETIC ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Next-Generation Sequencing Technologies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Next-Generation Sequencing (NGS)** refers to a set of high-throughput, massively parallel DNA sequencing technologies that allow millions to billions of DNA fragments to be sequenced simultaneously in a single instrument run, generating gigabases (Gb) to terabases (Tb) of sequence data per run, in stark contrast to the capillary-based, low-throughput Sanger (first-generation) sequencing method.

The term **"Next-Generation"** specifically excludes the dideoxy chain-termination (Sanger) method and includes all platforms developed from the mid-2000s onwards that rely on **clonally amplified templates** or **single-molecule real-time detection**, combined with **parallelization** and **miniaturization**.

KTU-relevant standard metrics you must memorize:

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Read Length | $L$ | **35 – 300** (Illumina), **10,000+** (PacBio/ONT) | bases (bp) |
| Throughput per Run | $T$ | **0.5 Gb – 6000 Gb** | gigabases (Gb) |
| Coverage Depth | $C$ | **30× – 100×** (typical for WGS) | dimensionless (×) |
| Genome Size (Human) | $G_h$ | **~3.1 × 10⁹ bp** | base pairs |
| Raw Error Rate | $e$ | **0.1 % – 15 %** | percent |
| Q-Score | $Q$ | **Q20 – Q40** | Phred units |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Library of Congress" Analogy for NGS**
>
> Imagine the human genome is a **3.1 billion-letter book** (Library of Congress analogy). **Sanger sequencing** is like reading this book one short sentence at a time using a single flashlight — accurate, but painfully slow (took the Human Genome Project ~13 years and $3 billion).
>
> **NGS** is like throwing the entire book into a paper shredder to produce millions of tiny fragments (~100–500 letters each), photographing **every fragment simultaneously** under a microscope, and then using a powerful supercomputer to assemble the puzzle back into the original book — finishing in **~24 hours** for a few hundred dollars.

This shift is fundamentally a **three-paradigm change**:

1. **Parallelization** — millions of reactions run concurrently (not serially).
2. **Miniaturization** — reactions occur on micron-scale beads or flow cells.
3. **Detection** — fluorescent/optical/pH sensors replace radioactive labels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** You are expected to compare and contrast **at minimum four** NGS platforms: **454 (Roche) Pyrosequencing, Illumina (Solexa), SOLiD (Applied Biosystems), and Ion Torrent (Life Technologies)**. Also understand **Third-Generation** single-molecule platforms: **PacBio SMRT** and **Oxford Nanopore**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Universal NGS Workflow (Seven Logical Steps)

Every NGS platform — regardless of chemistry — funnels DNA through the same conceptual pipeline:

1. **Sample / DNA Extraction** — isolate high-molecular-weight, pure genomic DNA (A₂₆₀/A₂₈₀ ≈ 1.8).
2. **Library Preparation** — fragment DNA (enzymatic, acoustic, or hydrodynamic), end-repair, dA-tailing, and ligate platform-specific **adapters**.
3. **Size Selection** — gel or bead-based selection of fragments in the desired length window (e.g., 200–500 bp for Illumina).
4. **Clonal Amplification** *(optional, second-gen only)* — bridge amplification (Illumina) or emulsion PCR (454, Ion Torrent, SOLiD).
5. **Sequencing Reaction** — cyclic incorporation of nucleotides, detected via light (Illumina, SOLiD, 454) or pH change (Ion Torrent).
6. **Imaging / Signal Detection** — CCD camera (optical) or sensor array (semiconductor / nanopore).
7. **Base-Calling & Quality Scoring** — software translates raw signals into FASTQ files (nucleotide + Phred quality).

## 2.2 Platform-by-Platform Deep Dive

### 2.2.1 Roche 454 Pyrosequencing (GS FLX / GS Junior)
- **Chemistry:** "Sequencing by synthesis" via **luciferase-driven bioluminescence**.
- **Steps:**
  - DNA fragments ligated to **Bead-Library Adapters** are captured on **streptavidin-coated beads** (one fragment per bead) inside water-in-oil **microreactors (emulsion PCR)**.
  - Beads are deposited into **PicoTiterPlate (PTP)** wells — millions of wells, each holding exactly one bead.
  - Sequential flow of **dATP, dTTP, dGTP, dCTP** (one at a time, in a fixed order) over the plate.
  - When a complementary nucleotide is incorporated by **DNA polymerase**, **pyrophosphate (PPi)** is released.
  - PPi is converted to ATP by **ATP-sulfurylase**; ATP drives **luciferase** to oxidize luciferin → **light**.
  - Light intensity is proportional to the **number of bases incorporated** (homopolymer quantification).
- **Read Length:** ~400–700 bp (one of the longest of 2nd gen).
- **Fatal Flaw:** **homopolymer errors** — cannot distinguish 5 A's from 6 A's in a run.
- **Status:** **Discontinued by Roche in 2013/2016**.

### 2.2.2 Illumina (Solexa) Sequencing — Current Market Leader
- **Chemistry:** **Reversible terminator / Sequencing-By-Synthesis (SBS)** with **4-channel fluorescent labels**.
- **Steps:**
  - Library fragments bind to a **flow cell** coated with **oligonucleotides complementary to adapters**.
  - **Bridge Amplification (cluster generation):** fragments fold over, hybridize to adjacent oligos, polymerase extends — forming **"bridges"**. Repeated denaturation/extension cycles create **clusters of ~1,000 identical copies** (clonal cluster).
  - Sequencing primer hybridizes; **DNA polymerase + 4 fluorescently labeled reversible terminator dNTPs** are added.
  - Only **one base** is incorporated per cycle (terminator blocks further extension), then imaged with **laser excitation**.
  - **Cleaving agent** removes both the terminator and the fluorophore → cycle repeats.
- **Read Length:** 2 × 35 bp (MiSeq v2 nano) up to **2 × 300 bp** (NovaSeq 6000 v1.5), and emerging **2 × 600 bp**.
- **Error Profile:** substitution errors (very low, ~0.1 %), **indel errors increase toward read ends**.
- **Strengths:** highest throughput, lowest per-base cost, best raw accuracy.

### 2.2.3 SOLiD (Sequencing by Oligonucleotide Ligation and Detection)
- **Chemistry:** **Sequencing by Ligation** using **di-base probes with fluorescent labels**.
- **Steps:**
  - Emulsion PCR on bead-attached fragments (similar to 454).
  - Beads deposited onto glass slide.
  - **Primer** (n) hybridizes → **octamer probe (8-mer)** with fluorescent label at positions 4 and 5 ligates.
  - Fluorescence read → record dinucleotide identity at probe positions 1–2 (offset).
  - Cleave off probe's last 3 bases → repeat ligation 7–10 times ("rolling circle" style).
  - Reset with **primer (n−1)**, shifted by 1 base → **5 primer-offset rounds** cover every base twice.
- **Read Length:** 50 + 35 bp (paired-end).
- **Strengths:** **"Two-base encoding"** — every base interrogated twice → exceptional accuracy (~99.94 % raw).
