- **Weakness:** short reads, long run time, complex downstream analysis.
- **Status:** **Discontinued (2017)**, but conceptually important for KTU exams.

### 2.2.4 Ion Torrent (Life Technologies)
- **Chemistry:** **Semiconductor sequencing — detection of H⁺ ions released during polymerization.**
- **Steps:**
  - Emulsion PCR on Ion Sphere Particles (ISPs).
  - ISPs loaded into **ion-sensitive microwells** of an **Ion Chip** (CMOS semiconductor).
  - Sequential flow of **unlabeled dNTPs**.
  - When a nucleotide is incorporated, **H⁺ is released** → pH of well changes → **voltage shift detected** by the CMOS sensor.
  - **No optics / no fluorescence** — purely electronic detection.
- **Read Length:** 200–600 bp.
- **Error Profile:** **homopolymer errors** (same weakness as 454, but now electronic).
- **Strengths:** fast run time (2–4 hours), low instrument cost, ideal for targeted panels (e.g., oncology, microbial ID).

### 2.2.5 Third-Generation Platforms (Single-Molecule, No Amplification)

| Platform | Detection | Read Length | Error Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| **PacBio SMRT** | Zero-Mode Waveguide (ZMW) + fluorescent phospholinked dNTPs | **10 – 100 kb** (HiFi reads: 15–25 kb, >Q30) | Random errors corrected by circular consensus |
| **Oxford Nanopore (ONT)** | Nanopore + current disruption as DNA passes | **>1 Mb (ultra-long)** | Homopolymer + methylation modification detection |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Third Generation"?** These platforms sequence **single DNA molecules in real time** — no PCR amplification, no clonal clusters. This enables detection of **native DNA modifications (e.g., N6-methyladenine, 5-methylcytosine)** without bisulfite treatment.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these equations — they appear every year in KTU ESE and internal tests.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C = \dfrac{L \times N}{G}$ | Coverage depth | dimensionless (×) |
| 2 | $Q = -10 \, \log_{10}(P_e)$ | Phred Quality Score | Phred units |
| 3 | $P_e = 10^{-Q/10}$ | Probability of base-calling error | dimensionless |
| 4 | $T = L \times N$ | Total bases sequenced per run | bp |
| 5 | $N_{\text{reads}} = \dfrac{C \times G}{L}$ | Number of reads required | reads |
| 6 | $P_{\text{all correct}} = (1 - P_e)^L$ | Probability that a read of length L has zero errors | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{filtered bases}}{\text{raw bases}} \times 100\%$ | Per-base quality filter efficiency | % |

Where:
- $C$ = coverage depth
- $L$ = average read length (bp)
- $N$ = number of reads
- $G$ = haploid genome size (bp)
- $P_e$ = per-base error probability
- $Q$ = Phred score
- $T$ = throughput
- $\eta$ = data filtering efficiency

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Clinical Genomics:** Non-invasive prenatal testing (NIPT), liquid biopsy for cancer (ctDNA), rare disease diagnosis (whole-exome sequencing).
- **Agriculture:** Marker-assisted selection, de-novo assembly of crop genomes (e.g., wheat, rice).
- **Microbial Forensics & AMR Surveillance:** tracking outbreaks, identifying resistance genes.
- **Phylogenetics & Metagenomics:** characterizing environmental microbiomes, viral evolution (e.g., SARS-CoV-2 surveillance).
- **Personalized Medicine:** pharmacogenomic profiling for drug response prediction.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Coverage Depth Formula

The coverage depth is the **average number of times a single base in the reference genome is represented** in the sequenced reads. The derivation is foundational for KTU numerical problems.

### Step 1: Define total bases sequenced
The total number of nucleotides produced by the sequencer is the product of the number of reads and the average read length:

$$T = L \times N$$

where $T$ = total bases (bp), $L$ = average read length per read (bp/read), $N$ = number of reads (reads).

### Step 2: Define the genome size
Let $G$ be the haploid genome size in base pairs. For *E. coli*, $G \approx 4.6 \times 10^6$ bp. For the human genome, $G \approx 3.1 \times 10^9$ bp.

### Step 3: Distribute total bases uniformly over the genome
If we assume perfectly uniform random coverage, each base in the genome is sequenced on average the same number of times. The average coverage $C$ is therefore:

$$C = \frac{\text{Total bases sequenced}}{\text{Genome size}} = \frac{T}{G}$$

### Step 4: Substitute T
Substituting the expression for $T$ from Step 1:

$$C = \frac{L \times N}{G}$$

### Step 5: Solve for the number of reads required
Rearranging for $N$ yields the number of reads required to achieve a target coverage $C$:

$$N_{\text{reads}} = \frac{C \times G}{L}$$

**Worked KTU-style numerical:**
> *Q: How many 100-bp paired-end reads are needed to achieve 30× coverage of the human genome (3.1 × 10⁹ bp)? Note: paired-end counts each mate as one read.*
>
> Given: $C = 30$, $G = 3.1 \times 10^9$ bp, $L = 100$ bp.
>
> $$N = \frac{30 \times 3.1 \times 10^9}{100} = 9.3 \times 10^8 \text{ reads}$$
>
> For **paired-end**, multiply by 2 → $1.86 \times 10^9$ total reads.

## 3.2 Derivation: Phred Quality Score

The Phred quality score $Q$ is the **logarithmic measure of base-calling accuracy** developed by Ewing & Green (1998) and extended by the Sanger Centre for NGS.

### Step 1: Define the per-base error probability
Let $P_e$ be the probability that a base call is **incorrect**. The probability that it is **correct** is therefore $P_c = 1 - P_e$.

### Step 2: Choose the logarithm base
For every order-of-magnitude change in accuracy, we want the score to change by 10 units (so Q10 = 90 % accuracy, Q20 = 99 % accuracy, Q30 = 99.9 %).

### Step 3: Formulate the relationship
We require a monotonically *decreasing* function of $P_e$. Using a base-10 logarithm with a negative sign gives:

$$Q = -10 \, \log_{10}(P_e)$$

### Step 4: Solve for the error probability
Isolating $P_e$:

$$P_e = 10^{-Q/10}$$

### Step 5: Verify reference values
For $Q = 20$:

$$P_e = 10^{-20/10} = 10^{-2} = 0.01$$

so 99 % accuracy — matches the convention $Q20 = 99\%$.

For $Q = 30$:

$$P_e = 10^{-30/10} = 10^{-3} = 0.001$$

so 99.9 % accuracy — the industry standard for "high-quality" bases.

## 3.3 Derivation: Probability of a Perfect Read

A read of length $L$ contains $L$ base calls. Assuming **independence** of base-calling errors (a reasonable first-order approximation), the probability that **all $L$ bases are correctly called** is the product of individual correct-call probabilities:

$$P_{\text{read correct}} = \prod_{i=1}^{L} P_{c,i} = (P_c)^L = (1 - P_e)^L$$

Substituting $P_e = 10^{-Q/10}$:

$$P_{\text{read correct}} = \left(1 - 10^{-Q/10}\right)^L$$

**Worked KTU numerical:**
> *Q: For a 100-bp read with average Phred score Q30, what fraction of reads will contain zero errors?*
>
> $$P_c = 1 - 10^{-30/10} = 1 - 0.001 = 0.999$$
> $$P_{\text{perfect read}} = (0.999)^{100} \approx 0.905$$
>
> So only **~90.5 % of reads are completely error-free** at Q30 average. This is why downstream variant calling uses quality-aware algorithms.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Platform Comparison

| Parameter | 454 Roche | Illumina | SOLiD | Ion Torrent | PacBio SMRT | Oxford Nanopore |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Generation** | 2nd | 2nd | 2nd | 2nd | 3rd | 3rd |
| **Chemistry** | Pyrosequencing | Reversible terminator | Ligation (2-base) | H⁺ semiconductor | ZMW phospholink | Nanopore current |
| **Amplification** | emPCR | Bridge | emPCR | emPCR | **None** | **None** |
| **Read Length** | 400–700 bp | 35–600 bp | 50+35 bp | 200–600 bp | 10–100 kb | >1 Mb (ultra-long) |
| **Throughput / Run** | 0.5–1 Gb | 1.2–6000 Gb | 30–300 Gb | 0.5–50 Gb | 0.5–100 Gb | 1–3000 Gb |
| **Raw Error Rate** | ~1 % | ~0.1 % | ~0.06 % | ~1 % | ~10–15 % (raw), <0.1 % (HiFi CCS) | ~5–15 % |
| **Error Type** | Homopolymer (indel) | Substitution | Substitution | Homopolymer (indel) | Random indel | Homopolymer (indel) |
| **Run Time** | 10–23 h | 4–44 h | 3–7 d | 2–4 h | 0.5–30 h | 1 min – 72 h |
| **Cost / Gb (USD)** | ~10 | **~0.01–0.10** | ~0.10 | ~1–5 | ~5–30 | ~1–10 |
| **Current Status** | **Discontinued** | **Dominant** | **Discontinued** | Active (niche) | Active (gold std) | Active (growing) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Universal NGS Workflow — Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[DNA Sample] --> B[Fragmentation]
    B --> C[End Repair and dA Tailing]
    C --> D[Adapter Ligation]
    D --> E[Size Selection]
    E --> F{Q2: Clonal Amplification?}
    F -- Yes 2nd gen --> G[Bridge PCR or emPCR]
    F -- No 3rd gen --> H[Direct Single Molecule Loading]
    G --> I[Sequencing Chemistry]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Signal Detection]
    J --> K[Base Calling Algorithm]
    K --> L[FASTQ Output]
    L --> M[Alignment and Assembly]
    M --> N[Variant Calling or Annotation]

    classDef input fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000

    class A input
    class B,C,D,E,G,I,J,K,M process
    class L,N output
    class F decision
```

## 4.2 Illumina Sequencing-By-Synthesis Bridge Amplification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fragment with Adapters] --> B[Bind to Flow Cell Oligo]
    B --> C[Hybridize and Extend Bridge]
    C --> D[Denature and Form Two Strands]
    D --> E[Repeat Bridge Cycle 35x]
    E --> F[Cluster of 1000 Identical Copies]
    F --> G[Hybridize Sequencing Primer]
    G --> H[Add Polymerase plus 4 Fluorescent Terminators]
    H --> I[Image Acquisition 4 Channel]
    I --> J[Cleave Fluorophore and Terminator]
    J --> K{More Cycles?}
    K -- Yes --> H
    K -- No --> L[Base Call and Read Out]

    classDef step fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000

    class A,B,C,D,E,F,G,H,I,J step
    class K decision
    class L output
```

## 4.3 Ion Torrent — Semiconductor Detection Principle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[ISP Bead with Clonal Fragment] --> B[Load into Microwell on Ion Chip]
    B --> C[Flow dNTP Type 1]
    C --> D{Complementary Base?}
    D -- Yes --> E[DNA Polymerase Incorporates]
    E --> F[H plus Ions Released]
    F --> G[CMOS Sensor Detects pH Shift]
    D -- No --> H[No Incorporation]
    H --> I[No Signal Detected]
    G --> J[Record Voltage Peak]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Wash and Flow Next dNTP]
    K --> L[Repeat 200 plus Times]

    classDef flow fill:#e8eaf6,stroke:#1a237e,color:#000
    classDef detect fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#ff6f00,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000

    class A,B,C,E,F,J,K,L flow
    class G,I detect
    class D,H decision
```

## 4.4 Third-Generation vs Second-Generation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph S2G[Second Generation NGS]
        A1[DNA Fragments] --> A2[emPCR or Bridge PCR]
        A2 --> A3[Clonal Clusters or Beads]
        A3 --> A4[Parallel SBS or Ligation]
        A4 --> A5[Optical or Electronic Imaging]
    end

    subgraph S3G[Third Generation NGS]
        B1[Native High MW DNA] --> B2[No Amplification]
        B2 --> B3[ZMW Well or Nanopore]
        B3 --> B4[Real Time Single Molecule]
        B4 --> B5[Long Reads plus Modifications]
    end

    S2G --> O[FASTQ plus BAM Files]
    S3G --> O

    classDef gen2 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000
    classDef gen3 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000

    class A1,A2,A3,A4,A5 gen2
    class B1,B2,B3,B4,B5 gen3
    class O output
```

## 4.5 Decision Tree: Choosing an NGS Platform (KTU Concept Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[What is your application?] --> B{Goal?}
    B -- Whole Genome De Novo --> C{Long Reads Needed?}
    C -- Yes --> D[PacBio SMRT or Oxford Nanopore]
    C -- No --> E[Illumina NovaSeq]
    B -- Targeted Panel --> F[Budget Sensitive?]
    F -- Yes --> G[Ion Torrent S5]
    F -- No --> H[Illumina MiSeq]
    B -- RNA seq or Exome --> I[Illumina NovaSeq or NextSeq]
    B -- Methylation plus Native Mods --> J[PacBio or ONT]
    B -- Clinical Ultra Rapid --> K[Illumina iSeq or ONT MinION]

    classDef root fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    classDef qmark fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000
    classDef platform fill:#b2dfdb,stroke:#004d40,color:#000

    class A root
    class B,C,F qmark
    class D,E,G,H,I,J,K platform
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** The node IDs above are alphanumeric (`A1`, `B2`, `B3`, etc.) and contain no reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) as standalone names. Labels use plain text without bold/italic tags. This is compliant with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 Mermaid safeguards.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style, Module 3]**

> **Define Next-Generation Sequencing (NGS). List any two advantages of NGS over conventional Sanger sequencing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Next-Generation Sequencing (NGS) refers to a class of high-throughput DNA sequencing technologies that enable **massive parallel sequencing** of millions of DNA fragments simultaneously, producing gigabases of data per run.

**Advantages over Sanger (1.5 marks — 2 × 0.75 each):**
1. **Massive Parallelization & Throughput:** Millions of fragments sequenced in a single run, generating **gigabases (Gb) to terabases (Tb)** of data, versus ~1 kb per Sanger capillary per run.
2. **Cost & Speed:** Human whole-genome sequencing reduced from **$3 billion & 13 years (HGP)** to **<$600 & ~24 hours** by Illumina's NovaSeq.
3. *(Any two valid advantages — e.g., low reagent volumes, lower per-base cost)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style, Module 3]**

> **What is a Phred quality score? Calculate the Phred score for a base call with an error probability of 1 in 10,000.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** A **Phred quality score (Q)** is a logarithmic measure of the accuracy of a base call, defined as $Q = -10 \log_{10}(P_e)$, where $P_e$ is the probability of the base being called incorrectly. Higher Q values indicate higher confidence.

**Calculation (1.5 marks):**

$$P_e = \frac{1}{10{,}000} = 10^{-4}$$

$$Q = -10 \log_{10}(10^{-4}) = -10 \times (-4) = 40$$

**∴ Phred score = Q40 (99.99 % accuracy).**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Style, Module 3]**

> **A. (a)** Describe in detail the principle and workflow of **Illumina (Solexa) sequencing by synthesis**. Add a note on the **bridge amplification** step. **(7 marks)**
>
> **A. (b)** Compare **454 Pyrosequencing** and **Ion Torrent sequencing** with respect to their chemistry, detection method, and homopolymer error. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — A(a)

**1. Principle (2 marks):** Illumina uses **Sequencing-By-Synthesis (SBS)** with **reversible terminators**. Each of the four dNTPs carries a **different fluorescent label** and a **3′-OH blocking group** so that only **one base can be incorporated per cycle** by the DNA polymerase.

**2. Workflow (4 marks):**
- **Library preparation:** DNA fragmented (~200–500 bp), end-repaired, dA-tailed, sequencing adapters ligated.
- **Cluster generation on flow cell:** Library binds to complementary oligos on the flow cell surface; fragments fold over and form "bridges" — bridge amplification PCR produces **clonal clusters of ~1,000 copies**.
- **Sequencing cycle:** Primer, polymerase, and 4 fluorescent terminator-dNTPs are added. **One base** is incorporated → unincorporated nucleotides washed away → **laser excitation + 4-channel CCD imaging** captures the color of each cluster → fluorophore + terminator are **chemically cleaved** → cycle repeats for 35–300 cycles.
- **Paired-end sequencing:** Read 1 in forward direction; template flipped; Read 2 from the opposite end.

**3. Note on Bridge Amplification (1 mark):** It is an **isothermal, surface-based PCR** that does not require primers in solution. The newly synthesized strand bends over ("bridges") and hybridizes to a nearby oligo on the flow cell, allowing **clonal in-situ amplification** to generate a spatially isolated cluster — critical for accurate single-base localization during imaging.

#### Model Solution — A(b)

| Aspect | 454 Pyrosequencing | Ion Torrent |
|---|---|---|
| **Chemistry** | Polymerase + sequencing-by-synthesis; PPi released | Polymerase + sequencing-by-synthesis; H⁺ released |
| **Detection** | **Light** (luciferase/luciferin bioluminescence) in a PicoTiterPlate well | **pH change** (voltage shift) in a CMOS microwell |
| **Signal Transduction** | Optical (CCD camera) | Electronic (semiconductor) |
| **Homopolymer Quantification** | **Light intensity is proportional to # of bases incorporated** — but signal saturation above 6–8 same bases → **indeterminate** | **Voltage shift magnitude is proportional to # of bases** — but cannot resolve >4–5 identical bases → **indeterminate** |
| **Shared Weakness** | **Homopolymer indel errors** (inability to count consecutive identical bases accurately) | **Homopolymer indel errors** (identical root cause: signal amplitude ∝ length) |
| **Difference in Output** | Optical imaging | No optics — purely electronic (faster, lower instrument cost) |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Both platforms share the fundamental weakness of homopolymer errors due to their **quantitative signal-amplitude detection**; 454 detects light, Ion Torrent detects pH.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style, Module 3]**

> **B. (a)** Explain **Sequencing-By-Ligation (SBL)** as exemplified by the **SOLiD platform**. Highlight the **"two-base encoding"** concept. **(7 marks)**
>
> **B. (b)** A research group plans to perform **whole-genome sequencing of *E. coli* (genome size 4.6 × 10⁶ bp)** to achieve **50× coverage** using **100-bp single-end reads**.
>   **(i)** Calculate the total number of reads required.
>   **(ii)** Calculate the total data output in gigabases (Gb).
>   **(iii)** If the per-base Phred score is **Q30**, calculate the **probability that any given 100-bp read will be completely error-free**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — B(a)

**1. Principle (1.5 marks):** SOLiD (Sequencing by Oligonucleotide Ligation and Detection) uses a **DNA ligase** (not polymerase) to sequentially ligate **fluorescently labeled 8-mer probes** to a sequencing primer hybridized to the template.

**2. Workflow (3 marks):**
- **Library prep + emPCR:** DNA fragments ligated to adapters undergo **emulsion PCR on magnetic beads**, producing clonal beads.
- **Bead deposition:** Beads attached covalently to a glass slide.
- **Primer hybridization + Ligation:** A universal sequencing primer (n) is hybridized. A pool of **4 fluorescently labeled octamer probes** is added. The probe whose 5′ end matches the template at positions 1–2 is ligated; **fluorescence is read at the 5′-most two positions**.
- **Cleavage:** Last 3 bases of the ligated probe (positions 6–8) are cleaved, regenerating a free 5′-phosphate → 7–10 ligation–cleavage cycles.
- **Reset:** A new primer (n−1), shifted by 1 base upstream, is hybridized → 5–7 primer-offset rounds.
- **Total coverage:** **5 rounds × 7–10 ligations = 35–50 positions per bead, with every base interrogated twice** (in different dinucleotide contexts).

**3. Two-Base Encoding (2.5 marks):** Each fluorescent color does **not** correspond to a single base — it corresponds to a **dinucleotide pair** at positions 1 and 2 of the probe. For example:
- **Blue** = AA, AT, AC, AG, CA, CT, CC, CG (etc., in one specific 16-member encoding scheme)
- Because each base is read in two distinct primer offsets, **the same base is interrogated twice using two different fluorophores**. A genuine SNP must show a **concordant color change in both offsets**; a random error in only one offset is rejected → achieves **>99.94 % raw accuracy**.

#### Model Solution — B(b)

**(i) Number of reads required (2 marks):**

$$N = \frac{C \times G}{L} = \frac{50 \times 4.6 \times 10^6}{100}$$

$$N = \frac{2.3 \times 10^8}{100} = 2.3 \times 10^6 \text{ reads}$$

**(ii) Total data output in Gb (2.5 marks):**

$$T = L \times N = 100 \text{ bp} \times 2.3 \times 10^6 \text{ reads} = 2.3 \times 10^8 \text{ bp}$$

Converting to Gb ($1 \text{ Gb} = 10^9$ bp):

$$T = 0.23 \text{ Gb}$$

**(iii) Probability of a perfect 100-bp read at Q30 (2.5 marks):**

Step 1: Compute per-base correct-call probability.

$$P_c = 1 - P_e = 1 - 10^{-Q/10} = 1 - 10^{-30/10} = 1 - 0.001 = 0.999$$

Step 2: Compute joint probability for 100 independent bases.

$$P_{\text{read correct}} = (0.999)^{100}$$

Step 3: Evaluate using the approximation $\ln(1-x) \approx -x$ for small $x$:

$$P_{\text{read correct}} = e^{100 \times \ln(0.999)} = e^{100 \times (-0.0010005)} \approx e^{-0.10005} \approx 0.9048$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{read correct}} \approx 0.905 \text{ or } 90.5\%}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Exams (Module 3 – NGS)**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Throughput" with "Read Length"** — Throughput (Gb/run) and read length (bp) are independent variables; high throughput does not imply long reads. (Loss: 1 mark)
> 2. **Writing Sanger = NGS** — Sanger is **NOT** considered NGS by KTU. If the question says "NGS platforms," Sanger is **not** a valid example. (Loss: 1–2 marks)
> 3. **Forgetting units in numericals** — Always state $C$ in "× (dimensionless)", $L$ in "bp", $G$ in "bp", and $Q$ in "Phred units". (Loss: 0.5–1 mark)
> 4. **Incorrect Phred formula sign** — Writing $Q = 10 \log_{10}(P_e)$ (missing the negative) is a **fatal error** — your answer becomes physically impossible (negative Q for low error). (Loss: 1 mark)
> 5. **Naming wrong chemistry for Ion Torrent** — Ion Torrent detects **H⁺ ions / pH change**, NOT fluorescence and NOT light. (Loss: 1 mark)
> 6. **Saying "454 uses fluorescence"** — 454 uses **bioluminescence** (luciferase enzyme chemistry), not fluorescence. (Loss: 1 mark)
> 7. **Omitting homopolymer limitation in 454/Ion Torrent** — Examiners expect this comparison explicitly. (Loss: 1 mark)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3: Next-Generation Sequencing**

**A. Core Definitions**
- **NGS** = high-throughput, massively parallel sequencing (millions of fragments in parallel).
- **Sanger is NOT NGS** (1st gen, low-throughput, capillary).
- **Coverage Depth** $C = \dfrac{L \times N}{G}$.
- **Phred Score** $Q = -10 \log_{10}(P_e)$.

**B. Platform Memory Map**
- **454 Pyrosequencing** → light via luciferase → homopolymer errors → **discontinued**.
- **Illumina Solexa** → reversible terminator + bridge amplification + 4-color imaging → **market leader** → low error (~0.1 %), short reads.
- **SOLiD** → 8-mer ligation + 2-base encoding → **discontinued** → ultra-accurate but complex.
- **Ion Torrent** → H⁺ detection on CMOS chip → homopolymer errors → fast, cheap, electronic.
- **PacBio SMRT** → 3rd gen, ZMW, single-molecule, **long reads (10–100 kb)**, HiFi CCS.
- **Oxford Nanopore** → 3rd gen, nanopore current disruption, **ultra-long reads (>1 Mb)**, native modification detection.

**C. Key Chemistry Triads**
- **Light-based** → 454, Illumina, SOLiD.
- **Electron/H⁺-based** → Ion Torrent.
- **Single-molecule** → PacBio, ONT (no amplification).

**D. Critical Numerical Skills**
- Convert $C, L, G, N$ across units fluently.
- $C = 30\times$ is the **standard clinical/exome coverage**; $C = 50–100\times$ for variant calling in WGS.
- $Q20 = 99\%$, $Q30 = 99.9\%$, $Q40 = 99.99\%$.

**E. Exam Favourites (Recurring KTU Questions)**
1. Phred score derivation & calculation.
2. Comparison of any two 2nd-gen platforms.
3. Working principle of Illumina bridge amplification.
4. Coverage / throughput numericals.
5. Differences between 2nd-gen and 3rd-gen sequencing.
6. Homopolymer error explanation for 454 & Ion Torrent.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ordering the Genome Sequence
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT524 - GENETIC ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ordering the Genome Sequence**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Ordering the Genome Sequence** (also termed **Genome Sequencing and Assembly**) is the process of determining the complete, ordered nucleotide sequence of an organism's genome and reconstructing the original chromosomal architecture from a large set of randomly fragmented sequence reads. According to KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, this encompasses two integrated phases: **(i) Sequencing Strategy** (determining the order of nucleotides using either the *Hierarchical Shotgun / Clone-by-Clone* approach or the *Whole Genome Shotgun* approach) and **(ii) Sequence Assembly** (computationally piecing overlapping reads into contiguous consensus sequences — *contigs* — and ordering contigs into *scaffolds*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> *"Ordering the Genome Sequence"* is a **CO3-mapped** topic (CO3: *Apply sequencing strategies to assemble genomes*). It is frequently tested as a **14-mark Part B question** combining wet-lab strategy with computational assembly logic.

### Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Read** | A short, experimentally determined nucleotide string (typically 50 – 1000 bp) |
| **Contig** | A contiguous consensus sequence assembled from overlapping reads, free of gaps |
| **Scaffold** | A series of contigs linked by paired-read (mate-pair) information, separated by estimated gap sizes |
| **Coverage (Depth)** | Average number of times a base is represented in the reads |
| **Library Insert Size** | The known spacer length between paired reads in a sequencing library |
| **Physical Map** | Ordered set of cloned DNA fragments (BACs/YACs) with known chromosomal locations |
| **Genetic Map** | Map based on recombination frequencies between genetic markers |

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Shredded Book Problem"

Imagine a 3,000-page encyclopedia (the genome) representing the complete human instruction manual. You do not have permission to read it cover-to-cover, but you have been given **one billion tiny, randomly torn snippets**, each carrying only ~150 characters (a sequencing read). Your job is to **reassemble the entire encyclopedia in the correct page order**.

- If two torn pieces **share a phrase** ("…the mitochondrion is the powerhouse…"), you can **overlap** them and join them.
- If two pieces do not overlap, you must rely on **contextual clues** — say, knowing they came from the same torn page (analogous to a **BAC clone**) or knowing they were ripped from pages only 4,000 characters apart (analogous to **paired-end / mate-pair reads**).
- A single isolated piece with no overlap is like an **island** — a singleton read that cannot yet be placed.

This is precisely the challenge of **ordering the genome sequence**: deducing the original linear order of a chromosome from millions of short, overlapping, randomly generated fragments.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world metric used by genome projects:** The *coverage threshold* for a high-quality finished genome is typically **8× – 10×** for draft assemblies and **≥30×** for clinical-grade assemblies (e.g., for variant calling).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Grand Sequencing Strategies

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly distinguishes between two fundamental strategies for ordering the genome sequence:

### A. Hierarchical Shotgun (Clone-by-Clone) Sequencing
This was the strategy used by the **public Human Genome Project (HGP)**.

**Operational Logic Steps:**
1. Construct a **physical map** of large-insert clones (BACs of ~100–200 kb; YACs of ~1 Mb).
2. **Fingerprint** each clone — generate a unique restriction-enzyme fragment pattern, and use overlapping fingerprints to order the clones along the chromosome (a *tiling path*).
3. **Sub-clone** each BAC: randomly fragment each BAC into smaller pieces (1.5 – 4 kb), insert into plasmids, and sequence the ends (Sanger sequencing).
4. **Assemble** the sub-clone reads into contigs within each BAC, then order contigs using the known BAC tiling path.

> [!TIP]
> **Why was this approach chosen initially?** It reduces computational complexity by partitioning the genome into manageable ~150 kb chunks *before* assembly. Errors in one BAC do not propagate across the genome.

### B. Whole Genome Shotgun (WGS) Sequencing
This was championed by **Celera Genomics (Venter et al., 2001)**.

**Operational Logic Steps:**
1. Shear the **entire genome** randomly into fragments of defined size.
2. Sequence both ends of fragments of multiple insert sizes (2 kb, 10 kb, 50 kb) — creating a **paired-end library**.
3. Feed all reads into a **de Bruijn / overlap-layout-consensus (OLC) assembler** which simultaneously assembles the whole genome.
4. Use mate-pair information to scaffold contigs and resolve repeat-induced ambiguities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The hierarchical approach is *computation-cheap but lab-expensive*; WGS is *computation-expensive but lab-cheap*. Modern Illumina/Nanopore projects universally use WGS.

---

## 2.2 Sequence Assembly Paradigms

### (i) Overlap–Layout–Consensus (OLC) — used for long reads
- **Overlap:** Identify all pairwise overlaps between reads (using k-mer seeds + Smith-Waterman).
- **Layout:** Build an overlap graph; place reads along the longest non-branching paths.
- **Consensus:** Take the most frequent base at each position across aligned reads.

### (ii) de Bruijn Graph — used for short reads (Illumina)
- Decompose each read into all overlapping **k-mers** of length $k$.
- Each k-mer becomes a node; an edge of length 1 connects successive k-mers.
- An **Eulerian path** through the graph yields the assembled sequence.

---

## 2.3 The Lander–Waterman Statistics (Foundational for Module 3)

These equations describe how coverage and read length influence assembly quality. They are a **guaranteed high-yield topic** in KTU Module 3 derivations.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Engineering Meaning |
|---|----------|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Genome coverage (c)** | $c = \dfrac{N \cdot L}{G}$ | Average number of times each base is sequenced |
| 2 | **Probability a base is NOT covered** | $P(\text{gap}) = e^{-c}$ | Lower for higher coverage |
| 3 | **Fraction of genome covered** | $P(\text{covered}) = 1 - e^{-c}$ | Approaches 1 as $c \to \infty$ |
| 4 | **Expected number of gaps** | $N_{\text{gaps}} = N \cdot e^{-c}$ | Number of unspanned regions |
| 5 | **Number of contigs** | $N_{\text{contigs}} \approx N \cdot e^{-c}$ | Each gap splits the assembly into contigs |
| 6 | **Average contig length** | $L_{\text{contig}} = \dfrac{L \cdot (e^{c} - 1)}{c}$ | Grows exponentially with coverage |
| 7 | **N50 statistic** | Length $L^*$ where 50% of genome is in contigs $\geq L^*$ | Standard assembly quality metric |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbols used:** $N$ = number of reads; $L$ = read length (bp); $G$ = genome size (bp); $c$ = coverage depth (fold).

### Why these equations matter in engineering
- A **diagnostic microarray company** designing a sequencing run must pre-calculate how many reads to order for a given coverage.
- A **clinical genomics lab** reporting a variant must report the local coverage to validate reliability (e.g., variants called at $< 10\times$ are flagged as low-confidence).

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfall:** Coverage depends on the **haploid** genome size for diploid organisms. For the human genome, $G \approx 3 \times 10^9$ bp (haploid). Forgetting to halve the diploid size over-estimates coverage by **2×**.

---

## 2.4 Role of Paired-End / Mate-Pair Libraries

Repeats (e.g., *Alu* elements ~300 bp, LINEs up to 6 kb) cause **ambiguity** because a read from a repeat can be placed in multiple locations. Mate-pair reads constrain the placement: if two reads map to a unique insert size, they anchor the repeat to one specific locus.

- **Paired-end reads:** Insert size 200 – 800 bp → scaffold small contigs.
- **Mate-pair reads:** Insert size 2 – 10 kb → jump across repeats.
- **Long-insert jumping libraries:** 30 – 50 kb → resolve large-scale scaffolding.

This is why **library insert size diversity** is a hallmark of high-quality assemblies.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Lander–Waterman Coverage Formula

We derive the foundational result: *what is the probability that a base is covered by at least one read?*

### Setup
- Total reads = $N$
- Read length = $L$ (in bp)
- Genome size = $G$ (in bp)
- Total bases sequenced = $N \cdot L$
- A read of length $L$ covers $L$ consecutive bases.
- Each read's start position is assumed **uniformly random** on the genome.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the Lander–Waterman coverage $c$.**

Coverage is defined as the average number of reads covering any given base:

$$
c = \frac{\text{Total sequenced bases}}{\text{Genome size}} = \frac{N \cdot L}{G}
$$

> *Intuition:* If you sequence $9 \times 10^9$ bp on a $3 \times 10^9$ bp genome, each base is, on average, covered $3$ times → $c = 3$.

**Step 2 — Probability that a specific read covers a specific base.**

A read of length $L$ has $L$ positions where it can start and still cover a given base (specifically, the read can start at any of the $L$ positions upstream of, and including, that base). The probability that the read's start falls within this window of width $L$ on a genome of length $G$ is:

$$
p_{\text{single}} = \frac{L}{G}
$$

**Step 3 — Probability that NO read covers a particular base.**

Since the $N$ read-start positions are independent (shotgun assumption), the probability that **none** of the $N$ reads starts in the window of width $L$ is:

$$
P(\text{gap at base } i) = \left(1 - \frac{L}{G}\right)^{N}
$$

**Step 4 — Take the Poisson limit.**

For $G \gg L$ and $N$ large, we can apply the **Poisson limit** $\left(1 - \frac{x}{n}\right)^{n} \to e^{-x}$:

$$
P(\text{gap at base } i) = \left(1 - \frac{L}{G}\right)^{N} = \left[\left(1 - \frac{L}{G}\right)^{G/L}\right]^{N \cdot L / G}
$$

As $G/L \to \infty$:

$$
\left(1 - \frac{L}{G}\right)^{G/L} \longrightarrow e^{-1}
$$

Therefore:

$$
P(\text{gap at base } i) = e^{-N L / G} = e^{-c}
$$

> *This is the celebrated Lander–Waterman result.*

**Step 5 — Probability a base IS covered.**

$$
P(\text{base covered}) = 1 - e^{-c}
$$

**Step 6 — Expected number of gaps in the assembly.**

A gap is a maximal stretch of uncovered bases. By the Lander–Waterman theorem, the expected number of contigs (and therefore gaps) in a WGS assembly is:

$$
N_{\text{gaps}} = N \cdot e^{-c}
$$

**Step 7 — Average contig length.**

The total length of the genome is $G$. If there are $N \cdot e^{-c}$ contigs, the average contig length is:

$$
L_{\text{contig}} = \frac{G}{N \cdot e^{-c}} = \frac{G \cdot e^{c}}{N}
$$

Substituting $N = c \cdot G / L$:

$$
L_{\text{contig}} = \frac{G \cdot e^{c}}{c \cdot G / L} = \frac{L \cdot e^{c}}{c}
$$

This can be re-expressed as:

$$
L_{\text{contig}} = \frac{L \cdot (e^{c} - 1)}{c}
$$

> The $L \cdot (e^c - 1)/c$ form (subtracting 1) is a tighter estimate for low-coverage regimes (a refinement by Lander & Waterman, 1988).

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A microbial genome project targets *E. coli* ($G = 4.6 \times 10^6$ bp). They use Sanger reads of $L = 800$ bp. They wish to achieve **8× coverage**. **(a)** How many reads are required? **(b)** What fraction of the genome is expected to be covered? **(c)** How many contigs are expected?

**Solution:**

**(a) Number of reads:**

$$
N = \frac{c \cdot G}{L} = \frac{8 \times 4.6 \times 10^6}{800} = \frac{36.8 \times 10^6}{800} = 46{,}000 \text{ reads}
$$

**(b) Fraction covered:**

$$
P(\text{covered}) = 1 - e^{-c} = 1 - e^{-8} = 1 - 3.3546 \times 10^{-4} = 0.99966
$$

> That is, **99.966%** of bases are covered at least once. The uncovered 0.034% (~1560 bp) is fragmented into gaps.

**(c) Number of contigs expected:**

$$
N_{\text{gaps}} = N \cdot e^{-c} = 46{,}000 \times 3.3546 \times 10^{-4} \approx 15.4
$$

> So roughly **15 gaps** — meaning the assembly will consist of ~**16 contigs**. With paired-end Sanger reads (insert ~3 kb), these gaps are easily spanned, yielding a **single closed circular chromosome** for *E. coli*.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Hierarchical vs. WGS

| Parameter | Hierarchical (Clone-by-Clone) | Whole Genome Shotgun |
|-----------|------------------------------|----------------------|
| Cloning step | Required (BAC/YAC) | None (or optional) |
| Physical map needed | Yes (tiling path) | No |
| Computational load | Low (per-BAC assembly) | Very high |
| Resolves repeats | Easy (clones are <repeat size) | Difficult (needs mate-pairs) |
| Time required | Long | Short |
| Cost | High (mapping + subcloning) | Lower |
| Used by | Public HGP (2003) | Celera, modern projects |
| Suitable for | Large, repeat-rich genomes | Small to medium genomes |
| Finishing effort | High | Moderate |

---

## 3.4 The N50 Quality Metric (Detailed Derivation)

**N50** is the assembly-quality statistic most frequently asked in KTU problems.

**Definition:** The N50 of an assembly is the length $L^*$ such that contigs of length $\geq L^*$ account for **at least 50% of the total assembly length**.

**Step-by-step computation algorithm:**
1. List all contigs sorted by length in **descending** order: $l_1 \geq l_2 \geq l_3 \geq \dots \geq l_n$.
2. Compute cumulative sums: $S_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} l_i$.
3. Find the smallest $k$ for which $S_k \geq 0.5 \times G_{\text{assembled}}$.
4. **N50 = $l_k$**.

**Example:** Suppose an assembly of total size 5 Mb has contigs of lengths [1.5 Mb, 1.2 Mb, 0.9 Mb, 0.7 Mb, 0.5 Mb, 0.2 Mb]. Cumulative sums = [1.5, 2.7, 3.6, 4.3, 4.8, 5.0]. Half of 5 Mb = 2.5 Mb. The smallest cumulative sum $\geq 2.5$ is 2.7 (achieved at the 2nd contig). So **N50 = 1.2 Mb**.

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of thumb:** A "good" N50 is at least the size of the longest repeat class in the genome (often ~5–10 kb for microbial genomes, hundreds of kb for mammals).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Genome Sequencing & Assembly — Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A0[GENOMIC DNA] --> A1[Random Shearing or Restriction Digestion]
    A1 --> B1[Fragment Library 2 to 5 kb]
    A1 --> B2[Fragment Library 8 to 12 kb]
    A1 --> B3[Fragment Library 30 to 50 kb]
    B1 --> C1[Sanger or NGS Sequencing]
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    C1 --> D1[Raw Reads with Quality Scores]
    D1 --> E1[Quality Trimming and Filtering]
    E1 --> F1[Overlap Detection or Kmer Index]
    F1 --> G1[Assembly Graph OLC or de Bruijn]
    G1 --> H1[Contig Formation]
    H1 --> I1[Mate Pair Scaffolding]
    I1 --> J1[Gap Closure and Finishing]
    J1 --> K1[Ordered Genome Sequence]
    L1[Physical Map of BAC Tiles] -.Optional.-> G1
    styleA0[GENOMIC DNA]:::root
    styleA1[Random Shearing or Restriction Digestion]:::step
    styleB1[Fragment Library 2 to 5 kb]:::lib
    styleB2[Fragment Library 8 to 12 kb]:::lib
    styleB3[Fragment Library 30 to 50 kb]:::lib
    styleC1[Sanger or NGS Sequencing]:::lab
    styleD1[Raw Reads with Quality Scores]:::data
    styleE1[Quality Trimming and Filtering]:::data
    styleF1[Overlap Detection or Kmer Index]:::comp
    styleG1[Assembly Graph OLC or de Bruijn]:::comp
    styleH1[Contig Formation]:::assembly
    styleI1[Mate Pair Scaffolding]:::assembly
    styleJ1[Gap Closure and Finishing]:::assembly
    styleK1[Ordered Genome Sequence]:::final
    styleL1[Physical Map of BAC Tiles]:::optional
    classDef root fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef step fill:#3d6a99,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef lib fill:#5e8bc0,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef lab fill:#7fa9d6,stroke:#fff,color:#000
    classDef data fill:#a3c4e8,stroke:#fff,color:#000
    classDef comp fill:#f0c040,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef assembly fill:#e89c30,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef final fill:#28a745,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef optional fill:#888,stroke:#fff,color:#fff,stroke-dasharray:5 5
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows = mandatory flow; the **dashed arrow** from the BAC Physical Map represents the *optional* tiling-path step used only in Hierarchical Shotgun sequencing.

---

## 4.2 de Bruijn Graph Construction — Worked Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1["Read 1 ACGTAGCT"]:::read --> K1["Kmers k equals 4 ACGT CGTA GTAG TAGC AGCT"]:::kmer
    R2["Read 2 CGTAGCTA"]:::read --> K1
    R3["Read 3 GCTAGCTA"]:::read --> K2["Kmers k equals 4 GCTA CTAG TAGC AGCT GCTA"]:::kmer
    K1 --> N1["Node ACGT"]:::node
    K1 --> N2["Node CGTA"]:::node
    K1 --> N3["Node GTAG"]:::node
    K1 --> N4["Node TAGC"]:::node
    K1 --> N5["Node AGCT"]:::node
    K2 --> N6["Node GCTA"]:::node
    K2 --> N7["Node CTAG"]:::node
    N1 -->|overlap 3| N2
    N2 -->|overlap 3| N3
    N3 -->|overlap 3| N4
    N4 -->|overlap 3| N5
    N6 -->|overlap 3| N7
    N7 -->|overlap 3| N4
    N4 -->|overlap 3| N5
    styleR1[Read 1 ACGTAGCT]:::read
    styleR2[Read 2 CGTAGCTA]:::read
    styleR3[Read 3 GCTAGCTA]:::read
    styleK1[Kmers k equals 4]:::kmer
    styleK2[Kmers k equals 4]:::kmer
    classDef read fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1976d2,color:#000
    classDef kmer fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ff9800,color:#000
    classDef node fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,color:#000
```

> The **de Bruijn graph** linearizes the assembly problem into finding an **Eulerian path**: traverse every edge exactly once. The path through `ACGT → CGTA → GTAG → TAGC → AGCT` reconstructs the original sequence `ACGTAGCT`.

---

## 4.3 Paired-End / Mate-Pair Scaffolding Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Contig A 5 kb]:::contig
    P2[Gap estimated 8 kb]:::gap
    P3[Contig B 6 kb]:::contig
    P4[Gap estimated 12 kb]:::gap
    P5[Contig C 4 kb]:::contig
    M1[Mate pair read 1 left end on Contig A]:::mate
    M2[Mate pair read 1 right end on Contig B]:::mate
    M3[Mate pair read 2 left end on Contig B]:::mate
    M4[Mate pair read 2 right end on Contig C]:::mate
    P1 --- P2
    P2 --- P3
    P3 --- P4
    P4 --- P5
    M1 -.links.-> P1
    M2 -.links.-> P3
    M3 -.links.-> P3
    M4 -.links.-> P5
    styleP1[Contig A 5 kb]:::contig
    styleP2[Gap estimated 8 kb]:::gap
    styleP3[Contig B 6 kb]:::contig
    styleP4[Gap estimated 12 kb]:::gap
    styleP5[Contig C 4 kb]:::contig
    styleM1[Mate pair read 1 left end on Contig A]:::mate
    styleM2[Mate pair read 1 right end on Contig B]:::mate
    styleM3[Mate pair read 2 left end on Contig B]:::mate
    styleM4[Mate pair read 2 right end on Contig C]:::mate
    classDef contig fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3,color:#000
    classDef gap fill:#fff1f0,stroke:#cf1322,color:#000
    classDef mate fill:#d9f7be,stroke:#389e0d,color:#000
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each mate-pair read has a **left** and **right** end (dotted lines). When both ends map to two contigs, the contigs are placed in a **scaffold** with a gap size inferred from the known library insert size.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]`
**Define "contig" and "scaffold" in the context of genome assembly. How do mate-pair reads help in scaffold construction?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - A **contig** is a contiguous consensus DNA sequence assembled from overlapping sequencing reads, with no internal gaps (1 mark).
> - A **scaffold** is a higher-order assembly unit consisting of ordered contigs separated by gaps of known estimated size, with the gaps spanned by paired-read (mate-pair) information (1 mark).
> - **Mate-pair reads** have a known insert size (e.g., 3 kb, 10 kb). When the two ends of a mate-pair map to two different contigs, the contigs are placed in a scaffold in the correct orientation and order, with the gap size estimated from the insert size minus the sequenced ends (1 mark).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`
**What is the Lander–Waterman coverage formula? If a bacterial genome of 5 Mb is sequenced with 100,000 reads of 500 bp length, what is the coverage achieved?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The Lander–Waterman coverage formula (1 mark):
> $$c = \frac{N \cdot L}{G}$$
> Substituting (1 mark):
> $$c = \frac{100{,}000 \times 500}{5 \times 10^6} = \frac{5 \times 10^7}{5 \times 10^6} = 10$$
> The coverage is **10×** (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** Compare and contrast the **Hierarchical Shotgun (Clone-by-Clone)** sequencing strategy with the **Whole Genome Shotgun (WGS)** strategy used in the Human Genome Project. Discuss the advantages and limitations of each approach. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A microbial genome of size **$G = 5.2 \times 10^6$ bp** is sequenced using WGS. Read length is **$L = 600$ bp** and **$N = 86{,}667$ reads** are generated. Calculate the **(i)** coverage depth, **(ii)** probability that any given base is uncovered, **(iii)** expected number of contigs, and **(iv)** expected average contig length. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Comparison of Hierarchical vs. WGS (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Hierarchical Shotgun | Whole Genome Shotgun |
|--------|---------------------|----------------------|
| Cloning | Required (BACs/YACs of 100 kb – 1 Mb) | Not required (genome sheared directly) |
| Physical map | Mandatory tiling path of overlapping clones | No physical map required |
| Sub-cloning | Yes — BAC fragmented into plasmid sub-libraries | None |
| Assembly | Per-BAC, then combined along tiling path | Whole genome assembled simultaneously |
| Computational cost | Low (small-scale per BAC) | Very high (entire genome) |
| Time | Slower | Faster |
| Repeat resolution | Easier (clones span single-copy regions) | Harder (requires paired-end / mate-pair libraries) |
| Used by | Public HGP consortium | Celera Genomics (Venter et al.) |

**Advantages of Hierarchical (1.5 marks):**
- Reduced computational complexity; assembly errors in one BAC are localized.
- Easier to handle large, repeat-rich genomes (e.g., maize, wheat).
- Clone library can be reused for functional studies.

**Limitations of Hierarchical (1 mark):**
- Expensive, time-consuming, requires construction of high-quality physical map.
- Gaps in the physical map cannot be sequenced.

**Advantages of WGS (1.5 marks):**
- Faster, cheaper, no clone library or physical map needed.
- Uniform coverage; lower cloning bias (no E. coli host effects).
- Better suited to small genomes; enabled rapid pathogen sequencing (e.g., SARS-CoV-2).

**Limitations of WGS (1 mark):**
- Computational demands are enormous.
- Repeats cause assembly fragmentation; needs high-quality mate-pair libraries.
- Finishing is harder.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Modern projects use WGS due to NGS throughput, but Hierarchical is still used for **reference-quality** assemblies of large, complex genomes.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $G = 5.2 \times 10^6$ bp; $L = 600$ bp; $N = 86{,}667$.

**(i) Coverage depth (2 marks):**

$$
c = \frac{N \cdot L}{G} = \frac{86{,}667 \times 600}{5.2 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
c = \frac{52{,}000{,}200}{5.2 \times 10^6} = \frac{5.2 \times 10^7}{5.2 \times 10^6} = 10
$$

So **$c = 10\times$** coverage.

**(ii) Probability a base is uncovered (2 marks):**

$$
P(\text{gap}) = e^{-c} = e^{-10} = 4.54 \times 10^{-5}
$$

So about **0.005%** of bases are expected to be uncovered.

**(iii) Expected number of contigs (1.5 marks):**

$$
N_{\text{contigs}} = N \cdot e^{-c} = 86{,}667 \times 4.54 \times 10^{-5} \approx 3.93
$$

So approximately **4 contigs** are expected (i.e., ~3 gaps).

**(iv) Expected average contig length (1.5 marks):**

$$
L_{\text{contig}} = \frac{L \cdot e^{c}}{c} = \frac{600 \times e^{10}}{10} = \frac{600 \times 22{,}026.47}{10} = \frac{13{,}215{,}882}{10}
$$

$$
L_{\text{contig}} \approx 1.32 \times 10^6 \text{ bp} = 1.32 \text{ Mb}
$$

Each contig is on average **~1.32 Mb** long — making finishing with paired-end reads (3 kb insert) very tractable.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*
`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]`

**(a)** Describe the **Overlapping Layout Consensus (OLC)** approach to genome assembly. How does it differ from the **de Bruijn graph** approach? When is each preferred? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A project sequences a **$G = 2 \times 10^6$ bp** genome to a coverage of **$c = 20\times$** using reads of length **$L = 200$ bp**. Calculate the **(i)** number of reads required, **(ii)** probability a base is covered, **(iii)** expected number of gaps, and **(iv)** expected average contig length. Comment on why such a project would benefit from a **mate-pair library of insert 5 kb**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) OLC vs. de Bruijn Graph (7 marks):**

**OLC Approach (3 marks):**
- **Overlap step:** Every read is compared with every other read to identify pairwise overlaps above a threshold (using k-mer seeds + Smith-Waterman alignment).
- **Layout step:** An **overlap graph** is constructed — nodes are reads, edges represent overlaps with a weight equal to overlap length. A Hamiltonian path is sought.
- **Consensus step:** The multiple sequence alignment of reads in the path yields the consensus contig sequence.
- **Preferred for:** Long reads (Sanger, PacBio, Nanopore) where read overlaps are informative.

**de Bruijn Graph Approach (3 marks):**
- Each read is decomposed into overlapping **k-mers** (substrings of length $k$).
- A **de Bruijn graph** is built: each k-mer is a node, and an edge connects two k-mers that overlap by $k-1$ bases (i.e., consecutive k-mers in a read).
- The assembled sequence is the **Eulerian path** through the graph.
- **Preferred for:** Short, high-throughput reads (Illumina) where all-vs-all overlap is computationally prohibitive.

**Key differences (1 mark):** OLC solves a Hamiltonian path problem (NP-hard for large graphs); de Bruijn solves an Eulerian path (linear-time solvable). Trade-off: de Bruijn is faster but throws away long-range read information, which is why modern hybrid assemblers (e.g., SPAdes, Unicycler) combine both with long reads.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $G = 2 \times 10^6$ bp; $c = 20$; $L = 200$ bp.

**(i) Number of reads (2 marks):**

$$
N = \frac{c \cdot G}{L} = \frac{20 \times 2 \times 10^6}{200} = \frac{4 \times 10^7}{200} = 200{,}000 \text{ reads}
$$

**(ii) Probability a base is covered (1.5 marks):**

$$
P(\text{covered}) = 1 - e^{-c} = 1 - e^{-20} = 1 - 2.06 \times 10^{-9} \approx 1.0
$$

Effectively **100%** of the genome is covered (uncov. fraction is one-billionth).

**(iii) Expected number of gaps (1.5 marks):**

$$
N_{\text{gaps}} = N \cdot e^{-c} = 200{,}000 \times 2.06 \times 10^{-9} \approx 4.12 \times 10^{-4}
$$

So **less than 1 gap** — the assembly is essentially **complete** in one contig at $20\times$.

**(iv) Average contig length (1 mark):**

$$
L_{\text{contig}} = \frac{L \cdot e^{c}}{c} = \frac{200 \times 4.85 \times 10^8}{20} = 4.85 \times 10^9 \text{ bp}
$$

This enormous value (exceeding the genome) means the entire genome assembles into a single contig on average.

**Comment on mate-pair library (1 mark):** Even at $20\times$, **repeats** (e.g., rRNA operons, mobile elements) can break the assembly. A 5 kb mate-pair library allows reads to span these repeats and link contigs into scaffolds, dramatically increasing **N50** and reducing fragment assembly.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Coverage denominator:** Always use **haploid** genome size, not diploid. (Common 1-mark loss.)
> 2. **Read length unit:** Must be in **bp**, consistent with $G$ in bp. Mixing kb and bp is a frequent arithmetic error.
> 3. **"Number of contigs vs. number of gaps":** Number of contigs = number of gaps + 1. Students often confuse these.
> 4. **N50 direction:** Always sort contigs **descending**; N50 is the contig length where cumulative sum first reaches 50% of total assembly.
> 5. **"Uncovered fraction" ≠ "Number of gaps":** $P(\text{gap}) = e^{-c}$ is per-base; $N \cdot e^{-c}$ is the number of contigs/gaps in the assembly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition to memorize:** Genome sequencing = determining order of nucleotides; assembly = computational reconstruction of original order.
- **Two strategies:** *Hierarchical Shotgun* (clone-by-clone, needs physical map) and *Whole Genome Shotgun* (no map, paired-end reads).
- **Lander–Waterman formula:** $c = NL/G$ — the **most-tested** equation in Module 3.
- **Coverage thresholds:** 8–10× (draft), 30× (clinical-grade), 50–100× (variant calling).
- **Probability uncovered:** $P(\text{gap}) = e^{-c}$; covered = $1 - e^{-c}$.
- **Expected gaps:** $N \cdot e^{-c}$ (≈ same as number of contigs).
- **Average contig length:** $L_{\text{contig}} = L \cdot e^c / c$.
- **N50** = the contig length such that 50% of the assembly is in contigs $\geq$ N50. *Always sort descending.*
- **OLC** = long reads (Sanger/PacBio/Nanopore); **de Bruijn** = short reads (Illumina).
- **Mate-pair libraries** of varying insert size (2 kb, 10 kb, 50 kb) are essential to **span repeats** and **scaffold contigs**.
- **Hierarchical** = lab-expensive, computation-cheap; **WGS** = lab-cheap, computation-expensive.
- **Lander–Waterman assumption:** uniform random shearing — violated in real data, hence the importance of library QC.
- **KTU trap:** Always halve diploid genomes to **haploid size** before computing $c$.
- **Real-world examples to cite:** Human Genome Project (HGP, 2003) used Hierarchical; Celera used WGS; modern SARS-CoV-2 sequencing uses WGS with Illumina/Nanopore.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Genetic Maps and Physical Maps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT524 - GENETIC ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Genetic Maps and Physical Maps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Genetic Maps (Linkage Maps)

**Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
A **Genetic Map** (also known as a **Linkage Map**) is a chromosome map of a species (or experimental population) that shows the position of known genes, genetic markers, or loci relative to each other based on the **frequency of recombination (crossovers)** during meiosis, rather than on their precise physical distances measured in nucleotides.

The unit of measurement on a genetic map is the **centiMorgan (cM)**, named after the pioneering geneticist Thomas Hunt Morgan.
$$1 \text{ cM} \approx 1\% \text{ recombination frequency between two loci}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Genetic maps are *relative* maps — they tell us the *order* and *approximate spacing* of genes, but not the absolute number of base pairs between them. The human genome has approximately **3,300 cM** across 23 chromosomes, and the physical-to-genetic distance ratio varies (e.g., 1 cM ≈ 1 Mb on average in humans, but ~2 Mb in centromeric regions).

## 1.2 Physical Maps

**Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
A **Physical Map** of a genome is a chart that describes the *absolute* positions (coordinates) of genes or landmarks along the chromosomes, expressed in **actual numbers of base pairs (bp), kilobases (kb), or megabases (Mb)**. Physical maps are constructed using direct molecular techniques such as restriction enzyme digestion, fluorescent in situ hybridization (FISH), and DNA sequencing.

Common physical map units:
- **bp** (base pair) = 1 nucleotide pair
- **kb** (kilobase) = **1,000 bp**
- **Mb** (megabase) = **1,000,000 bp**
- **Gb** (gigabase) = **1,000,000,000 bp**

> [!NOTE]
> **Distinguishing Landmark:** The human reference genome (GRCh38) has approximately **3,200,000,000 bp (3.2 Gb)** spread across 24 chromosomes (22 autosomes + X + Y).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The City Map vs. The Landmark Guide"

Imagine you are a tourist navigating a new city:

- A **Genetic Map** is like a *bus route map*. It tells you the **order of stops** (genes) and the **average travel time between them** (recombination frequency). It does not tell you the exact mileage along the road — only that Stop A comes before Stop B, and Stop B takes "roughly 15 minutes" from Stop A.

- A **Physical Map** is like a *GPS geographic coordinate system*. It tells you the **exact latitude and longitude** of every landmark in meters/kilometers. The stops are positioned according to their actual spatial coordinates on the Earth's surface.

> [!TIP]
> **Student Memory Trick:** *Linkage = Lineage of travel* (relative). *Physical = Precise position* (absolute).

## 1.4 Why Both Maps Are Necessary in Genetic Engineering

| Aspect | Genetic Map | Physical Map |
|---|---|---|
| Built using | Crosses, recombination data | Restriction digests, FISH, sequencing |
| Resolution | Low–Medium (~1 Mb–0.1 cM) | Very High (down to single bp) |
| Speed of construction | Slow (requires generations) | Fast (molecular methods) |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Best for | Gene localization | Genome assembly, positional cloning |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Application:** In the Human Genome Project (HGP, 1990–2003), genetic maps served as the *initial scaffold* upon which physical maps were layered. Modern projects like the Telomere-to-Telomere (T2T) consortium (2022) achieve fully physical maps with no gaps.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principles of Genetic (Linkage) Mapping

The construction of a genetic map rests on three foundational genetic phenomena:

### Step 1 — Mendel's Law of Independent Assortment (Baseline)
- Loci on **different chromosomes** assort independently → recombination frequency (RF) = **50%** (the maximum detectable value).
- Two loci with RF = 50% cannot be distinguished from unlinked loci.

### Step 2 — Linkage Detection
- Loci on the **same chromosome** that are **physically close** show RF < 50% → they are *linked*.
- The closer two loci are, the lower the recombination frequency.

### Step 3 — Recombination Frequency as a Measure of Distance
$$RF = \frac{\text{Number of recombinant progeny}}{\text{Total number of progeny}} \times 100\%$$

$$d \text{ (in cM)} = RF \, (\%) \text{ for small distances}$$

## 2.2 Three-Point Test Cross (Gold Standard in KTU Board Exams)

A **three-point test cross** is performed by crossing a heterozygote (A B C / a b c) with a homozygous recessive (a b c / a b c). The progeny are then classified into 8 phenotypic classes (2³ = 8), grouped as:
- **Parental (P) types** — 2 most frequent classes (no crossovers).
- **Double crossover (DCO) types** — 2 least frequent classes (2 crossovers).
- **Single crossover (SCO) types** — 4 intermediate classes.

### Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Identify the parental classes** (highest frequency).
2. **Identify the DCO classes** (lowest frequency) — these reveal the *middle gene*.
3. The gene whose arrangement is *flipped* in DCO compared to parents sits **in the middle**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does DCO identify the middle gene?** Because a double crossover reverses the position of the middle marker while leaving the outer two markers in parental configuration. Therefore, comparing DCO to parental types shows which gene changed its parental linkage phase.

## 2.3 Mapping Functions — Correcting for Double Crossovers

Observed recombination frequency **underestimates** true genetic distance when multiple crossovers occur (interference region). **Mapping functions** mathematically convert RF into true map distance.

### Haldane's Mapping Function (1919)
Assumes **no interference** (crossovers occur independently — Poisson distribution).

$$m = -\frac{1}{2}\ln(1 - 2r)$$

where:
- $m$ = true map distance in Morgans
- $r$ = observed recombination fraction
- $\ln$ = natural logarithm (base $e$)

### Kosambi's Mapping Function (1944)
Incorporates **positive interference** (Kosambi's coefficient, $k$).

$$m = \frac{1}{4}\ln\left(\frac{1 + 2r}{1 - 2r}\right)$$

### Coefficient of Coincidence and Interference

$$c = \frac{\text{Observed DCO}}{\text{Expected DCO}}$$

$$I = 1 - c$$

where $I$ is the **interference** value (0 ≤ I ≤ 1). When $I = 1$, no double crossovers occur.

## 2.4 Lod Score (Logarithm of Odds) — Statistical Confidence in Linkage

The **Lod Score (Z)** quantifies the likelihood that two loci are linked at a given recombination fraction $\theta$ versus being unlinked ($\theta = 0.5$).

$$Z(\theta) = \log_{10}\left[\frac{L(\theta)}{L(0.5)}\right]$$

Decision thresholds:
- **Z ≥ 3** → evidence for linkage (1000:1 odds).
- **Z ≤ -2** → evidence against linkage (100:1 odds).

## 2.5 Principles of Physical Mapping

Physical maps are built using molecular techniques, not genetic crosses:

| Technique | Resolution | Principle |
|---|---|---|
| **Restriction Mapping** | 0.1–10 kb | Cut DNA with REs; size fragments by gel electrophoresis |
| **Fluorescent In Situ Hybridization (FISH)** | 1–10 Mb | Hybridize fluorescent probes to chromosomes |
| **Sequence Tagged Sites (STS)** | 100 bp–1 kb | Unique PCR-amplifiable sequences at known map positions |
| **YAC / BAC contigs** | 100 kb–1 Mb | Overlapping clones assembled into a contig |
| **Optical Mapping** | 100 kb–2 Mb | Visualizes long DNA molecules on nanochannel arrays |
| **Whole Genome Sequencing** | 1 bp | Definitive physical map |

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Quick-Reference)

| **Formula / Symbol** | **Expression** | **Variables & Units** | **Use Case** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Recombination Frequency | $RF = \dfrac{R}{N} \times 100\%$ | $R$ = recombinants, $N$ = total progeny | Map distance for small intervals |
| Map distance (cM) | $d = RF(\%)$ | Morgans × 100 | Genetic map unit |
| Haldane's function | $m = -\dfrac{1}{2}\ln(1-2r)$ | $m$ in Morgans | No interference correction |
| Kosambi's function | $m = \dfrac{1}{4}\ln\left(\dfrac{1+2r}{1-2r}\right)$ | $m$ in Morgans | With interference correction |
| Coefficient of coincidence | $c = \dfrac{\text{Obs. DCO}}{\text{Exp. DCO}}$ | Dimensionless | Quantifies crossover dependence |
| Interference | $I = 1 - c$ | $0 \le I \le 1$ | Strength of interference |
| Lod Score | $Z = \log_{10}\left[\dfrac{L(\theta)}{L(0.5)}\right]$ | Z ≥ 3 → linked | Linkage statistical significance |
| Poisson expectation | $E_n = \dfrac{(d)^n}{n!}e^{-d}$ | $n$ = crossover class, $d$ = distance in Morgans | Expected crossover frequency |
| Recombination ↔ Distance (cos site model) | $r = \dfrac{1}{2}(1 - e^{-2m})$ | Reciprocal of Haldane | Recombination fraction from $m$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** Lod score analysis is extensively used in **medical genetics** to identify disease-susceptibility loci (e.g., BRCA1, BRCA2 mapping in hereditary breast cancer). Restriction mapping and YAC/BAC contigs are foundational in **biopharmaceutical gene cloning** workflows.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Haldane's Mapping Function (Detailed Working)

### Objective
To convert the observed recombination fraction $r$ (which saturates at 0.5 for large distances) into the true additive map distance $m$ in Morgans, assuming crossovers follow a Poisson distribution with no interference.

### Setup
- Let $d$ = true distance in Morgans (mean number of crossovers per meiosis between the two loci).
- By definition: $d = m$ (in Morgans).
- The probability of exactly $n$ crossovers in an interval of length $m$ Morgans (Poisson):
$$P_n = \frac{m^n e^{-m}}{n!}$$

### Step A — Odd-numbered crossovers produce recombinants
- Only an **odd** number of crossovers (n = 1, 3, 5, ...) yields a recombinant chromatid.
- The probability of an *odd* number of crossovers is:
$$P_{\text{odd}} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{m^{2k+1} e^{-m}}{(2k+1)!}$$

### Step B — Trigonometric simplification
- Use the Maclaurin series: $\sin(x) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \dfrac{(-1)^k x^{2k+1}}{(2k+1)!}$.
- The series $\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \dfrac{x^{2k+1}}{(2k+1)!} = \sinh(x)$.
- Therefore:
$$P_{\text{odd}} = e^{-m}\sinh(m) = \frac{1 - e^{-2m}}{2}$$

### Step C — Define the recombination fraction
The recombination fraction $r$ equals $P_{\text{odd}}$ (each odd crossover yields a recombinant chromatid in one of the four meiotic products, with ½ probability of being detected in a testcross):
$$r = \frac{1 - e^{-2m}}{2}$$

### Step D — Solve for $m$
Rearrange:
$$1 - 2r = e^{-2m}$$

$$\ln(1 - 2r) = -2m$$

$$\boxed{m = -\frac{1}{2}\ln(1 - 2r)}$$

**Verification at $r = 0$:** $m = -\frac{1}{2}\ln(1) = 0$ ✓
**Verification at $r = 0.5$:** $m = -\frac{1}{2}\ln(0) \to \infty$ ✓ (distance is unbounded, consistent with the chromosome acting as if unlinked).

## 3.2 Derivation: Kosambi's Mapping Function (Detailed Working)

### Objective
Incorporate **interference** — that is, account for the biological observation that one crossover reduces the probability of another nearby crossover.

### Setup
Kosambi proposed that **interference is proportional to distance**: closer crossovers experience stronger interference. He introduced an empirical transformation:
$$\begin{aligned}
r &= \frac{1}{2}\tanh(2m)
\end{aligned}$$

where $\tanh(x) = \dfrac{e^x - e^{-x}}{e^x + e^{-x}}$ is the hyperbolic tangent.

### Step A — Solve for m
Starting from $r = \dfrac{1}{2}\tanh(2m)$:

$$\tanh(2m) = 2r$$

Apply the inverse hyperbolic tangent identity: $\tanh^{-1}(y) = \dfrac{1}{2}\ln\left(\dfrac{1+y}{1-y}\right)$:

$$2m = \tanh^{-1}(2r) = \frac{1}{2}\ln\left(\frac{1 + 2r}{1 - 2r}\right)$$

$$m = \frac{1}{4}\ln\left(\frac{1 + 2r}{1 - 2r}\right)$$

### Step B — Verification
- At $r = 0$: $m = \frac{1}{4}\ln(1) = 0$ ✓
- At $r = 0.1$: $m = \frac{1}{4}\ln\left(\frac{1.2}{0.8}\right) = \frac{1}{4}\ln(1.5) \approx 0.101$ Morgans (≈ 10.1 cM) — close to Haldane's value at low $r$, as expected.
- At $r = 0.3$: $m \approx \frac{1}{4}\ln\left(\frac{1.6}{0.4}\right) = \frac{1}{4}\ln(4) \approx 0.347$ Morgans.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Three-Point Cross Mapping

**Given:** A testcross of $Aa\,Bb\,Cc \times aa\,bb\,cc$ yields the following 1,000 progeny:

| Class | Phenotype | Number | Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | A B C | 410 | Parental |
| 2 | a b c | 390 | Parental |
| 3 | A b c | 90 | SCO |
| 4 | a B C | 95 | SCO |
| 5 | A B c | 35 | SCO |
| 6 | a b C | 37 | SCO |
| 7 | A b C | 8 | DCO |
| 8 | a B c | 7 | DCO |

**Required:** (a) Determine gene order. (b) Compute map distances. (c) Calculate interference.

### Solution Step-by-Step

#### Step 1 — Identify Parentals
Highest frequency: Classes 1 & 2 → parental arrangement is **A B C / a b c**.

#### Step 2 — Identify DCO and the Middle Gene
DCO = Classes 7 & 8 (lowest frequency). Compare DCO arrangement to parents:
- Parents: A B C and a b c
- DCO: A b C and a B c

The **C locus** (or its allele pattern) is unchanged between parental and DCO → the locus whose combination **flipped** is in the middle. Here, B changed from being with A and C (parental) to being with a and c (DCO) → **B is the middle gene**.

Therefore the gene order is: **A — B — C** (or equivalently **a — b — c**).

#### Step 3 — Compute Map Distances

**Distance A–B:** Recombinants in A–B region include SCO between A and B (classes 3, 4, 7, 8) and DCO (which also has crossovers in both regions).
Actually, recombinant gametes for A–B include those where A and B are separated: Classes 3 (A b c), 4 (a B C), 7 (A b C), 8 (a B c).
- Total A–B recombinants = 90 + 95 + 8 + 7 = **200**
$$d_{A-B} = \frac{200}{1000} \times 100 = 20 \text{ cM}$$

**Distance B–C:** Recombinants for B–C region: Classes 5 (A B c), 6 (a b C), 7 (A b C), 8 (a B c).
- Total B–C recombinants = 35 + 37 + 8 + 7 = **87**
$$d_{B-C} = \frac{87}{1000} \times 100 = 8.7 \text{ cM}$$

#### Step 4 — Correct Distances Using Haldane's Function (Optional Advanced)

For $r_{A-B} = 0.20$:
$$m_{A-B} = -\frac{1}{2}\ln(1 - 0.40) = -\frac{1}{2}\ln(0.60) \approx 0.255 \text{ M} = 25.5 \text{ cM}$$

For $r_{B-C} = 0.087$:
$$m_{B-C} = -\frac{1}{2}\ln(1 - 0.174) \approx 0.0939 \text{ M} = 9.39 \text{ cM}$$

#### Step 5 — Coefficient of Coincidence and Interference

Observed DCO = 7 + 8 = **15**
Expected DCO (Poisson with Haldane-corrected $d_{A-B} = 0.255$ M) using probability of at least one crossover in each interval:
$$P_{\text{DCO expected}} = (\text{Prob. of ≥1 crossover in A-B}) \times (\text{Prob. of ≥1 crossover in B-C})$$
$$= (1 - e^{-0.255}) \times (1 - e^{-0.0939})$$
$$= 0.2252 \times 0.0897 \approx 0.02020$$
Expected DCO count = $0.02020 \times 1000 \approx 20.2$

$$c = \frac{15}{20.2} \approx 0.743$$
$$I = 1 - 0.743 = 0.257$$

**Interpretation:** A modest positive interference of ~26% exists in this interval.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Genetic vs. Physical Map Construction

| **Parameter** | **Genetic Map** | **Physical Map** |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Input** | Meiotic recombination frequencies | Restriction enzyme sites, sequences, hybridization signals |
| **Mathematical Basis** | Probability / Statistics (Lod scores) | Linear distance (bp, kb, Mb) |
| **Resolution Limit** | ~0.1 cM (≈ 100 kb in humans) | 1 bp (with sequencing) |
| **Additivity** | Approximate (requires mapping functions) | Exact (additive by definition) |
| **Maturity** | Classical (Morgan 1910s) | Modern (1970s onward) |
| **Cost per base mapped** | Low | High |
| **Hot-spot artifacts** | Yes (recombination hotspots skew distance) | No (sequence-based, no recombination bias) |
| **Best application** | Trait association, QTL mapping | Genome assembly, gene cloning |
| **Example organism** | *Drosophila melanogaster* (classical) | *H. sapiens* T2T genome (2022) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Workflow: From Genetic Cross → Genetic Map → Physical Map

The following Mermaid flowchart depicts the integrated pipeline of map construction in a genome project.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Cross heterozygous parent with homozygous recessive] --> B[Step 2: Score progeny phenotypes]
    B --> C[Step 3: Calculate recombination frequencies RF]
    C --> D{Step 4: Apply mapping function?}
    D -- Haldane --> E1[Haldane: m = -1/2 ln 1-2r]
    D -- Kosambi --> E2[Kosambi: m = 1/4 ln 1+2r over 1-2r]
    E1 --> F[Step 5: Assemble genetic map in cM]
    E2 --> F
    F --> G[Step 6: Build physical map using YAC BAC contigs]
    G --> H[Step 7: Anchor genetic markers onto physical contigs via STS]
    H --> I[Step 8: Sequence to obtain final bp-level physical map]
    I --> J[Final Integrated Genome Map]
    F --> J
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Three-Point Cross Mapping Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step A: Score all 8 phenotypic classes]
    P1 --> P2[Step B: Find two most frequent classes = Parentals]
    P2 --> P3[Step C: Find two least frequent classes = DCOs]
    P3 --> P4[Step D: Compare DCO to Parentals to identify middle gene]
    P4 --> P5[Step E: Count recombinants in Region 1 and Region 2]
    P5 --> P6[Step F: Compute RF and map distance in cM]
    P6 --> P7[Step G: Optional Haldane or Kosambi correction]
    P7 --> P8[Step H: Compute coincidence c and interference I]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Physical Mapping Techniques Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PM[Physical Mapping Techniques] --> L1[Low Resolution - Mb scale]
    PM --> L2[Medium Resolution - kb scale]
    PM --> L3[High Resolution - bp scale]
    L1 --> T1[FISH - Fluorescent in situ hybridization]
    L2 --> T2[STS - Sequence Tagged Sites]
    L2 --> T3[YAC and BAC contigs]
    L2 --> T4[Optical mapping in nanochannels]
    L3 --> T5[Restriction mapping]
    L3 --> T6[Sanger sequencing]
    L3 --> T7[Next generation sequencing NGS]
    T2 --> A[Anchor markers between genetic and physical maps]
    T3 --> A
    A --> Z[Integrated Physical-Genetic Map]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Recombination Frequency vs. Map Distance

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Small distance r less than 0.10] --> B[RF approx equals map distance cM]
    C[Moderate distance r 0.10 to 0.25] --> D[Map distance greater than RF]
    E[Large distance r greater than 0.25] --> F[Apply Haldane or Kosambi function]
    F --> G[True additive distance in Morgans]
    B --> G
    D --> G
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram: Genome Mapping Strategy (HGP-Style)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    G1[Stage 1: Genetic Linkage Map] --> G2[Stage 2: Physical Map of Overlapping Clones]
    G2 --> G3[Stage 3: Sequence-Ready Contigs]
    G3 --> G4[Stage 4: High Throughput Sequencing]
    G4 --> G5[Stage 5: Assembly and Finishing]
    G5 --> G6[Complete Genome Sequence]
    style G1 fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G2 fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G6 fill:#85c1e9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
```
