3. **Specific internal motifs:** Presence of **A at position 19** and **G/C at position 21** of the guide strand enhances Ago2 cleavage.
4. **Avoidance of stretches** of ≥ 4 G or C nucleotides (prevents G-quadruplex formation and internal slippage).
5. **Specificity check:** BLAST the guide sequence against the transcriptome; **≤ 15 contiguous matches** to any non-target mRNA to avoid off-target effects.
6. **Sense strand inactivation:** Use **2′-O-methyl or 2′-fluoro modifications** on the passenger strand to prevent it from loading into RISC.

**Importance of Thermodynamic Asymmetry:**
The siRNA duplex is **symmetric in sequence** but **asymmetric in thermodynamics**. Ago2 preferentially loads the strand whose 5′ end **unwinds more readily** (A/U-rich, weaker base pairs). This deterministic selection ensures:
- **Single active RISC** per siRNA molecule (no dilution of silencing potency).
- **Reproducible, predictable knock-down** across experiments.
- **Minimization of passenger strand-mediated off-target effects** (a major source of false positives in RNAi screens).

Mathematical expression:

$$
\Delta \Delta G = \Delta G_{5',S_{passenger}} - \Delta G_{5',S_{guide}} < 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{guide is selected}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — December 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast **siRNA and miRNA** with respect to their biogenesis, target complementarity, mode of action, and therapeutic applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the various **delivery strategies** used for therapeutic siRNA, highlighting the role of **lipid nanoparticles (LNPs)** and **GalNAc conjugation**. Mention at least **two FDA-approved siRNA drugs** and their target genes. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a):**

| Feature | siRNA | miRNA |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Exogenous (viral, transfected, shRNA vectors) | Endogenous (intronic or intergenic genomic loci) |
| **Biogenesis** | Dicer cleaves long dsRNA / shRNA | Transcription → pri-miRNA → Drosha → pre-miRNA → Dicer → mature miRNA |
| **Dicer dependence** | Yes | Yes |
| **Drosha dependence** | No (except for shRNA pathway) | Yes (canonical pathway) |
| **Length** | 21–23 nt | 21–23 nt |
| **Target complementarity** | **Perfect / near-perfect** | **Partial** (especially seed region nt 2–8) |
| **Number of targets** | **Single specific mRNA** | **Multiple mRNAs** (target networks) |
| **Mode of action** | **Cleavage** by Ago2 → mRNA degradation | **Translational repression** + mRNA destabilization (Ago1/2/3) |
| **Biological role** | Antiviral defense, transposon silencing, exogenous gene regulation | Endogenous gene regulatory networks, development, differentiation |
| **Therapeutic use** | Highly specific gene knock-down (e.g., patisiran) | Broader modulation (research only, no approved miRNA drugs yet) |
| **Off-target risk** | Lower (specificity ensured by perfect match) | Higher (seed-mediated repression of many transcripts) |

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**siRNA Delivery Strategies:**

**1. Lipid Nanoparticles (LNPs):**
- Composition: **ionizable cationic lipid** (e.g., DLin-MC3-DMA), **helper lipid** (DSPC), **cholesterol**, **PEG-lipid**.
- At acidic endosomal pH, the ionizable lipid becomes **protonated and cationic**, enabling:
  - Endosomal membrane disruption → **cytoplasmic release** of siRNA.
  - Protection from serum nucleases and renal clearance.
- **FDA-approved example:** **Patisiran (Onpattro®)** — targets *TTR* (transthyretin) mRNA in liver for **hereditary ATTR amyloidosis** (2018).

**2. GalNAc Conjugation:**
- **Triantennary N-acetylgalactosamine (GalNAc)** binds the **Asialoglycoprotein Receptor (ASGPR)** expressed at high density (~500,000/cell) on hepatocytes.
- Mechanism: Receptor-mediated endocytosis → endosomal escape → cytoplasmic RISC loading.
- Advantages: **High hepatocyte specificity**, low immunogenicity, **subcutaneous (SC) administration** (vs. IV for LNPs), long dosing intervals (every 3–6 months via "enhanced stabilization chemistry" — ESC).
- **FDA-approved examples:**
  - **Givosiran (Givlaari®)** — targets *ALAS1* for **acute hepatic porphyria** (2019).
  - **Inclisiran (Leqvio®)** — targets *PCSK9* for **hypercholesterolemia** (2021/2023). Twice-yearly dosing — a paradigm shift in LDL-C management.
  - **Vutrisiran (Amvuttra®)** — targets *TTR* for ATTR amyloidosis (2022).

**3. Other delivery methods:**
- **Polymeric nanoparticles** (PEI, PLGA)
- **Exosomes / extracellular vesicles** (engineered)
- **Aptamer-siRNA chimeras**
- **Antibody-siRNA conjugates**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Drug name confusion:** Patisiran = LNP; Givosiran/Inclisiran/Vutrisiran = **GalNAc**. Mixing them up is a **sure-shot 2-mark loss**.
> 2. **Approval year mismatch:** Many students write 2020 for patisiran; correct is **August 2018** (first FDA-approved siRNA drug ever).
> 3. **Confusing siRNA with antisense oligonucleotides (ASOs):** ASOs (e.g., nusinersen) are **single-stranded DNA-like** molecules; siRNA is **double-stranded RNA**. They have different mechanisms (RNase H1 vs. Ago2).
> 4. **Off-target effects:** Students often forget to mention **seed-region-mediated off-targeting** and **saturation of endogenous miRNA pathways** (the latter can be fatal — see Papers et al. *Nature* 2009, liver toxicity in mice from shRNA saturation).

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **siRNA = 21–23 nt dsRNA** with **2-nt 3′ overhangs** and **5′-phosphates**.
- ✅ **Dicer** cleaves long dsRNA → siRNA; **Ago2** cleaves target mRNA.
- ✅ **RISC** = effector complex; **Ago2 PIWI domain** has the **DEDH** catalytic tetrad.
- ✅ Cleavage site = **between positions 10 and 11** of the guide strand (counted from 5′).
- ✅ **Guide strand** = strand with **A/U-rich 5′ end** (low ΔG, less stable).
- ✅ **Passenger strand** = discarded after Ago2 slicing.
- ✅ **siRNA requires PERFECT complementarity**; miRNA is PARTIAL.
- ✅ **siRNA is exogenous**; miRNA is **endogenous** (genomic).
- ✅ **Delivery problem:** Naked siRNA cannot cross cell membrane → use **LNPs or GalNAc**.
- ✅ **GalNAc → hepatocytes** via **ASGPR**; **LNPs → liver** (predominant tropism).
- ✅ **FDA-approved siRNA drugs to memorize:**
  - **Patisiran (2018)** — *TTR* — LNP — ATTR amyloidosis
  - **Givosiran (2019)** — *ALAS1* — GalNAc — Acute hepatic porphyria
  - **Lumasiran (2020)** — *HAO1* — GalNAc — PH1
  - **Inclisiran (2021/2023)** — *PCSK9* — GalNAc — Hypercholesterolemia
  - **Vutrisiran (2022)** — *TTR* — GalNAc — ATTR amyloidosis
- ✅ **Nobel Prize 2006:** Fire & Mello for RNAi discovery.
- ✅ **Reynolds design rules:** 30–52% GC, A/U at 5′ of guide, A at position 19.
- ✅ **Off-target threshold:** ≥ **15 contiguous nt** matches = significant off-target risk.
- ✅ **First-order kinetics:** $[mRNA]_t = [mRNA]_0 \cdot e^{-k_d' t}$
- ✅ **siRNA vs. shRNA:** siRNA = transient; shRNA = DNA-vector-expressed, processed by Drosha + Dicer for **stable knock-down**.
- ✅ **siRNA vs. ASO:** siRNA = dsRNA + Ago2; ASO = ssDNA + RNase H1.
- ✅ **Clinical safety concerns:** **immune activation (TLR3/7/8)**, **off-target silencing**, **RISC saturation**, **dose-limiting toxicity**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Tip:** When asked "explain siRNA technology," always cover **(1) mechanism, (2) design rules, (3) delivery, (4) therapeutic applications with examples** — these four pillars fetch full marks in 14-mark questions. Always include a **labeled diagram** (Mermaid/hand-drawn) — it is worth 2–3 marks on its own.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MicroRNA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT524 - GENETIC ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** MicroRNA

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MicroRNA (miRNA)** is a class of small, endogenous, single-stranded, non-coding RNA molecules of approximately **21–23 nucleotides (nt)** in length that function as **post-transcriptional regulators of gene expression**. They are transcribed from genomic DNA (mostly by RNA Polymerase II), processed through a stepwise enzymatic maturation pathway, and incorporated into the **RNA-Induced Silencing Complex (RISC)** to guide sequence-specific repression of target messenger RNAs (mRNAs), either by transcript degradation or by translational inhibition.

In the official KTU 2024 scheme context, miRNAs are positioned as one of the central pillars of **RNA interference (RNAi)** biology, sitting alongside small interfering RNAs (siRNAs), but distinguished by their **endogenous genomic origin** and their mechanism of action that typically relies on **partial, seed-region complementarity** rather than perfect base pairing.

## 1.2 Key Quantitative Facts (KTU High-Yield Constants)

- **Average mature miRNA length:** **22 nucleotides**
- **Pre-miRNA hairpin length:** **~60–80 nucleotides**
- **Seed region:** nucleotides **2–8 from the 5′ end** of the mature miRNA
- **Number of annotated human miRNAs (miRBase v22+):** **> 2,500 mature sequences**
- **Estimated mRNAs regulated per miRNA:** **100–1,000 targets**
- **Estimated miRNAs regulating a single mRNA:** **multiple (cooperative regulation)**
- **Discovery landmark:** *Caenorhabditis elegans* **lin-4** (Lee, Feinbaum & Ambros, **1993**) — the first miRNA identified; let-7 (Reinhart et al., **2000**) — the first miRNA conserved across species.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Cellular Dimmer Switch"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Think of your genome as a giant electrical wiring diagram for a building, and mRNAs as the bulbs that actually emit light. **siRNAs act like a hard ON/OFF wall switch** — flip it, and the bulb is either fully lit or completely dark. **MicroRNAs, however, behave like a dimmer (rheostat) switch** — they do not abolish the bulb's function but *fine-tune* its output by gently nudging the mRNA's brightness downward.

This intuitive model captures three crucial properties of miRNA biology:

1. **Fine-tuning, not elimination** — miRNAs modulate, they rarely fully silence a target.
2. **Combinatorial control** — multiple miRNAs act on the same mRNA (the "many dimmers in one room" concept).
3. **Tissue-specific expression** — just as a dimmer panel is wired only in certain rooms of the building, a given miRNA is expressed in only specific cell types.

## 1.4 Historical Footnote & Nomenclature

- The mature strand retains the name *miR-X* (e.g., **miR-21**, **miR-155**); the complementary (passenger) strand from the same duplex was historically denoted *miR-X\** (miRNA-star), but this nomenclature is now deprecated in favour of explicit **-5p** and **-3p** suffixes indicating 5′ and 3′ arm origin.
- Example: **miR-21-5p** = mature strand from the 5′ arm of the pre-miR-21 hairpin.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Canonical Biogenesis Pathway (Stepwise)

The maturation of a miRNA follows an ordered, compartmentalized workflow:

1. **Nuclear Transcription (RNA Pol II / III)**
   - Most miRNA genes are transcribed by **RNA Polymerase II** → primary transcript **pri-miRNA**.
   - Pri-miRNA: **5′ m⁷G cap + 3′ poly(A) tail**, length ~500–3000 nt, contains a local hairpin (stem–loop) flanked by single-stranded RNA.
   - Some miRNAs (e.g., those in Alu repeats, some tRNA-associated) are transcribed by **RNA Pol III**.

2. **Microprocessor Complex (Drosha + DGCR8)**
   - **Drosha** = RNase III endonuclease (cuts dsRNA ~11 bp from the stem–ssRNA junction).
   - **DGCR8** (DiGeorge syndrome Critical Region 8) = dsRNA-binding partner that positions Drosha accurately.
   - Result: a **~70 nt hairpin** with a **2-nt 3′ overhang** called the **pre-miRNA**.

3. **Nuclear Export**
   - **Exportin-5** (XPO5) + **Ran-GTP** transports pre-miRNA from nucleus → cytoplasm.

4. **Cytoplasmic Processing (Dicer + TRBP)**
   - **Dicer** (another RNase III) cleaves ~22 nt from the loop end.
   - **TRBP** (TAR RNA-binding protein) and **PACT** stabilize the complex.
   - Product: a **~22 nt miRNA duplex** with 2-nt 3′ overhangs on both ends.

5. **RISC Loading & Strand Selection**
   - The duplex is loaded onto an **Argonaute (AGO1–AGO4 in humans)** protein.
   - **Strand selection** is thermodynamically asymmetric: the strand whose **5′ end is less stably base-paired** (the "guide strand") is retained; the opposite ("passenger" / "miRNA\*") strand is sliced (in plants) or simply unwound and degraded (in mammals).

6. **Target Recognition & Repression**
   - The mature RISC binds the **3′ UTR** of target mRNAs.
   - Base-pairing is anchored by the **seed region** (positions 2–8 of the guide miRNA).
   - Outcomes: **mRNA destabilization** (deadenylation, decapping, 5′→3′ decay) and/or **translational repression** at initiation or post-initiation stages.

## 2.2 Non-Canonical Pathways (Brief Overview for KTU Depth)

| Pathway Type | Key Feature | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Drosha-independent / Dicer-dependent** | Pre-miRNA mimics produced from introns (mirtrons) | mir-16, mir-34a mirtrons |
| **Drosha-dependent / Dicer-independent** | Drosha generates pre-miRNA that bypasses Dicer | miR-451 (very short stem) |
| **tRNA-derived** | tRNA endonuclease cleavage produces pre-miRNA-like fragments | tRNA-Leu fragments |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (miRNA Targeting & Thermodynamics)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU exam derivations, memorize the following set. **All energy values are in kcal/mol** at standard biological conditions (T = 310 K, pH 7.4, 1 M monovalent salt reference).

| Symbol / Term | Formula / Rule | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Seed region | Positions **2 → 8** of 5′ miRNA | Core determinant of target specificity |
| Seed match types | 8mer, 7mer-m8, 7mer-A1, 6mer | Decreasing binding strength L→R |
| **Watson–Crick pairing rule** | A–U, G–C base pairs only (with G:U wobble tolerated in 3′ supplementary region) | Defines binding free energy |
| **ΔG (binding free energy)** | $\Delta G = \Delta H - T \Delta S$ | More negative = stronger duplex |
| **Target site accessibility** | $\Delta G_{\text{total}} = \Delta G_{\text{hybrid}} - \Delta G_{\text{open}}$ of local mRNA structure | Less negative local mRNA folding ⇒ easier miRNA access |
| **Position weighting (TargetScan-style)** | $S_{\text{site}} = \sum_{i} w_i \cdot M_i$ | $w_i$ = position weight, $M_i$ = match indicator |
| **Conservation score** | $P_{\text{conserved}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{species\_conserved}}}{N_{\text{total\_species}}}$ | Higher ⇒ biologically functional site |
| **Combinatorial repression** | $\text{Repression} \approx 1 - \prod_{j} (1 - r_j)$ | $r_j$ = fractional repression by site $j$ |
| **Number of MREs** (miRNA Recognition Elements) | $n_{\text{MRE}}$ | Typically 1–8 functional sites per 3′ UTR |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 14-mark answers, the **seed match type** (8mer > 7mer-m8 > 7mer-A1 > 6mer) and the **A/U content flanking the seed** are the two highest-weight predictors. Always cite both when justifying target selection.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Therapeutic Utility

- **Anti-miR / Antagomir therapeutics** — chemically modified ssRNA complementary to the mature miRNA; used to *inhibit* oncogenic miRNAs (e.g., anti-miR-122 drug **Miravirsen** for Hepatitis C).
- **miRNA mimics** — synthetic double-stranded RNA that restores lost miRNA expression (tumour suppressor miR-34a mimics in cancer trials).
- **miRNA sponges** — engineered 3′ UTRs with multiple MREs to sequester a target miRNA.
- **Biomarker diagnostics** — circulating miRNAs in serum/exosomes serve as non-invasive cancer, cardiac, and neurodegeneration biomarkers.
- **CRISPR engineering of miRNA loci** — deletion or replacement of miRNA genes in cell lines and model organisms.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation — Quantitative Estimate of Combinatorial Repression

> **Problem (typical KTU Part-B style):** A target mRNA contains $n_{\text{MRE}} = 3$ independent miRNA recognition elements with fractional repression values $r_1 = 0.35$, $r_2 = 0.25$, $r_3 = 0.20$. Calculate the total fractional repression assuming independent (multiplicative) action.

### Solution

**Step 1 — State the multiplicative repression model.**
For independent miRNA sites acting on the same mRNA, the surviving protein fraction is the product of survival fractions at each site:

$$
F_{\text{survive}} = \prod_{j=1}^{n} (1 - r_j)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given values.**
The repression at site 1 gives survival $(1 - 0.35)$, site 2 gives $(1 - 0.25)$, site 3 gives $(1 - 0.20)$.

$$
F_{\text{survive}} = (1 - 0.35)(1 - 0.25)(1 - 0.20)
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical evaluation (first bracket).**
Subtract $0.35$ from $1$:
$$
(1 - 0.35) = 0.65
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical evaluation (second bracket).**
Subtract $0.25$ from $1$:
$$
(1 - 0.25) = 0.75
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical evaluation (third bracket).**
Subtract $0.20$ from $1$:
$$
(1 - 0.20) = 0.80
$$

**Step 6 — Multiply the three survival fractions.**
$$
F_{\text{survive}} = 0.65 \times 0.75 \times 0.80
$$

**Step 7 — First product.**
$$
0.65 \times 0.75 = 0.4875
$$

**Step 8 — Second product.**
$$
0.4875 \times 0.80 = 0.3900
$$

**Step 9 — Compute total fractional repression.**
The total repression is the complement of the surviving fraction:

$$
R_{\text{total}} = 1 - F_{\text{survive}} = 1 - 0.39 = 0.61
$$

**Final Result:** $R_{\text{total}} = 0.61 \;\; \text{or} \;\; \mathbf{61\%}$ combined repression.

> [!NOTE]
> **Biological insight:** Notice that even though each site alone represses only 20–35%, three together give **>60%** repression. This **super-additive (multiplicative)** behaviour is exactly why miRNAs are such powerful tuners — a few modest sites can collectively knock down an mRNA substantially.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation — Free Energy of a Seed Duplex

> **Problem:** A 7mer seed binds an mRNA target. Approximate the standard free energy using nearest-neighbour parameters. The duplex sequence is `5′-GUAGCAA-3′` (miRNA) pairing with `3′-CAUCGUU-5′` (mRNA), giving pairs `G–C, U–A, A–U, G–C, C–G, A–U, A–U`.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Recall the nearest-neighbour free energy formula.**
The duplex free energy at 37 °C is the sum of stacking parameters minus an initiation term:

$$
\Delta G^{\circ}_{37} = \Delta G^{\circ}_{\text{init}} + \sum_{k} \Delta G^{\circ}_{\text{stack},k}
$$

**Step 2 — Insert standard initiation parameter.**
For RNA duplexes, the standard initiation term is:

$$
\Delta G^{\circ}_{\text{init}} = +4.09 \; \text{kcal/mol}
$$

**Step 3 — Look up nearest-neighbour stacking parameters.**
For an RNA duplex at 37 °C, representative stacking parameters (kcal/mol) are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{GC/CG} &: -2.35 \\
\text{UA/AU} &: -2.08 \\
\text{AU/UA} &: -2.11 \\
\text{GC/CG} &: -2.35 \\
\text{CG/GC} &: -2.36 \\
\text{AU/UA} &: -2.11 \\
\text{AU/UA} &: -2.11
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum the stacking terms.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum \Delta G^{\circ}_{\text{stack}} = \; &(-2.35) + (-2.08) + (-2.11) + (-2.35) \\
 &+ (-2.36) + (-2.11) + (-2.11) \\
 = \; &-15.47 \; \text{kcal/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Add the initiation term.**

$$
\Delta G^{\circ}_{37} = (+4.09) + (-15.47) = -11.38 \; \text{kcal/mol}
$$

**Final Result:** $\Delta G^{\circ}_{37} = -11.38 \; \text{kcal/mol}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering meaning:** A binding free energy of approximately **−11.4 kcal/mol** for a 7mer seed duplex is consistent with biologically active seed matches (typical range −7 to −14 kcal/mol). More negative ΔG = more stable duplex = stronger repression, all else being equal.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — miRNA vs siRNA (KTU Favourite Comparison)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 examiners frequently award a 7-mark sub-question to a tabular comparison. Master this matrix.

| Feature | MicroRNA (miRNA) | Small Interfering RNA (siRNA) |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Endogenous (genome-encoded) | Exogenous (introduced) or endogenous (rare) |
| **Precursor** | pri-miRNA → pre-miRNA hairpin | Long dsRNA or short hairpin |
| **Drosha step** | **Yes** (canonical) | **No** |
| **Dicer step** | Yes | Yes |
| **Mature length** | ~22 nt | ~21 nt |
| **Complementarity to target** | **Partial** (seed-only essential) | **Perfect / near-perfect** |
| **Target location** | 3′ UTR of mRNA (typical) | Anywhere on transcript |
| **Biological outcome** | Translational repression + mRNA destabilization | mRNA cleavage (slicing) |
| **Number of targets** | Many (hundreds to thousands) | One (the cognate transcript) |
| **Function** | Fine-tuning / developmental regulation | Defensive (anti-viral, transposon silencing) |
| **Therapeutic example** | Miravirsen (anti-miR-122) | Patisiran (siRNA against TTR) |
| **Seed region role** | **Mandatory for target ID** | Less critical due to full complementarity |

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Worked Example — Identifying an 8mer Site

> **Problem:** Given mRNA 3′ UTR segment `…AUGCAUACGU…` and miRNA seed `5′-UAUGUAU-3′`, determine the seed-match type.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Write the miRNA seed aligned to mRNA.**
The seed `5′-UAUGUAU-3′` should base-pair anti-parallel to the mRNA target. Place the mRNA 3′→5′ under the seed:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{miRNA seed} \; 5'\!-\!&\text{U A U G U A U}\!-\!3' \\
 &\;\vert\;\;\vert\;\;\vert\;\;\vert\;\;\vert\;\;\vert\;\;\vert \\
\text{mRNA target} \; 3'\!-\!&\text{A U A C A U A}\!-\!5'
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Read the mRNA in 5′→3′ direction.**
The mRNA read 5′→3′ in the target region is `AUACAUA`, which matches the input `…AUACAU…` (with surrounding context).

**Step 3 — Check Watson–Crick pairing.**
All seven positions form canonical pairs (A–U, U–A, A–U, G–C, U–A, A–U, U–A). Perfect 7-nt seed match — this is a **7mer-m8** by itself.

**Step 4 — Check for position 1 anchor (A opposite target position 1).**
An additional match at miRNA position 1 (here U pairing with A in mRNA) upgrades the site to an **8mer**.

**Step 5 — Check AU content.**
If an A or U flanks position 1 on the mRNA side (e.g., a downstream A after the seed), this is a **7mer-A1** enhancement. If both position 1 match AND AU flanking are present, the site is a full **8mer** — the strongest seed type.

**Final Classification:** An **8mer** site (best-case seed match) ⇒ highest predicted repression.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — Argonaute Stoichiometry

> **Problem:** In an in vitro RISC assembly assay, 100 pmol of mature miRNA duplex is loaded onto 80 pmol of recombinant AGO2. Calculate (i) the maximum amount of active RISC formed and (ii) the percentage of miRNA loaded.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Recall the 1:1 stoichiometry.**
Each AGO2 binds exactly one mature miRNA strand (the other is discarded). So $1 \text{ mol AGO2} \equiv 1 \text{ mol active RISC}$.

**Step 2 — Limiting reagent analysis.**

$$
n_{\text{AGO2}} = 80 \; \text{pmol}, \quad n_{\text{miRNA duplex}} = 100 \; \text{pmol}
$$

AGO2 is the limiting reagent.

**Step 3 — Maximum RISC formed.**

$$
n_{\text{RISC,max}} = \min(80, 100) = 80 \; \text{pmol}
$$

**Step 4 — Percentage loading of miRNA.**

$$
\%\text{loaded} = \dfrac{80}{100} \times 100\% = 80\%
$$

**Final Results:**
- Active RISC formed = **80 pmol**
- miRNA loading efficiency = **80%**

> [!NOTE]
> **Biological note:** The unused 20 pmol represents mature miRNA that remained unincorporated and was degraded by cellular nucleases — this matches experimental loading efficiencies of ~70–90% in typical in vitro RISC assembly setups.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Canonical miRNA Biogenesis Pathway

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the canonical biogenesis pathway with all major enzymatic and compartmentalization events. **All node IDs are alphanumeric with letter prefixes; labels are kept clean (no markdown formatting inside the quoted labels).**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[GENOMIC MIRNA LOCUS] --> A2[RNAPOL II TRANSCRIPTION]
    A2 --> A3[PRI-MIRNA 5PRIME CAP POLY A TAIL APPROX 1 TO 3 KB]
    A3 --> A4[NUCLEAR MICROPROCESSOR COMPLEX]
    A4 --> A5[DROSHA RNASE III CLEAVES 11 BP FROM STEM SS JUNCTION]
    A4 --> A6[DGCR8 DSRBP POSITIONS DROSHA ACCURATELY]
    A5 --> A7[PRE-MIRNA HAIRPIN APPROX 70 NT WITH 2 NT 3PRIME OVERHANG]
    A6 --> A7
    A7 --> A8[NUCLEAR EXPORT VIA EXPORTIN 5 AND RAN GTP]
    A8 --> A9[CYTOPLASMIC DICER PROCESSING]
    A9 --> A10[DICER RNASE III TRBP PACT CLEAVES LOOP]
    A10 --> A11[MIRNA DUPLEX APPROX 22 NT WITH 2 NT 3PRIME OVERHANGS]
    A11 --> A12[RISC LOADING ONTO ARGONAUTE AGO1 TO AGO4]
    A12 --> A13[STRAND SELECTION 5PRIME LESS STABLE STRAND RETAINED]
    A13 --> A14[MATURE RISC GUIDE STRAND RETAINED PASSENGER STRAND DEGRADED]
    A14 --> A15[TARGET MRNA 3PRIME UTR RECOGNITION VIA SEED POSITIONS 2 TO 8]
    A15 --> A16[TRANSLATIONAL REPRESSION AND OR MRNA DESTABILIZATION]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Trace the path top-to-bottom — each downward arrow represents a temporal and compartmental step. The two vertical "helper" nodes `DGCR8 DSRBP` and `RAN GTP` participate as **co-factors** rather than as separate sequential reactions.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of the RISC Repression Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[MATURE MIRNA RISC COMPLEX] --> B2[CYTOPLASMIC SCANNING OF MRNA POOL]
    B2 --> B3{SEED MATCH PRESENT IN 3PRIME UTR}
    B3 -- YES --> B4[INITIAL SEED PAIRING POSITIONS 2 TO 8]
    B3 -- NO --> B5[RELEASE MRNA NO REPRESSION]
    B4 --> B6[SUPPLEMENTARY PAIRING POSITIONS 13 TO 16]
    B6 --> B7[RECRUITMENT OF GW182 AND DEADENYLASE COMPLEX]
    B7 --> B8[PABP DISSOCIATION AND POLY A TAIL SHORTENING]
    B8 --> B9[DECAPPING BY DCP1 DCP2]
    B9 --> B10[5PRIME TO 3PRIME DECAY BY XRN1]
    B8 --> B11[TRANSLATIONAL INITIATION BLOCK EIF4E CAP RECOGNITION INTERFERENCE]
    B11 --> B12[RIBOSOMAL RECRUITMENT FAILURE]
    B10 --> B13[GENE EXPRESSION REDUCED]
    B12 --> B13
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** The flow shows two parallel downstream effector arms — (i) the **mRNA decay branch** (via deadenylation → decapping → XRN1) and (ii) the **translational repression branch** (via eIF4E competition). Both feed into the common phenotypic endpoint: reduced protein output.

---

## 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram — miRNA vs siRNA Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[INPUT SMALL RNA] --> C2{ENDOGENOUS GENOMIC ORIGIN}
    C2 -- YES --> C3[PRIMARY MICRORNA PATHWAY]
    C2 -- NO --> C4[EXOGENOUS DSRNA OR SHORTHAIRPIN]
    C3 --> C5[DROSHA PLUS DGCR8 PROCESSING]
    C5 --> C6[PRE-MIRNA HAIRPIN APPROX 70 NT]
    C6 --> C7[EXPORTIN 5 NUCLEAR EXPORT]
    C7 --> C8[DICER CYTOPLASMIC PROCESSING]
    C4 --> C8
    C8 --> C9[APPROX 22 NT SMALL RNA DUPLEX]
    C9 --> C10{ARGONAUTE LOADED COMPLEMENTARITY PROFILE}
    C10 -- PARTIAL SEED ONLY --> C11[MIRNA LIKE RISC TRANSLATIONAL REPRESSION]
    C10 -- PERFECT OR NEAR PERFECT --> C12[SIRNA LIKE RISC SLICING AND MRNA CLEAVAGE]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU tip:** The critical decision node is **C10** — *complementarity profile to the target*. If the loaded small RNA pairs perfectly across its entire length (as siRNAs typically do), the Argonaute endonuclease activity is activated and the target is cleaved. If pairing is restricted to the seed (as miRNAs typically are), the slicer activity is suppressed and downstream GW182-mediated repression dominates.

---

## 4.4 Sequence-Annotation Diagram — Anatomy of a Pre-miRNA Hairpin

The following textual schematic shows the canonical features of a pre-miRNA stem-loop. The vertical bars `|` denote canonical Watson–Crick base pairs; colons `:` denote G:U wobble pairs tolerated in the supplementary region.

```
          5'-                    Loop region (variable, ~10-30 nt)                    -3'
               G   C                                  A
               | : |                                 U-A
               U   A                                 A-U
               C   G                                 C-G        ← Upper stem
               G   C                                 G-C
               U-A
               G-C
               C-G
               A-U                          ← Mature miRNA strand (red, ~22 nt)
               U-A
               C-G
               A-U
               G-C
               U-A
               C-G                          ← Passenger miRNA strand (blue, ~22 nt)
               U-A
               G-C
               C-G
               A-U
               U-A
            ↓ Drosha cut site                                    ↓ Dicer cut site
         (2-nt 3' overhang)                                  (2-nt 3' overhang)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Highlight:** Note the **two 2-nt 3′ overhangs** produced by the staggered cuts of Drosha (nuclear) and Dicer (cytoplasmic). This overhang geometry is the molecular "fingerprint" that Exportin-5 recognises to ferry the pre-miRNA out of the nucleus.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

### Q1. Define microRNA. Mention any two distinguishing features between miRNA and siRNA.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark):** MicroRNAs (miRNAs) are small, endogenous, single-stranded, non-coding RNA molecules of ~21–23 nucleotides that regulate gene expression post-transcriptionally by guiding the RNA-induced silencing complex (RISC) to target mRNAs.

**Distinguishing features (2 marks):**

| Feature | miRNA | siRNA |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | Endogenous (genome-encoded) | Exogenous / long dsRNA |
| Complementarity | Partial (seed region) | Perfect / near-perfect |
| Target spectrum | Many mRNAs | Single cognate transcript |

---

### Q2. What is the seed region of a miRNA and why is it critical for target recognition?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** The seed region consists of nucleotides **2 → 8** at the 5′ end of the mature miRNA. It is the **core determinant of target specificity** because it forms the initial, thermodynamically favourable Watson–Crick base-pairing with the 3′ UTR of the target mRNA.

**Significance (1.5 marks):** A perfect seed match — particularly the **8mer > 7mer-m8 > 7mer-A1 > 6mer** hierarchy — is the single most predictive feature in computational target prediction algorithms such as **TargetScan** and **miRanda**. Without seed pairing, even fully complementary 3′ regions cannot compensate for target engagement.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Biogenesis & Mechanism

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / 2024 Scheme]**

**Q.A (a)** With a neat labelled diagram, describe the canonical biogenesis pathway of microRNA. Highlight the role of Drosha, Dicer, and Argonaute.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Pri-miRNA transcription — 1 mark.**
Most miRNA genes are transcribed by **RNA Polymerase II** (some by Pol III) to produce a long primary transcript called **pri-miRNA**. The pri-miRNA carries a 5′ m⁷G cap, a 3′ poly(A) tail, and contains a local stem–loop (hairpin) structure of ~30–70 bp flanked by single-stranded RNA.

**(ii) Microprocessor cleavage (Drosha + DGCR8) — 1.5 marks.**
In the nucleus, the **Microprocessor complex** comprising the RNase III enzyme **Drosha** and its dsRNA-binding partner **DGCR8** recognizes the pri-miRNA hairpin. Drosha cleaves ~11 bp from the stem–single-stranded junction, releasing a ~70 nt hairpin with a 2-nt 3′ overhang called the **pre-miRNA**.

**(iii) Nuclear export (Exportin-5) — 0.5 mark.**
The pre-miRNA is recognised by **Exportin-5** in complex with Ran-GTP and shuttled into the cytoplasm.

**(iv) Dicer processing — 1.5 marks.**
In the cytoplasm, the RNase III enzyme **Dicer**, assisted by **TRBP** and **PACT**, cleaves the pre-miRNA loop end. The product is a ~22 nt **miRNA duplex** with 2-nt 3′ overhangs at both ends.

**(v) RISC loading & strand selection — 1.5 marks.**
The duplex is loaded onto an **Argonaute (AGO1–AGO4)** protein. Strand selection is governed by the thermodynamic asymmetry of the duplex ends — the strand with the **less stably base-paired 5′ end** becomes the **guide strand** (mature miRNA); the other (**passenger strand**) is unwound and degraded.

**(vi) Refer to Mermaid diagram in SECTION 4.1 — 1 mark** (for neat labelled diagram).

---

**Q.A (b)** Explain the mechanism of miRNA-mediated gene silencing. Discuss seed-mediated target recognition and the roles of GW182 and the deadenylase complex.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Seed-mediated target recognition — 2 marks.**
The mature RISC scans mRNA pools in the cytoplasm. Initial binding occurs at the **3′ UTR** through Watson–Crick pairing of the miRNA **seed region (positions 2–8)**. Optimal sites are **8mer** (full seed + position 1 match + AU flanking) > **7mer-m8** (full seed + position 1 or AU) > **7mer-A1** > **6mer**. Supplementary pairing at positions 13–16 stabilises the interaction but is not strictly required in mammals.

**(ii) RISC composition and GW182 recruitment — 2 marks.**
The mature RISC contains **Argonaute** bound to the guide miRNA and the scaffolding protein **GW182 (TNRC6 in humans)**. GW182 serves as the central hub that recruits downstream effector complexes.

**(iii) mRNA destabilisation pathway — 1.5 marks.**
GW182 recruits the **CCR4–NOT deadenylase complex**, which shortens the poly(A) tail. Deadenylation triggers dissociation of **PABP** and recruitment of **decapping enzymes DCP1/DCP2**. The decapped mRNA is then degraded by the **5′→3′ exonuclease XRN1**.

**(iv) Translational repression — 1 mark.**
Independently of decay, RISC interferes with translation at the **initiation step** by competing with eIF4E for the 5′ cap and by preventing 60S subunit joining. Post-initiation repression (ribosome drop-off) has also been reported.

**(v) Outcome — 0.5 mark.**
Net biological result: **fine-tuning of protein output** with minimal change in steady-state mRNA levels (in mammalian systems) or with marked mRNA decay (in plant-like systems with near-perfect complementarity).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part-B biogenesis):**
> Students frequently lose **2–3 marks** by:
> 1. Confusing the **Drosha vs Dicer cleavage positions** — remember: Drosha cuts ~11 bp from the stem–ssRNA junction (basal side), Dicer cuts ~22 nt from the loop end (apical side).
> 2. Forgetting to mention **Exportin-5 + Ran-GTP** as the nuclear export mechanism.
> 3. Omitting **2-nt 3′ overhangs** at the pre-miRNA ends (a favourite 1-mark trap question).
> 4. Failing to identify the **seed region (positions 2–8)** when asked about target recognition.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Comparative & Application-Focused

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / 2024 Scheme – Alternative Module Choice]**

**Q.B (a)** Compare miRNA and siRNA with respect to their (i) origin, (ii) biogenesis, (iii) complementarity requirement, and (iv) mechanism of gene silencing.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Origin — 1 mark.**
- **miRNA:** Endogenous; encoded by the host genome; transcribed from intronic, intergenic, or exonic loci.
- **siRNA:** Exogenous (introduced experimentally) or rarely endogenous (e.g., transposon-derived, natural antisense transcripts).

**(ii) Biogenesis — 2 marks.**
- **miRNA canonical pathway:** Pri-miRNA → Drosha/DGCR8 → pre-miRNA → Exportin-5 → Dicer/TRBP → miRNA duplex → AGO loading.
- **siRNA pathway:** Long dsRNA or short hairpin → Dicer (no Drosha step) → siRNA duplex → AGO loading.

**(iii) Complementarity requirement — 2 marks.**
- **miRNA:** **Partial** complementarity — only the seed (positions 2–8) needs near-perfect pairing. 3′ supplementary pairing enhances stability but is dispensable.
- **siRNA:** **Perfect / near-perfect** complementarity across the central region — required for catalytic slicing by AGO2.

**(iv) Mechanism of silencing — 2 marks.**
- **miRNA:** Translational repression (initiation block + post-initiation) + mRNA destabilisation via GW182/deadenylase/decapping/XRN1.
- **siRNA:** Catalytic cleavage of the target mRNA at the position opposite the centre of the siRNA guide (between positions 10–11 of the siRNA) by AGO2's RNase-H-like endonuclease activity.

---

**Q.B (b)** Discuss the therapeutic applications of microRNAs. Briefly describe the design and mechanism of **antagomirs** and **miRNA sponges**.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Therapeutic rationale — 1 mark.**
miRNAs are **druggable** because (a) they have well-defined ~22 nt sequences, (b) their dysregulation is causal in many diseases (cancer, cardiovascular, viral, metabolic), and (c) chemically modified oligonucleotides can mimic or inhibit them with high affinity.

**(ii) Antagomirs / Anti-miR oligonucleotides — 3 marks.**
**Design:** Single-stranded RNA analogues, 19–23 nt long, perfectly complementary to the mature target miRNA. To enhance stability and cellular uptake, they are modified with:
- **2′-O-methyl** or **2′-O-methoxyethyl (2′-MOE)** ribose modifications,
- **Phosphorothioate (PS)** backbone linkages (nuclease resistance),
- A **cholesterol conjugate** at the 3′ end (for in vivo delivery).

**Mechanism:** The antagomir base-pairs with the mature miRNA, sequestering it from RISC and preventing target engagement. Duplex formation triggers degradation of the miRNA by cellular RNases.

**Example:** **Miravirsen** (anti-miR-122) — first miRNA-targeted drug to enter clinical trials, used against Hepatitis C virus.

**(iii) miRNA sponges — 2 marks.**
**Design:** Engineered transcripts (often viral vector-encoded) containing **multiple tandem miRNA recognition elements (MREs)** complementary to a target miRNA — typically 4–8 imperfectly matched bulged sites to prevent AGO-mediated slicing of the sponge itself.

**Mechanism:** The sponge acts as a **competitive inhibitor**, titrating out the miRNA from endogenous targets. It is expressed from strong RNA Pol III promoters (e.g., U6, H1) for high cellular levels.

**Advantage over antagomirs:** Long-term, heritable inhibition (when integrated into the genome) — useful in transgenic models.

**(iv) Other applications — 1 mark.**
- **miRNA mimics** (tumour suppressor restoration, e.g., MRX34 — miR-34a mimic in liver cancer).
- **Circulating miRNA biomarkers** for non-invasive diagnostics.
- **CRISPR/Cas13-based miRNA editing** (next-generation tools).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part-B comparative/therapeutic):**
> Students frequently lose **2–3 marks** by:
> 1. Writing that antagomirs are **DNA** molecules — they are **modified RNA**, not DNA.
> 2. Forgetting the **cholesterol conjugate** and **phosphorothioate backbone** as essential design elements of antagomirs.
> 3. Confusing **miRNA sponges** with **siRNAs** — sponges are **target mimics**, not silencing RNAs.
> 4. Missing the **bulged MRE design** in sponges — perfect complementarity would lead to AGO2-mediated cleavage of the sponge itself.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section and revise 24 hours before the KTU exam.**

- ✅ **Definition:** miRNA = ~22 nt endogenous non-coding RNA → post-transcriptional gene silencing.
- ✅ **Discovery:** lin-4 (1993, *C. elegans*), let-7 (2000) — first conserved miRNA.
- ✅ **Biogenesis enzymes (memorize order):**
  Nuclear → **RNA Pol II** → pri-miRNA → **Drosha + DGCR8** → pre-miRNA → **Exportin-5 / Ran-GTP** → cytoplasm → **Dicer + TRBP** → miRNA duplex → **Argonaute** → mature RISC.
- ✅ **Pre-miRNA hallmark:** ~70 nt hairpin with **2-nt 3′ overhang**.
- ✅ **Mature miRNA:** ~22 nt duplex → **strand selection** by 5′ thermodynamic asymmetry → guide strand loaded onto AGO.
- ✅ **Seed region:** nucleotides **2–8** of the 5′ end — non-negotiable for target ID.
- ✅ **Seed match hierarchy (best → worst):** **8mer > 7mer-m8 > 7mer-A1 > 6mer**.
- ✅ **RISC effectors:** **GW182 → CCR4–NOT (deadenylation) → DCP1/2 (decapping) → XRN1 (5′→3′ decay)** *and* **eIF4E cap competition (translational block)**.
- ✅ **miRNA vs siRNA:** partial vs perfect complementarity; many vs single targets; translational repression/deadenylation vs slicing.
- ✅ **miRNA nomenclature:** `miR-21-5p` = mature strand from 5′ arm of pre-miR-21; deprecated `miR-21\*` notation should not be used.
- ✅ **Therapeutic tools:** **Antagomirs** (chemically modified 2′-O-methyl/PS/cholesterol anti-miRs), **miRNA mimics**, **miRNA sponges** (tandem MREs), **Miravirsen** (anti-miR-122).
- ✅ **Quantitative formulas to remember:**
  - $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$
  - $R_{\text{total}} = 1 - \prod (1 - r_j)$ for combinatorial repression
  - $\Delta G^{\circ}_{37} = \Delta G^{\circ}_{\text{init}} + \sum \Delta G^{\circ}_{\text{stack}}$ for seed duplex free energy.
- ✅ **Common KTU pitfalls to avoid:**
  - Calling antagomirs "DNA" — they are modified **RNA**.
  - Confusing Drosha and Dicer cleavage directions.
  - Omitting 2-nt 3′ overhangs.
  - Forgetting to cite seed region when describing target recognition.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Construction of siRNA Vectors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT524 - GENETIC ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Construction of siRNA Vectors**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **siRNA Vector (Definition – KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)**
> A **siRNA expression vector** is a recombinant DNA plasmid or viral construct engineered to intracellularly transcribe a short double-stranded RNA molecule (~19–25 bp) that triggers the **RNA interference (RNAi)** pathway, leading to sequence-specific post-transcriptional gene silencing of a target mRNA.

In the context of KTU Module 4, an siRNA vector is essentially a **"molecular delivery truck"** that brings the genetic blueprint for a small RNA weapon into a host cell. Once inside, the host's own enzymes (Drosha/Dicer) process this blueprint into mature siRNA, which then silences a specific disease gene.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Photocopier Sabotage"**
> - Imagine a **photocopier (the cell)** that keeps producing **fake currency (target protein)** from a **master template (mRNA)**.
> - A **detective (siRNA)** is hired to shred the master template so the photocopier stops making the fakes.
> - The **siRNA vector** is simply the *briefcase* the detective arrives in — it carries the *exact shredder instructions* to the photocopier room.
> - Without the briefcase (vector), the detective (siRNA) cannot get inside the building (cell).

So when we "construct an siRNA vector," we are essentially **packing the right instructions into a DNA suitcase** that, once delivered, will tell the cell to destroy a specific mRNA.

## 1.3 Key Vocabulary You Must Memorize

| Term | Meaning (KTU standard) |
|---|---|
| **siRNA** | Small Interfering RNA (~21–23 nt dsRNA with 2 nt 3′ overhangs) |
| **shRNA** | Short Hairpin RNA (precursor transcribed from vector) |
| **RISC** | RNA-Induced Silencing Complex (Argonaute 2 + siRNA) |
| **Pol III Promoter** | RNA Polymerase III promoter (e.g., **H1, U6**) — preferred for siRNA vectors |
| **Pol II Promoter** | RNA Polymerase II promoter (e.g., **CMV**) — used for miRNA-based vectors |
| **Knockdown** | Reduction (not elimination) of gene expression |
| **OFF-TARGET Effect** | Unintended silencing of non-target genes |
| **Transfect / Transduce** | Delivery of vector into eukaryotic cells |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Strategic Logic of Vector Design — Why Each Element Exists

An siRNA vector must accomplish **four engineering objectives**:
1. **Drive strong transcription** of a short RNA inside the mammalian cell.
2. **Fold into a stem-loop (shRNA)** that mimics pre-miRNA structure.
3. **Be processed correctly** by Drosha/Dicer to mature siRNA.
4. **Provide selection & traceability** for the transfected cells.

Each element in the vector architecture directly satisfies one of these objectives.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Four Architectural Modules of an siRNA Vector**
> - **Module 1 — Promoter Block** (drives transcription)
> - **Module 2 — shRNA Coding Cassette** (sense + loop + antisense + terminator)
> - **Module 3 — Selection/Reporter Block** (Neomycin / GFP / Puromycin)
> - **Module 4 — Bacterial Propagation Block** (Ori + AmpR for plasmid amplification)

## 2.2 High-Yield Component Map

### Module 1: Promoter Selection

| Promoter Type | Example | Length (bp) | Pol | Why Used |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pol III** | **H1, U6, 7SK, tRNA** | ~250–400 | III | Defines clean start/end; produces short transcripts |
| **Pol II** | **CMV, EF1α, CAG** | ~600 | II | Allows tissue-specific / inducible expression |
| **Tissue-specific** | **NSE, Synapsin** | varies | II | Restricts siRNA expression to specific cell types |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Standard:** For gene-silencing studies, **U6 and H1 promoters are GOLD STANDARD** because they produce **short, well-defined transcripts** with a definite 5′ start (G for U6) and a termination signal of 4–5 consecutive T residues.

### Module 2: shRNA Cassette Anatomy

The shRNA cassette is the heart of the vector. Its sequence is:

$$5'-\text{[Promoter]}-\underbrace{\text{Sense (19–29 nt)}}_{\text{target-identical}}-\underbrace{\text{Loop (5–9 nt)}}_{\text{spacer}}-\underbrace{\text{Antisense (19–29 nt)}}_{\text{complementary to sense}}-\underbrace{\text{T_{4-5}}}_{\text{Pol III terminator}}-3'$$

#### Design Rules (Critical for Exam):

| Parameter | Recommended Value | KTU Exam Tip |
|---|---|---|
| **siRNA target length** | 19–25 nt (typically 19 + 19 with 2 nt overhangs) | Mention 21–23 nt most common |
| **GC content of stem** | **30–52 %** | Avoid >55 % → secondary structure issues |
| **Loop length** | **5–9 nt** (classic: `TTCAAGAGA`) | Standard: 9 nt from *Brummelkamp et al.* |
| **Thermodynamic asymmetry** | **5′ antisense end must be less stable** | A/U-rich at 5′ antisense = better RISC loading |
| **Termination signal** | **TTTTTT** (4–6 T's) | Mandatory for Pol III termination |
| **BLAST specificity** | Must pass NCBI BLAST | Avoid ≥16 nt contiguous homology to off-targets |
| **Avoid internal stretches** | No run of ≥4 same nt, no hairpin inside siRNA | Reduces efficiency |

### Module 3 & 4: Selection & Propagation

- **Bacterial selection:** Ampicillin (`AmpR`) or Kanamycin (`KanR`) resistance gene.
- **Eukaryotic selection:** Neomycin (`NeoR`) → G418, or Puromycin resistance.
- **Reporter (optional):** GFP, mCherry, or LacZ for visual tracking.
- **Origin of replication:** *pUC ori* (high copy) for bacterial amplification; **SV40 ori** optional for mammalian replication.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Rule | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|
| $T_m = 64.9 + 41 \times \frac{(G+C - 16.4)}{L}$ | Wallace rule for short oligos (L = oligo length) | Predict annealing of siRNA strands |
| $T_m = 81.5 + 16.6\log_{10}[Na^+] + 0.41(\%GC) - \frac{675}{L}$ | Salt-adjusted formula for longer dsDNA | Used for shRNA stem |
| $\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K_{eq}$ | Gibbs free energy of duplex formation | Predict RISC-loading strand |
| $\%\text{GC} = \frac{G+C}{A+T+G+C} \times 100$ | GC content calculation | Design quality check |
| 5′ Antisense $\Delta G$ rule | $ \vert \Delta G_{5'\text{-AS}} \vert < \vert \Delta G_{5'\text{-S}} \vert$ | Khvorova/Schwarz asymmetry rule |
| Loop: `TTCAAGAGA` (9 nt) | Brummelkamp standard | Classic shRNA loop |
| Terminator: 4–6 × T | TTTTT | Pol III stop |
| Stealth modification | 2′O-Me at position 2 of sense strand | Reduces off-target + immune response |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Therapeutics:** siRNA vectors are used to silence oncogenes (e.g., *BCL-2*, *KRAS*), viral genes (HBV, HIV-1), and neurodegenerative targets (HTT in Huntington's).
- **Functional Genomics:** Genome-wide **shRNA libraries** (e.g., TRC, RNAi Consortium) contain hundreds of thousands of siRNA vectors — one per gene.
- **Agriculture:** siRNA vectors silence pest/virus genes in transgenic crops.
- **Drug Discovery:** Loss-of-function screening to identify drug targets.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exam:** siRNA vectors = **stable, long-term gene silencing** (integrating lentiviral vectors or episomal plasmids); synthetic siRNA transfection = **transient silencing** (3–7 days).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The shRNA Sequence — Worked Construction Problem

> **Worked Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)**
> **Target mRNA region (sense strand, 5′→3′):**
> `5′-AAG GTC TAC GTG AAG AAC GAG TTC AAG-3′`
> You are asked to design a Pol III (U6) promoter-driven shRNA vector to silence this gene.

### Step 1 — Identify the 19-nt siRNA target

Pick a 19-nt region from the open reading frame (ORF), preferably 50–100 nt downstream of the start codon, and check GC content.

$$\text{Selected sense: } 5'\text{-GGUCUACGUGAAGAACGAG-3'}$$

Length $L = 19$ nt.

### Step 2 — Calculate the GC content

Count the bases in `GGUCUACGUGAAGAACGAG`:

$$\text{G} = 6,\ \text{C} = 2,\ \text{A} = 5,\ \text{U} = 6$$
$$\%\text{GC} = \frac{6+2}{6+2+5+6} \times 100 = \frac{8}{19} \times 100 \approx 42.1\%$$

This is within the **30–52 %** sweet spot ⇒ design passes.

### Step 3 — Derive the antisense strand

Antisense = reverse complement of sense (written 5′→3′):

$$\text{Antisense: } 5'\text{-CUCGUUCUUCACGUAGACC-3'}$$

Verify by reverse complementing:

$$5'\text{-GGUCUACGUGAAGAACGAG-3'} \xrightarrow{\text{complement}} 3'\text{-CCAGAUGCACUUCUUGCUC-5'} \xrightarrow{\text{reverse}} 5'\text{-CUCGUUCUUCACGUAGACC-3'}$$

### Step 4 — Calculate Tm of the sense:antisense duplex (Wallace rule)

$$T_m = 64.9 + 41 \times \frac{(G+C - 16.4)}{L}$$

For the 19-nt duplex, the count is the same (since it's a duplex): G+C = 8, L = 19.

$$T_m = 64.9 + 41 \times \frac{(8 - 16.4)}{19}$$
$$T_m = 64.9 + 41 \times \frac{-8.4}{19}$$
$$T_m = 64.9 + 41 \times (-0.4421)$$
$$T_m = 64.9 - 18.13$$
$$T_m \approx 46.8\ ^\circ\text{C}$$

This is reasonable for a 19-nt siRNA (literature range ~40–55 °C).

### Step 5 — Check 5′ antisense thermodynamic asymmetry (Schwarz Rule)

Compute approximate ΔG values for the two ends of the duplex (using nearest-neighbor approximations for RNA, kcal/mol):

| Position | Nearest-Neighbor Stack | Approx. ΔG (kcal/mol) |
|---|---|---|
| 5′ Sense end | `5'-GG/UC-3'` (very stable) | ≈ −2.8 |
| 5′ Antisense end | `5'-CU/CG-3'` (less stable) | ≈ −1.4 |

Since $\vert \Delta G_{5'\text{-antisense}} \vert < \vert \Delta G_{5'\text{-sense}} \vert$, the antisense strand will be **preferentially loaded into RISC** — a desirable feature.

### Step 6 — Insert the loop and terminator

Final shRNA cassette (RNA form, 5′→3′):

$$\underbrace{\text{GGUCUACGUGAAGAACGAG}}_{\text{Sense (19 nt)}}\ -\ \underbrace{\text{UUCAAGAGA}}_{\text{Loop (9 nt)}}\ -\ \underbrace{\text{CUCGUUCUUCACGUAGACC}}_{\text{Antisense (19 nt)}}\ -\ \underbrace{\text{UUUUUU}}_{\text{Pol III terminator}}$$

The corresponding **DNA oligo** (top strand, to be cloned) is:

$$\boxed{5'\text{-GG TCTACGTG AAGAACGAG TTCAAGAGA CTCGTTCTTCACGTAGACC TTTTTT-3'}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Pitfall:** Students often forget that the **sense strand of the shRNA is identical to the target mRNA**, while the **antisense strand is the one that guides RISC**. Marking will penalize this confusion.

## 3.2 Cloning Strategy — The Two-Oligo Annealing Method (Step-by-Step)

This is the **standard KTU Module 4 construction method** for siRNA vectors.

### Step 1 — Design two complementary oligonucleotides

| Oligo | Composition (5′→3′) |
|---|---|
| **Top oligo** | `[restriction site 1] + [sense] + [loop] + [antisense RC] + [terminator] + [restriction site 1 RC]` |
| **Bottom oligo** | Complement of top oligo (anneals perfectly) |

Example using **BamHI** (`GGATCC`) and **HindIII** (`AAGCTT`) sites:

$$\text{Top: } 5'\text{-GATCC GGUCUACGUGAAGAACGAG UUCAAGAGA CUCGUUCUUCACGUAGACC TTTTTT A-3'}$$
$$\text{Bottom: } 3'\text{-G CCAGAUGCACUUCUUGCUC AAGUUCUCU GAGCAAGAAGUGCAUCUGG AAAAAA TTCGA-5'}$$

### Step 2 — Anneal the two oligos

Mix equal molar amounts, heat to **95 °C for 5 min**, then cool slowly to room temperature (1 °C/min). This generates a **sticky-end duplex** with BamHI/HindIII overhangs.

### Step 3 — Digest the vector backbone

Cut the destination plasmid (e.g., **pSUPER**, **pLKO.1**, **pSilencer**) with **BamHI + HindIII**.

### Step 4 — Ligate

Use **T4 DNA ligase** at 16 °C overnight (or 25 °C for 30 min with quick ligase).

$$\text{Vector (BamHI/HindIII-cut)} + \text{Annealed insert} \xrightarrow{\text{T4 ligase}} \text{Recombinant siRNA vector}$$

### Step 5 — Transform into competent *E. coli* (DH5α)

Plate on **Ampicillin (100 µg/mL)** LB agar.

### Step 6 — Screen colonies

- **Colony PCR** using vector-flanking primers.
- **Restriction digestion** with BamHI/HindIII to release the ~58 bp insert.
- **Sequencing** with U6 or H1 forward primer.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Vector Backbone

| Vector System | Promoter | Delivery | Integration | Silencing Duration | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **pSUPER** | H1 (Pol III) | Transfection | Episomal | Transient–Stable | Easy in vitro work |
| **pLKO.1** | U6 (Pol III) | Lentiviral | **Integrating** | **Permanent** | Hard-to-transfect cells |
| **pLVTHM** | H1 (Pol III) + EF1α-GFP | Lentiviral | Integrating | Permanent + visual | Tracking transduction |
| **BLOCK-iT Pol II miR** | CMV (Pol II) | Transfection/Lentiviral | Variable | Tissue-specific | miRNA-embedded shRNA |
| **AAV-shRNA** | U6/H1 | Adeno-associated virus | Episomal (long-term) | Months in vivo | Gene therapy |
| **Adenoviral shRNA** | U6 | Adenoviral | Episomal | 1–2 weeks | High-titer transient |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU exam:** If the question asks *"design a vector for stable silencing in primary mammalian cells,"* answer = **lentiviral pLKO.1 backbone**. If the question asks *"for in vitro screening in a cancer cell line,"* answer = **pSUPER or pSilencer** (transfection-grade).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

All node IDs are **purely alphanumeric** (e.g., `prom1`, `shRNAcassette`, `ori1`); no reserved Mermaid keywords are used as node IDs; all labels are **clean uppercase alphanumeric text** with no markdown formatting inside double quotes.

## 4.2 Master Architecture — Anatomy of a siRNA Expression Vector

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ori_block [BACTERIAL PROPAGATION MODULE]
        ori1[ORI pUC HIGH COPY]
        ampr1[AMPR BETA LACTAMASE]
    end

    subgraph sel_block [EUKARYOTIC SELECTION MODULE]
        promcmv1[CMV PROMOTER]
        neor1[NEOR G418 RESISTANCE]
        polya1[SV40 POLY A]
    end

    subgraph shrna_block [shRNA EXPRESSION CASSETTE]
        promu6[U6 OR H1 POL III PROMOTER]
        sense1[SENSE 19 NT]
        loop1[LOOP TTCAAGAGA 9 NT]
        anti1[ANTISENSE 19 NT]
        term1[TERMINATOR TTTTT T6]
    end

    promu6 --> sense1 --> loop1 --> anti1 --> term1
    promcmv1 --> neor1 --> polya1
    ampr1 --- ori1
```

**Reading the diagram (left → right = direction of transcription / replication function):**
- Top cassette: shRNA expression — produces the silencing RNA.
- Middle cassette: Mammalian selection — enriches transfected cells.
- Bottom cassette: Bacterial propagation — amplifies plasmid in *E. coli*.

## 4.3 Functional Flow — From Vector to Gene Silencing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[siRNA VECTOR PLASMID DNA] --> B[TRANSFECT OR TRANSDUCE INTO MAMMALIAN CELL]
    B --> C[POL III TRANSCRIBES shRNA PRECURSOR]
    C --> D[shRNA EXPORTED TO CYTOPLASM BY EXPORTIN 5]
    D --> E[DICER TRIMS shRNA INTO MATURE siRNA 21 NT]
    E --> F[RISC LOADS siRNA STRAND INTO ARGONAUTE 2]
    F --> G{RNA INDUCED SILENCING COMPLEX FORMED}
    G --> H[TARGET mRNA SCANNED FOR COMPLEMENTARITY]
    H --> I[AGO2 SLICES mRNA AT POSITION 10 11 OF siRNA]
    I --> J[GENE EXPRESSION KNOCKED DOWN 70 TO 95 PERCENT]
```

## 4.4 Cloning Workflow — Two-Oligo Annealing Method

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[DESIGN TOP OLIGO SENSE LOOP AS TTTTTT] --> B1[DESIGN BOTTOM OLIGO COMPLEMENT]
    B1 --> C1[ANNEAL 95 C SLOW COOL]
    A2[CUT pSUPER OR pLKO 1 WITH BAMHI AND HINDIII] --> C1
    C1 --> D1[GEL PURIFY INSERT AND VECTOR BACKBONE]
    D1 --> E1[T4 DNA LIGASE 16 C OVERNIGHT]
    E1 --> F1[TRANSFORM DH5 ALPHA COMPETENT CELLS]
    F1 --> G1[SCREEN ON AMPICILLIN LB PLATES]
    G1 --> H1[COLONY PCR AND SEQUENCING]
    H1 --> I1[RECOMBINANT siRNA VECTOR VERIFIED]
```

## 4.5 Decision Tree — Promoter & Backbone Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{NEED STABLE OR TRANSIENT SILENCING}
    Q1 -- TRANSIENT IN VITRO --> P1[PSUPER OR PSILENCER PLASMID]
    Q1 -- STABLE LONG TERM --> Q2{HARD TO TRANSFECT CELLS}
    Q2 -- YES --> P2[LENTIVIRAL pLKO 1 SYSTEM]
    Q2 -- NO --> P3[STABLE TRANSFECTION WITH pSUPER PURORESIST]
    Q3{NEED TISSUE SPECIFIC} -- YES --> P4[POL II CMV OR TISSUE SPECIFIC PROMOTER]
    Q3{NEED TISSUE SPECIFIC} -- NO --> P5[POL III U6 OR H1 STANDARD]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
> **Q1. Define an siRNA vector. Mention two advantages of using a vector-based siRNA system over synthetic siRNA transfection.**

**Model Answer:**
- An siRNA vector is a plasmid or viral construct engineered to express a short hairpin RNA (shRNA) inside mammalian cells, which is processed by Dicer into mature siRNA triggering RNAi-mediated gene silencing.
- **Advantage 1:** Enables **stable, long-term gene knockdown** (weeks–months) via genomic integration (lentiviral system), unlike transient synthetic siRNA.
- **Advantage 2:** Allows **sustained in vivo delivery** (e.g., lentivirus/AAV), and **tissue-specific / inducible expression** via Pol II promoters — not feasible with synthetic siRNA.

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
> **Q2. Why are Pol III promoters (H1, U6) preferred over Pol II promoters for siRNA/shRNA expression vectors?**

**Model Answer:**
- Pol III promoters have a **well-defined transcription start site** and a simple **TTTTTT-based terminator**, producing **short, homogeneous transcripts** ideal for shRNA.
- They **bypass nuclear processing** (capping, polyadenylation) required by Pol II, yielding transcripts that are directly exported and processed by Dicer.
- They provide **high, constitutive expression** in most cell types, ensuring robust siRNA production.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4, Choice 1]**

### **Question A (14 Marks):** Design and Construction of a Pol III-Driven shRNA Vector
**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** List and explain the major cis- and trans-acting elements required for the construction of a Pol III-driven siRNA expression vector.

**Model Solution:**

| Element | Type | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **U6 / H1 promoter** | Cis | Pol III transcription of shRNA; defines start site |
| **shRNA cassette (sense–loop–antisense)** | Cis | Encodes the silencing RNA |
| **TTTTTT terminator** | Cis | Stops Pol III transcription cleanly |
| **Selection marker (NeoR/PuroR)** | Cis (with its own promoter) | Selects stably transfected cells |
| **Reporter (GFP/LacZ)** | Cis (optional) | Visual tracking of transfection |
| **Bacterial Ori (pUC)** | Cis | Plasmid replication in *E. coli* |
| **AmpR / KanR** | Cis | Bacterial antibiotic selection |
| **RNA Pol III enzyme** | Trans (host) | Transcribes the shRNA |
| **Drosha / Dicer** | Trans (host) | Processes shRNA → siRNA |
| **Ago2 + RISC** | Trans (host) | Executes mRNA cleavage |

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Describe the two-oligo annealing method for cloning an shRNA insert into pSUPER vector. Add a flowchart in your answer.

**Model Solution:**

1. **Design two complementary 60–70 nt oligos** containing (5′→3′): restriction site (BamHI) + sense (19 nt) + loop (9 nt) + antisense RC (19 nt) + TTTTTT terminator + restriction site (HindIII).
2. **Phosphorylate the 5′ end** of each oligo using T4 PNK.
3. **Anneal** equimolar oligos (95 °C, 5 min → slow cool to 25 °C).
4. **Double-digest pSUPER** with BamHI + HindIII; gel-purify the linearized 6.4 kb backbone.
5. **Ligate** insert:backbone (1:3 molar ratio) with T4 DNA ligase, 16 °C, overnight.
6. **Transform** DH5α; plate on Amp+ LB agar.
7. **Screen** colonies by colony PCR and confirm by **Sanger sequencing** with U6 forward primer.

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4, Choice 2]**

### **Question B (14 Marks):** Design Rules and Thermodynamics of shRNA
**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Discuss the key design rules for an efficient siRNA/shRNA. Mention the role of the loop sequence `TTCAAGAGA`.

**Model Solution:**
- **Target selection:** 19 nt from the ORF, 50–100 nt downstream of start codon, with **30–52 % GC** content.
- **Specificity:** BLAST against the transcriptome; avoid ≥16 nt contiguous homology to off-targets.
- **Thermodynamic asymmetry:** 5′ antisense end should be A/U-rich (low stability) for **preferential RISC loading** (Schwarz rule).
- **Avoid internal structures:** No runs of ≥4 identical nucleotides; no strong internal hairpins in the siRNA duplex.
- **Sense-strand avoidance:** Apply chemical modifications (2′-O-Me at position 2 of sense) to reduce off-target effects and immune activation.
- **Loop sequence `TTCAAGAGA`:** A 9-nt loop derived from human microRNA **miR-23** that is **naturally recognized by Drosha and Dicer** in the nucleus, ensuring efficient shRNA → siRNA processing. Its size and flexibility are optimal for proper stem-loop folding.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Calculate the GC content, Tm, and check 5′ antisense stability for the following siRNA target:
`5′-GCATCGATTAAGTGCTAGG-3′` (sense, 19 nt).

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — GC content:**
Count: G = 5, C = 3, A = 4, T/U = 3 + 4 = 7. Total = 19.
$$\%\text{GC} = \frac{5+3}{19}\times 100 = \frac{8}{19}\times 100 \approx 42.1\%$$

→ **Acceptable (30–52 %).**

**Step 2 — Tm (Wallace rule):**
$$T_m = 64.9 + 41 \times \frac{(G+C - 16.4)}{L} = 64.9 + 41 \times \frac{8 - 16.4}{19}$$
$$T_m = 64.9 + 41 \times (-0.4421) = 64.9 - 18.13 \approx 46.8^\circ\text{C}$$

→ **Acceptable (40–55 °C).**

**Step 3 — Antisense 5′ end stability check:**
- Antisense (5′→3′): `5′-CCTAGCACTTAATCGATGC-3′`
- 5′ antisense end: `5′-CC/TA-3'` → stacking ΔG ≈ −1.8 kcal/mol (less stable)
- 5′ sense end: `5′-GC/AT-3'` → stacking ΔG ≈ −2.5 kcal/mol (more stable)

Since $\vert \Delta G_{5'\text{-AS}} \vert < \vert \Delta G_{5'\text{-S}} \vert$, the **antisense strand is preferred by RISC** — a desirable feature. **Design passes all criteria.**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing sense vs. antisense:** Many students write the antisense strand as identical to the target mRNA — that is **the sense strand**. The antisense is the **reverse complement**. (−2 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting the Pol III terminator `TTTTTT`:** Examiners explicitly look for it after the shRNA stem. (−1 mark)
> 3. **Using 2′-O-Me modifications without justification:** Don't just mention it; explain it *reduces off-target effects and innate immune activation (TLR7/8)*. (−1 mark)
> 4. **Skipping the loop sequence explanation:** Always mention that the loop is required for Drosha/Dicer recognition — not just "to fold the RNA into a hairpin." (−1 mark)
> 5. **Forgetting to mention selection marker in vector design:** A complete vector has *both* bacterial (AmpR) and eukaryotic (NeoR/PuroR) selection. (−1 mark)
> 6. **Mixing up Pol II vs Pol III:** Pol II needs poly(A) signal & cap; Pol III uses `TTTTTT` terminator and is preferred for short shRNA. (−2 marks)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- An **siRNA vector** is a DNA construct that expresses **shRNA** intracellularly → processed to **siRNA** → loaded onto **RISC** → silences target **mRNA**.
- **Pol III promoters (U6, H1)** are the gold standard for siRNA vectors; they use a simple `TTTTTT` terminator.
- The **shRNA cassette** = **Sense (19 nt) + Loop (TTCAAGAGA, 9 nt) + Antisense (19 nt) + Terminator (T₄–T₆)**.
- **Key design rules:** 30–52 % GC, no run of ≥4 identical nt, 5′ antisense A/U-rich (Schwarz asymmetry), 21 nt final mature siRNA.
- **Two-oligo annealing** is the standard cloning method: design two complementary oligos → anneal → ligate into BamHI/HindIII-digested pSUPER/pLKO.1 → transform DH5α → sequence-verify.
- **Stable silencing** → use **lentiviral (pLKO.1)** or integrating plasmid; **transient** → use **pSUPER** or synthetic siRNA.
- **Vector components** = Promoter (Pol III/II) + shRNA cassette + Terminator + Eukaryotic selection (NeoR/PuroR) + Reporter (optional) + Bacterial Ori + AmpR.
- **The antisense strand** of the siRNA is the one **loaded into Ago2** to cleave the target mRNA.
- **Off-target effects** are minimized by BLAST, chemical modification (2′-O-Me at sense position 2), and **thermodynamic asymmetry**.
- **RISC slicing site:** Ago2 cleaves target mRNA between **position 10 and 11** of the siRNA guide strand.
- **Therapeutic relevance:** siRNA vectors underlie **FDA-approved therapies** (e.g., patisiran, givosiran) and most preclinical gene-therapy RNAi pipelines.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principle and Application of Gene Silencing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT524 - GENETIC ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Principle and Application of Gene Silencing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gene Silencing** is the regulation of gene expression in a cell to prevent the expression of a particular gene. It may occur during either **transcription** (Transcriptional Gene Silencing – TGS) or **translation** (Post-Transcriptional Gene Silencing – PTGS). It is a fundamental epigenetic and post-transcriptional regulatory mechanism used by cells to suppress the activity of specific genes without altering the underlying DNA sequence.

Gene silencing can be achieved through several molecular strategies that are central tools in modern recombinant DNA technology:

1. **RNA Interference (RNAi)** – sequence-specific degradation of mRNA by small interfering RNAs (siRNA).
2. **MicroRNA (miRNA)-mediated silencing** – endogenous small non-coding RNAs that repress translation.
3. **Antisense RNA technology** – complementary RNA strands that hybridize with target mRNA.
4. **Ribozymes** – catalytically active RNA molecules that cleave specific RNA targets.
5. **CRISPR Interference (CRISPRi)** – catalytically deactivated Cas9 (dCas9)-guided transcriptional repression.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Note (Board-relevant):** The discovery of RNA interference in *C. elegans* by **Andrew Fire and Craig Mello (1998)** earned them the **Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine in 2006**. This is a frequently asked 3-mark question in KTU exams.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a large **library (the genome)** filled with thousands of **instructional recipe books (genes)**. Normally, the librarian (RNA polymerase) photocopies recipes and sends them to the kitchen (ribosome) to be cooked (translation).

**Gene silencing is like handing the librarian a pair of "tweezers" (siRNA/miRNA) that specifically erase or block ONE single recipe so the chef never sees it.** The book still exists on the shelf, but it can never be used.

A more relatable analogy:

| Real-World System | Biological Counterpart |
|---|---|
| A redacted document | Silenced mRNA (degraded, no protein made) |
| A book removed from the shelf but not destroyed | Chromatin-condensed gene (TGS) |
| A key that fits but cannot turn the lock | Antisense RNA bound to mRNA (translation blocked) |
| A "Do Not Photocopy" sticker on a book | Methylation of promoter (TGS) |

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

> [!NOTE]
> - **Typical siRNA length:** **21–23 base pairs (double-stranded)** with **2-nucleotide 3′ overhangs**.
> - **Typical mature miRNA length:** **~22 nucleotides (single-stranded)**.
> - **Effective in vivo siRNA concentration (therapeutic):** **1–100 nM** in mammalian cells.
> - **Dicer cleavage cycle** generates siRNAs approximately every **21–23 nt**.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Gene Silencing — A Structured Breakdown

### A. Transcriptional Gene Silencing (TGS)
Operates at the **DNA level**, preventing transcription.
- **Mechanisms:** Promoter methylation (CpG island methylation), Histone deacetylation, Heterochromatin formation.
- **Outcome:** Chromatin becomes tightly packed (heterochromatin); RNA polymerase II cannot access the promoter.
- **Example:** X-chromosome inactivation; paramutation in maize; genomic imprinting.

### B. Post-Transcriptional Gene Silencing (PTGS)
Operates **after transcription**, on the mRNA product.
- **Mechanisms:**
  - **siRNA-mediated mRNA degradation** (exogenous/RNAi pathway)
  - **miRNA-mediated translational repression** (endogenous pathway)
  - **Antisense RNA blocking ribosome access**
  - **Ribozymatic cleavage**
- **Outcome:** mRNA is degraded or translationally blocked before a functional protein is made.

---

## 2.2 The Two Major Small-RNA Pathways

### Pathway 1 — siRNA (Exogenous) Pathway
**Trigger:** Exogenous long dsRNA (viral, transposon, transfected).
1. **Dicer** (RNase III endonuclease) cleaves long dsRNA → **siRNA duplex (21–23 nt, 2-nt 3′ overhang)**.
2. siRNA is loaded onto the **RNA-Induced Silencing Complex (RISC)**.
3. Argonaute 2 (Ago2) within RISC unwinds the duplex; the **sense (passenger) strand is discarded**.
4. The **antisense (guide) strand** directs RISC to perfectly complementary target mRNA.
5. Ago2 endonucleolytically cleaves the target mRNA at the center of the siRNA:mRNA duplex (between nt 10–11 of the guide).
6. Cleaved mRNA is degraded by exonucleases → **gene silenced**.

### Pathway 2 — miRNA (Endogenous) Pathway
**Trigger:** Endogenous primary miRNA (pri-miRNA) transcribed by Pol II.
1. **Drosha–DGCR8 microprocessor complex** in the nucleus crops pri-miRNA → **pre-miRNA (~70 nt hairpin)**.
2. **Exportin-5** exports pre-miRNA to the cytoplasm.
3. **Dicer** trims pre-miRNA → **mature miRNA duplex (~22 nt)**.
4. Loaded onto **RISC**; guide strand retained.
5. Guide miRNA binds target mRNA with **partial (imperfect) complementarity** — typically in the 3′ UTR.
6. Outcome: **Translational repression** and/or mRNA deadenylation and decay.

---

## 2.3 Antisense RNA, Ribozymes, and CRISPRi

| Strategy | Molecule | Mechanism | Cleavage Site |
|---|---|---|---|
| Antisense RNA | ssRNA complementary to mRNA | Steric block of ribosome; RNase H recruitment | Translation initiation |
| Ribozyme | Catalytic RNA (hammerhead, hairpin) | Self-cleavage of target RNA | After NUX triplet |
| CRISPRi | dCas9 + sgRNA | dCas9 blocks transcription elongation/polymerase binding | Promoter/TSS region |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **dCas9 (Dead Cas9)** has mutations **D10A and H840A** in its RuvC and HNH nuclease domains, abolishing cleavage activity while retaining DNA-binding specificity. This is the foundation of **CRISPR interference (CRISPRi)**.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression / Value | Unit / Note |
|---|---|---|
| siRNA length | $\text{L} = 21 \text{ to } 23$ | nucleotides |
| 3′ overhang length | $\text{O} = 2$ | nucleotides |
| Cleavage position on target mRNA | Between nt 10 and 11 of guide | — |
| RISC composition | Argonaute (Ago) + GW182 + TRBP | — |
| Dicer domain | RNase IIIa, IIIb, PAZ, helicase, dsRBD | — |
| Pre-miRNA length | $\approx 70$ | nt (hairpin) |
| Mature miRNA length | $\approx 22$ | nt (ssRNA) |
| Target complementarity (siRNA) | Perfect / 100% | — |
| Target complementarity (miRNA) | Partial / seed region nt 2–8 | — |
| Seed region (miRNA) | nt 2–8 of mature miRNA | Critical for target recognition |
| Energy threshold (siRNA design) | $\Delta G_{asymmetric} < -1 \text{ kcal/mol}$ | Thermodynamic asymmetry rule |
| Optimal GC content (siRNA) | 30–52% | % |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering / Biotechnology Fields

- **Therapeutics:** FDA-approved **patisiran (Onpattro®, 2018)** — first RNAi therapeutic for hereditary transthyretin-mediated amyloidosis (hATTR).
- **Functional Genomics:** Genome-wide loss-of-function screens using shRNA/siRNA libraries.
- **Agriculture:** Host-Induced Gene Silencing (HIGS) — transgenic plants expressing dsRNA against pest/virus genes (e.g., **Monsanto's SmartStax PRO** corn targeting corn rootworm).
- **Synthetic Biology:** Tunable CRISPRi logic gates for gene circuits.
- **Drug Discovery:** Target validation by silencing disease-associated genes in cell lines and animal models.
---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why siRNA Length is 21–23 nt — A Kinetic/Structural Rationale

The minimal siRNA length is dictated by **Argonaute (Ago) binding pocket geometry**. Let $L$ be the siRNA length.

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{min}} &= \text{MID domain footprint on 5′ end} + \text{PAZ domain footprint on 3′ end} \\
&= 5 \text{ nt (5′ anchor)} + \text{seed-region binding (nt 2–8)} + \text{3′ supplementary (nt 12–16)} + \text{PAZ 2-nt 3′ overhang} \\
L_{\text{min}} &\approx 8 + 4 + 4 + 2 = 18 \text{ nt (theoretical lower bound)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For thermodynamic stability and Dicer processing fidelity:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{opt}} &= L_{\text{min}} + \Delta L_{\text{stability}} \\
&= 18 + (3 \text{ to } 5) \\
&= 21 \text{ to } 23 \text{ nt}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering logic:** Below 21 nt, the thermodynamic stability of the duplex drops below the threshold needed for Ago2-mediated cleavage, and the guide strand dissociates prematurely. Above 23 nt, the duplex triggers **interferon (IFN) response** in mammalian cells through PKR activation, causing off-target toxicity.

> [!NOTE]
> The 2-nt 3′ overhang is the signature left by **Dicer cleavage** of long dsRNA and is essential for proper loading into Argonaute's PAZ domain.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: siRNA Design for the *EGFR* Oncogene (Step-by-Step)

**Scenario:** Design a siRNA to silence the human *EGFR* gene (NM_005228.5, NM_005228.5).

### Step 1 — Select Target Region

Choose a 19–23 nt region within the **open reading frame (ORF)**, ideally **50–100 nt downstream of the start codon** to avoid 5′ UTR regulatory proteins.

$$
\text{Select region: } 5'\text{-GCUAUGUCAUUGGAUAAUU-}3' \quad (\text{sense strand})
$$

### Step 2 — Apply Thermodynamic Asymmetry Rule

The 5′ end of the **antisense (guide)** strand must have **lower internal stability** (A/U-rich) than the 3′ end.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta G_{5'\text{ end, antisense}} &\approx -7.2 \text{ kcal/mol} \\
\Delta G_{3'\text{ end, antisense}} &\approx -9.8 \text{ kcal/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $\Delta G_{5'} > \Delta G_{3'} \Rightarrow$ correct strand selection into RISC ✓

### Step 3 — Add 3′ dTdT Overhang (UU preferred for chemical stability)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sense: } & 5'\text{-GCUAUGUCAUUGGAUAAUU dTdT-}3' \\
\text{Antisense: } & 5'\text{-AAUUAUCCAAUGACAUAGC dTdT-}3'
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — BLAST Check

BLAST the 19-nt guide against the human transcriptome (refseq_rna). Ensure **≥ 3 mismatches** to any non-target transcript to minimize **off-target effects**.

### Step 5 — In Silico Score (Optional — Reynolds score, Ui-Tei score)

$$
\text{Reynolds score} = A + B + C + D + E + F + G + H \quad (\text{max} = 10)
$$

If Reynolds score ≥ 6, the siRNA is predicted to be ≥ 70% silencing efficient.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — siRNA vs miRNA vs Antisense RNA vs Ribozyme vs CRISPRi

| Parameter | siRNA | miRNA | Antisense RNA | Ribozyme | CRISPRi |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Trigger source** | Exogenous dsRNA | Endogenous gene | Synthetic oligonucleotide | Catalytic RNA | dCas9-sgRNA |
| **Length** | 21–23 nt | ~22 nt | 15–30 nt | 40–80 nt | sgRNA ~20 nt + PAM |
| **Complementarity** | Perfect | Partial (seed nt 2–8) | Perfect | Perfect | Watson-Crick (DNA) |
| **Endonuclease** | Ago2 | Ago1–4 (no cleavage) | RNase H (when DNA analog used) | Self-cleaving ribozyme | dCas9 (no cleavage) |
| **Site of action** | Cytoplasm | Cytoplasm (3′ UTR) | Nucleus + cytoplasm | Cytoplasm | Nucleus (TSS/promoter) |
| **Level of silencing** | TGS / PTGS | PTGS only | PTGS / translational block | PTGS | TGS |
| **Heritability** | Transient | Transient | Transient | Transient | Stable (if integrated) |
| **Off-target risk** | Moderate–High | High (seed family) | Low | Low | Very low |
| **Delivery method** | Lipid nanoparticle, electroporation | Endogenous | ASO injection | Lipid / viral | Plasmid / viral |
| **FDA-approved drug** | Patisiran, Givosiran | None yet | Eteplirsen, Nusinersen | None | None |
| **Cost per experiment** | Moderate | Low (endogenous) | Moderate | High (design) | Moderate |

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Mechanism of RNAi — Worked Numerical Conceptual Walkthrough

**Problem:** A researcher transfects 10 nM of a 25-bp dsRNA targeting *GAPDH* mRNA into HeLa cells. Dicer cleaves dsRNA every 21–23 nt. How many siRNA molecules are theoretically produced per dsRNA?

### Step 1 — Determine cleavage events

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Dicer cleavage spacing} &= 22 \text{ nt} \\
\text{Number of siRNAs per dsRNA} &= \left\lfloor \frac{25}{22} \right\rfloor = 1 \text{ siRNA + small fragment}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Molar yield

$$
\begin{aligned}
[\text{siRNA}]_{\text{produced}} &\approx 1 \times 10 \text{ nM} = 10 \text{ nM}
\end{aligned}
$$

In reality, Dicer produces ~1 siRNA per ~22 nt of dsRNA with ~85% efficiency.

### Step 3 — Effective silencing concentration

Therapeutic efficacy requires **5–100 nM** of loaded RISC-siRNA. Therefore 10 nM input dsRNA produces sufficient siRNA for **partial (40–60%) knockdown** of GAPDH.

> [!WARNING]
> **Off-target & Saturation Effects:** At concentrations > 100 nM, the **RISC complex becomes saturated**, endogenous miRNA pathways are inhibited, and **non-specific interferon response** is triggered. This is a frequent KTU viva question.
---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — siRNA/miRNA Pathway (Cytoplasm)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["LONG dsRNA (Exogenous)"] --> B["DICER RNase III Cleavage"]
    P["pri-miRNA (Endogenous)"] --> Q["Drosha-DGCR8 Microprocessor"]
    Q --> R["pre-miRNA 70 nt hairpin"]
    R --> S["Exportin-5 Nuclear Export"]
    S --> B
    B --> C["siRNA Duplex 21 to 23 nt with 2 nt overhang"]
    C --> D["RISC Loading Complex RLC"]
    D --> E["Argonaute 2 Ago2 Unwinds Duplex"]
    E --> F["Passenger Sense Strand Discarded"]
```
