# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]`

**What are the 25-bp border sequences in the T-DNA of the Ti plasmid? Why are they essential for gene transfer?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The T-DNA region of the Ti plasmid is flanked by two **25-bp imperfect direct repeat sequences** — the **Left Border (LB)** and the **Right Border (RB)**, with consensus `5'-TGG/ACAGGATATAT/AC-3'`. They are essential because:

1. **(1 mark)** The **VirD1/VirD2 endonuclease complex** specifically recognizes and nicks the bottom strand at these border sequences.
2. **(1 mark)** Nicking at RB initiates the generation of the **single-stranded T-strand** which is the actual molecule transferred to the plant cell.
3. **(1 mark)** The border sequences define the **left and right boundaries** of the T-DNA — any DNA placed between LB and RB will be transferred to the plant genome, making them the **essential cis-acting elements** for T-DNA mobilization.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Model Paper 2024]`

**Name the wound signal molecule that activates the *vir* genes. Briefly explain the two-component regulatory system involved.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The wound signal molecule is **acetosyringone** (4'-hydroxy-3',5'-dimethoxyacetophenone), a phenolic compound released from damaged plant cells at acidic pH (~5.5–6.0).

The two-component regulatory system consists of:
1. **(1.5 marks)** **VirA** — a sensor histidine kinase embedded in the bacterial inner membrane. Upon binding acetosyringone, VirA autophosphorylates on a conserved histidine residue using ATP.
2. **(1.5 marks)** **VirG** — a response regulator. VirA transfers the phosphate group to an aspartate residue on VirG. VirG-P then dimerizes, binds to conserved *vir* box sequences (TNCAATTGAAAC) upstream of all *vir* operons, and activates their transcription, switching on the entire T-DNA transfer machinery.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a)** Describe in detail the structure of the Ti plasmid with a neat diagram. Explain the role of **virA, virG, virD1/virD2, and virE2** in T-DNA transfer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **binary vector system** of *Agrobacterium*-mediated plant transformation. Compare it with the co-integrate vector system. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer to Question A (a):

The Ti (Tumor-inducing) plasmid is a large (~200 kb) circular extrachromosomal DNA of *A. tumefaciens* containing four regions: **(1 mark)** the T-DNA region (~10–30 kb) flanked by 25-bp LB/RB borders, encoding oncogenes and opine synthase genes; **(1 mark)** the *vir* region (~35–40 kb) containing 7 operons (*virA, virB, virC, virD, virE, virG, virH*); **(1 mark)** the opine catabolism region; and **(1 mark)** the origin of replication (*oriV*).

Roles of key vir proteins:
- **virA (1 mark):** Sensor histidine kinase; autophosphorylates upon binding acetosyringone; transfers phosphate to VirG.
- **virG (1 mark):** Response regulator; upon phosphorylation, dimerizes and binds *vir* box promoters to switch on all other *vir* operons.
- **virD1/virD2 (1 mark):** VirD1 is a helicase that unwinds the border region; VirD2 is an endonuclease that nicks the 25-bp border sequences and remains covalently attached to the 5' end of the T-strand, piloting it into the plant cell.
- **virE2 (1 mark):** Single-stranded DNA-binding protein that coats the T-strand to form the mature T-complex; protects the T-strand from nucleases; contains NLS for nuclear import.

---

#### Model Answer to Question A (b):

**Binary Vector System (4 marks):**

In the binary vector system, the T-DNA transfer functions are split between **two separate replicons** that co-exist in the same *Agrobacterium* cell:

1. **Disarmed Helper Ti Plasmid (~150–200 kb):** Contains the entire *vir* gene region but **lacks T-DNA borders** and oncogenes. Cannot transfer itself but provides Vir proteins **in trans**.
2. **Binary Vector (~8–15 kb, e.g., pBIN19, pCAMBIA1301):** A small, easily manipulable plasmid containing:
   - **LB and RB borders** (for T-DNA definition)
   - **Multiple cloning site (MCS)** for GOI insertion
   - **Plant selection marker** (e.g., *nptII* — kanamycin resistance)
   - **Bacterial selection marker** (e.g., *kanR*, *tetR*)
   - **Reporter gene** (e.g., *gusA* with nos or 35S promoter)
   - **Origins of replication** for both *E. coli* (pUC ori) and *Agrobacterium* (pVS1 or pRi)

The GOI is cloned in *E. coli*, then the binary vector is moved into *Agrobacterium* (containing the helper plasmid) via triparental mating or electroporation. Upon plant infection, the *vir* genes act **in trans** to mobilize only the T-DNA (defined by LB/RB) of the binary vector into the plant.

**Comparison with Co-integrate System (3 marks):**

| Feature | Binary Vector | Co-integrate Vector |
|---------|---------------|---------------------|
| Number of plasmids in *Agrobacterium* | Two (helper + binary) | One (recombinant Ti) |
| Cloning ease | Easy in *E. coli* | Difficult — large Ti plasmid manipulation |
| Recombination required | **No** | **Yes** (homologous recombination with resident Ti) |
| Time consumption | **Short** (weeks) | **Long** (months) |
| Stability | Stable | May be unstable due to recombination |
| Use in modern labs | **Universal** | Mostly obsolete |
| Examples | pBIN19, pCAMBIA series | pGV3850, pMON systems |

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, July 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the principle and instrumentation of the **biolistic / gene gun method** of plant transformation. What are the advantages and limitations of this method? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **Agrobacterium-mediated transformation protocol** in detail — from bacterial culture preparation to selection of transformed plant cells. Why is this method preferred for dicot transformation? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer to Question B (a):

**Principle (3 marks):**

The biolistic method (Biolistic Particle Delivery System, Sanford et al., 1983–1988) is a **physical, direct gene transfer technique** in which DNA-coated microscopic metal particles (gold or tungsten, 0.6–1.6 μm diameter) are accelerated to high velocity (~300–600 m/s) using a high-pressure helium burst. The microcarriers physically penetrate the plant cell wall, plasma membrane, and nuclear envelope, delivering the DNA directly into the nucleus where it can integrate into the host genome by illegitimate recombination.

**Instrumentation (2 marks):**

The standard Bio-Rad PDS-1000/He device consists of:
- **Helium cylinder** with pressure regulator
- **Rupture disk** (rated 900–1,800 psi)
- **Macrocarrier** — a plastic disc that holds microcarriers
- **Macrocarrier launch assembly** — moves the macrocarrier forward
- **Stopping screen** — a metal mesh that halts the macrocarrier
- **Target tissue platform** — adjustable distance (6–15 cm)
- **Vacuum pump** — evacuates chamber to 25–28 inches Hg
- **Control unit** — regulates pressure and timing

**Procedure Summary:** DNA is precipitated onto gold particles using CaCl₂ + spermidine, dried on the macrocarrier, and fired. Microcarriers continue past the stopping screen and penetrate the tissue.

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- Works on **all plant species** (including recalcitrant monocots)
- Suitable for **organelle (chloroplast) transformation**
- No biological vector required
- Direct delivery — no species-specificity barriers
- Can deliver multiple genes simultaneously (co-transformation)

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- **High equipment cost** (gene gun + helium + vacuum)
- **Fragmented, rearranged, multi-copy insertions**
- May cause **significant cell damage** at high pressures
- Lower transformation efficiency (~1–10%) than Agrobacterium for dicots
- Requires **extensive optimization** for each species

---

#### Model Answer to Question B (b):

**Step-by-Step Agrobacterium-Mediated Transformation Protocol (5 marks):**

1. **Day 0 — Starter Culture:** Inoculate 5 mL LB medium (with rifampicin 50 μg/mL for *Agrobacterium* selection) with a single colony of recombinant *A. tumefaciens* (containing helper Ti + binary vector with GOI). Grow overnight at 28°C, 200 rpm.
2. **Day 1 — Subculture:** Dilute 1:10 into 50 mL LB + appropriate antibiotics + 100 μM acetosyringone (optional pre-induction). Grow to **OD₆₀₀ = 0.6–0.8** (log phase).
3. **Day 2 — Co-cultivation:** Pellet bacteria by centrifugation (5,000 × g, 10 min), resuspend in **Murashige & Skoog (MS) liquid medium** + acetosyringone (200 μM) + 2% sucrose to OD₆₀₀ = 0.5. Submerge sterile explants (leaf discs, hypocotyls, cotyledons) in bacterial suspension for 10–30 minutes.
4. **Day 2 — Blotting & Co-culture:** Blot explants dry on sterile filter paper and place on **co-cultivation medium** (MS + 1 mg/L NAA + 0.5 mg/L BAP + 200 μM acetosyringone, pH 5.6). Incubate in dark at 25°C for **48–72 hours** — T-DNA transfer occurs during this phase.
5. **Day 5 — Washing & Selection:** Wash explants thoroughly with **MS liquid + 500 mg/L cefotaxime/augmentin** (kills residual *Agrobacterium*) and transfer to **selection-cum-regeneration medium** (MS + growth regulators + 50–100 mg/L kanamycin or hygromycin + 250 mg/L cefotaxime).
6. **Weeks 2–6 — Callus & Shoot Induction:** Subculture every 2 weeks onto fresh selection medium. Untransformed cells bleach/necrose; transformed calli proliferate.
7. **Shoot Elongation:** Transfer healthy calli to **shoot elongation medium** (lower cytokinin, higher gibberellin).
8. **Rooting:** Excise shoots and transfer to **rooting medium** (MS + IAA/NAA, no antibiotics except cefotaxime).
9. **Hardening & Acclimatization:** Transfer plantlets to soil in greenhouse.
10. **Molecular Confirmation:** Verify integration by **PCR** (GOI-specific primers), **Southern blot**, and expression by **RT-PCR / GUS assay / Western blot**.

**Why Agrobacterium is Preferred for Dicot Transformation (2 marks):**

1. **High efficiency (10–80%)** — far superior to biolistic in dicots
2. **Low copy number, clean integrations** — typically 1–3 transgene copies
3. **Stable, Mendelian inheritance** of the transgene
4. **Large transgene capacity** (up to 150 kb using BIBAC vectors)
5. **Simple, inexpensive equipment** — no gene gun needed
6. **Well-characterized T-DNA borders** and abundant vector options (pCAMBIA series)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse** *virA/virG* (regulatory) with *virB* (T4SS structural) — many students mix these up. The kinase–regulator pair is **VirA–VirG**, NOT VirA–VirB.
> 2. **Do NOT state** that the entire Ti plasmid is transferred — only the **T-strand (single-stranded copy of T-DNA)** is transferred, NOT the whole plasmid.
> 3. **Acetosyringone** is a **phenol** (specifically a substituted acetophenone), not a sugar or protein — avoid this common error.
> 4. **VirD2** nicks the **bottom strand** of the T-DNA, NOT the top strand. The T-strand is the bottom strand.
> 5. In **co-integrate vectors**, the recombination is **homologous** (between shared sequences), NOT random.
> 6. For **biolistic calculations**, do not forget to convert μm to m (1 μm = 10⁻⁶ m) — this is a frequent mark-losing error in numerical problems.
> 7. **T-DNA integration** in the plant genome occurs by **illegitimate recombination (NHEJ)**, NOT homologous recombination. Agrobacterium T-DNA does NOT target specific loci.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Ti plasmid** = ~200 kb circular plasmid; **T-DNA** = the transferred segment (~10–30 kb); **vir region** = ~35–40 kb.
- [ ] **25-bp direct repeat borders (LB and RB)** flank the T-DNA — **VirD2** nicks the bottom strand here.
- [ ] **Acetosyringone** is the phenolic wound signal that activates the **VirA → VirG two-component system**.
- [ ] **T-strand** is a **single-stranded copy of the T-DNA bottom strand** with VirD2 covalently attached at the 5' end.
- [ ] **VirE2** coats the T-strand → forms the **mature T-complex**; contains NLS for nuclear import.
- [ ] T-complex is exported through a **Type IV Secretion System (T4SS)** encoded by *virB* operon and powered by **VirD4 coupling protein**.
- [ ] **Binary vector system** = 2 plasmids (helper Ti with *vir* genes only + small binary vector with T-DNA + GOI).
- [ ] **Co-integrate system** = homologous recombination between *E. coli* plasmid and resident Ti T-DNA — older, less efficient.
- [ ] Common binary vectors: **pBIN19, pCAMBIA1301/2301, pBI121, pGA482**.
- [ ] Common plant selection markers: ***nptII* (kanamycin), *hpt* (hygromycin), *bar* (glufosinate/phosphinothricin)**.
- [ ] Common reporter genes: ***gusA* (GUS — β-glucuronidase), *gfp* (green fluorescent protein), *luc* (luciferase)**.
- [ ] Agrobacterium is **preferred for dicots**; **biolistic is preferred for monocots and chloroplasts**.
- [ ] **Biolistic microcarriers:** Gold or tungsten, **0.6–1.6 μm**; helium pressure **900–1,800 psi**; chamber vacuum **25–28 inches Hg**.
- [ ] Biolistic was developed by **John C. Sanford (Cornell, 1983–1988)**.
- [ ] Biolistic integration is **high-copy, fragmented, and often rearranged**; Agrobacterium is **low-copy and clean**.
- [ ] **T-DNA integration** occurs by **illegitimate (non-homologous) recombination** using the plant NHEJ machinery (Ku70/80, XRCC4, DNA ligase IV).
- [ ] Agrobacterium transforms **nuclear DNA only**; biolistic can transform **nuclear, chloroplast, and mitochondrial DNA**.
- [ ] **Floral dip method** (Clough & Bent, 1998) allows *Arabidopsis* transformation without tissue culture — a major breakthrough.
- [ ] Heat shock (42°C, 5 min) of *Agrobacterium* increases transformation efficiency; **vir* gene induction requires acidic pH 5.5–6.0**.
- [ ] **Phenolic compound activators** of *vir* genes: acetosyringone, hydroxyacetosyringone, sinapinic acid, syringaldehyde.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gene Knockouts: Creation of Knockout Mice
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT524 - GENETIC ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Gene Knockouts: Creation of Knockout Mice

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gene Knockout (KO):** A genetic technique in which one or more specific genes of an organism are engineered to be non-functional ("knocked out") through targeted disruption, deletion, or replacement of the gene's coding sequence with an inert DNA cassette, thereby enabling the study of gene function through loss-of-function phenotypic analysis.

A **Knockout Mouse** is a genetically engineered mouse (typically *Mus musculus*) in which researchers have inactivated, or "knocked out," one or more endogenous genes by introducing a designed mutation into the germline via homologous recombination in embryonic stem (ES) cells. The first successful knockout mouse was reported by **Mario Capecchi, Martin Evans, and Oliver Smithies** in 1989, work that earned them the **Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine in 2007**.

The inactivation is typically achieved by replacing the target gene's critical **exon(s)** with a **positive selection marker** (e.g., the *neo* gene conferring G418/neomycin resistance) and flanking the construct with a **negative selection marker** (e.g., the *HSV-tk* gene, sensitive to ganciclovir). This strategy is termed the **Positive–Negative Selection (PNS)** method, originally devised by **Mansour, Thomas, and Capecchi (1988)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEBBT524 — Module 4):** Students must be able to explain the rationale for using mouse as a model organism, describe the step-by-step construction of knockout mice using embryonic stem cells, and interpret the role of selectable markers, homologous recombination, blastocyst injection, and breeding strategies to obtain homozygous (−/−) knockout lines.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Library Analogy:** Imagine a giant library (the mouse genome) containing thousands of instruction manuals (genes) that tell workers (cells) how to build and run a factory (the organism). To figure out what Manual #247 actually *does*, you carefully tear out the critical pages of that manual and replace them with blank, harmless paper (a selection cassette). Now when the cell tries to "read" Manual #247, it gets nonsense. The resulting changes in the factory (phenotype) tell you what the manual was responsible for — perhaps a specific enzyme, a structural protein, or a developmental signal.

**Why the mouse?** A child-friendly way to remember:
- Mice are small, breed fast, share ~**85% protein-coding gene similarity** with humans.
- Their embryos are easy to manipulate.
- This makes them the **"stand-in twin"** for studying human gene function, especially when direct human experimentation is unethical.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical/Genetic Constants

| Parameter | Typical Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Mouse gestation period** | **~19–21 days** | Determines speed of transgenic line generation |
| **Mouse litter size** | **6–10 pups** | Defines breeding colony scaling |
| **Gene homology (mouse–human)** | **~85%** of protein-coding genes | Justifies mouse as a model |
| **ES cell doubling time** | **~24 hours** | Dictates selection timeline |
| **Targeting vector size** | **5–15 kb** | Standard homologous arm length |
| **Homologous arm length** | **0.5–5 kb each** | Critical for recombination efficiency |
| **G418 (neomycin) selection** | **200–400 µg/mL** | Standard ES cell selection concentration |
| **Ganciclovir selection** | **2 µM** | Eliminates random integrants |
| **Blastocyst age for injection** | **E3.5 (3.5 days post-coitum)** | Standard host embryo stage |
| **Number of ES cells injected per blastocyst** | **10–15 cells** | Optimizes chimera contribution |

> [!NOTE]
> These figures should be cited during exam answers as **standard operating parameters** in knockout mouse production.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Core Concept: Targeted Disruption by Homologous Recombination

**Why is gene knockout so powerful?**
In classical forward genetics, mutations arise randomly. In reverse genetics (the knockout approach), the researcher *starts with a known DNA sequence* and intentionally mutates it. This is the conceptual backbone of the entire module.

### 2.1.1 The Two Competing DNA Repair Pathways

When a DNA double-strand break (or a linear targeting vector with homologous ends) enters a cell, it can be repaired via two pathways:

1. **Non-Homologous End Joining (NHEJ)** — the *default* pathway in mammalian cells. It re-ligates DNA ends *without* sequence homology requirement, producing **random insertions** (often with small deletions). This is undesirable for targeted knockout because it produces random, not site-specific, events.
2. **Homologous Recombination (HR)** — uses an *exogenous* DNA template (the targeting vector) to repair the break via sequence homology, allowing **precise gene replacement** at a chosen locus. This is the pathway exploited for knockouts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Challenge:** In mammalian cells, the **HR : NHEJ ratio is roughly 1 : 1000 to 1 : 10,000**. Only ~1 in every 1,000 to 10,000 transfected cells undergoes the desired homologous event. This is why **selection strategies** (PNS) are absolutely critical.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Positive–Negative Selection (PNS) Strategy

The PNS method enriches for correctly targeted ES cell clones by simultaneously applying two drugs. Here is the *why* behind each component:

### Step 1: Design the Targeting Vector
- The vector is a plasmid carrying:
  - **Two "homology arms"** (5′ and 3′), each a few kb of DNA identical to the target gene.
  - **A positive selection cassette** (e.g., *neo^R*) inserted within an *essential exon* of the target gene — this **disrupts** the gene and confers resistance to G418.
  - **A negative selection cassette** (e.g., *HSV-tk*) placed **outside** the homology arms, typically at the 3′ end of the vector.

### Step 2: Transfect ES Cells
- Linearized vector is introduced into cultured mouse ES cells by **electroporation** (most common; ~800 V, 3 µF) or lipofection.
- Cells begin repairing the linear DNA.

### Step 3: Apply Double Selection
- **Positive selection (G418/neomycin):** Only cells that *integrated* the vector (randomly OR by HR) survive. The *neo* gene product (aminoglycoside phosphotransferase) inactivates G418.
- **Negative selection (ganciclovir or FIAU):** HSV-thymidine kinase (HSV-TK) converts ganciclovir into a toxic phosphorylated nucleotide that **kills dividing cells**. *HSV-tk* is **lost** during homologous recombination (because it lies outside the homology arms and is *not* copied into the genome). *HSV-tk* is **retained** during random integration (because the entire vector is inserted).

> [!TIP]
> **Selection Outcome Summary:**
> - **HR (correct targeting)** → *neo^R* present, *HSV-tk* lost → **survives both G418 and ganciclovir** ✅
> - **Random integration** → *neo^R* present, *HSV-tk* present → **survives G418, killed by ganciclovir** ❌
> - **No integration** → dies in G418 ❌

**Result:** PNS enriches for correctly targeted clones by **100× to 1000×**, converting the 1-in-10,000 natural HR event into a manageable screen.

---

## 2.3 Construction of the Knockout Mouse — The Full Pipeline

The full process from gene to animal takes approximately **10–14 months**:

| Stage | Process | Duration | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Targeting vector design & construction | 1–2 months | Plasmid DNA |
| 2 | Transfection into ES cells & PNS selection | 2–3 weeks | Drug-resistant clones |
| 3 | Screening of ES cell clones (PCR + Southern blot) | 2–3 weeks | Confirmed targeted ES clone |
| 4 | Microinjection of ES cells into blastocysts | 1 day | Injected blastocysts |
| 5 | Implantation into pseudopregnant foster mother | 1 day | Pregnant foster |
| 6 | Birth of chimeric mice | 3 weeks | Chimeric (mixed coat color) pups |
| 7 | Breeding chimeras with wild-type mice | 3 weeks gestation + 3 weeks weaning | **Heterozygous (+/−) F1 offspring** |
| 8 | Intercross of heterozygotes | 3 weeks gestation + 3 weeks weaning | **Homozygous (−/−) knockout mice** |

---

## 2.4 Production of Homozygous Knockout Mice — The Breeding Math

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mendelian Inheritance Principle:** When two heterozygotes (+/− × +/−) are crossed, offspring follow a 1:2:1 ratio:
> - **25% Wild-type (+/+)**
> - **50% Heterozygous (+/−)**
> - **25% Homozygous knockout (−/−)**

This is because each parent contributes one allele:
- Parent 1 gametes: ½(+) and ½(−)
- Parent 2 gametes: ½(+) and ½(−)

The Punnett square yields four equally probable zygote genotypes.

> [!WARNING]
> **Embryonic lethality caveat:** If the knocked-out gene is *essential* for development, the homozygous (−/−) embryos die *in utero*. The expected 25% knockout class will be missing, and the ratio becomes **1:2** (no −/− pups). This is itself a useful biological result, indicating the gene is essential.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Definition / Formula | Application / Boundary |
|---|---|---|
| $P_{HR}$ | Probability of homologous recombination = $1 / 10^3$ to $1 / 10^4$ | Used to justify PNS enrichment |
| $E_{PNS}$ | PNS enrichment factor = $\frac{\text{HR clones surviving double selection}}{\text{Random integrants surviving}}$ | Typically 100–1000× |
| $G$ | Mouse gestation period $\approx$ **19–21 days** | Defines breeding schedule |
| $L$ | Mean litter size = **6–10 pups** | Determines colony size |
| $N_{gen}$ | Number of generations to homozygosity from chimera | $F_1$ (heterozygote) + $F_2$ (intercross) = **2 generations** |
| $f_{Mendelian}$ | Expected −/− frequency from +/− × +/− = $\frac{1}{4}$ = **25%** | Used in colony planning |
| $f_{lethal}$ | If gene essential, observed $f_{-/-} = 0$ | Indicates developmental role |
| $R_{target}$ | Targeting vector recombination efficiency $\propto L_{arm}^3$ (cubic dependence on arm length) | Justifies using $\geq$ **2–4 kb** arms |
| $C_{G418}$ | G418 working concentration in ES cell medium $\approx$ **200–400 µg/mL** | Standard culture condition |
| $C_{GANC}$ | Ganciclovir working concentration $\approx$ **2 µM** | Negative selection |
| $D_{blast}$ | Blastocyst developmental stage for injection = **E3.5** | Inner cell mass stage |
| $\rho_{HR/NHEJ}$ | HR to NHEJ ratio in ES cells $\approx 1 : 1000$ to $1 : 10{,}000$ | Why PNS is mandatory |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of Knockout Mice in Engineering & Biomedical Fields

Although the subject is biological, knockout technology has major applied impact:

1. **Drug Target Validation (Pharmaceutical Engineering):** A gene is knocked out, then the animal is challenged with a drug. If the drug's effect disappears, the gene product is confirmed as the drug's molecular target.
2. **Disease Modeling:** Knockout mice for tumor suppressor genes (*p53*, *BRCA1*) develop cancers mirroring human disease, used in oncology drug screening.
3. **Immunology:** *RAG1/RAG2* knockouts lack mature lymphocytes — used to study innate vs. adaptive immunity.
4. **Metabolic Engineering:** Knockout of *ob* (leptin) gene produces *ob/ob* obese mice — a model for type-2 diabetes research.
5. **Toxicology:** Knockout mice missing drug-metabolizing cytochrome P450 enzymes are used to predict drug toxicity in humans.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Mendelian Ratio of Homozygous Knockout Offspring

**Problem:** A male chimeric mouse heterozygous (+/−) for the targeted allele is crossed with a wild-type (+/+) female. The F1 progeny are intercrossed. Derive the expected genotypic ratio in F2.

**Step 1 — Parental gamete formation.**
- Each heterozygous (+/−) parent produces two types of gametes during meiosis:
  - With probability $\frac{1}{2}$: gamete carries the wild-type (+) allele.
  - With probability $\frac{1}{2}$: gamete carries the knockout (−) allele.

Let the gamete from parent 1 be $G_1 \in \{+, -\}$ and from parent 2 be $G_2 \in \{+, -\}$.

**Step 2 — Joint probability of each zygote genotype.**

The probability of any specific zygote is the product of independent gamete probabilities:

$$
P(G_1, G_2) = P(G_1) \times P(G_2) = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{2} = \frac{1}{4}
$$

**Step 3 — Enumerate all four zygotic combinations.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(+/+) &= P(+) \times P(+) = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{2} = \frac{1}{4} \\
P(+/-) &= P(+) \times P(-) + P(-) \times P(+) = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{1}{4} = \frac{1}{2} \\
P(-/-) &= P(-) \times P(-) = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{2} = \frac{1}{4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify total probability.**

$$
P(+/+) + P(+/-) + P(-/-) = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{4} = 1
$$

✅ Probability conservation is satisfied.

**Step 5 — Final Mendelian ratio.**

$$
\boxed{\;(+/+) : (+/-) : (-/-) \;=\; 1 : 2 : 1\;}
$$

i.e., **25% wild-type, 50% heterozygous, 25% homozygous knockout.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: PNS Enrichment Factor

**Problem:** A researcher transfects $N_{total} = 1{,}000{,}000$ ES cells with a linearized targeting vector. Natural HR frequency is $f_{HR} = 1/5000$, and random integration frequency is $f_{RI} = 1/100$. Calculate the number of correctly targeted clones that survive double selection, given that PNS eliminates 99.9% of random integrants.

**Step 1 — Count HR events.**

$$
N_{HR} = N_{total} \times f_{HR} = 1{,}000{,}000 \times \frac{1}{5000} = 200 \text{ HR events}
$$

**Step 2 — Count random integration events.**

$$
N_{RI} = N_{total} \times f_{RI} = 1{,}000{,}000 \times \frac{1}{100} = 10{,}000 \text{ random integrants}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply PNS survival rates.**

- HR clones retain *neo^R* but lose *HSV-tk* → survive G418 **and** ganciclovir.
- Assume HR survival rate $\approx 100\%$.

$$
N_{HR}^{survive} = 200 \times 1.00 = 200
$$

- Random integrants retain both *neo^R* and *HSV-tk* → killed by ganciclovir with efficiency $\eta_{GANC} = 99.9\% = 0.999$.

$$
N_{RI}^{survive} = 10{,}000 \times (1 - 0.999) = 10{,}000 \times 0.001 = 10
$$

**Step 4 — PNS enrichment factor.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{PNS} &= \frac{N_{HR}^{survive} / N_{total}}{N_{RI}^{survive} / N_{total}} = \frac{N_{HR}^{survive}}{N_{RI}^{survive}} \\
&= \frac{200}{10} = 20\times
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering note:** In typical real-world experiments, the PNS enrichment factor reaches **100× to 1000×** because ganciclovir killing of random integrants is more efficient (~99.99%) than assumed here. The cubic dependence of HR on homology arm length (longer arms → higher HR) further amplifies the enrichment.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: How Selection Markers Behave in Each Event

The following table consolidates the *biochemical logic* of PNS — a frequently asked KTU question.

| Integration Event | *neo^R* status | *HSV-tk* status | G418 Effect | Ganciclovir Effect | Final Outcome |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Homologous Recombination (correct targeting)** | Present (inserted in target locus) | **Lost** (outside homology arms) | Survives (neoR inactivates G418) | Survives (no HSV-TK enzyme) | **Selected ✅** |
| **Random Integration (5′ end)** | Present | Present | Survives | Killed (HSV-TK phosphorylates ganciclovir) | **Eliminated ❌** |
| **Random Integration (3′ end)** | Present | Present | Survives | Killed | **Eliminated ❌** |
| **No Integration** | Absent | Absent | Killed (no neoR) | Survives (irrelevant) | **Eliminated ❌** |

**Mechanism of ganciclovir toxicity (deeper dive):**

HSV-thymidine kinase has a much broader substrate specificity than mammalian TK. It phosphorylates ganciclovir to **ganciclovir monophosphate**. Cellular kinases then convert it to **ganciclovir triphosphate**, which is incorporated into DNA during replication. Because ganciclovir triphosphate lacks a 3′-OH, it acts as a **chain terminator**, halting DNA synthesis and triggering apoptosis in dividing cells.

---

## 3.5 Verification: Why the Inner Cell Mass (ICM) is Critical

ES cells are derived from the **inner cell mass (ICM)** of a **E3.5 blastocyst**. Only ICM cells are **pluripotent** — capable of contributing to *all* tissues of the adult mouse, including the **germline**. This is why injecting targeted ES cells into a host blastocyst can produce a **chimera** that transmits the engineered allele to its offspring.

> [!WARNING]
> If a non-pluripotent cell type (e.g., a differentiated fibroblast) were used, no germline transmission would be possible. Hence, **ES cell pluripotency is non-negotiable**.
