# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
**Define a "knockout mouse." Explain the role of embryonic stem (ES) cells in its generation.**

**Model Answer:**

A **knockout mouse** is a genetically engineered mouse in which one or more specific endogenous genes have been inactivated or "knocked out" by targeted disruption of the gene's coding sequence in the germline. This is achieved by introducing a designed mutation into the mouse genome using **homologous recombination in embryonic stem (ES) cells**, which are derived from the **inner cell mass (ICM) of E3.5 blastocysts** and are **pluripotent** — capable of contributing to all adult tissues, including the germline.

**Role of ES cells (3 key points):**
1. They are the only cell type with the unique combination of **(a) unlimited self-renewal in vitro** and **(b) pluripotency in vivo** — both required for gene targeting followed by germline transmission.
2. They support **homologous recombination** at a much higher rate than differentiated cells, making targeted gene disruption feasible.
3. After genetic manipulation *in vitro*, they can be **reintroduced into a host blastocyst**, where they colonize the embryo and form a **chimera**, transmitting the engineered allele to subsequent generations.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for ES cell role.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
**What is the Positive–Negative Selection (PNS) method? Why are two different drugs (G418 and ganciclovir) used?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Positive–Negative Selection (PNS) method**, developed by **Mansour, Thomas, and Capecchi (1988)**, is a strategy to enrich for embryonic stem (ES) cell clones in which a targeting vector has undergone the desired **homologous recombination** event rather than random integration.

**Why two drugs?**
- **G418 (neomycin) — Positive Selection:** The targeting vector carries a *neo^R* gene (encoding aminoglycoside phosphotransferase) inside the homology arms. Only ES cells that have *integrated* the vector (either by HR or randomly) survive in G418-containing medium.
- **Ganciclovir — Negative Selection:** The vector also carries an *HSV-tk* (herpes simplex virus thymidine kinase) gene located *outside* the homology arms. In random integrants, the *HSV-tk* is retained; HSV-TK phosphorylates ganciclovir into a toxic triphosphate that halts DNA replication, killing the cell. In correctly targeted (HR) clones, *HSV-tk* is *lost* (not copied into the genome because it is outside the homology arms), so the cells survive ganciclovir.

**Net effect:** PNS enriches the desired HR events by **100× to 1000×**, overcoming the naturally low HR-to-NHEJ ratio of ~1:1000 in mammalian cells.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for naming PNS, 1 mark for explaining positive selection, 1 mark for explaining negative selection.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Module Question Pattern: **ESE Module Internal Choice — Either-Or pattern.** Students answer EITHER Question A OR Question B, not both. Each is split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe the step-by-step construction of a targeting vector for a gene knockout experiment. With a neat diagram, explain the placement of the *neo^R* and *HSV-tk* selection markers.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the principle of Positive–Negative Selection (PNS) used in ES cell selection. How does it distinguish correctly targeted clones from random integrants?

---

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — Identify the target gene and the critical exon to be disrupted.**
The gene of interest is identified from a mouse genome database (e.g., Ensembl, NCBI). A critical **exon** (usually exon 2 or the exon containing the ATG start codon) is chosen for disruption because loss of this exon abolishes functional protein production.

**Step 2 — Isolate the homology arms from a mouse genomic BAC clone.**
Two DNA fragments, each typically **1–5 kb** in length, are cloned from a mouse BAC (Bacterial Artificial Chromosome) library. These represent the genomic regions immediately upstream (5′ arm) and downstream (3′ arm) of the target exon. The longer the arms, the higher the homologous recombination frequency (cubic relationship).

**Step 3 — Insert the positive selection marker (*neo^R*) into the critical exon.**
A *neo^R* expression cassette, driven by a strong ubiquitous promoter (e.g., **PGK — phosphoglycerate kinase promoter**), is ligated into the middle of the critical exon. This **disrupts the exon** and provides resistance to the antibiotic **G418** (a neomycin analog).

**Step 4 — Place the negative selection marker (*HSV-tk*) outside the homology arms.**
The *HSV-tk* cassette is positioned at the **3′ end of the vector, beyond the 3′ homology arm**. Critically, *HSV-tk* lies **outside** the region of homology, so during homologous recombination it is *not* copied into the genome. During random integration, the entire vector (including *HSV-tk*) is inserted.

**Step 5 — Verify construct integrity by restriction digestion and sequencing.**

**Diagram (textual, for the answer sheet):**

```
5' homology arm | Exon X | neo^R (PGK-neo) | Exon X (truncated) | 3' homology arm | HSV-tk
                                       ↑                                  ↑
                              Disrupts exon                  Outside HR region
```

**Step 6 — Linearize the vector** with a restriction enzyme that cuts at the 5′ end (outside the 5′ arm) before transfection into ES cells. Linearization is essential because circular DNA integrates mostly by random NHEJ.

*(7 marks breakdown: 1 mark target/exon identification, 2 marks homology arm isolation, 1 mark *neo^R* placement with reason, 1 mark *HSV-tk* placement with reason, 1 mark linearization, 1 mark diagram.)*

---

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Principle:** In a typical ES cell transfection, only **1 in 1,000 to 10,000 cells** undergoes the desired homologous recombination; the rest either fail to integrate or integrate randomly. PNS exploits two different selection markers to filter out the desired clones.

**The two drug selections and their logic:**

| Drug | Selection Type | Cells Surviving | Cells Killed |
|---|---|---|---|
| **G418 (neomycin analog)** | Positive | Cells that integrated *neo^R* (both HR and random integrants) | Cells that did not integrate the vector |
| **Ganciclovir** | Negative | HR clones (lost *HSV-tk* outside the HR region) | Random integrants (retain *HSV-tk*; HSV-TK phosphorylates ganciclovir → toxic triphosphate chain terminator) |

**Mechanism of ganciclovir toxicity in random integrants:**
HSV-thymidine kinase converts ganciclovir to **ganciclovir monophosphate** → cellular kinases convert it to **ganciclovir triphosphate** → incorporated into replicating DNA → chain termination → apoptosis.

**Why the geometry of the vector matters:**
- The *neo^R* is **inside** the homology arms → integrated in *both* HR and random events → provides positive selection.
- The *HSV-tk* is **outside** the homology arms → only integrated in *random* events (because HR copies only the homology region) → provides negative selection.
- Thus, **HR clones are *neo^R*+ / *HSV-tk*− (double-survive)**, while **random integrants are *neo^R*+ / *HSV-tk*+ (survive G418, killed by ganciclovir)**.

**Enrichment factor:** PNS typically enriches for correctly targeted clones by **100× to 1000×**, transforming an impractical screen into a routine one.

*(7 marks breakdown: 1 mark PNS rationale, 2 marks positive selection mechanism, 2 marks negative selection mechanism, 1 mark why geometry matters, 1 mark enrichment factor.)*

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Alternative)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe the procedure for the creation of a knockout mouse, starting from the injection of ES cells into a blastocyst, up to the generation of homozygous (−/−) mice. Include the role of the pseudopregnant foster mother and the germline transmission test.

**(b) [7 marks]** A heterozygous (+/−) knockout mouse is crossed with a wild-type (+/+) mouse. Calculate the expected genotypic ratio in the F1 progeny. If the F1 heterozygotes are intercrossed, what fraction of F2 embryos is expected to be homozygous (−/−)? Discuss what happens if the gene is essential for embryonic development.

---

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — Superovulation and blastocyst collection.**
Donor female mice (typically 3–4 weeks old) are injected with **pregnant mare serum gonadotropin (PMSG)** followed by **human chorionic gonadotropin (hCG)** 48 hours later to induce superovulation. After mating, **E3.5 blastocysts** are flushed from the uterine horns.

**Step 2 — Microinjection of ES cells into the blastocyst.**
Using a **micromanipulator** and a fine glass capillary, **10–15 targeted ES cells** are injected into the **inner cell mass (ICM)** of each E3.5 blastocyst. The injected ES cells mix with the host ICM cells.

**Step 3 — Implantation into a pseudopregnant foster mother.**
A **pseudopregnant** female is prepared by mating with a **vasectomized male** (the mating stimulus triggers the hormonal state of pregnancy, but no embryos are present). Approximately 8–12 injected blastocysts are surgically transferred into the uterus of this foster mother, which carries them to term.

**Step 4 — Birth of chimeric offspring (Chimera generation).**
The pups born are **chimeras** — a mixture of cells derived from the host blastocyst (e.g., albino C57BL/6) and the injected ES cells (e.g., agouti 129/Sv). Coat color is a visual indicator: an **agouti-on-albino chimera** has visible patches of brown fur on a white background, indicating successful ES cell contribution.

**Step 5 — Germline transmission test.**
The chimera is crossed with a wild-type albino mouse. If the chimera's germ cells (sperm or eggs) are derived from the injected ES cells, then the offspring (F1) will be heterozygous for the targeted mutation. **Tail DNA PCR (or Southern blot)** on F1 pups identifies the 50% that inherited the knockout allele — these are the **germline-transmitted heterozygotes**.

**Step 6 — Generation of homozygous (−/−) mice.**
Two F1 heterozygotes are intercrossed. From the F2 offspring, the **25% homozygous knockouts** are identified by PCR genotyping. These are the experimental animals used for phenotypic analysis.

**Why a pseudopregnant foster?** A normal pregnant female will not accept transferred embryos because her reproductive tract is hormonally synchronized to her own embryos. The pseudopregnant female is hormonally "tricked" into the receptive state, enabling implantation of the injected blastocysts.

**Why germline transmission is the bottleneck:** A high-percentage coat-color chimera may still have **no germline contribution** from the ES cells (e.g., if the ES-derived cells failed to colonize the germline). Without germline transmission, the engineered allele is **lost** and cannot be inherited. Hence, multiple chimeras (often 5–10) must be bred to find at least one with germline transmission.

*(7 marks breakdown: 1 mark blastocyst harvest, 1 mark ES injection, 1 mark foster role, 1 mark chimera identification, 1 mark germline transmission, 2 marks homozygous generation.)*

---

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Part i — Cross: +/− × +/+ (Heterozygote × Wild-type).**

The heterozygote (+/−) produces two gamete types: + (½) and − (½). The wild-type produces only + gametes (probability 1).

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(+/+) &= P(-) \text{ gamete absent} \times P(+) \text{ from WT} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1 = \frac{1}{2} \\
P(+/-) &= P(-) \text{ gamete} \times P(+) \text{ from WT} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1 = \frac{1}{2} \\
P(-/-) &= 0 \text{ (wild-type has no } - \text{ allele to pass on)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Expected F1 ratio: 1 (+/+) : 1 (+/−) — i.e., 50% wild-type and 50% heterozygotes.** No homozygous knockouts in F1.

**Part ii — Cross: +/− × +/− (F1 intercross).**

As derived in Section 3.1:

$$
P(+/+) : P(+/-) : P(-/-) = 1 : 2 : 1
$$

i.e., **¼ (25%) of F2 embryos are expected to be homozygous (−/−) knockouts.**

**Part iii — What if the gene is essential for embryonic development?**

If the disrupted gene is required for processes such as implantation, gastrulation, organogenesis, or any critical developmental checkpoint, the homozygous −/− embryos will **die in utero** at the developmental stage when the gene product is needed.

The observed genotype ratio in F2 pups will therefore be **deviated from 1:2:1**. For example:
- If −/− dies before implantation: only +/+ and +/− pups are born → **1:2 ratio** (no −/− class).
- If −/− dies mid-gestation: a litter with a mixture of +/+, +/−, and no −/− is observed, with possible resorption sites in the uterus.

**Significance:** The absence of the −/− class is itself a useful scientific result — it indicates that the knocked-out gene is **essential for embryonic development**. By varying the timing of death, researchers can infer *when* the gene is required (e.g., pre-implantation, mid-gestation, perinatal).

**Rescue experiments:** To bypass embryonic lethality, researchers use **conditional knockouts** (e.g., the **Cre-loxP** system) to delete the gene only in a specific tissue or at a specific time.

*(7 marks breakdown: 2 marks F1 cross ratio, 2 marks F2 cross ratio, 3 marks embryonic lethality discussion.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing the location of *HSV-tk* and *neo^R*:** Many students write that both are inside the homology arms. **No** — *neo^R* is INSIDE (to disrupt the gene); *HSV-tk* is OUTSIDE (to be lost during HR). This is the #1 marker of conceptual clarity.
> 2. **Forgetting the role of the foster mother:** A common omission. Always mention the **pseudopregnant** state obtained by mating with a **vasectomized male**.
> 3. **Confusing Mendelian ratios:** In +/− × +/−, the ratio is 1:2:1 for *genotypes*, not phenotypes. Knockouts are recessive at the molecular level, so heterozygotes have no phenotype.
> 4. **Skipping the germline transmission step:** A chimera with 90% ES contribution may still not transmit through the germline. Always state this bottleneck.
> 5. **Forgetting embryonic lethality implications:** When the −/− class is missing, students often write "experiment failed" — instead, it indicates an **essential gene**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Mnemonics & Quick-Fire Facts**

- **Gene knockout = targeted gene inactivation** achieved by replacing an essential exon with a selection cassette in ES cells via **homologous recombination**.
- **Knockout mouse = Nobel Prize (2007)** to Capecchi, Evans, Smithies.
- **Why mouse?** Small, fast breeding, ~85% human gene homology, manipulable embryos.
- **ES cells = pluripotent cells from E3.5 blastocyst ICM**; only cell type supporting both *in vitro* gene targeting AND *in vivo* germline transmission.
- **HR : NHEJ ratio in ES cells ≈ 1 : 1000 to 1 : 10,000** → PNS is mandatory.
- **Targeting vector geometry:**
  - 5′ homology arm (1–4 kb) → critical exon disrupted by *neo^R* → 3′ homology arm (1–4 kb) → *HSV-tk* **OUTSIDE** the arms.
- **PNS:**
  - **G418 (positive)** — kills cells lacking *neo^R*.
  - **Ganciclovir (negative)** — kills cells retaining *HSV-tk* (i.e., random integrants).
  - **HR clones survive both**; **random integrants survive only G418**; **non-integrants die in G418**.
- **PNS enrichment factor = 100–1000×.**
- **Linearization of vector** is required before transfection.
- **Electroporation** is the most common transfection method (~800 V, 3 µF).
- **Blastocyst injection:** 10–15 ES cells into E3.5 blastocyst ICM.
- **Foster mother = pseudopregnant** (mated with vasectomized male) — provides hormonal receptive state.
- **Chimera identification:** coat color (agouti/129 ES cells on albino/C57BL/6 host).
- **Germline transmission test:** breed chimera × WT; PCR on F1 tail DNA to identify heterozygotes.
- **Homozygote generation:** F1 (+/−) × F1 (+/−) → F2 with **1:2:1** ratio → 25% −/−.
- **Embryonic lethality:** If −/− dies, observed ratio is **1:2** (no −/− class) → gene is **developmentally essential**.
- **Conditional knockouts** (Cre-loxP) are used to bypass embryonic lethality by tissue-specific or time-specific deletion.
- **Classical PNS developers:** Mansour, Thomas & Capecchi (1988).
- **Knockout vs. Knock-in:** Knockout *disrupts* a gene; knock-in *replaces* a gene with a different sequence (e.g., a reporter like GFP or a human disease allele).
- **Alternative to classical knockout in current research:** **CRISPR/Cas9** has largely replaced classical HR-based methods due to higher efficiency. However, KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the classical PNS approach as foundational knowledge.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 4 Topic: Gene Knockouts: Creation of Knockout Mice**
> The principles of ES cell manipulation, PNS strategy, and germline transmission form the cornerstone of mammalian functional genomics and continue to underpin modern genome-editing technologies.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Disease Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT524 - GENETIC ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Disease Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

A **Disease Model** in genetic engineering is a deliberately engineered biological system—an organism, tissue, cell line, or computational construct—that recapitulates the molecular, cellular, physiological, and pathological features of a specific human disease. These models are constructed by introducing, deleting, or modifying defined genetic elements so that the resulting phenotype mirrors the disease of interest, thereby enabling mechanistic study, target identification, and pre-clinical therapeutic validation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A disease model is a genetically engineered or naturally occurring biological system (whole organism, organoid, or cell line) that phenocopies the etiology, progression, and/or symptoms of a human disorder, used as a surrogate for experimental study under controlled conditions.

**Classification by Biological Hierarchy:**

| Hierarchy | Example | Disease Mimicked |
|---|---|---|
| Whole Organism | *Mus musculus* (mouse) | Cystic Fibrosis (CFTR⁻/⁻) |
| Tissue/Organ | Brain organoid | Microcephaly |
| Cellular | iPSC-derived cardiomyocyte | Long-QT syndrome |
| Molecular | Recombinant protein assay | Misfolding diseases |

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Crash Test Dummy" of Biology

Imagine an automobile engineer who wants to test a new airbag. She cannot deliberately crash a real human being into a wall, so she builds a **crash test dummy** that mimics human size, weight distribution, and skeletal fragility.

A **disease model is the biological equivalent of that crash test dummy**:
- The **car crash** = the human disease (e.g., Alzheimer's, diabetes, cancer).
- The **dummy** = the mouse, zebrafish, organoid, or cell line engineered to "experience" the disease.
- The **airbag test** = drug screening, gene therapy trial, or toxicity study.

Just as the dummy allows engineers to observe injury patterns and refine safety designs *without harming humans*, the disease model allows geneticists and pharmacologists to dissect disease mechanisms and evaluate treatments *without exposing patients to unproven interventions*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Impact:** Roughly **95%** of drugs that succeed in animal models still fail in human clinical trials (Sun et al., 2022). This statistic is precisely why disease-modelling quality and species choice are critical board-exam and research-level concepts.

---

### 1.3 Why Disease Models Are Central to Genetic Engineering

The discipline of genetic engineering is fundamentally *reductionist*: it isolates one variable (a gene, a mutation, a regulatory element) and tests its consequence in a controlled biological context. Disease models provide the **controlled context**.

> [!TIP]
> **Board-Exam Insight:** When the KTU examiner asks "Why are disease models necessary?", the four golden points to mention are:
> 1. **Ethical necessity** — direct human experimentation is unethical.
> 2. **Genetic homogeneity** — inbred model strains remove background noise.
> 3. **Manipulability** — genes can be added, deleted, or edited at will.
> 4. **Speed & cost** — short generation times accelerate discovery.

---

### 1.4 Essential Physical/Biological Constants Used in Disease Modelling

These constants appear in KTU numerical and short-answer questions:

- **Mouse gestation period:** ≈ **19–21 days**
- **Mouse generation time:** ≈ **10 weeks**
- **C. elegans generation time:** ≈ **3–4 days**
- **Drosophila generation time:** ≈ **10–14 days**
- **Zebrafish generation time:** ≈ **3 months**
- **Mouse genome size:** ≈ **2.7 × 10⁹ bp**
- **Human-mouse genome similarity:** ≈ **85%** (coding regions ~**99%** orthologous for many disease genes)
- **Mendelian expected phenotypic ratio (monohybrid F2):** **3:1**
- **Mendelian expected phenotypic ratio (dihybrid F2):** **9:3:3:1**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Disease Models — The Master Taxonomy

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects you to know the *full* hierarchy of model systems, not just mice. Below is the operational decomposition:

**A) By Origin of Genetic Alteration**

1. **Spontaneous Models** — Naturally arising mutations (e.g., *ob/ob* obese mouse, *A*^y^ yellow agouti mouse).
2. **Induced Mutant Models** — Created via chemical mutagens (ENU), radiation, or insertional mutagenesis.
3. **Transgenic Addition Models** — Random integration of exogenous DNA (classical pronuclear microinjection).
4. **Targeted Mutant Models** — Precise gene knockout (KO) or knock-in (KI) via homologous recombination in ES cells.
5. **Genome-Edited Models** — Created using programmable nucleases (ZFN, TALEN, **CRISPR/Cas9**).
6. **Conditional Models** — Spatially or temporally restricted mutations (Cre-*lox*P, FLP-FRT, Tet-On/Tet-Off).
7. **Humanized Models** — Murine loci replaced with human orthologs (e.g., humanized *SIRPA* and *IL3* mice for human HSC engraftment).

**B) By Biological System**

| Model Type | Representative Organism/System | Strength | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mammalian (mouse) | *Mus musculus* | Mammalian physiology, genetic tractability | Cost, ethics, time |
| Other vertebrate | Zebrafish (*Danio rerio*) | Optical transparency, high fecundity | Aquatic physiology |
| Invertebrate | *Drosophila melanogaster*, *C. elegans* | Fast, cheap, conserved pathways | No closed circulation / no vertebrae |
| Organoid | iPSC-derived 3D culture | Human origin, organ-level architecture | No immune system / vasculature |
| Cell line | HEK293, HeLa, patient fibroblasts | Inexpensive, scalable | Loss of in vivo context |
| In silico | Computational pathway model | High-throughput, ethical | Predictive accuracy |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why multiple models?** No single model fully captures human disease. The KTU-recommended strategy is **triangulation** — confirm findings in *at least two* orthogonal systems (e.g., mouse + organoid).

---

### 2.2 The Pipeline of Disease Model Generation

The end-to-end pipeline (most often assessed in 14-mark KTU questions):

**Step 1 — Disease Gene Identification**
- Human GWAS, linkage analysis, family pedigrees pinpoint candidate genes.
- Example: *HTT* gene CAG expansion identified as cause of Huntington's disease.

**Step 2 — Model Selection**
- Choose organism based on conserved biology, cost, ethics, and assay availability.
- Decision tree: gene ortholog exists? → YES → mouse; NO → zebrafish or *Drosophila*.

**Step 3 — Construct / Guide Design**
- Classical: targeting vector with homology arms, Neo cassette, negative selection marker (HSV-TK).
- Modern: sgRNA + Cas9 RNP, ssODN donor template for precise knock-in.

**Step 4 — Delivery into Germ Line**
- Mouse ES cells → electroporation → blastocyst injection → chimeric founder.
- Or direct zygote injection (CRISPR era) → founder mosaic.

**Step 5 — Breeding & Genotyping**
- Intercross heterozygotes to obtain homozygous mutants.
- PCR + Southern blot + sequencing to verify targeted allele.

**Step 6 — Phenotypic Characterization**
- Behavioral assays (Morris water maze, open field test), physiological readouts (EEG, ECG), histopathology, molecular biomarkers.

**Step 7 — Validation Against Human Disease**
- Compare pathology, transcriptomic signatures, drug response to patient data.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory for numerical problems.** Memorize the units and boundary conditions.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Limitation / Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hardy-Weinberg Equilibrium | $p^2 + 2pq + q^2 = 1$ | $p, q$ = allele frequencies (dimensionless, $p+q=1$) | Valid only in large, randomly-mating, non-evolving populations |
| 2 | Allelic Frequency Update | $p' = p - spq \cdot s$ where $s$ = selection coefficient | $s$ dimensionless | Used for recessive disease modelling |
| 3 | Recombination Frequency (Map Distance) | $RF = \left(\dfrac{\text{Recombinant progeny}}{\text{Total progeny}}\right) \times 100\%$ | $RF$ in centiMorgans (cM) | $1\,cM \approx 10^6\,bp$ in mouse |
| 4 | Donor Genome % After *N* Backcrosses | $\%\text{Donor} = 100\% \times \left(1 - \left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{N}\right)$ | $N$ = number of backcross generations | 10 backcrosses ⇒ ≈ **99.9%** donor (congenic) |
| 5 | Penetrance | $\text{Penetrance} = \left(\dfrac{\text{Individuals expressing phenotype}}{\text{Individuals carrying genotype}}\right) \times 100\%$ | Percentage | Complete penetrance = 100% |
| 6 | Expressivity Index | $\sigma_{E} = \dfrac{\sigma_{\text{phenotype}}}{\mu_{\text{phenotype}}} \times 100\%$ | Dimensionless % | High = variable expressivity |
| 7 | CRISPR Indel Efficiency (TIDE) | $\%\text{Editing} = \dfrac{R_{\text{treated}} - R_{\text{control}}}{1 - R_{\text{control}}} \times 100\%$ | $R$ = fraction of unmodified reads | Uses sequencing decomposition |
| 8 | Sample Size for Disease Detection | $N = \left(\dfrac{Z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{p(1-p)} + Z_{\beta}\sqrt{p_1(1-p_1)}}{p_1 - p_0}\right)^2$ | $Z$ = z-score, $p$ = proportion | For two-proportion comparison |
| 9 | Mendelian F2 Phenotypic Ratio (Monohybrid) | $3:1$ (dominant) or $1:2:1$ (codominant) | Genotype frequency | Single-gene autosomal trait |
| 10 | Locus Heterogeneity Probability | $P(\geq 1 \text{ hit}) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1-p_i)$ | $p_i$ = mutation frequency at locus *i* | Used for polygenic disease risk |

> [!WARNING]
> **Table-Syntax Note:** All vertical bars in formulas above use the LaTeX `\vert` or implicit notation to avoid breaking Markdown. Do not write `|x|` in raw form inside any KTU answer sheet either — always typeset as $\lvert x \rvert$.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Biomedical Utility

1. **Pre-clinical Drug Discovery** — Every FDA-approved small molecule has typically been validated in at least a mouse and a non-rodent model.
2. **Toxicology (Tox Studies)** — Required by regulatory agencies before human trials (OECD guidelines).
3. **Gene Therapy Vector Testing** — AAV, lentivirus, and CRISPR delivery systems are first validated in disease models (e.g., *Spp1*-deficient mice for osteoporosis gene therapy).
4. **Personalized/Avatars** — Patient-derived tumor xenografts (PDX) in NSG mice act as "avatars" for individualized oncology.
5. **Vaccine Development** — SARS-CoV-2 vaccines were validated in K18-hACE2 transgenic mice and rhesus macaques.

---

### 2.5 The "Why & How" Logic for Each Model Class

**Why use Mouse?** — Mammalian physiology, conserved immune and nervous systems, established genetic tools, well-annotated genome.
**How is it done?** — Pronuclear injection, ES-cell targeting, or CRISPR zygote electroporation.

**Why use Zebrafish?** — Embryos are transparent (live imaging), high fecundity (>200 eggs/female), conserved vertebrate organogenesis.
**How?** — Microinjection of mRNA/sgRNA into 1-cell embryo; pigmentation mutants (e.g., *casper*) allow adult imaging.

**Why use *Drosophila*?** — 60% of human disease genes have orthologs; balancer chromosomes maintain lethal alleles; cheap.
**How?** — P-element transposition, FLP/FRT, phiC31 integrase.

**Why use *C. elegans*?** — Fully mapped connectome, fixed cell lineage (959 somatic cells), RNAi libraries available.
**How?** — Microinjection, CRISPR, feeding RNAi.

**Why use Organoids?** — Human origin captures species-specific biology (e.g., SARS-CoV-2 entry requires human ACE2).
**How?** — iPSC differentiation in Matrigel with defined growth factors.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation 1** is the most frequently asked numerical in KTU Module 4 boards. **Derivation 2** tests logical reasoning. **Derivation 3** is a biostatistics staple.

---

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Donor Genome Contribution After *N* Backcross Generations

**Problem Setup:** A researcher backcrosses a transgenic mouse (strain A) with a wild-type mouse (strain B) repeatedly. After each generation, she selects offspring carrying the transgene and backcrosses to strain B. After how many generations will the strain be effectively congenic (≥ 99% strain B background)?

**Given:**
- Starting generation (F1): 50% strain A, 50% strain B.
- Each subsequent backcross dilutes strain A genome by 1/2 (Mendelian segregation + independent assortment across chromosomes).

**Step 1 — Define the proportion of strain A genome in generation *N*:**
Let $f_N$ be the fraction of strain A genome after *N* backcrosses.

$$
f_{N} = f_{N-1} \cdot \frac{1}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve the recurrence relation:**
With initial condition $f_0 = 1$ (pure strain A at generation 0):

$$
f_{N} = 1 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{N}
$$

**Step 3 — Express strain B fraction as 1 minus strain A fraction:**
$$
\text{Strain B fraction} = 1 - \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{N}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the ≥ 99.9% congenic threshold:**

$$
1 - \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{N} \geq 0.999
$$

$$
\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{N} \leq 0.001
$$

$$
N \log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) \leq \log_{10}(0.001)
$$

$$
N \cdot (-0.30103) \leq -3
$$

$$
N \geq \frac{3}{0.30103} = 9.97
$$

**Conclusion:**
$$
\boxed{N = 10 \text{ backcross generations}}
$$

This is the empirical basis of the **"10-generation rule"** for producing congenic mouse strains, standard practice in Jackson Laboratory protocols.

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Insight:** After 5 backcrosses, the strain is only **96.875%** background — often insufficient to attribute phenotypic differences to the transgene alone rather than linked modifier loci.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hardy-Weinberg Equilibrium & Disease Allele Frequency

**Problem Setup:** Phenylketonuria (PKU) affects 1 in 10,000 newborns in a population at Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium. The disease is autosomal recessive. Find the carrier (heterozygote) frequency.

**Step 1 — Identify the recessive disease genotype frequency:**
The affected individuals are homozygous recessive: $q^2 = \dfrac{1}{10000}$.

**Step 2 — Solve for allele frequency *q*:**

$$
q = \sqrt{\frac{1}{10000}} = \frac{1}{100} = 0.01
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for dominant normal allele frequency *p*:**

$$
p = 1 - q = 1 - 0.01 = 0.99
$$

**Step 4 — Compute heterozygote (carrier) frequency:**

$$
2pq = 2 \times 0.99 \times 0.01 = 0.0198
$$

**Conclusion:**
$$
\boxed{\text{Carrier frequency} \approx 1.98\% \approx 1 \text{ in } 50}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** For any rare autosomal recessive disease, the carrier frequency is approximately $2q$, while the disease frequency is $q^2$. So **carriers are far more common than affected individuals** — a critical point when modelling disease in heterozygote vs. homozygote animals.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Recombination Frequency Between a Disease Gene and a Marker

**Problem Setup:** In a mouse backcross, a researcher scores 480 progeny for a disease allele (*d*) and a linked microsatellite marker (*m*). Parental types: 216 (*d m*) and 224 (+ +). Recombinant types: 22 (*d +*) and 18 (+ *m*). Calculate the genetic distance.

**Step 1 — Sum recombinant progeny:**

$$
R_{\text{recombinant}} = 22 + 18 = 40
$$

**Step 2 — Sum total progeny:**

$$
R_{\text{total}} = 216 + 224 + 22 + 18 = 480
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the map-distance formula:**

$$
RF = \frac{40}{480} \times 100\% = 8.33\%
$$

**Conclusion:**
$$
\boxed{\text{Map distance} \approx 8.33\,cM}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters for disease modelling:** A marker within ~5 cM of a disease gene enables **linkage-based preclinical diagnosis** in animal colonies — used to track disease alleles during backcrossing.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Properties of Major Model Organisms

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize this matrix** — KTU frequently frames Part A questions as "Compare and contrast *X* and *Y* as disease models" (3 marks).

| Property | Mouse | Zebrafish | *Drosophila* | *C. elegans* | Human iPSC Organoid |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Phylogenetic distance | Mammal | Vertebrate | Invertebrate | Invertebrate | Human (in vitro) |
| Generation time | ~10 wk | ~3 mo | ~10 d | ~4 d | ~weeks (differentiation) |
| Fecundity | 6–10 pups | 200+ embryos | 50+ eggs | 300+ eggs | Unlimited (passage) |
| % Disease gene orthologs | ~99% | ~80% | ~60% | ~40% | 100% (human gene) |
| Optical transparency | No (adult) | Yes (embryo) | No | Yes | Yes |
| Conserved immune system | Yes | Yes (innate + adaptive) | Innate only | Innate only | Absent |
| Cost (USD/animal) | $2–5 | $0.50 | $0.10 | $0.01 | $10–100/well |
| Knockout feasibility | Easy (ES cell) | Easy (CRISPR) | Easy (CRISPR) | Easy (RNAi) | Moderate (CRISPR in iPSC) |
| Ethical barrier | Moderate | Low | Low | Low | Minimal |

---

### 3.5 Worked Example — Mendelian Segregation in a Disease Model Cross

**Scenario:** Two heterozygous carriers of the cystic fibrosis mutation (*Cftr*⁺/⁻) are intercrossed. Predict the genotypic and phenotypic ratios in F1.

**Step 1 — Set up the Punnett square:**

|  | *Cftr*⁺ | *Cftr*⁻ |
|---|---|---|
| ***Cftr*⁺** | +/+ | +/- |
| ***Cftr*⁻** | +/- | -/- |

**Step 2 — Read the ratios:**

$$
\text{Genotype ratio} = 1 : 2 : 1 \;(+/\!+ \;:\; +/\!- \;:\; -/\!-)
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to phenotype ratio (recessive disease):**

$$
\text{Phenotype ratio} = 3 \;(\text{normal}) : 1 \;(\text{CF-affected})
$$

**Step 4 — Expected frequency of homozygous affected pups in a litter of 8:**

$$
N_{\text{affected}} = 8 \times \frac{1}{4} = 2
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget that heterozygous (+/-) *Cftr* mice are **phenotypically normal** carriers — they do NOT exhibit CF symptoms. Only homozygous (-/-) mutants are the disease model.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — CRISPR-Based Knockout Mouse Disease Model Pipeline

The following Mermaid diagram maps the operational workflow for generating a CRISPR knockout mouse model of a human monogenic disease. Node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters per protocol.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    n1["STEP 1: Identify Human Disease Gene via GWAS and Pedigree"]
    n2["STEP 2: Validate Ortholog in Mouse Genome via BLAST"]
    n3["STEP 3: Design sgRNA Targeting Exon 2-3 Boundary"]
    n4["STEP 4: Synthesize sgRNA and Cas9 mRNA In Vitro"]
    n5["STEP 5: Microinject RNP Complex into C57BL/6 Zygote"]
    n6["STEP 6: Implant into Pseudopregnant Surrogate"]
    n7["STEP 7: Screen Founder Pups by T7E1 Assay and Sequencing"]
    n8["STEP 8: Breed F0 Mosaic x WT to Get F1 Heterozygotes"]
    n9["STEP 9: Intercross F1 to Generate Homozygous Knockout"]
    n10["STEP 10: Phenotype Characterization Behavior Histology Molecular"]
    n11["STEP 11: Validate Against Human Disease Transcriptome"]
    n12["STEP 12: Publish and Share Strain via Jackson Laboratory"]

    n1 --> n2 --> n3 --> n4 --> n5 --> n6 --> n7 --> n8 --> n9 --> n10 --> n11 --> n12

    qa["Quality Checkpoint: Off-Target Sequencing using GUIDE-seq"]
    qb["Quality Checkpoint: Confirm Germline Transmission"]
    qc["Quality Checkpoint: RT-qPCR for Loss of Expression"]

    n7 -.-> qa
    n8 -.-> qb
    n10 -.-> qc

    classDef stepBox fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px,color:#0D47A1
    classDef qcBox fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:1px,color:#BF360C,stroke-dasharray: 5 5

    class n1,n2,n3,n4,n5,n6,n7,n8,n9,n10,n11,n12 stepBox
    class qa,qb,qc qcBox
```

---

### 4.2 Decision Tree — Choosing the Right Disease Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1["Disease Gene Identified"] --> s2{"Conserved Ortholog in Mouse?"}
    s2 -- "Yes" --> s3["Use Mouse Model"]
    s2 -- "No" --> s4{"Vertebrate-Specific Function?"}
    s4 -- "Yes" --> s5["Use Zebrafish"]
    s4 -- "No" --> s6{"Pathway Conserved in Drosophila?"}
    s6 -- "Yes" --> s7["Use Drosophila"]
    s6 -- "No" --> s8["Use C. elegans or In Silico"]

    s3 --> s9{"Whole Organism or Tissue Needed?"}
    s5 --> s9
    s7 --> s9
    s8 --> s9

    s9 -- "Whole Organism" --> s10["Proceed with Transgenic KO/KI"]
    s9 -- "Human Tissue Context" --> s11["Use iPSC-Derived Organoid"]

    s10 --> s12["Validate Phenotype"]
    s11 --> s12
    s12 --> s13["Cross-Validate in Two Systems Triangulation"]

    classDef decisionNode fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#E65100
    classDef actionNode fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#1B5E20

    class s2,s4,s6,s9 decisionNode
    class s1,s3,s5,s7,s8,s10,s11,s12,s13 actionNode
```

---

### 4.3 Schematic — Conditional Knockout Using Cre-loxP System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    a1["Floxed Allele loxP - Exon 3 - loxP"] --> a2{"Inject into ES Cell"]
    a2 --> a3["Select Neomycin-Resistant Clones"]
    a3 --> a4["Inject into Blastocyst"]
    a4 --> a5["Chimeric Founder"]
    a5 --> a6["Cross with Tissue-Specific Cre Line"]
    a6 --> a7{"Target Tissue"}
    a7 -- "Brain Cortex" --> a8["CamKII-Cre"]
    a7 -- "Liver" --> a9["Albumin-Cre"]
    a7 -- "T-Cells" --> a10["LCK-Cre"]

    a8 --> a11["Exon 3 Excised Only in Cortex"]
    a9 --> a12["Exon 3 Excised Only in Liver"]
    a10 --> a13["Exon 3 Excised Only in T-Cells"]

    classDef floxBox fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#6A1B9A,stroke-width:2px,color:#4A148C
    classDef tissueBox fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#0277BD,stroke-width:1px,color:#01579B
    classDef resultBox fill:#DCEDC8,stroke:#33691E,stroke-width:2px,color:#1B5E20

    class a1,a2,a3,a4,a5,a6,a7 floxBox
    class a8,a9,a10 tissueBox
    class a11,a12,a13 resultBox
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read this for KTU answers:** Begin with "A *floxed* allele has the target exon flanked by *loxP* sites in the same orientation. Crossing with a tissue-specific Cre line expresses Cre recombinase **only** in the target tissue, causing site-specific recombination and exon deletion. This produces a *conditional knockout* that avoids embryonic lethality common in global knockouts."

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q1.** Define a disease model. List any four characteristic features of an ideal animal disease model.

**Model Answer:**

A **disease model** is a genetically engineered or naturally occurring biological system (animal, organoid, or cell line) that recapitulates the etiology, pathophysiology, and clinical features of a specific human disorder.

**Four characteristic features of an ideal disease model:**
1. **Construct Validity** — shares the same genetic cause or molecular mechanism as the human disease.
2. **Face Validity** — symptoms, histopathology, and biomarkers resemble the human condition.
3. **Predictive Validity** — responds to standard-of-care therapies similarly to humans.
4. **Practical Utility** — affordable, ethically acceptable, short generation time, manipulable genome.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for four features with one-line justification each)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q2.** Distinguish between a **transgenic addition model** and a **gene-targeted knockout model**. Give one example of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Transgenic Addition Model | Gene-Targeted Knockout Model |
|---|---|---|
| Genomic event | Random integration of foreign DNA | Homologous replacement of endogenous locus |
| Endogenous gene | Usually unaltered | Functionally disrupted |
| Mechanism | Pronuclear microinjection | ES-cell homologous recombination or CRISPR |
| Expression | Often ectopic, driven by exogenous promoter | Under control of native regulatory elements |
| Example | **Oncomouse** (MMTV-*myc* driven mammary tumors) | **Cftr⁻/⁻ mouse** (cystic fibrosis) |

*(3 marks: 1 for correct distinction table + 1 for valid examples)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

#### ⭐ Question A — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**Q A.** *(a)* Discuss in detail the **strategies used for the generation of transgenic animal disease models**, with suitable examples. Mention the role of embryonic stem (ES) cells in homologous recombination-based gene targeting. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* Explain the principle of **Cre-*lox*P conditional knockout** with a neat labelled diagram. How does it overcome the limitations of conventional knockouts? *(7 marks)*

---

##### ⭐ Model Answer to Q A(a)

**Introduction:**
Transgenic animal disease models are generated by introducing, deleting, or modifying specific genetic elements so that the resulting organism phenocopies a human disease. Multiple complementary strategies are employed.

**Strategy 1 — Pronuclear Microinjection (Transgenic Addition)**

1. Fertilized eggs (zygotes) are harvested from superovulated donor females within hours of fertilization.
2. A linearized DNA construct containing the gene of interest plus a tissue-specific promoter is microinjected into the male pronucleus.
3. The injected zygotes are surgically implanted into the oviduct of a pseudopregnant surrogate mother.
4. The resulting pups (founders) are screened for transgene integration by PCR or Southern blotting.
5. Founders are bred to establish transgenic lines.

**Example:** The **Oncomouse** (Harvard, 1988) carrying the *c-myc* oncogene driven by the MMTV promoter develops spontaneous mammary tumors and is a model for human breast cancer.

**Strategy 2 — ES-Cell-Mediated Gene Targeting (Knockout / Knock-In)**

1. A **targeting vector** is constructed containing:
   - Two **homology arms** (5′ and 3′, each ~5–8 kb) flanking the desired mutation site.
   - A **positive selection cassette** (e.g., *neo*^*R*^*) inserted within the targeted exon.
   - A **negative selection marker** (e.g., HSV-*tk*) outside the homology arms for enrichment against random integrants.
2. The vector is electroporated into murine ES cells (typically from the 129/Sv strain).
3. Cells are subjected to **positive selection** (G418 kills non-resistant cells).
4. Cells that integrated randomly also carry the *tk* gene and are eliminated by **negative selection** (ganciclovir).
5. Surviving ES clones are screened by PCR and Southern blot for correct homologous recombination.
6. Correctly targeted ES cells are microinjected into C57BL/6 blastocysts and implanted into surrogate mothers.
7. Chimeric males are bred with wild-type females; germline transmission yields heterozygous mutant offspring.

**Example:** The ***Cftr*⁻/⁻ mouse** (Snouwaert et al., 1992) was generated by disrupting exon 10 of the *Cftr* gene, recapitulating cystic fibrosis pathology in the lung, pancreas, and intestine.

**Strategy 3 — CRISPR/Cas9 Genome Editing**

1. A single-guide RNA (sgRNA) is designed to direct Cas9 nuclease to the target locus.
2. The sgRNA + Cas9 protein (as RNP) is microinjected into zygotes or electroporated into ES cells.
3. Cas9 induces a double-strand break, repaired by **non-homologous end joining (NHEJ)** → indels → frameshift → knockout.
4. With a single-stranded DNA donor template, **homology-directed repair (HDR)** enables precise knock-in.

**Example:** ***Trp53* knockout rats** generated by CRISPR for cancer biology studies (Tong et al., 2010).

**Role of ES Cells:**

- ES cells are **pluripotent** — they can contribute to all tissues, including germ cells, when injected into a blastocyst.
- They support **homologous recombination** at much higher frequencies than somatic cells.
- They can be **expanded clonally in vitro**, allowing selection and molecular verification before generating an animal.
- The discovery of ES cells (Evans & Kaufman, 1981; Nobel Prize 2007) was the technical foundation of modern disease modelling.

*(7 marks: 2 for microinjection, 3 for ES targeting details with example, 1 for CRISPR, 1 for ES cell role summary)*

---

##### ⭐ Model Answer to Q A(b)

**Principle of Cre-*lox*P Recombination:**

- **Cre recombinase** is a 38-kDa tyrosine site-specific recombinase encoded by bacteriophage P1.
- It recognizes **34-bp *loxP* sites**, each composed of two 13-bp inverted repeats flanking an 8-bp asymmetric spacer.
- The spacer's orientation defines the **directionality** of the *loxP* site.

**Modes of Outcome:**

1. Two *loxP* sites in the **same orientation** on a linear DNA → Cre excises the intervening DNA as a circular product (deletion).
2. Two *loxP* sites in **opposite orientation** → Cre inverts the intervening DNA (inversion).
3. Two *loxP* sites on **different chromosomes** → Cre mediates translocation.

**Application to Disease Models:**

A "**floxed**" allele is engineered such that one or more critical exons of the disease gene are flanked by *loxP* sites in the same orientation. The mouse carrying the floxed allele is phenotypically normal until crossed with a **Cre-expressing transgenic line**.

**Tissue-Specific Knockout Example:**

- Mouse 1: *Gene X*^flox/flox^ (exon flanked by *loxP*).
- Mouse 2: *Albumin-Cre* (Cre expressed only in hepatocytes).
- Cross yields offspring that delete *Gene X* **exclusively in liver**, sparing all other tissues.

**Conditional Timing:**
By using an **inducible Cre** (e.g., CreER^T2^ activated by tamoxifen), gene deletion can be triggered at a chosen developmental stage or in adults.

**Diagram (Mermaid equivalent — to be drawn in answer sheet):**

```
[5' loxP] --- Exon 3 --- [3' loxP]  ===>  After Cre:  [5' loxP] + circular excised fragment
```

**Advantages Over Conventional Knockouts:**

| Conventional KO | Conditional KO |
|---|---|
| Gene deleted in **all** cells from conception | Gene deleted **only in target tissue / time** |
| Often causes **embryonic lethality** | Bypasses lethality — adult-onset disease modelling |
| Cannot study **tissue-specific function** | Enables **tissue-specific phenotype dissection** |
| Compensatory adaptation possible | Acute deletion reduces compensation |

*(7 marks: 2 for Cre-loxP mechanism, 2 for application with example, 2 for advantages, 1 for labelled schematic)*

---

#### ⭐ Question B — **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Adapted]**

**Q B.** *(a)* Compare **mouse, zebrafish, and *Drosophila*** as disease model organisms. Highlight at least **six points** of comparison. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* With a neat flow diagram, describe the **generation of a CRISPR/Cas9-based knockout mouse model** for studying a monogenic disease. Mention the methods used to verify gene knockout. *(7 marks)*

---

##### ⭐ Model Answer to Q B(a)

| Parameter | Mouse (*Mus musculus*) | Zebrafish (*Danio rerio*) | *Drosophila melanogaster* |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Phylogeny** | Mammal (highest conservation) | Vertebrate (lower vertebrate) | Invertebrate (insect) |
| **Generation time** | ~10 weeks | ~3 months | ~10–14 days |
| **Fecundity** | 6–10 pups/litter | 200+ embryos/wk | 50+ eggs/female/day |
| **% Disease gene orthologs** | ~99% | ~80% | ~60% |
| **Embryo transparency** | No | **Yes** (live imaging possible) | Yes (early stages) |
| **Genome editing ease** | ES cell + CRISPR | CRISPR only | CRISPR + phiC31 |
| **Conserved immune system** | Adaptive + Innate | Adaptive + Innate | Innate only |
| **Cost per animal (approx.)** | USD 2–5 | USD 0.5 | USD 0.1 |
| **Example disease model** | *Cftr*⁻/⁻ (CF), *SOD1*^G93A^ (ALS) | *SCN1a* (epilepsy), *MYH7* (cardiomyopathy) | *PINK1/Parkin* (Parkinson's), *huntingtin* (HD) |
| **Main limitation** | Cost, ethics, no live embryo imaging | Aquatic physiology, no closed circulation | No closed circulation, no vertebrae |

**Synthesis:** Each organism occupies a complementary niche in modern disease modelling. **Mouse** remains the gold standard for mammalian physiology; **zebrafish** excels in high-throughput small-molecule screens owing to embryo transparency; ***Drosophila*** is unmatched in cost-effective genetic pathway dissection.

*(7 marks: 1 mark each for ~6 well-explained points + 1 for synthesis summary)*

---

##### ⭐ Model Answer to Q B(b)

**Step-by-Step Pipeline for CRISPR/Cas9 Knockout Mouse:**

**Step 1 — Target Identification:**
Select an exonic region (preferably exon 2–5) where frameshift indel leads to nonsense-mediated decay. Verify uniqueness via BLAST to avoid off-targets.

**Step 2 — sgRNA Design:**
- Use online tools (e.g., CRISPOR, Benchling) to select a 20-nt protospacer with high on-target and low off-target scores.
- Synthesize sgRNA by in vitro transcription (T7 RNA polymerase) or purchase chemically synthesized sgRNA.

**Step 3 — RNP Complex Assembly:**
Mix Cas9 protein with sgRNA (molar ratio 1:1 to 1:2) at 37 °C for 15 min to form the ribonucleoprotein (RNP) complex.

**Step 4 — Zygote Microinjection or Electroporation:**
- Harvest C57BL/6 zygotes at the 1-cell stage.
- Inject ~2–5 pL of RNP into the cytoplasm or pronucleus using a microinjection needle.
- *Alternative:* electroporation with a Bio-Rad Gene Pulser for higher throughput.

**Step 5 — Embryo Transfer:**
Culture injected zygotes to the 2-cell stage, then surgically transfer 20–25 embryos into the oviduct of a pseudopregnant ICR surrogate mother.

**Step 6 — Founder Screening (F0):**

**Methods to Verify Gene Knockout:**

1. **T7E1 / SURVEYOR Mismatch Assay:** PCR-amplify the target region; denature-reanneal; cleave mismatched heteroduplexes with T7E1 endonuclease; quantify indel efficiency by band densitometry.
2. **Sanger Sequencing + TIDE / ICE Analysis:** Sequence the PCR product; decompose the chromatogram into wild-type and indel alleles to compute editing efficiency.
3. **Next-Generation Sequencing (Amplicon-Seq):** Most accurate; provides per-allele indel spectrum.
4. **RT-qPCR:** Quantifies reduction in mRNA level of the target gene.
5. **Western Blot:** Confirms loss of protein expression.
6. **Southern Blot:** Confirms genomic structure (rarely needed for simple CRISPR KOs).

**Step 7 — Breeding:**
Cross F0 mosaic founders with wild-type C57BL/6; screen F1 pups for heterozygous knockout. Intercross F1 heterozygotes to obtain homozygous knockout (F2).

**Step 8 — Phenotyping:**
Conduct disease-relevant behavioral, histological, and molecular assays on F2 homozygotes and compare with wild-type littermate controls.

**Flow Diagram (text form for answer sheet):**

```
sgRNA Design → RNP Assembly → Zygote Microinjection → Embryo Transfer →
F0 Screening (T7E1 + Sequencing) → F1 Breeding → F2 Homozygotes →
Phenotype Analysis → Disease Model Validated
```

*(7 marks: 4 for step-by-step pipeline, 2 for verification methods, 1 for flow diagram representation)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing random integration with targeted modification** — Transgenic addition ≠ Knockout. Examiners deduct heavily if not distinguished.
> 2. **Skipping germline transmission** — F0 chimeras must be bred to confirm the mutation is in the germ line; merely showing phenotype in F0 is insufficient.
> 3. **Forgetting negative selection** — Many students omit the HSV-*tk* / ganciclovir negative selection step in ES-cell targeting, losing 1 mark.
> 4. **Mis-stating Cre-*loxP* sizes** — *loxP* = **34 bp**, spacer = **8 bp**, repeats = **13 bp each**. Wrong numbers = 0 for that sub-part.
> 5. **Not drawing a labelled diagram in Q A(b)** — A neat *loxP*-flanked allele schematic is mandatory; no diagram = -2 marks.
> 6. **Mixing up Mendelian ratios** — Monohybrid 3:1; Dihybrid 9:3:3:1; Test cross 1:1. Examiner checks this carefully.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📌 Important Things to Remember for KTU Module 4 — Disease Model**

- ✅ **Definition:** Engineered or natural biological system that phenocopies a human disease.
- ✅ **Core rationale:** Ethics, manipulability, speed, genetic homogeneity.
- ✅ **Mouse generation time:** ~10 weeks; gestation ~19–21 days.
- ✅ **10-backcross rule** → congenic strain (≥ 99.9% background).
- ✅ **Hardy-Weinberg:** $p^2 + 2pq + q^2 = 1$; for rare recessive disease, carriers ≈ $2q$.
- ✅ **Map distance** in cM = (recombinants / total) × 100.
- ✅ **Three classical strategies:** Pronuclear microinjection, ES-cell targeting, CRISPR/Cas9.
- ✅ **Knockout vs Knock-in:** KO disrupts gene function; KI introduces specific mutation.
- ✅ **Targeting vector components:** 5′ homology arm, 3′ homology arm, *neo*^*R*^ (positive), HSV-*tk* (negative).
- ✅ **ES cells** = pluripotent, support homologous recombination, clonally expandable.
- ✅ **Cre-*loxP*:** *loxP* = 34 bp (2 × 13-bp repeats + 8-bp spacer); same-orientation sites → deletion; opposite → inversion.
- ✅ **Conditional KO** overcomes embryonic lethality of global KO.
- ✅ **Oncomouse** = first patented transgenic disease model (Harvard, 1988).
- ✅ **CF mouse** = *Cftr*⁻/⁻; **mdx mouse** = *Dmd*⁻/⁻ (Duchenne MD); **SOD1^G93A^** = ALS model.
- ✅ **Zebrafish strengths:** transparency, fecundity, drug screening.
- ✅ **Verification methods:** T7E1, Sanger/TIDE, amplicon-seq, RT-qPCR, Western blot.
- ✅ **Triangulation principle:** validate findings in ≥ 2 orthogonal model systems.
- ✅ **Pipeline memory aid:** *Identify gene → Choose model → Design construct/guide → Deliver → Breed → Genotype → Phenotype → Validate.*

> [!TIP]
> **Final Board Strategy:** For any 14-mark KTU question on disease models, structure your answer as:
> 1. *Brief definition + context* (1 mark)
> 2. *Strategy breakdown with vector/cross details* (4 marks)
> 3. *Diagram or schematic* (2 marks)
> 4. *Specific example with phenotype* (3 marks)
> 5. *Advantages/limitations/comparisons* (3 marks)
> 6. *Conclusion with validation statement* (1 mark)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gene Therapy: Somatic and Germ-line Therapy (in vivo and ex vivo)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT524 - GENETIC ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Gene Therapy: Somatic and Germ-line Therapy (in vivo and ex vivo)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gene Therapy (KTU Syllabus Definition)**
> Gene therapy is a **therapeutic strategy** that involves the deliberate modification, insertion, or deletion of genetic material (DNA or RNA) within an individual's cells to treat, prevent, or cure a disease. It targets the **root molecular cause** of genetic disorders rather than merely managing symptoms.

Gene therapy is broadly classified along **two orthogonal axes**:

| Axis | Category 1 | Category 2 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Target Cell Type** | **Somatic Gene Therapy** | **Germ-line Gene Therapy** |
| **Delivery Strategy** | **In vivo Therapy** | **Ex vivo Therapy** |

This produces **four operational combinations** that form the conceptual core of Module 4.

---

## 1.2 Somatic vs Germ-line Gene Therapy

### A. Somatic Gene Therapy (SGT)
> [!NOTE]
> **Somatic Gene Therapy** targets the **somatic (body) cells** of a patient — i.e., any cell *except* sperm, eggs, or their precursors. The genetic modification **affects only the treated individual** and is **NOT inherited** by future generations.

**Intuitive Analogy — The Local Repair Shop:**
Imagine a car (your body) with a faulty engine spark plug (a defective gene in a body cell). A mechanic (the therapist) replaces *only* that spark plug. The repair fixes the car's performance, but your children's cars (offspring) are built from the original factory blueprint — they are unaffected. This is **somatic therapy**: a **localized, non-heritable fix**.

### B. Germ-line Gene Therapy (GLGT)
> [!NOTE]
> **Germ-line Gene Therapy** targets the **reproductive cells** — sperm, ova, or early embryonic cells (zygote / pre-implantation embryo). The genetic modification **becomes a permanent part of the individual's genome** and is **transmitted to all future generations** (offspring, and their offspring in turn).

**Intuitive Analogy — Editing the Factory Blueprint:**
Instead of fixing a single car's spark plug, the mechanic goes back to the **factory's master blueprint** and permanently corrects the spark plug design. Every car produced from that blueprint thereafter — today, tomorrow, and a hundred years from now — is built correctly. This is **germ-line therapy**: a **heritable, species-altering fix**.

---

## 1.3 In vivo vs Ex vivo Delivery

### A. In vivo Gene Therapy
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In vivo** (Latin: *"within the living"*) — The therapeutic gene is delivered **directly into the patient's body** using a vector (viral or non-viral). Cells are modified **inside the body**.

**Analogy — Vaccination:** A flu shot injects the agent directly into your arm; your body handles the rest internally. Similarly, in vivo therapy injects a vector (e.g., AAV, lentivirus) into the bloodstream, target tissue, or organ.

### B. Ex vivo Gene Therapy
> [!NOTE]
> **Ex vivo** (Latin: *"out of the living"*) — Target cells are **harvested from the patient**, **cultured and genetically modified in the laboratory**, and then **re-infused/transplanted back** into the same patient.

**Analogy — Sending a Package to a Repair Workshop:** You don't fix the package at home. You **ship it to a specialized workshop** (the lab), where experts repair it, and **send it back** to you. The cells (the "package") are taken out, fixed, and returned.

---

## 1.4 Critical Distinction Matrix

| Feature | Somatic Therapy | Germ-line Therapy |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Target cells** | Body cells (e.g., liver, blood, muscle) | Gametes / zygote / early embryo |
| **Heritability** | **Non-heritable** (dies with patient) | **Heritable** (passed to offspring) |
| **Ethical status** | Widely accepted; clinically approved | **Banned in most countries** (ethical, eugenics concerns) |
| **Current regulation** | FDA / EMA approved products exist | Prohibited in humans (40+ countries) |
| **Reversibility** | Generally reversible per patient | **Irreversible** at population level |

| Feature | In vivo | Ex vivo |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Cell location during modification** | Inside the body | Outside the body (in culture) |
| **Cells involved** | Difficult-to-harvest cells (neurons, cardiomyocytes) | Easily harvestable cells (HSCs, T-cells, keratinocytes) |
| **Immunogenicity risk** | Higher (vector exposed to immune system) | Lower (cells washed before re-infusion) |
| **Quality control** | Limited | **High** (transduced cells screened/selected) |
| **Example vector** | AAV, adenovirus, lipid nanoparticles | Retrovirus, lentivirus, electroporation |
| **Landmark example** | Luxturna® (RPE65, AAV2) | CAR-T therapy (Kymriah®, Yescarta®) |

---

## 1.5 Real-World Approved Examples (Kerala Board Favourites)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board-Relevant Approved Gene Therapies to Memorize:**
> - **Luxturna® (voretigene neparvovec)** — In vivo, somatic, **AAV2** vector, treats **RPE65-mutation Leber congenital amaurosis** (inherited blindness).
> - **Kymriah® (tisagenlecleucel)** — Ex vivo, somatic, lentiviral vector, **CAR-T cells** for B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia.
> - **Zolgensma® (onasemnogene abeparvovec)** — In vivo, somatic, **AAV9** vector, treats **Spinal Muscular Atrophy (SMA)** in infants.
> - **Strimvelis®** — Ex vivo, somatic, retroviral vector, treats **ADA-SCID** ("Bubble Boy" disease).

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Operational Strategies — Combined Logic

A complete gene therapy protocol requires **three sequential decisions**:
1. **What disease?** → Identify the defective gene and target tissue.
2. **Which cell type?** → Somatic (safe, contained) or Germ-line (heritable, ethically fraught).
3. **Which delivery route?** → In vivo (direct) or Ex vivo (cell-mediated).

This produces the **2 × 2 therapeutic matrix** that examiners love to test.

---

## 2.2 Strategy 1 — Somatic In vivo Therapy

### Operational Steps
1. **Vector construction** — A viral capsid (often **AAV** or adenovirus) is engineered to carry the therapeutic transgene. The viral replication genes are removed to render it **replication-deficient**.
2. **Direct delivery** — The vector is injected:
   - **Intravitreally** (eye, e.g., Luxturna®)
   - **Intravenously** (systemic, e.g., Zolgensma® for SMA crosses the blood-brain barrier via AAV9)
   - **Intrathecally** (spinal fluid)
   - **Intramuscularly** or **subcutaneously**
3. **Cellular uptake** — Vector binds cell-surface receptors → receptor-mediated endocytosis.
4. **Nuclear entry & expression** — Therapeutic gene is transcribed and translated by the host machinery.
5. **Therapeutic effect** — Functional protein corrects the deficiency locally or systemically.

### Why This Strategy?
- Avoids the need for cell harvesting (important for **post-mitotic cells** like neurons, photoreceptors, cardiomyocytes).
- Single administration can give **long-term or permanent expression** (AAV episomes are stable in non-dividing cells).

### Limitations
- **Immune response** to viral capsid.
- **Off-target transduction** of non-target tissues.
- **Limited packaging capacity** (~4.7 kb for AAV).
- Difficult to verify which cells were successfully transduced.

---

## 2.3 Strategy 2 — Somatic Ex vivo Therapy

### Operational Steps
1. **Cell harvest** — Patient undergoes **apheresis** or **bone marrow extraction** to obtain target cells (e.g., **HSCs** — hematopoietic stem cells, or **T-lymphocytes**).
2. **In vitro culture** — Cells are expanded in culture media with growth factors (e.g., IL-2 for T-cells, SCF + FLT3L for HSCs).
3. **Genetic modification** — A viral vector (typically **retrovirus or lentivirus**) or non-viral method (electroporation, mRNA transfection) introduces the therapeutic gene.
4. **Selection/expansion** — Successfully transduced cells may be selected (antibiotic resistance marker) and expanded.
5. **Quality control** — Cells are tested for:
   - Transduction efficiency
   - Insertional mutagenesis risk
   - Sterility, viability, potency
6. **Re-infusion** — Modified cells are infused back into the patient (often after **myeloablative conditioning** for HSC therapies).

### Why This Strategy?
- **Superior quality control** — Cells can be screened, sorted, and verified before return.
- **Lower immunogenicity** — Vector is never exposed to the whole immune system.
- **Enables complex modifications** — e.g., **CAR-T** receptors require chimeric gene construction *and* T-cell activation outside the body.

### Limitations
- Requires **manufacturing infrastructure** (GMP facility).
- Time-intensive (2–4 weeks).
- Limited to cells that can be **harvested, expanded, and re-infused**.

---

## 2.4 Strategy 3 — Germ-line Therapy (Theoretically)

### Operational Steps (Hypothetical / Animal Models)
1. **Gamete or zygote isolation** — Sperm, oocyte, or fertilized egg obtained (in humans: ethically prohibited).
2. **CRISPR/Cas9 or viral editing** — DNA introduced via microinjection, electroporation, or sperm-mediated transfer.
3. **Embryo development** — Modified embryo implanted into surrogate (in animal research).
4. **Germ-line transmission** — All cells, including future gametes, carry the edit.

### Why This Strategy? (Theoretical Advantages)
- **Permanent cure** for inherited disorders in a single generation.
- Eradication of disease from family lineage.
- Could correct **mitochondrial DNA disorders** transmitted maternally.

### Critical Limitations
- **Off-target mutations** become heritable.
- **Mosaicism** risk — not all cells edited.
- **Ethical catastrophe** if used for enhancement (designer babies).
- Prohibited in humans by the **Council of Europe (Oviedo Convention)** and the **Indian Council of Medical Research (ICMR) National Guidelines for Gene Therapy, 2019**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** The ICMR Guidelines (2019, updated 2020) explicitly **prohibit germ-line gene therapy in India** and restrict somatic gene therapy to clinical trial settings with stringent oversight. Examiners frequently test this.

---

## 2.5 Vectors: The Delivery Vehicles (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vector Selection Drives the Therapy Type — Memorize This Table**

| Vector | Genome | Capacity | In vivo / Ex vivo | Dividing Cells? | Integration? | Key Examples |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Retrovirus (MLV)** | RNA | ~8 kb | **Ex vivo** | Required | **Yes** (random) | Strimvelis® (ADA-SCID) |
| **Lentivirus** | RNA | ~8–10 kb | **Ex vivo** | Not required | **Yes** (safer) | Kymriah®, CAR-T |
| **Adenovirus** | dsDNA | ~36 kb | **In vivo** | Either | **No** | Oncolytic therapy |
| **AAV** | ssDNA | **~4.7 kb** | **In vivo** | **Non-dividing** | **No** (episomal) | Luxturna®, Zolgensma® |
| **Liposomes / LNPs** | NA | Large | In vivo | Either | No | Patisiran (siRNA), mRNA vaccines |
| **CRISPR RNP** | NA | NA | Both | Either | Site-specific | **exa-cel (Casgevy®)** — sickle cell, ex vivo HSC editing |

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Concept Density Matrix

| Concept / Parameter | Definition / Value | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **AAV packaging limit** | $4.7 \text{ kb}$ | Kilobases of single-stranded DNA |
| **Adenovirus capacity** | $\approx 36 \text{ kb}$ | Double-stranded DNA |
| **Lentivirus capacity** | $\approx 8$ to $10 \text{ kb}$ | RNA genome |
| **MOI (Multiplicity of Infection)** | $\text{MOI} = \dfrac{\text{Number of viral particles}}{\text{Number of target cells}}$ | Typically 1–100 for ex vivo |
| **Transduction efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{N_{\text{transduced}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage |
| **Therapeutic index (TI)** | $\text{TI} = \dfrac{\text{Lethal dose}_{50}}{\text{Effective dose}_{50}}$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| **Viral titer** | $\text{TU/mL} = \dfrac{\text{transducing units}}{\text{millilitre}}$ | TU/mL |
| **Vector copy number (VCN)** | Average transgene copies per cell | Typically $< 5$ for safety |

> [!NOTE]
> **Application Note for Engineers:** The MOI, VCN, and transduction efficiency are the **three critical QC metrics** examined by regulatory bodies (FDA, EMA, DCGI) before approving any gene therapy product. Students in KTU's biotechnology/biomedical engineering streams should recognize these as analogous to **process control parameters** in any engineering system.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Gene therapy vector design draws directly from:
- **Chemical Engineering:** LNP formulation, fluid dynamics of intravenous delivery, bioreactor design for ex vivo expansion.
- **Electrical Engineering:** Viral capsid surface engineering (e.g., **directed evolution of AAV capsids** for tissue-specific tropism).
- **Computer Science:** CRISPR guide-RNA design algorithms, off-target prediction, and AI-driven vector engineering.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Microfluidic devices for cell sorting and high-throughput transduction screening.

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — Detailed Walkthrough of Key Protocols

## 3.1 Exhaustive Case Study — Ex vivo CAR-T Cell Therapy (Kymriah® Model)

This is a **high-yield KTU exam scenario**. The model answer requires step-by-step logical articulation.

### Stage 1 — Patient Apheresis
1. Patient diagnosed with **relapsed/refractory B-cell Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia (ALL)**.
2. Undergoes **leukapheresis** — blood is passed through a cell separator that enriches **peripheral blood mononuclear cells (PBMCs)**, particularly **T-lymphocytes**.
3. Output: ~10⁹ to 10¹⁰ T-cells suspended in apheresis buffer.

### Stage 2 — T-cell Activation
4. T-cells are cultured in **X-VIVO 15 or AIM-V serum-free media**.
5. **Anti-CD3/CD28 magnetic beads** (e.g., Dynabeads®) are added to provide the **primary and co-stimulatory signals** required for T-cell activation and proliferation.
6. Cells are incubated at **37 °C, 5% CO₂** for 24–48 hours.
7. Activated T-cells express surface markers CD25 and CD69 (verification of activation).

### Stage 3 — Lentiviral Transduction
8. Activated T-cells are transduced with a **replication-incompetent lentiviral vector** carrying the **anti-CD19 CAR transgene**.
9. The CAR construct encodes:
   - **Single-chain variable fragment (scFv)** — anti-CD19 antibody domain
   - **Hinge / transmembrane region** — typically CD8α
   - **Co-stimulatory domain** — 4-1BB (CD137) *or* CD28
   - **CD3ζ signaling domain** — T-cell activation motif
10. Multiplicity of infection (MOI) is typically set between **2 and 10 TU/cell** to balance efficiency and safety.
11. Incubation continues for 24–48 hours; polybrene or DEAE-dextran may enhance binding.

### Stage 4 — Expansion
12. Transduced T-cells are expanded in **G-Rex® or rocking-bag bioreactors** with **IL-2 (50–100 IU/mL)** or **IL-7/IL-15** for 7–14 days.
13. Cell number expands **100- to 1000-fold**, reaching **10⁸ to 10⁹ CAR-T cells** per dose.

### Stage 5 — Quality Control
14. **Transduction efficiency** measured by flow cytometry (anti-CD19 CAR surface staining). Acceptance criterion typically $\geq 20\%$.
15. **Vector copy number (VCN)** measured by qPCR/ddPCR. Acceptance: $\text{VCN} \leq 5$ copies/cell to minimize insertional oncogenesis risk.
16. **Potency assay:** Co-culture with CD19⁺ target cells → measure IFN-γ release.
17. **Sterility:** Mycoplasma, endotoxin, sterility cultures.
18. **Identity:** CD3⁺ purity $\geq 95\%$.

### Stage 6 — Patient Conditioning & Infusion
19. Patient receives **lymphodepleting chemotherapy** (cyclophosphamide + fludarabine) to create homeostatic space and reduce Treg suppression.
20. CAR-T cells are washed, resuspended in cryopreservation medium, thawed at bedside, and infused IV over 5–15 minutes.
21. Cells home to lymphoid tissues, recognize CD19⁺ B-cells (malignant and normal), and execute cytotoxic killing.

### Stage 7 — Monitoring
22. **Cytokine release syndrome (CRS)** monitored — treated with tocilizumab (anti-IL-6R) if severe.
23. **Immune effector cell-associated neurotoxicity syndrome (ICANS)** monitored.
24. **Persistence** of CAR-T cells tracked by qPCR or flow cytometry (months to years).

> [!NOTE]
> **The 7-stage pipeline above is the canonical KTU model answer for ex vivo gene therapy questions.** Memorize the *phases*, not just the names.

---

## 3.2 Mathematical Derivation — MOI and Transduction Efficiency Relationship

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Poisson distribution governs viral transduction statistics.** This is a frequent 14-mark derivation question.

For a viral vector applied at multiplicity $m$ (viral particles per cell), the probability $P(k)$ that a given cell receives exactly $k$ viral particles follows a **Poisson distribution**:

$$
P(k) = \frac{m^{k} \, e^{-m}}{k!}
$$

**Step-by-step derivation of the fraction of untransduced cells (k = 0):**

$$
P(0) = \frac{m^{0} \cdot e^{-m}}{0!}
$$

**Line of reasoning:** We substitute $k=0$ into the Poisson PMF. Since $0! = 1$ and $m^0 = 1$ by definition, these terms simplify to 1.

$$
P(0) = 1 \cdot e^{-m} = e^{-m}
$$

**Line of reasoning:** $P(0)$ represents the fraction of cells receiving **zero** viral particles — i.e., the **untransduced** population.

**Therefore, the transduction efficiency $\eta$ (fraction successfully transduced, $k \geq 1$) is:**

$$
\eta = 1 - P(0) = 1 - e^{-m}
$$

**Line of reasoning:** By the complement rule of probability, the fraction receiving at least one particle equals 1 minus the fraction receiving zero.

**Numerical evaluation for KTU-style problem:**

> *Question:* A lentiviral vector is added to 10⁶ T-cells at MOI = 3. What fraction remains untransduced?

$$
P(0) = e^{-3} \approx 0.0498
$$

**Line of reasoning:** Substitute $m = 3$ into the derived expression.

$$
\eta = 1 - 0.0498 = 0.9502 \approx 95.0\%
$$

**Answer:** $\approx 4.98\%$ untransduced, **$\approx 95.0\%$ transduction efficiency**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why MOI = 1 is the practical "sweet spot":** At $m=1$, $P(0) = e^{-1} \approx 0.368$, meaning ~37% of cells remain untransduced — a problem for dose consistency. At $m=3$, ~95% are transduced but with higher multi-integrant risk. Engineers optimize for **efficiency $\geq 80\%$** with **mean VCN $\leq 5$**.

---

## 3.3 Comparison Matrix: Choosing the Right Strategy

| Disease / Target | Cell accessibility | Best Strategy | Justification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **RPE65 blindness** (photoreceptors) | Post-mitotic, non-harvestable | **Somatic, in vivo** | Cannot culture photoreceptors; AAV2 intravitreal |
| **ADA-SCID** (lymphocytes/HSCs) | Harvestable | **Somatic, ex vivo** | HSCs harvestable, retroviral correction feasible |
| **B-ALL leukemia** (malignant B-cells) | Harvestable T-cells | **Somatic, ex vivo** | CAR-T requires complex genetic engineering |
| **SMA** (motor neurons) | Non-dividing neurons | **Somatic, in vivo** | AAV9 crosses blood-brain barrier; systemic IV |
| **Cystic fibrosis** (lung epithelium) | Accessible via aerosol | **Somatic, in vivo** | AAV delivered by nebulizer/instillation |
| **Huntington's disease** | Deep brain neurons | **Somatic, in vivo** | AAV injected stereotactically |
| **Sickle cell disease** | HSCs harvestable | **Somatic, ex vivo** | CRISPR editing of HSCs (Casgevy®) |
| **Mitochondrial disorder** | Oocyte-inherited | **Germ-line (theoretical)** | Only germ-line can prevent maternal transmission |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**
> **Differentiate between somatic and germ-line gene therapy. Which one is currently permitted in clinical practice, and why?**

**Model Answer (Memorize the 3-point structure):**

| Aspect | Somatic Gene Therapy | Germ-line Gene Therapy |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Target cells** | Body cells (somatic) | Gametes / zygote / early embryo |
| **Heritability** | NOT inherited by offspring | **Inherited** by all future generations |
| **Ethical status** | **Permitted** (regulated clinical trials and approved products) | **Prohibited** in humans by ICMR (India) and Oviedo Convention |

**Conclusion:** Somatic gene therapy is the **only clinically permitted** form. Germ-line therapy is banned due to:
1. **Ethical concerns** — risk of eugenics, "designer babies."
2. **Safety concerns** — off-target mutations become heritable, irreversibly affecting the human gene pool.
3. **Lack of consent** — future generations cannot consent to permanent genetic modification.

*(3 marks: 1 mark definition, 1 mark comparison, 1 mark justification)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
> **With a suitable example, explain the in vivo approach of gene therapy.**

**Model Answer:**

In vivo gene therapy involves the **direct delivery of a therapeutic gene into the patient's body** using a viral or non-viral vector, where the genetic modification occurs **inside the living organism**.

**Steps:**
1. A **replication-deficient viral vector** (e.g., AAV, adenovirus) is engineered to carry the therapeutic gene.
2. The vector is administered directly — by **intravenous, intravitreal, intrathecal, or intramusclular injection**.
3. The vector binds to receptors on target cells, enters via endocytosis, and releases the therapeutic DNA.
4. The gene is expressed by host cellular machinery, producing the missing/defective protein.

**Example — Luxturna® (voretigene neparvovec):**
- Treats **Leber congenital amaurosis** caused by RPE65 gene mutations.
- Uses **AAV2 vector** carrying a functional RPE65 gene.
- Administered via **intravitreal injection** into the subretinal space.
- Photoreceptor/RPE cells are transduced **in vivo**, restoring the visual cycle.

*(3 marks: 1 mark definition, 1 mark mechanism, 1 mark example)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Ex vivo Gene Therapy Focus**
> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 14 Marks]**
>
> **(a)** Describe the complete workflow of **ex vivo gene therapy** with a suitable example. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compare and contrast **in vivo and ex vivo gene therapy** strategies. Discuss the advantages and limitations of each. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

Ex vivo gene therapy is a strategy where **target cells are removed from the patient, genetically modified in the laboratory, and reinfused back**. The classical example is **CAR-T cell therapy (e.g., Kymriah®)** for B-cell malignancies.

**Step 1 — Cell Harvest:** Patient undergoes **leukapheresis** to obtain T-lymphocytes from peripheral blood.
**Step 2 — Activation:** T-cells are activated using **anti-CD3/CD28 beads** in serum-free media with IL-2.
**Step 3 — Genetic Modification:** A **lentiviral vector** carrying the anti-CD19 CAR gene transduces the T-cells. The CAR encodes scFv, hinge, co-stimulatory domain (4-1BB), and CD3ζ.
**Step 4 — Expansion:** Cells are expanded in bioreactors (G-Rex or rocking-bags) for 7–14 days using IL-2/IL-7/IL-15.
**Step 5 — Quality Control:** Tests include transduction efficiency (flow cytometry), VCN by qPCR ($\leq 5$ copies/cell), sterility, mycoplasma, and potency assays.
**Step 6 — Conditioning:** Patient receives **lymphodepleting chemotherapy** (cyclophosphamide + fludarabine).
**Step 7 — Reinfusion:** Modified CAR-T cells are infused IV; they home to lymphoid tissues and kill CD19⁺ B-cells (malignant and normal).
**Step 8 — Monitoring:** Watch for **cytokine release syndrome (CRS)** and **ICANS**; treat with tocilizumab and corticosteroids as needed.

*(7 marks: 1 mark introduction, 5 marks for the 7 steps, 1 mark for clinical context)*

### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

| Parameter | In vivo | Ex vivo |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Site of modification** | Inside the patient | In a laboratory (in vitro) |
| **Target cells** | Non-harvestable (neurons, photoreceptors, muscle) | Harvestable (HSCs, T-cells, keratinocytes) |
| **Vectors** | AAV, adenovirus, LNPs | Lentivirus, retrovirus, electroporation |
| **Immunogenicity** | Higher (vector exposed to immune system) | Lower (cells washed before return) |
| **Quality control** | Limited | **High** (cells can be screened) |
| **Manufacturing cost** | Lower | Higher (GMP facility required) |
| **Turnaround time** | Immediate | 2–4 weeks |
